title: Garlicky Shrimp With Zucchini and Beans Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: This was nice and bright.  My only change is I cooked the shrimp first, seasoned with salt, pepper, garlic powder and a pop of smoked paprika, then set aside.  I also seared the zucchini for some color but only partially cooked, then set aside.  Proceeded with the recipe, added zucchini back in to finish cooking, added shrimp on top before serving.
@MHCF I like your version!
@MHCF yes! we almost always cook shrimp first and separate so it doesn’t get overcooked
Clam juice... Is this a "must?"
@Jane clam juice always adds great flavor and it’s pretty easy to find in most grocery stores! don’t knock til you try it
@Jane There wasn’t any at the store I was at, so I used water and a splash of fish sauce. I’d say it was a great substitution
@Nadia thank you! As someone who is allergic to clams, I always just skip that ingredient. This is an excellent substitute idea.

I added pepper flakes with garlic to allow them to release their oils, then beans and clam juice. I let the beans cook for about 5 minutes before adding shrimp to cook them down a little and release the starch. Then shrimp and lemon juice, turn down the heat. Don’t «cook» shrimp, just let it sit in hot pan until pink and cooked through. 

Last add butter to make a sauce. It was delicious!

Tasty! Be sure to follow the instructions and stick to just the white and light green parts of the leeks. Felt like it could use a hint more acid, so I may try adding a small summer tomato with the beans and shrimp.
Double the lemon! You’ll thank me later!!
I had leftover without schrmp - made a lovely breakfast topped with poached egg.
The recipe is delicious on its own, but I added thinly sliced fresh fennel to the leeks which added a perfect pop to this.
Similar to Lemony Shrimp and Bean Stew but I like this more. The zucchini and clam juice work well and give it a very nice flavor.
@Carol I thought the same thing! I make that dish quite a lot. I will try this new variation.
Use two cans of Beans
I didn't have clam juice, but I had some dashi packets and used that. Came out great!

Perfect!  Our garden is exploding with zucchini, yellow and pattypan squash, best picked when they are only a couple of inches in size, some with flowers still attached.  I'll add fresh shrimp and revel in a fantastic summer meal.  Leftovers are even better the next day.   Thank you for this one, NYT!
This dish has lovely, fresh flavor and is perfect for a light spring/summer meal. Would work well as a first plate with a heartier meal too.
It would also work for a light lunch. Or a heavier mid-day meal as well.
Great easy, healthy dinner.  Made as written except I cooked my own Rancho Gordo beans instead of using canned.  The clam juice really adds a good flavor to the broth.
Instead of clam juice you could make a quick stock with shrimp shells
@Jean That’s what I was thinking! We’ll be shelling the shrimp anyway, why throw them away and buy something else?
I'm not sure the shrimp shells add anything. It would be much better to use shrimp heads

@Pepe Luis I found it surprising what I got out of simmering shrimp shells.   To All:  For best flavor, simmer shrimp shells ONLY 5 MINUTES.  Google for Fish Stock from Shrimp shells. There’s a few sites that show the research on this shorter stewing time.
Could I replace the clam juice with white wine?
I'm planning half chicken stock and half white wine. Clam juice is hard to find around here.
I order clam juice from Amazon.
@Carol clamazon was right there
A new riff on one of our favorites, the lemony shrimp and white bean stew! Excellent way to refresh the recipe.
I used my own fish stock instead of clam juice, turned out great.

Large shrimp (16-18 per pound) need only 3 minutes cooking max (the 4-5 min called for here will have the shrimp overcooked).  Smaller shrimp need even less time. I like my zucchini crisp-tender so put them in at same time as shrimp (cooked 3 min).  Very delicious and fresh dish!
This is not a post fromThis Tessa... I am the OG. But she sounds correct, so I will follow her advice.
To make this a more Thai-adjacent recipe, substitute lime juice for the lemon.
@JD”Thai-adjacent”- terrific phrase!
@JD And sub fish sauce for the clam juice. Have fun w a Thai pepper, Thai basil and/or cilantro.
Like others have said, boiling  the shells for a few minutes makes a flavorful broth.   While clam juice adds lots of flavor, I find it a bit too salty

This was an awesome dish, even better if I added the red pepper flakes earlier!

Though this was a poor way to discover my fiancée was allergic to either shrimp or clam juice (both of which she’s had plenty of before)!
I live in Europe....clam juice is impossible to find.  What can I use instead?
If you can get fish broth, that would work, otherwise buy shrimp with shell and make a broth with the shells.
This was a delightful, simple but tasty meal. Crusty garlic bread a must. The only addition I made was to add lemon zest with the lemon juice. A little white wine in the sauce would be good, too.

This is like a shrimp scampi dish over beans and veggies instead of spaghetti. In fact it would be good over spaghetti if you want more carbs. I cooked my beans from scratch and added some white wine; otherwise followed the recipe. Next time I’ll follow a reader’s suggestion to pre-cook the shrimp in garlic and spices/herbs for more taste, then add to the top of the stew to reheat. 
Comes together very quickly!
What could you substitute for the clam juice?
@Don P. Fish broth or cook the shrimp shells to make a shrimp broth
@Theater Goer, good idea—flavor with less sodium.
Can I use frozen shrimp?
Could you sub chicken stock for clam juice? Not a fan of clam juice....
I was wondering the same.
This sounds yummy but my sis is allergic to shellfish.  What about subbing chicken for the shrimp and using white wine instead of the clam juice?
What could we use besides clam juice? We’re on a lower sodium diet for health reasons.
With what can the clam juice be replaced? In my country this is not available.
@Suzanne if your shrimp has shells don’t throw away. Sauté them a little then add water or a mix of water and

Sauté/simmer the shrimp shells for 5 minutes only. The flavor compounds are highly volatile, so cooking longer will yield a very bland stock. You’re better off planning ahead and making your own clam juice if you can get your hands on some clams or mussels: shrimp stock doesn’t compare.
@Sarah F , We live in southern Portugal and am going to try this cooking local clams in garlic, white wine and butter - use this liquid as clam broth and add cooked clams in final step

Just made this tonight! SO good!! I did cook the shrimp separately and briefly, as suggested in the comments. Also cooked the zucchini separately with the leeks, garlic and red pepper and set aside. Then added the beans to the clam broth and simmered for a bit. Added back the zucchini and leeks when the liquid had reduced and finally the shrimp and butter and chives for just a warming period. This all helped the zucchini stay somewhat crunchy and the shrimp moist and not overcooked. Definitely serve with good crusty bread for sopping up the juices.

I loved this recipe! Instead of using clam juice, I did a half cup of chicken broth mixed with a half cup of Sauvignon Blanc. Perfect! At the end, drizzled a little bit of Calabrian chili oil over the top. 😋
I used vegetable broth instead of the clam juice and it worked nicely.

Delicious.  Made exactly per recipe and my wife and I really enjoyed it.  Maybe add a little extra lemon to brighten up the flavors.  Did need a few pinches of salt to improve overall flavor.   The red pepper flakes are a must, IMO!

This was absolutely delicious, especially considering the little work involved. My daughter took one bite and said "this tastes like summer". I followed the recipe exactly, other than adding more squash than called for. Served it with a rosemary sourdough bread for dipping, and the flavors complimented each other quite nicely. Today, I took the leftovers and turned them into a pasta salad as mentioned in the caption, and I think that's almost better than dinner last night!

This was a delicious recipe. I increased most of the ingredient measures by a bit to support a 1.25 lbs of shrimp and instead of upping the clam juice added some white wine to the poaching liquid. Delish.

5 Stars! I cooked this EXACTLY as recipe dictated, even timing the sautéing exactly as indicated, and using 5 garlic cloves, even though mine were huge (we love garlic) and this was AMAZING! I used half zucchini and half yellow squash, maybe more red pepper flakes than indicated and fresh baked hot crusty bread. This was so good my husband commented “We broke our Friday night Hot Wings ritual with something healthy and just as tasty!” Score!!!
As long as you are using clam juice,why not try recipe with clams versus shrimp.  Think will try as with other ingredients, may be very tasty.
Delicious! I don’t like clam juice and used vegetable broth.

This was excellent but I couldn’t resist cooking the leeks and zucchini longer to deepen the flavor. Seven minutes for the leeks, another seven-ish for the zucchini. Otherwise made as instructed and it was a delight.

I caramelized the heck out of the leeks and got some good char on the zucchini to add flavor, and then end result was ok, but I wouldn't use clam juice again - it gave it a little bit of a fishy flavor. Next time I might try white wine and chicken broth. Overall it's a good concept and it was easy to prepare.
This was perfect for a quick after work dinner.  Very tasty, very easy, filling. Perfect with a side salad.
I really liked this but made a few additions with some trepidation including some fresh ginger, coconut milk, and a handful of chard for a little color. Served with rice, came out great.
Has anyone used white wine instead of clam juice? Asking for a friend.
I just made this and just happened to have clam juice. But as I poured it in, I thought I could have used white wine -(head slap) which I always have on hand. Go for it.
Fantatic as written. Topped with grated parm, chopped crispy bacon and sourdough croutons. Oh, my. Keeper!

Protein, fiber, quick, easy, and delicious! This one will be a dinner staple as long as squash is in season. Only change I made was a dollop of Calabrian chili paste instead of red pepper flakes for a bigger kick that carried throughout the dish.

This such a lovely, bright summer dish, and really can be prepped in 30 mins if you start with shelled and deveined shrimp. We substitute butter beans and use two cans instead of one. And 5 large garlic cloves (probably equal to 10 medium). Perfect!

We couldn't find clam juice at our grocery so we subbed chopped clams in their juices (6oz) which worked out well since we were also short on shrimp. We also didn't rinse the beans. Came out so so good, definitely added to our rotation!
Can this be made ahead of time?
Very similar to the OG Hershey cocoa  recipe I’ve used for years. It calls for buttermilk.  This batter won’t look like any cake batter you ever made, but trust the process. It is wonderful
@Jo KUKUKA oh oh wrong recipe!

Replacing zucchini with yellow summer squash because an influential partner doesn’t like bitterness that zucchini skins sometimes have. This is quite similar to the bean and shrimp stew elsewhere in Cooking.

Made this tonight & took the advice of some others by adding a 2nd can of beans & more lemon juice.  It was really good!  I had some frozen, cooked shrimp, so used that.  Thawed 1st then added 4 minutes after adding the beans, just to warm through.  Will definitely make this again.

I made this last night for my husband and we both loved it! I cut the amount of oil and butter by 1/3. I substituted chicken broth with a splash of fish sauce and white wine. Doubled the lemon juice.  Paired with my homemade sourdough bread and white wine. A nice, light summery dish.

Clam juice is so flavorful! Treat yourself to any kind of bivalve mollusk (except scallops and oysters - too expensive and their broth is too subtle), steamed with some wine/water until the mollusks open and release their juices. freeze the liquid from the bottom of the pot for the next time you need clam juice. Anyplace near the sea will have some kind edible mollusk, Cockles, mussels, razor clams, quahogs, soft shell clams (called steamers in New England), and will be better than bottled.
I’m allergic to zucchini. Would yellow squash (which I can eat) work?

Lovely, delicately flavored country stew. The cooking times were off throughout; leek and squash needed more time. If no shrimp on hand, any stable white fish (hake, haddock, cod, tilapia) would suffice. If no "crusty" bread available, croutons would be good. Final comment, this is not a full meal - something else needed to complete/complement the stew.

Oh what to say here … this is an overrated recipe for flavor vs effort and the estimated  time of prep plus cook is laughable.   The recipe is extremely mid.   I put all effort into it.   I doubled the recipe — made a reduced shrimp stock from the shells - (+ paprika, garlic)  for 50% and purchased a high quality clam juice for the other 8oz.   I followed the most helpful hints for when to add and remove the zucchini and leeks etc so to preserve crunch — and 2 1/2 hours later in this total 30” prep …we ate an extremely mediocre recipe.     This is not my usual experience with NYT recipes —& I will never make this again.     My family agrees.   It’s okay.  Not great.
How could this possibly have taken you 2.5 hours to make? I can't fathom what you did.
I found it a little bland, even with the crushed red pepper. I added some Herb de Provence and this made it much more flavorful.

Cooked pretty much as written except juiced whole lemon, maybe 3 T. Love the shrimp/white bean combination. We alway sub aleppo pepper for pepper flakes.  Quick — complète mise en place is crucial.  Might try some saffron some time.
Clam broth is often found near the canned tuna and anchovies. Not in the aisle with chicken, beef and soups.
I doubled the recipe and used a teaspoon of crushed red pepper. Only had 2 bottles of clam juice but had seafood stock. So I added 2 cups of seafood stock. This recipe is a keeper.

Added a 4 zucchini
Double leek
2lb shrimp
More red pepper 
All else same

A more dense soup, which made it amazing.
Being Keto, I omitted the beans. Terrific.

Was skeptical because it looked bland but so good! I marinated the shrimp briefly in olive oil, chipotle powder and Old Bay. Old Bay is salty so will cut down on salt in recipe next time. Added more lemon juice than recipe calls. Next time will herb it up: mint, basil, etc…Delicious.
This was fantastic! We made it last night but increased it to 1 and 1/2. We stretched the clam juice with about a half cup of white table wine. Perfect dinner for a perfect night outside!

Doubled the recipe. It worked out great! Followed the recipe to a tee. 
Delicious!

I doubled the garlic and added finely chopped chorizo 
Delicious!!!

My Safeway claimed they had clam juice, and I spent twenty minutes searching. Took one of the butchers to find it hidden on a top shelf above the canned tuna fish. I'm using two bottles of it and added more beans and lemon juice.

Very tasty. I may have slightly overcooked the shrimp. I will have to investigate how to prevent it from continuing to cook once ready while still in the Dutch oven. 

First time deveining shrimp too. Next time I’ll have the fish store do it.
Why would one add the crushed red pepper at the end rather than bloom it in the oil at the beginning?
Agree with the comment that more lemon juice would make this even better, but great and easy dish.

Such a lovely dish and very easy to make! I doubled the zucchini, lemon and crushed red pepper. Otherwise followed the recipe and instructions. Next time I might add even more lemon. Don’t be afraid of clam juice…it adds great flavor. I never thought zucchini and beans would go with shrimp but this dish is perfect. Highly recommend!

Like MHCF, I quickly and separately seared the shrimp (16-20 Argentine Reds) and the squash (mix of zuke and summer) to part-way, added them at the finish. Also added fresh corn, steamed, stripped from the cob (threw the cob in w/the juice add, also a large lemon peel strip). A good glug of Thai anchovy sauce right after the leek/garlic sauté. S&P in small doses throughout. Some garlic toasts to accompany. Bright flavors, but still savory; worth it.

This tastes like Spring! Made as described. I was worried I didn't cook the zucchini enough, but it was perfect with a little crunch. Used my own "Big Pot of Beans" with a little of the broth which made it divine.
I was excited to try this. Followed the instructions exactly but I found it to be a little meh. Too soupy and not enough zing.

Love, love, love this recipe.  I didn’t have zucchini so I added spinach and shiitake mushrooms and it was delightful.  I think the recipe is a good base that can be customized to what’s in your pantry.

This was the worst NYT recipe I've made. I made the recipe exactly as written, and did not skimp on the salt and pepper; it was very bland and lackluster. My husband said it was almost inedible. I was disappointed because it sounded really good, but the flavors just weren't there. Will not make again. Sue Li's Lemony Shrimp and Bean Stew is much better and yields a delicious dinner!
This was SO easy & delicious. Used half zucchini and yellow squash for color and served on a bed of fresh spinach with quartered grape tomatoes.
Easy and fabulous!
I didn't have leeks, so I used an entire bunch of scallions. I chopped them but not very finely, and added after cooking the zucchini and garlic. Turned out delicious!
The only change I made was to cut the shrimp in thirds before adding them, so they were closer to the size of the other ingredients.  Delicious and easy.

Cooking for 2 so did 3/4 of a lb of shrimp but then accidentally added the whole thing of clam juice so had too much broth. Took the shrimp out then cooked down the clam juice for a while. Unfortunately this made the beans dissolve a bit but we added a ton of parm and it was DELICIOUS. I tried to lick the spoon. I really think this dish needs parm.
This was good, not great. There was something missing and I couldn't put my finger on it. Very quick and easy to put together.
I thought the same -- something missing but not sure exactly what. White wine? Extra lemon juice? Another herb (dill, parsley, fennel)?

Thank you for such precise measurements. Although, like most cooks, I  don’t measure ingredients when I’m just making a meal without a recipe. But when I do follow a recipe I like to make it as the writer did, which involves more than bunches and handfuls!
Kinda meh.  A bit bland and muddy. Served with white rice.

@hilary- yes, you can. Or white Vermouth. . Vermouth is a handy fortified wine to have in the pantry. It stores well, even when it has been opened. It is infused with herb so it is very food friendly.

We grilled the shrimp & zucchini then placed them in top of the finished beans. Served with crusty sourdough bread. Triple the recipe if you want leftovers. I recommend being extra generous with both garlic & lemon quantities. My kids licked their plates clean.

I made the recipe pretty much as written. I actually doubled it, added the red pepper flakes at the beginning and forgot to sauté the garlic so I threw it in with the shrimp and beans. As others suggested, I added extra lemon juice. This is a keeper. So easy and so delicious.
Loved it! Made it twice in a week!
Simple, delicious and elevated mid week meal.  I add a splash of white wine,  extra red pepper and lemon. I like the idea of seasoning and searing  the shrimp first. Will try next time.

This is really delicious. I omitted the zucchini because I forgot to purchase but it turned out great and very easy. This is a great use of pantry/freezer supplies. I used frozen shrimp with no issues just watched them closely so as not to overcook. I keep sauted leeks in the freezer too and always have beans and lemon on hand. Definitely going into rotation.
There are very few New York Times recipes that I won’t make again. This is one of them.

I subbed a couple of tablespoons fish sauce for the clam juice with water to make 8 oz of liquid. Too salty. Next time, 1 tablespoon of fish sauce. Or I'll just buy clam juice. I also doubled the lemon juice. A delicious and simple meal. Note: don't cook smaller shrimp more than 3 minutes, medium shrimp 4 minutes tops!
I doubled the clam juice 16 oz. This dish tasted special. Making it again this weekend.

Absolutely delicious, and so simple to make! I took the advice of other commenters and added a teaspoon or so of lemon zest and a few tablespoons of grated pecorino romano. I add white wine to nearly everything, so in went 1/4 c of chardonnay. Cooked zucchini and leeks a little longer than stated to soften them up a bit more. Great summer meal!
Definitely need to cook the shrimp first and set aside.
cook shrimp first , remove and return with lemon juice and chives.  add 1/2 cup white wine before clam broth

title: Beef, Asparagus and Tofu Stir-Fry Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I made this dish according to the recipe and it was very tasty.  I will definitely make this again.  My only substitution was avocado oil for safflower oil because that's what I had.  I love that it stretches ground beef, especially with current prices.  It is a perfect recipe to use the abundance of asparagus in the spring.
Excellent recipe, but I had to double to feed 4.
I used broccolini in Australian winter and it worked great!

Fabulous recipe! For crunchier tofu, slice into 1/2 inch cubes. Toss with a little oil and cornstarch. Bake in 425-F oven for 10-15 minutes. I also recommend doubling it if you're feeding 4 heathy appetites.

Isn't the point of eating tofu, to dispense with meat? 
Tofu should replace the meat, shouldn't it?
@S Woo It can, but it doesn’t always.  Recipes like this can help reduce dependency on animal protein for those not ready to give it up.  And they can just be tasty recipes.

Tofu is delicious and also good for you, (and the planet.) Also, super economical. It is not a punishment, or a replacement, or a substitute, per se. I plan on making this with Impossible Burger or Beyond Burger, as I cook for someone who does not eat meat; an easy recipe substitute for anyone who does not want to consume beef.

@Someone Who Cooks lol the carbon footprint of the production of beyond beef and the impossible burger is worse than actual beef. Look it up.
Plus, those things are filled with chemicals

Made as written, this is tasty comfort food. The tofu soaks up the sauce and pairs well with the texture of the ground beef. My only issue was that I couldn’t get the tofu crumbles to crisp, despite pressing and draining  the tofu. I could try baking them next time, but the lack of crispiness didn’t seem to affect the overall dish. I doubled the recipe to serve four and have some leftovers for lunch the next day. Served with quick pickles and lots of basil, mint, and cilantro.
This is easily a 2 hour dinner, I’m not sure where the 30 minutes comes from. I even used microwaved rice.
Great flavors!  Could it be made with skirt steak?
@Me I think so, just adjust cooking time.
This is dinner tonight, but I think I'll make it with half ground beef, half ground pork.

Delicious recipe!  Thank you!  Quick and seasonal with the asparagus. I used a pound of ground beef, but stretching with tofu is a good idea. I plan to share with my WIC clients who can choose tofu. Mushrooms were a good addition.

Reasonably good recipe. Appreciated the opportunity to use tofu & seasonal asparagus. Once the mise en place is done, which took time, it does come together quickly. While it’s an international audience here, I wish the recipe was scaled for US sized packages of tofu & beef.
@S Woo like mapo tofu, it includes both. I use plant-based ground in recipes like this.

Tried this recipe this evening and it was a big hit. I thought I had tofu, but didn't, so added some sliced mushrooms instead. Threw in a few halved cherry tomatoes towards the end to add a bit of color. This serves two or possibly three, but not four. Moving it to my "Make Again" folder.
Impossible meat works great with this recipe.
Great base to explore. Used groind turkey and loved it.  Will add mushrooms, Thai basil and some green peppers next time.
Beef!? In this economy?
I'm not a big fan of tofu... I want to double the gr beef... thoughts?
Any thoughts on what happens if you add more tofu and skip the ground beef to make vegetarian?

We don’t eat meat so I’m considering using a mixture of mushrooms (processed into crumbles), cooked on high heat to get rid of the moisture/add flavor & tj’s extra firm tofu, crumbled. I think I’d add a bit of fish sauce (or vegan equivalent) to the tofu for additional umami (we’re pescatarians).  Then proceed with the recipe. I like using a mix of mushroom and tofu to mimic ground beef.
Too many ingredients for a busy working mom.
Why do they have to keep finding new “neutral” oils with names I’ve never heard of and definitely don’t keep in my pantry?
"Low" sodium broth, soy sauce plus oyster sauce = lots of sodium.
I made this with ground chicken only (no tofu) and it’s delicious. The sauce gives it plenty of flavoring. I will make a bit extra sauce next time since I like to have more to top the rice.

This was absolutely delicious and will become a regular for us. We substituted ground turkey and it worked perfectly. The combination of ground meat and crumbled tofu is so light and delicious. We think it would work equally well with well chopped broccoli or snow peas as well.

This was a great meal however I substituted beef for ground bison. So I needed more oil in the pan.
I also wanted it more veggie heavy and added one chopped zucchini and served it with soba noodles. 
Delicious. I would increase the volume of sauce.
I’ve never met an asparagus that gets “crisp-tender” (?) in two minutes…needed to add some water to speed up the cooking. But nice flavors.

I was so disappointed in this recipe. All of the flavors should have been great, but it tasted like nothing and I found myself frowning trying to eat it. Wish I hadn’t bothered, do not make this recipe.
@anne is wrong. This was fine.

@anne

Totally agree, this was one of the most bland recipe I have tried from NYT.
Easy and delicious. My tofu didn’t caramelize even with heat and time, but I’m attributing that to the brand.

This is a lovely recipe, easy and tasty.  One hour from entering the kitchen to plating.  Made precisely as written.  The only downside is using part of a package of tofu, my household is not great at finishing off odds and ends.

I made this pretty much as written. It was quite good. Next time I would replace the ground beef with ground pork. I would also add shallots to the meat as I cook it. For me, this recipe needed shallot in addition to the green onion. The asparagus was perfect in this.
Four servings???? I doubled the recipe.  My son and I ate the whole thing, with rice, and could have eaten more. Absolutely delicious.

Delicious and surprisingly easy to make. After reading the comments, I microwaved the tofu on high for 2 minutes before adding it to the pan, which helped it crisp up nicely. I also cut the asparagus into small ½-inch pieces, as the recipe suggested, and they cooked perfectly. I made with 90% ground beef ( vs 80%) and it still tastes great!

Loved this recipe for its overall simplicity and deliciousness. I decided to double the recipe because I didn't want to end us with a half package of tofu. Doubling this recipe works well and serves 4 to 5, or makes a great leftover option for a two.

I replaced the asparagus for broccolini I had on hand. delicious, with a clean and bright finish. My five year old and husband both enjoyed, which is a rarity. paired with steamed cabbage and trumpet mushrooms.  Will definitely make again.

Great use of asparagus in a dinner recipe! Timing guidance was great! And pretty quick for after-work cooking. We doubled the recipe mainly because we had a pound of (Beyond) beef and a pound of tofu. Used vegan versions of sauce ingredients and didn't double the sugar, though. Served over brown rice. Nice that the base recipe is flexible enough to welcome hot sauce for those that like it and flavorful for those that enjoy it as-is. I think it would be great with green beans later in the season.
I am new to the tofu world. This recipe has convinced me that I’ve been missing out. I made this tonight and wow, it was a hit. Very healthy, very filling and now on my list of “make again” recipes!
Doubled this and made it with green beans because I couldn't find asparagus - it was fantastic as meal prep for a few dinners.
Took closer to an hour to prepare, not 30 minutes. Not a fan of ginger, I skipped it.  Absolutely delicious and will make again, doubling the recipe and freezing part for later.  Enjoy!

followed the recipe with some tweaks: used a pound of ground beef (while keeping the tofu 8oz). I also tripled the amount of sauce, because in my family we love sauce. also deglazed the pan with more chicken broth after browning the tofu. we loved it and found it very tasty and umami forward. to make it even better, i feel like it could have had a touch of brightness at the end. maybe vinegar or citrus. and/or try another commenters suggestions of fresh herbs on top. will make it again!
Any suggestions for a substitute for oyster sauce ? I have an allergy to crustaceans….
@Tchr I wonder if fish sauce would work?

@Tchr if you have only a crustacean allergy oyster should be ok for you as it is a mollusk. But if you have a full blown shellfish allergy you can use items like hoisin sauce, some fish sauce, or Worcestershire sauce.
@Tchr oyster sauce is made from mollusks and not crustaceans. But if you are allergic to all shellfish you can try hoisin sauce.
Will this work using all ground beef?
I’d like to make a veg version. Anybody try this with Abbots faux ground beef sub for beef?  How best to  sub oyster sauce?
@MonicaM You can buy vegetarian “oyster” sauce made with mushrooms
think this world work with ground poultry?
Do you think it would work with ground chicken? I have some in the freezer that I would like to use up.
I made it tonight with ground chicken and it was great. I had a 16 oz package of chicken and a 14-oz package of tofu, so I doubled everything else. Next time I'll make even more sauce.
Could you use ground chicken or turkey? Would that work?
Any suggestions for a tofu substitute?  I really dislike tofu
My husband does not like tofu.  Could I use boneless, skinless chicken thigh pieces as well?
Would it freeze well?
I would like to make this, but will sub seitan for beef. No fish sauce because of allergies.
What’s a good substitution for oyster sauce? One of my crew has a shellfish allergy
@Miriam You can find vegan oyster sauce!
What can I use in place of oyster sauce?
They make vegan/mushroom “oyster” sauce. I know Lee Kim kee makes one. I buy mine from Whole Foods: not sure of the brand it’s the organic vegan one.
My family very much enjoyed this.
Rather than making the sauce (step1), is there a bottled substitute?
Laughable how 45 'reviews' warrant a 5 star, it'll be 3 stars in a week.
I'm planning to make this. Can I use vegan ground meat?
seems like a super recipe, but as I'm not allowed to eat tofu I'm just ging to leave it out

Hi: I don't think the tofu was dry enough, as it never turned golden after 10 mins.  I too substituted avocado oil.  I could have double the asparagus. I think it would be great with toasted sesame seeds. And I used regular sesame oil, but toasted would have been better even though it's a drop. It turned out well and tasted even better!
I used homemade bone broth instead of the chicken broth and mixed green beans with aspargus. It was a real success!

I loved the spice/sauce combination but my husband doesn’t like tofu and I don’t like to eat beef so we compromised and made this with a pound of ground turkey and no tofu or ground beef!  I added mushrooms and it was delicious!
This recipe is definitely a keeper! I used ground turkey as that's all I had and it was good but I will use beef next time.

This was just perfect! I used a pound of lean ground beef along with the tofu so we'd have leftovers. Didn't have oyster sauce so used hoisin and a bit of extra chicken broth. All the prep added significantly to the time, but it was so worth it!!

I made this according to the recipe in every way, but I used vegan ground "beef" and reduced the scallions to half (my daughter doesn't like them). Although our daughter liked the final dish, my husband and I were not that thrilled with it. It really wasn't that flavorful to us. Sorry.

Delicious! I actually don't have any neutral oil at the moment, so I used my "sizzle" olive oil, and it came out great. Cutting the asparagus into short pieces (I cut at an angle) meant that the fibrous bits were quite edible. A warning: the tofu stuck to my cast iron pan worse than eggs. I guess I need to  work on the pan's seasoning.
I quadrupled sauce ingredients and added extra broth, but this was still fairly dry. Even with low sodium ingredients, this recipe was way too salty. Prep and cook time was at least an hour.
Very nice quick weeknight dinner.  Followed the recipe as is, it was perfect.
This was so yummy and so easy! I added more tofu and some broccoli but otherwise I followed the recipe word for word.
We're pretty adventurous eaters and we thought this was terrible. I absolutely love tofu and asparagus but this didn't work for us.
Delicious recipe. Great flavors in the sauce. I had trouble with my tofu sticking to the pan and not turning golden. Any suggestions? Otherwise I really enjoyed the dish.

Excellent recipe!  Used Canola oil.  Don't add more tofu or beef in order to use up a package.  8 oz of each is perfect for the sauce amount.  My husband wanted double the sauce (he's a super salt guy).  I try to be healthy and was happy with the amount in this recipe.  Serve with white rice.  Delicious!  Going on rotation!
Very tasty and easy to make for a quick meal. Made as directed but doubled it for leftovers. Will keep in the rotation
Made exactly according to the recipe except for using Beyond meat instead of ground beef.  It was SO good. Will keep it in the rotation! But I agree, it is NOT a 30min prep. Delicious meal!

I thought this was OK. I added sirracha for a little more punch. It was fairly quick and easy, for sure, but I'm not sure I would make it again. I did like the sauce. I think, for me, it might be better with chicken chunks. I admit I'm not a huge fan of ground beef. I was happy for the addition of tofu.
Pretty good, but I usually use ground beef with less fat. It’s. It about not eating meat; it’s about adding more tofu to your eating habits.

I used frozen then thawed, squeezed and crumbled tofu; texturally pleasing and easy to deal with. My tofu-adverse husband enjoyed this dish, and the leftover protein portion was delicious incorporated into some ramen. Threw on some chopped peanuts for crunch

This was delicious! Served over ramen. My only complaint is now I have thawed half a pound of beef and drained half a block of tofu so it would probably have been better to have just doubled the entire meal. Also the tofu stuck to my stainless steel pan so I’ll have to figure that out.

I followed the recommendation in the Most Helpful comments of pre-baking the tofu (cut into 1/2" size pieces) with some oil and cornstarch, adding it at the end of the stirfry process.   This was a delicious meal--the prep of all the items does take longer than 1/2 hour.  More like an hour for prepping the items and then 15-30 minutes for the actual stir-fry.  Will make this again.

title: Brothy Spinach and Peas Pasta  Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Could use a hit of acid, maybe some lemon juice or preserved lemon.
@Val Lemon juice elevated this dish a lot! Delicious!
This is very nice, but not vegetarian, unless you substitute for the chicken stock.
@David Indeed, and parmesan
@David It calls for vegetable broth not chicken stock. Perhaps the recipe has been modified since you commented?

Took some fun turns with this and it was incredible. Used bigger pasta noodles, I always double the garlic in recipes, threw in some frozen corn, leftover pesto that was gonna go bad and added in zest & juice of a lemon. Served with bread and pork tenderloin. Phenomenal meal!

This is really good. I used tepary beans because I'm not a huge fan of blackeyed peas, only had 5 oz of baby spinach so I supplemented with some pea shoots. Next time I'd consider chopping the spinach roughly, there were just a lot of stems. Might increase the parm. Definitely will make this again!
@MJ I was thinking cannelini beans.
I used cannelini beans - it was good.
What’s the best substitute for those who are not a fan of black-eyed peas? Chickpeas? White beans?
@MJ I like the idea of garbanzo beans, because then you’ve got a springtime pasta e ceci!

This was delicious and very filling. I used chard instead of spinach and orzo because that's what I had. A squeeze of lemon at the end was nice. I doubled this so we'd have leftovers and I'm glad I did. This is my new go-to beans and greens stew.
I thought this was really good - light comfort food!

I added extra peas, carrots and black-eyed peas than the recipe calls for. I also added extra broth, garlic and Herbs de Provence creating a more savory flavor. I served this with some toasted sourdough, extra cheese and it is a new family favorite.
I didn’t have any dill, so I added a tablespoon of prepared pesto. I’m sure it would have been delicious with the dill, but the pesto was a more than adequate substitute.
I forgot — I skipped the dill! It was more of chicken noodle soup without the chicken. So good!

Perhaps the dish was too hot.  I recently did a deep
dive into Cacio e Pepe and learned the cheese becomes a clumpy mess if you add it to the pasta when it’s too hot.
@kmorrow I am preparing it right now and have decided I’m going to hold off on adding the Parmesan since I plan to enjoy it over a few days. Thanks for substantiating my approach.
We added some red pepper flakes with the garlic, it added a little punch to the broth.

can't eat dried beans/peas so used black lentils, and had Tuscan kale so used instead of spinach. also I always have parmesan broth cubes in my freezer made from all those rinds and added that. Great recipe and as others have said good for variations of convenience, too.
This is great! Highly recommend. I wanted a little kick to balance the sweet (a little sweet heat if you will), so I added some crushed red pepper and I must say I recommend it if you like spice.

I was impressed by how much flavor this had for something so simple. The cheese is essential for bringing it together. I used the better than bouillon roasted vegetable base and followed other commenters' advice to finish with a squeeze of lemon.
Made as directed.  Added a squeeze of lemon at the end.  I might add more spinach next time.  Loved it!

This recipe is perfect as written (the measurements don’t have to be exact, it’s not a cake). For what appears to be a simple dish reveals much complexity. I don’t agree that it needs vinegar or lemon, as many have suggested, because without the acid we are left to enjoy all these more subtle notes. This is comfort in a bowl and such an easy way to add veggies to your diet. Adding it to my regular rotation.

This is a really innovative recipe. I usually snort at the "If you do substitute every other ingredient, this recipe is FANTASTIC!" Anyway, I used my husband's good homemade poultry stock and sprayed in probably half a cup of boxed white wine, and I also used twice as much butter as the recipe calls for. That's not going to kill you. One additional note: don't skip the crushed red pepper or parmigiano. And I think it's a fantastic recipe.

Made this for an easy dinner and it turned out so good. My only changes were to add cremini mushrooms and some fresh shrimp that I had on hand and didn’t want to go to waste. Delicious and hearty meal. I think I will add some level of citrus next time, otherwise…definitely a win!
How would one use dried black-eyed peas instead of canned?

Was hoping to find a go-to veggie one pot meal. Unfortunately this was not a hit at my house. Not enough flavor. I used black eyed peas and black beans and it needed substantially more seasoning and broth.

Mmmmm…this is SO good! I followed my own advice I gave to another Comment Writer and subbed in garbanzo beans for the black-eyed peas, mostly because I didn’t want to run out to the store, but also because then this is a delightful springtime pasta e ceci. I used 3/4 cup diced yellow onion, a mix of chicken stock and vegetable Better than Bouillon, and Pecorino Romano in place of the Parmesan. My two tablespoons of butter were salted and wow, is that luxurious! I also stirred in the juice of half a lemon with the dill and the cheese. Most of my broth was soaked up by the ditalini, and my spinach sort of disappeared, so I’ll add probably 1 1/2 cups water, the rest of my 12 oz bag baby spinach, and the juice from the other half of the lemon when I heat the leftovers up on the stove.
This was an easy tasty dish! Added a parmesan rind when adding the broth. Added extra carrots and used spinach from our garden. Also served with homemade croutons!
This recipe is so yummy!! I swapped out broth for homemade chicken stock and cannellini beans for the black eyes peas and now obsessed with this dish.

Delicious! I used about 1.25 - 1.5 tsp Diamond Crystal salt in total. I wish recipes included a range of salt when they say salt to taste. 
I followed the quantities in the recipe exactly, though a few ingredients were substituted: 2/3 cup white wine in place of some broth, a medley of cooked beans and lentils that were in my freezer instead of black eyed peas, and basil (from the indoor garden) instead of dill. I would definitely include the white wine again. The basil was overpowered by other flavors, so I will make a point to get some dill next time.

We enjoyed this very much, and it's not much work. As recommended by others, I stirred about 1/2 t. red pepper flakes in with the garlic. Used cannellini beans and added them along with the ditalini and carrots to build their flavor. I recommend stirring in the spinach after turning off the heat. That way it wilts but doesn't overcook. Stirred in juice of half a lemon along with the dill and served the cheese at the table.

Thanks everyone who added comments- it was helpful. My family really enjoyed this recipe- the dill made it interesting. Like other commenters, I also added a dash of lemon, upped the butter and cheese a wee bit.  I had some extra mushrooms around so just threw those in as well and it was great. I love how this recipe can be adjusted to what you have around.The made enough for 3 and a good bit of left overs.  Next time, I’ll
I haven’t had any luck introducing Parmesan to a liquid. It always separates from the liquid. What am I doing wrong?
@April  I get the same result. In this case, don't mind because I will put it on for serving

@April
I used a microplane to grate a chunk of parm and that seemed to work

first of all-- use Reggiano-Parmigiano. Not kraft off the shelf or any domestic. Sorry I know that sounds snobby-- but you gotta use the REAL thing.  Introducing Parmesan or Pecorino to a liquid and making it glossy or creamy  just takes heat and a little patience. Seriously-- practice with cacio e pepe or alfredo-- which use no butter or cream.  Use your hot pasta liquid and keep stirring.  Believe me--- you WILL get it!! You WILL get the hang of it...
Is this better made a day ahead or is it good to eat right away?

@Joanne

The pasta may get mushy if you make it ahead
What would be a good substitute for spinach?

Extra peas
Add corn. Can sub kale or a mixture
Add pesto and add lemon juice at end or in bowl
Great recipe! After I tasted it with the Parmesan cheese I added nutritional yeast as well. Definitely improved the taste and consistency.

Wow, I’m amazed that I’m such an early Commenter!!! I made this because I had everything home for it (so I thought!), it seemed very fast—which it is!—and it’s relatively healthy. Two things happened that I think may have improved the dish:
1. I only had 2 bundles of soba noodles, so I made 6.5 oz of noodles instead of 8 oz, and in NYT Cooking app noodle math, this is the correct amount! 
2. My only smooth nut butter was almond butter. The only other comment on here is questioning the potentially bizarre combination of peanut butter and salmon, and my mistake eliminates this. Honestly, since I can’t speak to the experience the combination, I think I could go either way, but for now, Trader Joe’s creamy raw almond butter is working me!

I used nearly all the broccoli stems in addition to the florets. I had a half lime sitting in my fridge, so I juiced it and added it to the sauce. I also added waaaayyy more cilantro than 2 T, as well as 1/2 cup thinly sliced scallions. I then drizzled chili crisp and sprinkled toasted sesame seeds over everything. 

I’ll be making this again, for sure, but incorporate more lime juice.

I love to make this for meal prep - you can make such large portions and it keeps really well! Added mild + hot italian sausage and deglazed with some white wine. Used cannelini beans instead of black eyed peas and omitted the dill! Would probably 1.5x the spinach to make it greener

CZ - when you say you deglaze with some white wine, are you cooking the sausage in a pan and then de-glazing the little bits from the sausage, or are you using the wine during step 1 before the broth? In addition, when you meal prep, do you wait to add the pasta, or is it still good days later?
I added chopped fresh asparagus and English peas. No black eyed peas. Delicious, healthy and pretty.

Absolutely delicious as written. I made this for my 10-year old vegetarian daughter and it came together very quickly, has lots of fantastic and healthy ingredients, and tastes savory and nourishing, while also feeling light enough to be a spring/summer soup. I made some french bread to go alongside and sop up all that delicious broth. A total winner.
For a veggie and pasta lover like me, this one really hit a home rin. I amped up some of the veggies.  Divine.

this is akin to a minestrone with more cheese and a bit of dill, which i skipped because it's the kind of herb that overpowers everything. also if you're not finishing the entire portion, the pasta loses that great al-dente texture from absorbing the broth. nice to try, but doesn't earn a spot in my rotation

I made it exactly as written and it was delicious. It doesn't take long for the small pasta to cook so make sure you have everything else ready to go into the pot. A "few minutes shy of al dente" might only take a couple of minutes, depending on your pasta.
Wow that came out way better than i would have expected! No notes, 5 stars,

title: Lemony Miso Cabbage Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I can’t believe I get to comment first. This was good, and I assume similar to the addictive cabbage recipe. I rinsed the cabbage beforehand and the water took a while to evaporate. Also, I added quite a bit of lemon. It worked well with the fish I served, but I’d tone it down a bit next time to let the earthiness of the miso shine through.
@MHO did it hold up as a leftover?

This was excellent. I wanted to make it right away so I used a bag of shredded cabbage I already had on hand - cook time was a little shorter as a result and it did not need to go in the oven. Paired it with simple salmon grilled with mayo/sriracha marinade. Will definitely make again.
This was absolutely delicious.  Didn't have dill and didn't substitute.  Easy on the lemon.  Will make many more times.

One of the best things I’ve made in a long time, truly shows the beauty in simplicity! I used a big purple cabbage and made it for meal prep. I’ve been taking bites every time I’ve opened the fridge. It’s so addicting. Love that it tastes great hot or cold. New recipe obsession.

@Ami Check out Yasmin Fahr’s Miso-Roasted Broccoli if you enjoyed this cabbage. I haven’t tried this cabbage yet, but I certainly will soon. The miso broccoli immediately went into my regular rotation.

Very nice and easy as is. I stirred in tofu at the end (1 packed sliced and torn into dime sized pieces, tossed with corn starch, salt, pepper, sprayed with oil and roasted in the oven for 20-30 min). Will make a bit of additional sauce to better cover the tofu next time. Devoured it very warm, seems like it’ll be great chilled, too. Crispy chow mein noodles or peanuts could be nice to try on top.
I loved this! Used up a chunk of leftover cabbage and ate with a dollop of kimchi for lunch. Flexible, simple recipe. The butter miso combo is salty and creamy and delicious.
kimchi with this cabbage sounds bombbb, good rec will add it next time

great utilitarian side! made it alongside the “sheet-pan chicken and potatoes with feta, lemon and dill” and both turned out fantastic. would benefit from using a high quality miso so that that flavor really sings.
Delicious and super easy. Used ghee in place of butter. I should’ve waited till I added the miso to salt because it was a bit too salty.

I agree with the other cooks.  This is an easy fabulous recipe.  What a great way to make cabbage.  It's such an overlooked vegetable, but it's so cheap and plentiful all year round that I'm going to make it more often.  Once again, thank you NYT Cooking!
Really liked this. Made exactly as written. Easy prep and a unique flavor. Served with roasted salmon. Would make again.
This was divine and so easy. I could have eaten the whole pan myself and maybe next time I will!

This was good, but I wish I hadn't stirred up the brown bits on the bottom of the pan. The miso that stuck to the pan and was scooped up tasted burned and overpowered the cabbage, which was good overall.
Has anyone veganized this? Would olive or coconut or avocado oil work instead of the butter?
It never mentions when the cabbage should to into the oven, neither at what temperature. I guess it’s between steps 2 and 3.

title: Ginger Scallion Broccoli Salad Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Wouldn't steaming the broccoli allow more control over the cooking as well as avoiding the potential problem of excess water?

@carmencatt - I normally blanch my broccoli (and most green vegetables for that matter). I find it leaves them brighter and crisper. I find steaming inconsistently cooks things, and it’s harder to control how cooked things get.
If you are going to boil ( I’d say blanch) the broccoli, shock it by putting the cooked broccoli in an ice water bath. This stops the cooking and keeps the green vibrant.
@Helen unfortunately shocking the broccoli won’t let you dress it while warm, which should help the dressing absorb better.
Why not just steam the broccoli?
this is blanching the broccoli so it stays green after cooking.
@Jennifer I dream broccoli all the time, and it stays green. So I think either method can work, based on your preference.
@Name Terry M, I dream of broccoli all the time too ;-)
This was great. Simple with great flavor. I added a can of white beans to make it more of a meal (instead of a side).
@Amii what a great idea!

Can’t think of broccoli and ginger without adding garlic, even if just used to wipe the serving bowl all over with a halved clove or two. It’s the same way i imbue Caesar salad, just by swiping the bowl with garlic. Depending on freshness of garlic, may use 1 or 3.
@Anut. How about adding minced garlic to the dressing? Raw or sautéed in the oil? Or … garlic powder or garlic salt? (I’m a garlic lover, but I have to be careful because my husband is only so so.)

I love this! I made this salad using broccoli that was left behind in the veg drawer. It is key to dress this while the broccoli is warm, which is mentioned in the prelude to the recipe. I understand why folks may want to shock the broccoli in ice water, but that will cause it to absorb less flavor. I kept in the fridge for a few days & added it as a side to almost every meal. Really good.
Rachel S, Thanks for sharing. I plan on keeping this for leftovers, too. Did you find that it lost its bright color after time, as Jane reported in her note?
Even better, microwave it. It preserves more of the nutrients.
@Tough Shopper totally agree with this comment and also you can control how much you cook it much more easily
@Tough Shopper how long do you microwave it for?

I agree that this is delicious, but having dressed my salad well in advance of guests, I discovered that the lemon has turned the broccoli an unattractive shade of green. If I make this again, I will combine closer in time to when I eat…

@Jane the recipe, and several comments, mention letting the warm (blanched) broccoli absorb the dressing so it flavors up optimally. If you wait until the last minute, it seems the absorption won’t have time. Unless you’re adding the lemon juice independently of the dressing? I haven’t made it yet so I’m just guessing.

I felt this needed a little maple syrup to balance the salt and acid. Otherwise a great recipe. Didn’t find that boiling created any issues wirh excess water (I did drain in a paper towel lined mixing bowl first), and didn’t bother shocking in ice water. Two minutes of boiling and a few minutes drain yielded bright, crisp-tender florets.
Why not roast the broccoli so that the dressing doesn’t get diluted?
This dressing is greater than the sum of its parts.
The description calls for "blanching" the broccoli.  That means it should be shocked in cold water after boiling to stop the cooking process.  I think the recipe missed that step.
What a fresh tasting, tasty, easy salad!

I've made this with organic broccoli florets from Whole Foods, and it works very well. They are already blanched and cut bite-sized, so it makes it very easy.  Just thaw the frozen broccoli on a clean kitchen/bar towel and then blot to get rid of all excess water.  Then toss with the dressing.  I liked it as is, but I added some chili crisp and it was great. I'm thinking of making it again replacing some of the olive oil with sesame oil and sprinkling with toasted sesame seeds.
I put the broccoli in just till the water started reboiling, came out al dente perfect. Definitely a keeper.
This recipe caught my eye because I have scallions and about a pound of broccoli stalks left over from using the crowns.  I see no reason why I could not shred them and use them instead of crowns.
We slice the stems and cook them along with the florets - same amount of time - and I prefer them to the florets at this point.
Great tip for shredding fresh ginger from à produce manager at a Safeway on Oahu — freeze the ginger (you can keep a large piece this way) then easy to peel and grate on a Microplane as needed.
I prepare my broccoli this way also for warm salads. It is so hot that most of the water evaporates and it is evenly cooked.  Great recipe!
I loved the al dente broccoli with the miso sauce. It was surprisingly good. I will make this regularly.

Shocking the broccoli in cold water cnacels the effect stated in the recipe: "Blanched broccoli florets are tossed in the vibrant dressing while warm, which helps the vegetable soak up all of the dressing’s tangy flavor. "

I microwaved the broccoli in the same Pyrex measuring cup I mixed in and stored. Two minutes. Easy as pie. No water accumulated in the bottom. Love the mix of flavors. Next time, I might add garlic and/or soy sauce instead of salt. It might also benefit from finely grated zest.
I would steam the broccoli too and add the salt to the dressing or even leave it out?
What can I use instead of miso?  allergies...

@Grit
Coconut aminos or anchovies are my go-tos.
I steamed it too.
best to steam rather than boil broccoli.
@rubi … or dream Broccoli…😂
turned out great! I subbed thinly chopped red onion for scallions as that's what I had. Nice little touch of color too
Delicious. Added just a bit of agave

I made this but added a char in the bottom of a dutch oven to the broccoli after steaming. This helped drive off the excess water and added a great flavour while keeping the broccoli warm before going into the dressing.
Has anyone used this dressing with roasted broccoli instead?  I'd be curious to hear the results, because I prefer roasted florets.
@Steve N. Go for it! It will be different than this but delicious.

A friend showed me how she cooks broccoli ahd I do it this way always now.  Cut into flowerets,  stems also, roll out 3 or 4 pieces of  paper toweling, as many as you need, wet them, wrap broccoli in them, put in the microwave for as many minutes as you want.  2 minutes for me in a recipe like this is plenty.  Of course choose paper towels wisely.

@Diana
I use a silicon microwave dish with a cover and a rack.  Put a little water 💦 in the bottom and vegs on the rack. Cover. Choose time according to vegs. I live at 6000 feet and it works well to brighten broccoli
Wow this is so good. It’s good when it’s warm, it’s good at room temperature, and it’s good cold. I did add a hefty pinch of chili flake to the dressing as well as a clove of garlic.
Why not just microwave the broccoli?
Used sesame oil instead of olive oil.  Added a yummy Asian flavor.
So easy, so quick, so delicious.  I steamed the broccoli, and also some chopped up garlic scape (because I could!).  Yum.
Best to serve soon after preparing as the broccoli color turns yellow in a couple hours.
If you make it ahead of time - 4-6 hours - you can skip cooking the broccoli. The oil and salt will tenderize it, leaving it bright green with a tender snap.

DOUBLE THE SAUCE! Oh this sauce is SO good! So simple but so addictive. I love the way the broccoli soaked it in. I did not, however, rest it on paper towels as directed. So I found there was quite a bit of water in the bottom of my bowl at the end of stirring. I will do this step next time.

But getting back to the SAUCE!! I made a side of seared fish to go with this salad for a quick dinner and used the extra sauce as a marinade and topping for the fish and it was outstanding! (Just an extra squeeze or two of lemon for the fish once it was on the plate, next to a huge portion of broccoli). The sauce brought everything together so well! My boyfriend and I ate the entire salad along with the fish in one sitting!

A stunningly good recipe, deceptive in its complexity. I ended up with a small bit of excess liquid in the bowl hours later - so I added a drained can of chickpeas to the broccoli and juice, which was just perfect to make for a full meal.

I can never manage to whisk miso "until well combined" -- there are always little beads of miso remaining. Solution? Use a stick blender! Perfectly smooth dressing in five seconds. As others suggested, I microwaved the broccoli and froze the ginger before microplaning. Excellent ideas.
We love this recipe. I have been sharing with friends and family.  Super easy and delicious!

Yes, as someone mentioned, the finished dish will fade in color over not a very long time period.  Also, I was heavy handed on the lemon juice and don’t recommend.  Of course, quality broccoli goes a long way.
To uncomplicate things, grate ginger with a coarse grater. Fine graters take too long.
When this calls for miso, is it miso powder, paste, liquid?
@Pixi The directions state to whisk the dressing mashing the miso until the miso dissolves, so I would use miso paste.

@Pixi
Google “miso”.
Perhaps roast the broccoli in the oven?  my favorite way to cook it, actually, and you could even use frozen broccoli in that case.
How would roasted broccoli be with this recipe. TIA
What can you substitute for the miso?
Do you have a glutenfree substitute for miso?

@Christina Hikari Organic White Miso is certified gluten-free. It’s fermentation base is made with rice, not wheat or barley. It also happens to be a brand that is highly recommended and used by chefs, so you can use it with confidence. It’s available at most grocery stores where you would find miso paste and also on Amazon. Now you can enjoy a great quality white miso paste that is also gluten-free!
Could there be a substitute for white miso? Don't ever use it, don't want to buy it.

@Anne Wallace I use white miso all the time— there are a lot on this site that use it. See if any appeal to you. Trader Joes sells it in a pouch so it’s not that much (compared to Asian supermarkets) to give it a go.
This is an Asian inspired dish, and no Asian worth their salt uses olive oil to cook with. It’s expensive and will flavor the oil unnaturally as compared to non-flavored oils.
I can’t eat ginger. Any ideas for a substitute?
@Dale garlic
Can this be made with frozen broccoli florets?

Susan, yes, I used frozen broccoli florets which were already conveniently cut smaller. I steamed them then took off the lid for a few minutes while I finished the dressing, which allowed extra moisture to evaporate, no need to mess with a towel.
Soooo good!! Delicious exactly as written. I didn’t feel the need to add any salt or pepper.
Really delicious especially with farm-fresh broccoli. I'll make this again and again!
what about just making it with uncooked broccoli?
Didn't have any miso, so used a tablespoon of tahini and soy sauce to taste -- yummy!  That said, will buy one of those miso packets at Trader Joe's and give it another go. I have high hopes.

I made this and it is delicious. To speed up prep, I whirred the ginger, miso, lemon juice and olive oil in my mini food processor. I chilled it for 5-6 hours and had it for dinner with some carmel pork chops. I was out of scallions and it was great anyway - it will be even better with them next time. Chilled its perfect for a hot summer night.

I roast broccoli just for a few moments with a little olive oil prior to using it in salads and it works 
wonderfully.
@Doron Narkiss brilliant! I've never been sure when you're supposed to start the timer..

Delicious. And so fast made as written
I will try the roasted broccoli suggestion in the winter

For those of you who prefer the boiling water method, I have 2 “tricks” I use after boiling and draining. The first is to place the broccoli in a large, clean kitchen towel and whirl it about.  A surprising amount of water is released into the towel.  The second is to use a decent salad spinner, which gives the same centrifugal force.  I find the towel works best, but hold onto to it or risk flying broccoli.

Our family LOVED this. We had it on the side with stake and potatoes and it was the absolute perfect summer meal! Will be an ongoing staple. Side note--- I added radishes and carrots and let those marinate overnight then added the broccoli next day with the steaks!

I do a lot of wok cooking and rely on Woks of Life website. They consistently recommend cooking broccoli briefly in boiling water before stir frying. The method works to keep the broccoli crisp tender.
It was good I thought but not great. I did add corn fresh off the cob to add a bit more variety to it which worked out well.
This was so tasty! I added white beans and a fried egg on top and some chile flakes and it was the perfect light dinner. Will definitely make again.

I followed the recipe almost exactly. Except we used what we had which was "Hacho miso" in the fridge which is more of a dark color but it was absolutely delicious. I steamed the broccoli. Super easy with a great flavor profile. I eat broccoli a lot and can see why this recipe lends itself to a steamed broccoli vs. roasted. But thats just me. I liked that the author gave suggestions on how to serve next day. I will make this again.
How about frozen and thawed broccoli florets in a pinch?

Outstanding! 
I used 3 TB EVOO/1 TB roasted sesame oil & tossed w/ 1/2 C toasted slivered almonds alongside salt & pepper shrimp. Reserved 2 scoops broccoli & tossed w/ toasted walnuts & snow peas for next-day lunch. Also fab.

This is delicious! Nice and green and fresh. Added some chopped cucumbers since I didn’t have enough broccoli on hand. I thought afterwards that fresh English peas might be a nice textural addition as well.

I do not understand all of the positive comments. I was really looking forward to this salad and followed the recipe to a T. The result was a mushy, wet, gray slop that I could not eat. Yes, I drained the cooked broccoli well on paper towels for even longer than the recommended time. It’s a good thing my partner likes broccoli in any form because he has committed to eating it.
I added asparagus tips and broccolini that I had as well as some chopped parsley.

title: Cabbage, Potato and Cheese Dumplings With Dill Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I had all the filling ingredients so I thought why not? I didn't have wonton wrappers and I was too lazy to make empanada dough. I confess I used premade piecrust rolled out extra thin and cut circles. Then I brushed them with egg yolk, and baked them. Turned out delicious!

@lyn
!!! I doubt I would have had the nerve to try pie dough (unless I was under the influence of an edible), but RESPECT! Taste is everything, yes?

I love the sound of this recipe: it fuses Asian, Eastern European, Irish influences to deliver something divine. I made pierogies once, with leek and goat cheese (in lieu of onion and low fat cottage cheese) and it turned out blah. I now know what I needed: lovely sweet cabbage and crunchy sauerkraut, and dill. I am braving a freezing rain to get me some wanton wrappers. Happy New Year everybody. May it be better than the last!
Has anyone done a veganized version?  I’m thinking of nutritional yeast, tahini and a little of the sauerkraut juice to replace the cheese. Maybe a touch of white miso and grainy mustard.
@Rachel I think that could work. I think I would consider hummus or thinning the tahini a bit.
I don't think you need to replace the cheese. It will still be good without it, and introducing a new flavor like tahini or mustard will make it whole other thing.

@Christine I agree. I tasted as I went along and the filling was amazing.. Maybe some vegan butter to hold it together but I think tahini or hummus would detract from the leek/dill/potato/kraut deliciousness
I'm not into making dumplings but the filling ingredients sounded so amazing so I just diced and boiled the potatoes without mashing, and mixed them with the cabbage ingredients. Fantastic side dish!

Long time listener, first time caller. These were delicious. I used green cabbage because the store was out of Napa. I roughly chopped the leeks and cabbage and pulsed each in the food processor. I did the same with the potatoes once they were cooled. I added a teaspoon of red pepper flakes to the dumpling mixture for a little kick. Finally, I was too lazy to make the dumplings so I sprayed the wontons with cooking spray and put them on mini muffin tins before adding the dumpling mixture. I baked them at 400 for ten minutes and served them with a dip made with Greek yogurt, fresh chopped dill, and a pinch of salt. 😋
@Micki B this was great!
Any ideas about a gluten free way to make these or any other dumplings?
What about using spring roll wrappers? They are gluten free.
There are a lot of gluten-free recipes for dumpling dough floating around. You could make these with a more "perogy" style dough and shape and they'd be excellent!
@Joanie spring roll wrappers are not gluten free by default. You may be able to find some that at are GF though.

A big hit. In case this is helpful for others planning ahead, we made these, boiled them all and cooled them on sheet pans. Then, right before dinner, we fried them crisp in butter in two large sauté pans, holding them in a warm oven until all were done. Sprinkled generously with dill and they were delish. I think a little Greek yogurt or sour cream would have gilded the lily in a nice way.
Never, ever mix the potatoes and cheese mix with the cabbage and sauerkraut mix. And don't use napa cabbage, it's way too tough, use regular green cabbage.
@Stephen G this is a bizarre comment, you give no reason not to mix. My mixture turned out beautiful and tasty as per instructions
@Stephen G please share why you recommend not to mix ! Thank you!

there is no potato and cheese mixture in this recipe, just mashed potatoes to which you add cabbages and cheeses at the end. even if it were, I'd mix them without a second thought, as I have many a time before in different recipes, to no ill effect.
i hate sauerkraut and dill and I may still make these!
@mll I also don’t like those ingredients.  Can you think of any substitutions?
@Susan these would be delicious with kimchi instead of saurkraut. Preserved lemons could also be an unusual and punchy replacement!
I love that recipe but I’ve never eaten butter and never use it I find that it’s not necessary in this recipe and they are wonderful dumplings I use avacado oil thank you for sharing this recipe.

I'm wondering about the technique for cooking the potatoes.  Peeling them raw, dicing them, boiling them, and then draining them is never a technique I would use to cook potatoes.  Peeling potatoes raw removes their most nutritious part, which is the layer of flesh directly under the skin.  And then, boiling them in water pre-diced and draining them removes flavor and even more of the nutrition.  Is it done this way to achieve a certain texture?

Exciting to see a polish-influenced vegetarian dumpling! Filling was great, but next time I would reduce the dill and add a little more sauerkraut. Turns out wonton wrappers have a lot of options at the store - I went with "thin", but in the video it looks like she might have used a medium thickness, which I would consider using next time to give them a bit more sturdiness. Couldn't help but add a dollop of sour cream for serving. Served as a main with roasted carrots.
Has anyone used simple green cabbage?  I find myself with a huge supply of it and need some ideas for using it.  Too lazy to make sauerkraut or kimchee!
@Greta I used savoy cabbage and they went down a treat. I imagine it will be the same for green cabbage!

This has to be one of my favourite NYT recipes so far. My Polish friend dared to say they were like a superior version of pierogi.
We formed the dumplings all together before a group dinner which was a fun activity for all ages

I liked these, went super well with a pot of borscht. But I had problems with many of the dumplings falling apart. I got wonton wrappers and they looked good, but as they boiled they started leaking and some outright fell apart. Anyone else have any hot tips? I’ll try keeping them at a simmer next time I guess?

Agree that these were good. I was eating the filling out of the bowl as I wrapped. I used extra sharp cheddar in place of the Swiss. Honestly I don't think the cheese is really necessary, though sure it's good. But don't be afraid to omit it if you avoid cheese (but I'd hate to not top these with butter and sour cream!)
I’m so fortunate that we have a pierogi store with both savory and sweet,  but I might try making these on a weekend.
I think I willl make this as a side dish with no wrapper.  Any thoughts?  I dont have so much time to wrap.
this was absolutely delicious! it definitely took pre-planning and I had to watch a youtube video to understand the wonton fold, but totally totally worth the effort.
Loved this recipe and the dumplings are even better the next day cold.
These were relatively easy to make and had a really nice mix of flavors. Froze about 15 and have had them both boiled and pan fried. Both were excellent.
so bland, not interesting at all. and way too much work.
Excellent directions and I was very grateful for the video.  Dumpling have great flavor!

Made the filling as written and thought it was delicious. It seemed perfectly suited to pelmeni, and since I couldn't find wonton wrappers without various unpronounceable ingredients, I made pelmeni dough instead. Using this recipe: https://www.pantsdownapronson.com/pelmeni-recipe/#recipe and 10g of filling (about 2 tsp.; I have a #100 cookie scoop) I got about 100 pelmeni, using setting #5 on my pasta machine, and re-rolling the dough. Planning to serve with butter and sour cream...classic!
These dumplings are absolutely fabulous. I will be making again.

title: Fiery Vindaloo Chili Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: If I were to use powdered Kashmiri chili, how much should I sub for the whole chilis?
That cinnamon stick is going to blendable? I feel like there will be splinters in the mix. Tell me I’m wrong

@Betsy Marks you don’t need a lot of it even a tiny piece is fine and skip the cardamom. The main flavor to accentuate is garlic as Vindalho originated from Portugal where it’s called Carne de vinha d'alhos ("meat in wine and garlic"). It’s not an aromatic dish like many North Indian dishes. It was brought to Goa by Portuguese sailors and modified to make it spicier to preserve it better. We rarely use cardamon in our food  except in desserts where it’s quite predominant.

@Helga do Rosario Gomes the British added cardamom in their version during the British Raj. After the British took over Goa from the Portuguese, they also took over all the cooks and used (stole) the recipes as well.

@Betsy Marks
Most recipes will remind us to remove the cinnamon stick. It’s already imparted its flavor during cooking.
You could serve on a bed of lettuces for a salad or on tortilla chips for nachos. A change from the usual rice/roti/naan.

This sauce goes well with plant based meat substitutes. I liked beans and tempeh combo for texture, and added a beef bouillon cube in case.  Don’t judge please: i am heart healthy, not ethical vegan. Sauce is medium hot not fiery. Worth the depth of flavor of whole spices but powder or paste is still a nice variant on southwest standard chili, a staple with us.
I wonder if this could be made in a slow cooker?
Seeing "fiery" in the title, I went with caution, emptied the seeds from the Kashmiri chili pods and skipped the Thai chilis. The result was a tasty dish with essentially no heat.

I used guajillo chiles as they were easy for me to get - is the recipe incorrect?  I tend to like spicy food, and I read 'fiery vindaloo' as something like a 4 or 5 spice level on a scale of 1-5.  Using 3 guajilo chiles, I ended up around a 2 on that same relative scale.  Should it be '8' guajillo chiles?  Or should it really be Kashmiri or nothing?

CJ, the recipe does specify a bird's eye chili, which is there for the heat (shouldn't be "optional" in a "fiery" dish!). Kashmiri are quite mild, and Guajillo not not much hotter, but both will contribute colour and flavor, which the bird's eye won't. Or use a finely minced habanero instead of the bird's eye.
Can a couple of tablespoons of premixed vindaloo spice powder be substituted for step 1?
I had a box of MDH Kashmiri Mirch that I used about 3 tsp (left out the birds eye chili since I didn't have it) and it was a good solid but not overpowering heat.
I just noticed that 1 small tomato chopped should be a cup? That sounds….impossible.

title: Salmon With Mustard Piccata Sauce Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Very tasty! Made as written, except I only used 2 salmon fillets (as I was cooking for two people). There was more sauce than needed for 2 fillets, but the amount of cabbage was just right. If making for 4 people, I’d double the cabbage and add maybe just 1 more tablespoon of butter.

Used 2lb salmon and just cut into filets rather than cubes. Used reg green cabbage instead of Napa. Doubled sauce. Quick and delicious. And one garlic clove? Come on now. That’s not a real measurement. 1/2 to whole bulb.
@APyou had me at 1/2 to whole bulb.
@AP agreed. 1 clove is a rounding error.
@AP agreed and added a couple of tbsp of cream to the sauce. Perfection!

Legit, this is delicious! While carmelizing cabbage (admittedly plus a rough-chopped onion, and minced garlic, which I add to basically everything, I chose to add sauce ingredients to THAT pan; I used my Air Fryer to make perfect salmon (legit, I'm a novice with an Air Fryer but it is THE fish method! No turning, no checking, perfect and juicy every. single. time. Just adjust for weight!!! #perfect). Okay, now that I'm off my pedestal (lol), It's really so, so good!!

The air fryer is the absolute best way to cook salmon! I don't eat salmon at restaurants any longer because it just doesn't compare to air-fried. I'm always on the lookout for great sauces to go with it.
@~Chrissie O.  We always grill salmon but at -2 here in Minneapolis it's a suboptimal method. I'm willing to become an air-fried salmon convert. Can you give me a quick
@Jan M brown sugar and Dijon mustard.
Can anyone comment why the salmon appears seared in photo but is steamed in recipe? Thanks!

@KMR unfortunately, food magazines use stylists to cook dishes for visual appeal rather than what the dish looks like. It’s why videos with recipes are such a boon, as you can see what it’s supposed to look like.

This was super! I used regular cabbage but otherwise made as written. I was surprised but the salmon really did only take seven minutes of steaming. It didn't look done to me, but when I put a finger on it, it sprang back. Also, instead of S&P, I used Penzey's Outrage of Love which is kind of a spicy lemon pepper salt blend. It was delicious on this! The cabbage was cooked perfectly; carmelized, tender, but still a bit toothsome. It's in the monthly rotation for sure!
Finish cooking the salmon, then another 5-6 minutes to prepare the piccata sauce.....doesn't the fish cook significantly in this time?  (My husband is a fiend about having food served hot!)
@Deborah You could partially cook salmon and then add at end to warm it up. That's what I did.
Really enjoyed this! I added some chicken stock w/ the water to add more flavor. Would probably be good with a little bit of a white wine reduction too.
We cooked this just as described (using a coleslaw bag though) and it was delicious. Cabbage gives this recipe a great flavor. This is a wonderful re.

The whole family loved this. I took note of comments and cooked my salmon in a skillet as the cabbage cooked, then chopped it and added it at the end. I used Dijon, and I forgot the chives, but this was great anyway. For those having trouble with the cabbage boiling, please note that on my stove, I had to go up to 8 to get a good golden char on the cabbage. At this temperature, I think the liquid must have cooked off immediately because all of the cabbage ended up golden and caramelized.
Any idea why this specifies a skillet with a lid? I don't see anywhere in the recipe where you're instructed to cover the dish, perhaps I am overlooking?
In step 2, second sentence,  it says to cover.
@Jenn Hi! In step 2, it says “ Arrange fish on top of cabbage in a single layer, cover and reduce heat to low.”
Instructions to cover the skillet are under step 2, after you add the salmon atop the par-cooked cabbage.
This was really good, and easy too. I didn't have cabbage so I used kale and it worked well.
