Excellent.  Served with Trader Joe’s cauliflower gnocchi.  I’ll probably double the sauce to out on the gnocchi next time.  I did use chicken broth instead of water and a splash of white wine.
delicious all the way

I have been stuck in the house in NC for 4 days and came across this recipe yesterday. I just so happened to have all these ingredients on hand. I added frozen peas because I am in my 'frozen peas for protein' era, and made the recipe exactly as written.  Delicious! Thank you for a special dinner on day 4 of being stuck at home.
How does the salmon look browned in the picture if you are steaming it?

Tastes great! Followed the recipe as written. I didn't have the hot mustard so, Dijon it was and I served it with steamed rice. It's so easy to eat it all up... like comfort food. Another appealing thing about this recipe is that it was actually ready in the 30 minutes it's supposed to take.

It’s just the two of us & frequently cook with leftovers in mind. I used 1.5 lbs salmon & half a head of Napa cabbage, served with quinoa. The leftovers reheated beautifully in the InstantPot (18 mins, steam setting with leftover quinoa in an open aluminum foil packet, nestled on top). Absolutely delicious recipe that will be in regular rotation. The only change I’d recommend is to use the entire head of cabbage

After reading a lot of reviews, i decided to follow the directions as written, with one exception. I used whine wine in the sauce vs water. Warning, alcohol evaporates very quickly and the sauce gets really dry Fast. I loved the flavor vs water, but either use more wine or have a cup of water handy to thin the sauce. (Don't thin with more wine as the alcohol will not burn off in time). 
Overall, delicious!

This was just OK. The recipe doesn't mention how much liquid comes out of the napa cabbage when it cooks - I had to drain a bunch off before I made the sauce (or maybe I should have used it in lieu of the 1/2 cup water?).
I’ve made this with regular green cabbage and Napa cabbage. The Napa cabbage came out soggy and didn’t brown up as well. I much preferred the green cabbage.

OMG, so delicious! I love reading through the notes from my fellow home chefs and their suggestions. Wine and some chicken stock for the sauce, it was divine! I added a chopped onion and minced a handful of garlic cloves with the cabbage. I always have capers in my pantry but alas I had used the last I had in the tuna fish I made for lunch, so I substituted delicious small green olives my husband has bought at our Italian market. I am looking forward to leftovers for lunch!! Wil make this often!

I eat olives out of the jar. My boyfriend refuses to eat them. I loved this recipe. My boyfriend did not. If you’re cooking for someone who can’t deal with brine, this may not be the recipe for them!

That said, loved how simple and easy this was. I used green cabbage and it worked well. And I did a little less butter. (I also only did 2 salmon filets).
I love the components of this dish but thought that steaming the cabbage and the salmon was not quite right. I will make again broken apart and then add all together.
Any way to make this with less butter?  This is a Tablespoon per person.
Substitute olive oil in step 1 for the cabbage cooking. Keep the butter for the sauce step (step 3)

The role of the butter here is to help thicken the sauce and temper the piquancy of the mustard and lemon. So if you're like me and don't enjoy fatty sauces (or if you're trying to reduce fat/calories), you need to replace the butter with something else that satisfies the same functions--a bit of cornstarch slurry, a spoonful of mild yogurt, a tablespoon of apricot jam, etc.--or just omit the butter for a thinner, tangier sauce.
@Vivek I was thinking of doing that!
Could you swap the cabbage for eg bok choy?
@Jenn Step 2 has you cover the pan after adding the salmon to the cabbage.
I have baby bok choy- can I use that instead of cabbage?
@evie made it today with bok choy! Excellent!

Took way longer than 30 minutes to prep and cook.  After steaming fish and cabbage there was a fair amount of liquid in pan and there were no brown bits to stir up.  I used rosé instead of water, Dijon (since we always have Dijon) instead of Colman's, and bottled lemon juice.  Served with wild rice because that's what my wife asked for.  

Next time, a regular (not non-stick) pan, and less mustard.  And skinless salmon because taking the skin off those filets was a real pain).

So delish! I actually used a bag of frozen surimi that my husband picked up in error, instead of crab legs. When I saw this recipe, I thought, why not? The sweetness of the surimi paired perfectly with the tangy sauce. Served on a bed of 50-50 jasmine rice-riced cauliflower. Didn’t have Napa cabbage on hand but a big ole head of green cabbage was a good sub. Adding to our rotation of comfort dishes.
Meh!  Nothing special.

Addition of a shallot and a lot of extra garlic to the cabbage, as others have suggested, is good. I also found the addition of about 2 tsp of honey to the sauce to be tasty, just a hint of sweetness for balance.
Great weeknight elegant main course plus veg!  Easy prep.  All ingredients on hand except for fresh fish to buy.
Meh. There are so many better things you can do with your salmon. Flavors neither came together nor played in nice counterpoint.

Delicious! Not quick. 45 minutes to prep and cook.

Made dinner for two, so based on other comments, I cooked half the amount of cabbage and salmon, but full amount of sauce. I can't imagine serving less sauce! 

Had only large skin-on salmon fillet, so cut large serving size slices. Cooked the salmon atop cabbage skin side up. It was easier to peel the skin off after salmon was cooked. Used standard white cabbage. 4 medium garlic cloves. Served with white rice.
I had to look up Napa Cabbage. Never tried it before. How is the flavor different from other cabbage? Where else would I use it?

@Joan Pachner. It’s more tender than regular green cabbage and the flavor is more delicate. You can use it in soup or as a substitute for green cabbage in most recipes. It is often found in Chinese cuisine.
I didn’t read closely enough and used 2T Dijon mustard. Way too much and we like mustard. Be careful out there.
Had to use regular (probably not the right word!) cabbage and I couldn’t get any color on it. No worries. It’s easy and very, very good. Definitely going in my cook again folder
Yummy!  Used regular green cabbage. Would have enjoyed a bit more sauce - but it was delicious.
Absolutely loved this, and so easy. I used green cabbage as I had a quarter head I needed to get rid of, so I let it sear for a bit longer. Served with orecchiette and peas - yum!
My husband doesn’t like salmon but said this is delicious. I used a tiny bit of butter in the sauce. And I forgot to make orzo but it was fine with just the cabbage. Will make again. Super easy.
I used regular cabbage.  I cooked the salmon filet, whole, on the cabbage and chunked it after cooking. Easy. Delicious recipe.
Easy and excellent. I used vermouth instead of water, but white wine would also be great.
The sauce is good.  But steamed fish is jut not.
This is a solid weeknight recipe that comes together quickly.  I will definitely make this again.  I also adjusted the recipe for two pieces of salmon easily.

Meh. 

Love salmon. Live in the PNW, so get it fresh and always wild. Love cabbage and all other ingredients too. Followed the recipe to a T. It was OK, but nothing to repeat.

Delicious weeknight dinner. Funny how the sauce changes everything so I suggest at least doubling the sauce, probably more if you want it to flavor the orzo (what I used) or rice. Used green cabbage and Dijon mustard.
I disliked the sauce, it just did not pair well. The salmon and cabbage were fabulous though

Added a full bunch of asparagus chopped into 2 inch pieces to the cabbage. Salmon took maybe two extra minutes than state cook time. Served with garlicky orzo. Sauce is delicious. My husband and kids gobbled up the meal and it will stay in the repertoire.

title: One-Pot Salmon, Spinach and Lentil Salad Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I'm astounded - 1280 gms of sodium per serving???  I looked at the ingredients and am stumped at how that can be.  Is most of that in the canned lentils? Or are they using salt as seasoning with a very heavy hand?

This also caught my attention. I really wish the NYT would include specific information about how the sodium is calculated in the nutrition information. I end up doing my own calculations if the recipe looks like something I would enjoy, and the stated sodium content seems high.
@Jane Milligrams*

Yes, very strange sodium amount. I've never used canned lentils since cooking puy or black lentils and then freezing in freezer ziplocks for future use is so easy.
Also, 2 Tablespoons of oil for small cubes of salmon cooked 5 minutes over Medium heat, then steamed for two minutes, seems unnecessary. I'll go for half that.
This was wonderful. Cooked my own lentils and only had spinach-arugula mix but otherwise followed recipe pretty closely. Tasty and healthy — a definite keeper.
The zucchini is not doing much, I’d skip it next time. Would add a mustard vinaigrette for more flavor.
@W1ndowMaker Agree — the grated zucchini was completely lost in this recipe. I’ll include it next time because it’s a veg, but I’ll cut it up into chunks.
@W1ndowMaker totally agree that this recipe does not need the zucchini!

Will try to make even easier with tinned sardines. Use oil from tin to cook garlic, then warm the spinach, lemon, maybe a bit of chopped fruit, then off stove, add canned beans, veg, seasoning, herbs. Seems like a very versatile formula.
Appreciated the suggestion of adding a honey mustard vinaigrette as it really added a great flavor. Also,  I just cut the zucchini in quarter inch half moons. Grating it too much work and mushy.
Canned lentils are very high in sodium. You can get low, or no, sodium versions and washing them helps a lot but the best option is too cook your own. Lentils are naturally low in sodium

I made this as written. It’s not bad but it’s underwhelming. It desperately needs some kind of dressing since the lemon juice and olive oil here just isn’t enough. The next time I will add a mustard vinaigrette perhaps, as mustard goes well with lentils.
Really good. I used baby bok choi instead of spinach and added a little ginger with the garlic. Like others I used dried lentils instead of canned.

"Cook, turning occasionally, until fish is lightly golden in spots, 5 minutes. " 
This is a small thing, but I do think it can make a difference for us home cooks. 
In restaurant kitchens, when we cooked proteins like salmon, chicken, steaks, etc. - we only turned the item being cooked once. We tend to poke and prod food too much. Put it down in the pan, "let it cook" and turn it once at the end. Especially, with salmon. No need to flip it multiple times.
This was excellent - healthy and very flavourful. Do  prepare all your ingredients at the start. The recipe comes together very quickly.
@JM Totally agree !

I read the other comments first, so I used only one can of lentils to cut down on the portions. I cooked the filet as one piece with skin on, high heat and lots of olive oil, salt, and pepper. Skin was easy to take off once it was 80% cooked through. I sautéed the zucchini and cut into thin slices only. Added everything else in the instructed order. The zucchini was soft but not mushy.
I didn’t have dill so I used mint and it was lovely! This recipe is easy and healthy. I will be making this again!
@Amanda yes! I used mint and cilantro

The flavors in this work far better together than one would expect. Followed the recipe in terms of ingredients. Made my own green lentils. My salmon came with skin on so I baked it (another pan) but using the guidance of step 1. Then cut the cooked salmon. Far easier to wash a pan than skinning and cutting into cubes while raw.
@Jeff I loved having the salmon skin releasing those fish oils into the whole dish .

Hi I made this and agree that the zucchini added nothing and had a mushy texture despite squeezing out the liquid. Also before adding the lentils, etc, I removed the salmon and tossed the lentils, folding it all in. After that I divided into bowls and divided the salmon in the top.
@Mimi I feel that adding the salmon to the main dish and incorporating the flavors together gets that terrific fish oil onto all of the components especially the spinach .
I bought canned lentils but the texture was so mushy I ditched them in favor of cooking dried. They cook easily, quickly, and you can control the texture.
Delicious! I cooked my own lentils. It’s so easy, why not? This will serve more than 4. I made a half recipe, and my husband and I will get another meal from it.

Not 'salad' for us; salmon in air fryer (!), dried lentils in broth. Prepped rest while lentils were cooking. Quartered zucchini in 1/4" rounds. Baby spinach mix wilted. Rather than waste the whole thing, blistered tomatoes, added ginger/jalapeno/cilantro, then a can of coconut milk plus curry spices. Turned it into a lentil-salmon curry with zucchini, tomato, spinach. Over Basmati rice? Delish! 4 stars tho DRASTIC changes cuz base ing were stupid-good together!! Will repeat! Yum!!

The flavor combination was very tasty, and this was a satisfying dinner. However, next time I'll cook the salmon, remove it, continue with recipe, and then add salmon back in. Also think it would be great with uncut salmon - cook whole portions per recipe and use the rest of the recipe as a base for it.
This recipe is good, but as others have noted, the addition of some dijon mustard (maybe 1 1/2 Tb) really adds some punch.

This was a filling and delicious meal!
My recommendations:
- Use dried lentils and soak them the night before to reduce sodium content
- Cut the zucchini into thin slices instead of grating for more crunch

Agree with all the comments about the zucchini not adding much and the fact that it needs more acid/bite. Next time I will add either a mustard or a balsamic vinaigrette as the last step and I think then it will be a really good dish instead of just an ok one. PS my husband sprinkled some balsamic vinaigrette on his second helping and it was night and day!

Never heard of canned lentils so I bought it in a bag. I couldn't find salmon without skin so bought it and cut the skin off. I had this really nice lemon dressing so used that instead of squeezed lemons.  It was so tasty.

It was expensive to make due to the salmon, I also had to skin the salmon and debone it (tedious.)  It makes a lot, so I'm going to try freezing the remainder, but I won't make it again.  Bland in flavor.

@Cassandra Costco bagged salmon . And I think the skin adds to the flavor so I don’t  take it out . Between the fish oil and ginger you can feel this finished with a little personal soy sauce at the table !

This recipe looks right up my alley, but when I checked the nutritional information, it said it was 723 cal per serving. How can that be? It’s basically vegetables, lentils and salmon. Appreciate any thoughts

I made this on a whim as I had dried lentils in the cabinet and a big freezer bag of salmon from Costco and it was a weeknight winner ! Easy Easy and proportions and seasoning are flexible . Now this is an easy weeknight one..or two depending on if you cook your beans from dry …pot dinner .

Okay, I am a luddite in the kitchen and only try easy recipes.

This was a great one for me, easy as pie. Easier than pie, actually.

My only comment was that just using the "times allotted" in the recipe, the salmon didn't really get done through and through. I had to keep the salmon -- and the spinach -- on a super low heat to make sure the salmon was fully cooked but the spinach didn't start to get cooked down/reduced. 

In the future I'll take more time with the salmon.

Made this yesterday, Saturday. More of a one pot for me than a salad but a good recipe. I struggled getting the skin off w/o wasting meat. Wanted to pass along a tip if you only have skin-on salmon. Just saw it this morning on YouTube. Put the salmon in a strainer skin up. Slowly pour boiling water over it. The skin will tighten and pull up and then pull off easily!

This was just ... meh. It doesn't know what it wants to be. A salad? A lentil bowl? Hot? Cold? It just ended up somewhere in between. Thank goodness I followed the suggestion to add a mustard vinaigrette, which helped. On a positive note, it packs a protein punch for people like me who don't eat any meat except fish. But I won't make this one again.
does anyone really cook with canned lentils? they're so easy to prepare.

cooked my own lentils (1 cup dry) also added asparagus to the lentils about 20 mins in to about 30 mins cook in salted water.  was kind of plain overall, though very fresh - will add some Dijon when I eat the leftovers as other have said.  question: I couldn't taste the scallions at all - anyone else have that issue??
With a little tweaking of seasoning and lentils (i.e., Trader Joe’s), this dish was simply superb!

Loved this.  After I turned over the salmon I added all the scallions and shredded zucchini and let it steam. Had it as a warm dish and it was delicious.  BTW they make a shredding tool that looks like a potato peeler but shreds.  Best tool ever for shredding carrots, zucchini etc...

This recipe does not rinse the canned lentils after draining.  I would never buy beans or lentils canned with so much sodium.  
I generally make my own lentils with much less salt and more aromatics, herbs and seasonings other than salt.   Make a big pot of lentils, peas or beans over the weekend and use in various ways for quick weeknight meals.

Recommendations - 1 1/2 pounds of fish in the Dutch oven to cook all at once is too much. Ends up steaming, not browning. Cook in batches. Cook your own dry lentils - takes about 20 minutes - to cut down on the high sodium in canned lentils. I wanted more than 4 oz. of tomato. A splash of red wine vinegar at the end along with the lemon doesn't hurt. Add a bit of nonfat Greek yogurt with lemon juice and dill on top for serving. Actually better chilled the next day.
Agreed!  I used one pound of fish and it was plenty!
Could not find canned lentils and made them separately from dried - kind of changed the texture from what was expected I think.
Am I missing something? So does half the lemon zest go into the vinaigrette? It says add the other half
there is no lemon zest. 1 tbs is drizzled on salmon (step 2) and the remaining lemon juice is added in step 3

I’m thinking you could instantly upgrade this recipe (avoiding soggy canned lentils, chopping salmon, etc) by adapting these ingredients to the simple technique described in the “Warm Rice Salad with Salmon and Arugula”.
I cannot find canned lentils at any grocery, only lentil soup. Is that what the recipe is referring to?

Today I learned lentils have to be cooked if you don’t get canned ones! Tried to eat this and almost broke a tooth on a lentil. I had never cooked with lentils before and checked the recipe multiple times to see if any prep was needed for them.
If the recipe calls for canned lentils, but it's "totally fine if the salmon flakes apart" then why not use canned product for both?
Grating the zucchini is overkill, makes it watery and mush. It was an interesting luke warm dinner
I used Trader Joe's refrigerated lentils. I would not recommend because it turned out mushy and the lentils overpower the other flavors.

This was ok — but will definitely do this with dried lentils rather than canned next time. I had a checkup the day after eating this and my blood pressure was up! Waaaay too much sodium. I drained and rinsed the canned lentils too. I would really like it if NYT recipes paid more attention to the nutritional value of food and recommended lower sodium/lower fat alternatives within each recipe so people without a ton of experience adapting recipes could make their versions healthier if they wanted to.

Also, I don’t like dill, so I left it out and it was very bland. I’ll probably add basil next time.

Ron
The nutritional data stated 1280 milligrams per portion, so only 1.28 grams.

@Jane,
I was surprised at the 1280 mg sodium per serving as well. I think it will largely depend on the particular can of lentils. Some are much lower in sodium than others. The cans are labeled as having 3.5 servings per can, and this recipe uses 2 cans, for 4 servings, so you'd have to multiply the label sodium amount by about 2. So i would buy reduced sodium cans of lentils, and would also rinse the lentils to minimize the sodium from the liquid.
Nice presentation, but somewhat bland.  Could have used a lemon vinaigrette.
Wonderful whole meal salad. Handy for Lent!

This was very good. The next day I added the leftovers to some Chicken Broth to make it into a soup. I added some dill, garlic powder, and pepper and it turned out fantastic with a slight of toasted bread!

This recipe gets points for being quick and one pot, but we found it seriously lacking in flavor. I think, if a proper dressing was made (instead of a sprinkling of lemon with salt and pepper), this could’ve been much more tasty. Perhaps a vinaigrette with some honey- it needed sweetness
It was great. Better to make the lentils yourself.
Yes to Dijon honey vinaigrette!  Didn’t have zuke so used some chopped green beans which added a nice crunch. Oh, and I forgot the bread but didn’t miss it!

I really enjoyed this! I agree with the comments that cooking dry lentils will yield less sodium.  I used French lentils which also provided great texture.  Also agree with the comment on slicing the zucchini instead of shredding it.  I quarted it lengthwise and sliced it into bite-sized chunks.  The fresh dill added so much flavor, and I didn't feel the need to add mustard as some suggested.  I always add more garlic called for! My wife and I got two dinners and a lunch out of this. So good!

Thanks to the comments I made sure to rinse my canned lentils, and I only used one 19 oz can. With 1.5 lbs of salmon, that was plenty of lentils! Zucchini, tomatoes, dill made this feel very fresh and wholesome. I did add some maple and dijon per recommendations here but they didn't really cohere into a sauce so not sure how much they helped.

This is so easy and delicious. I think you could double the spinach and skip the zucchini. I have read some of the comments about it being not flavorful enough. I’ve never found that but I am heavy handed on the lemon juice.

Finally made it, so glad, it's very good, simple and fast. Cooked puy lentils earlier in the day. Used my U shaped tool to make zucchini matchsticks rather than grate it. Followed other commenters to make dressing w/dijon.

Loved this fish- so healthy and came together quickly. Found it to be a bit bland, so I mixed in some Thai Chile vinegar (thank you, Good Things by Samin Nosrat), and finished it with a dollop of Greek yogurt and olive oil. Will def make it again!

title: One-Pot Salmon and Dill Rice With Peas Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Salmon needs way longer to cook - no instruction on raising the temp back up from low.
@CW yep this. Did 10 min on low and thinking now it’s going to need another 10 on medium but don’t want the rice to scald
@CW just lifted the lid to see raw salmon haha came to the comments to say the same thing!

@CW I rarely cook the fish or shrimp on top of rice, lentils, etc.  I bake it separately in the oven to make sure it is done to my liking.  Also, that way it is easy to remove the skin if there is skin.

Yes. Not “one pot” material! Jasmin Rice in rice cooker. Salmon seared w olive oil, coriander, muscat, cumin salt pepper and lemon juice in pan. Peas cooked separately too.  Zatziki needed lemon juice too. Yum!
Have to agree with the other comments. The fish was still raw and the rice was still hard after the stated cooking time. The directions need some work.
This would be a skip for me. Made it tonight-was not impressed.
Excellent! Used 1 pound of salmon and half the rice and liquid. All of the coriander! Added a bit of lemon juice to the sauce.

I made this for dinner tonight. I'm a widow but I made the 4 servings and am freezing 2, one for a couple of days from now.  I think it's quite good. I added more salt and some salt to the cucumber sauce. I would make again, even for company.

the fastest and tastiest preparation ever. Of
course I made changes to suit the family’s preferences : salmon rub for coriander; broccoli for peas; jasmine rice for badmati, and a drizzle of Korean bbq sauce.  Will definitely use prep method for future rice bowls!
The salmon was barely cooked after 10 minutes over the rice. What did I do wrong?
@Marjorie same for me!
@Marjorie You likely did nothing wrong.  Next time, prepare the salmon separately, then add it to the rice.
Not my family's favorite. The rice was still crunchy when the salmon was done, and it stuck to the fish making it hard to cook longer. Timing seemed off and the flavors weren't there for us!
This recipe really does not work. The salmon was still completely raw after 10 minutes. This one was a swing and a miss.

A tasty and easy recipe, with quick clean-up. The frozen peas stopped the simmer so we temporarily increased three heat. We also had to add a few tablespoons more water to keep the rice from drying out while the salmon finished cooking.

This was delicious. Incredibly easy to make. Used only 1lb of skinless salmon fillets (salmon is so expensive now-a-days), followed all other directions exactly, all cooking times were spot on. This recipe is definitely going into the rotation. Thank you Kay Chun!
The salmon was not cooked at all and because it needed to cook longer, the rice was crunchy. Will not make again.
Delicious!
Bland. Disgusting. Takes much longer than the suggested cooking time. Entire family rejected it.

Since many complained that this recipe was not working out, I am responding that it does work, but I needed to leave the salmon in the pot about 5 minutes longer (total cook time 15 minutes). This is a nice mild dish — a novelty! I find that many of the recipes are heavy on spices (spicy hot) and having GERD, I cannot do spicy anymore (sadly). This dish is also gluten free. My only other adaptation was to add chopped scallions on top at the end. Will make again.

i don’t know. 1 1/2 lb of salmon is 4 portions, minimum. or 6 portions  using standard dietary guidelines of 1/4 lb or size of deck of cards, per person. but only 1/2 cup frozen peas ? for 4 people ? just reading recipe tells me what other comments state: salmon will be half raw on very low rice-cooking temp. if you raise temp, rice will scorch. i cook salmon and rice, mostly brown, all the time. always separately.
Made this tonight. Pretty flavorless. Hard pass.
Very disappointing. Love the concept but it was bland. A one-time experiment for me.

Assuming those who said the fish was still raw have never had sushi with salmon.  Salmon, like tuna, can be eaten raw.  Ahi tuna is often served seared with the middle portions just barely cooked.  Same goes for salmon.  Steaming the one-inch cubes for 10 minutes should be sufficient to cook them.
yeah. but sushi is not just half-raw salmon cooked in pot with rice and yogurt.
Could this be made with canned salmon and a pressure cooker?
I wouldn't think so.  Canned salmon is already cooked.
I liked this. Rice and salmon well cooked. Will add more peas and place dill over salmon at end of cooking. Too hard to lift off rice. Didn't like the yogurt sauce. Will not include next time.
Has anyone tried this just in the rice cooker instead of on the stove?

New way to cook salmon for me. Overall I like this new technique - easy, fast, salmon is tender. Plus one pot! But I wish the recipe gave more guidance about how much salt to use. At first I thought it was pretty bland but then I added more salt and it was much, much better.

Thank for this recipe Kay Chun. I find it a dish with delicate balanced flavors. The second time I prepared it I cooked the rice in fish broth. This gave the dish a bit more umami. Looking forward to your next recipe.

Add a dash of soy sauce instead of salt if you feel it isn’t salty enough. If the salmon is cut in cubes 10 min is plenty to cook it to medium. We liked this a lot and found the prep simple and perfect.

Love this preparation technique, as per my prior salmon review.  Tonight i used halibut (with sesame oil and furikake-like seasoning), and snap peas.  Drizzle of soy sauce for me, Japanese bbq sauce for hubby. 10/10!

Loved it! Salmon was, however, completely raw after 10 minutes, so I put the entire pot in the oven (with the lid on) for 10 more minutes on 180C but just with ventilator and top grill. 
The rice was fluffy, peas were perfect and so was salmon. The yoghurt sauce is a must.
Didn’t have and doggone coriander seed, but subbed some cumin. Loved it. Easy and tasty and light. Great for a weeknight!
i thought the instructions were novel so i followed them precisely. the salmon was way under.  i would have been better off to simply boil the rice, roast the salmon, do the add-ons and make a bowl.

On advice from other commentators, I used way more seasoning for the salmon than the recipe called for, and added some lemon juice to the yogurt sauce instead of water. I just had some of the leftovers for lunch, cold, and I actually enjoyed it much more! I did over salt it, but that's a me issue, not the recipe.
Is sockeye salmon too lean for this dish?
I thought it was quite bland. I would consider making it again but way more spice on the salmon.  I didn’t have an issue with the salmon cooking but I did dice mine quite small.

I use most recipes as suggestions. I didn't have a large dutch oven so cooked dish in a deep frying pan. Didn't have the lime so used Roses Lime Juice which added the necessary sweetness. Frying pan had a glass cover so it was easy to see when salmon was done. Crushed coriander seed on salmon was DELICIOUS.  Sauce was great. Made a mango inflected salad with Chinese cabbage and will add mango to the dish next time. I think any firm fish will do just fine. May try tuna or cod next time.
@Alana You could also cook the rice in fish broth next time.
Very easy and pretty yummy. Yogurt sauce is very good.

I also had raw fish after 12 minutes.  I had turned the flame to low and put a flame diffuser under the pot.  Ended up adding more water and cooking at higher heat for another 10-12 minutes. Wound up with soggy rice but it was good.  I think that the best solution might be to add the fish and peas just when you turn the rice down after it boils.  Also a little more water might be needed to get the rice soft.
Pretty good, and quick. Hlf this recipe makes 2 good dinners.
Followed the instructions exact.
It was good.  Salmon needed more time to cook.  The rice was very boring.

I kept the flame between 1-2 setting and kept an eye on the fish through a clear lidded pot until it was no longer shiny/raw, definitely a bit longer than 10 minutes. The yogurt sauce is integral to the flavor of this dish! I added freshly squeezed lemon juice and some dill to the sauce. Next time, I will add more than 1/2 cup of frozen peas.
This one was a bust. Salmon had no flavor, dill overpowered the rice flavor.
This is the first recipe on NYT Cooking that we actively did not like and we’ve made hundreds at this point. I would skip this one.

Thank you for this!  I cooked it a bit longer and it was perfect. So easy and refreshing for hot weather- I will be cooking this all summer. The coriander and the yogurt sauce made it really delicious.
Salmon needs longer to cook. Rice needs seasoning. Visually appealing but flawed recipe.
This had literally no taste whatsoever

salmon looks more raw than cooked in the pic, not sure why folks thought it would turn out otherwise.In the past I would look at a pic of a recipe and the ingredients and try it,only going to the comments during or after. Now I never try a recipe without reading the comments first. I always find good suggestions and clarifications in the comments. This is the first time I've come across a recipe where a supermajority of the commenters not only changed it but disliked it.
I pan seared my salmon - did not cook through with rice. Added some shallot to yogurt/ cucumber. Served more thinly sliced cucumber with it. Delicious!

I always do 2 to 1 ratio, liquid to rice, with Basmati rice - My standard is 2 cups salt free chicken stock to 1 cup of rice, so with 1.5C of rice, 3C of water/stock would be a better ratio.  And, it usually takes about 35 to 40 minutes to cook the rice until all the liquid is absorbed.  This recipe needs some tweaking.
This was delicious and we like salmon medium, so 10 minutes was fine.  However, I followed the recipe and there’s WAY too much rice.  I would use 1/2 cup and double the peas.

Okay, after reading 57 comments - most of them negative- I decided to prove most of you wrong by adding salt & pepper to the rice, adding honey & ginger to the salmon, and extending the cooking time to avoid sushi. Oh, and I added capers to the tzatziki sauce. 

Y’all were right. I just can’t get excited about this recipe.
Basically make each component (salmon / rice & peas / cucumber salad) fully separate and you’ll be successful at the end ;-)
Any other option other than Dutch oven?
I used a rice cooker. But I also pan fried the salmon!
I loved this! After reading other's comments, I decided to pan fry my salmon, especially since it was skin-on. I also used fresh peas from my garden. Really delicious.

This was good. I had the fish monger cut the salmon in chunks. That made all of the difference. I also used microwaveable jasmine rice. That cut the time so I could sauté the salmon without it being overcooked. 
Then just added the peas, and some soy sauce. I then made the cucumber mixture. The family loved it
I agree with some of the other comments. I had to cook the salmon about 5 minutes longer. I found the dish rather bland. I wouldn’t make it again.

title: Ground Beef Biryani With Cumin Raita Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody:
Super delicious and gobbled up by Indian husband and two littles. So quick and easy for a week night meal. The spices are common to Indian cuisine so we had them on hand already. 
We love exposing our children to different cuisines and flavors to widen their palate and world view and teaches them to be respectful of other cultures.
@himself a well stocked pantry pays dividends on weeknights

I have spent a lot of time making biryanis over the years. This recipe results in an excellent version in a quarter of the time (assuming you have the spices on hand). 

Small changes I made:
- I used ground lamb as this is closer to my old fashioned lamb biryani. 
- I doubled the spices. I’ll admit to loving spice-filled biryanis. 
- I doubled the garlic and ginger.  To save a lot of time, you can buy garlic / ginger paste from Amazon (I like Rani brand). It keeps forever and is useful in many recipes. 
- it’s an extravagance, but to add a little crumbled roasted saffron in the milk that’s poured over the rice at the end makes it even more authentic

@Rick in SF 

I like your idea of using lamb, and I thought the dish was great but would probably double the spices next time.
@Rick in SF is the garlic /ginger the combo option Rani makes?

This was lovely.  Cut recipe in half because there are just two of us but kept spices for full recipe.
Changes I made beside the spices were to infuse the milk with some saffron treads  ( warm the milk and  add 1/4 teaspoon or so of threads and let sit for 10 min)
I also sauted some Chopped cashews and currents ( you could use raisins) in some ghee until well browned/reddish and added it to the top before serving
Could the beef be subbed with chicken or turkey? My gf doesn’t eat beef or pork.
@Nick K same here. Anyone tried w chicken?
I'm going to try turkey.
@Nick K IMO, yes; get dark meat if you can.

This is a delicious albeit time consuming recipe. I say time consuming because it’s not exactly week night simple, as stated. I used very lean ground beef which was fine but I’d do the 80% next time. Not having ghee, I used melted butter and it was perfect. The six cardamom pods can be subbed with a scant tsp of ground cardamom. I used the 1/4tsp cayenne and it was plenty hot. Cilantro is a no-go for us so didn’t use it. It’s a fabulous recipe and the flavors are a great combination. Worth the effort.
Can I use ground lamb to miake this?
I would think that would make it even more flavorful!
@Categrace I think lamb would be much better.
This would be more closer to a pulao than biryani as biryani means layered rice dish! :) Lovely recipe, though!
@Nidhi this was my exact thought. Pulao or keema rice as my family called it.

A great recipe, incredible flavours!
As usual, timing is off.  Two minutes not enough to soften the onions; count on 4.  Three minutes to brown the beef so there's no pink showing isn't possible; took 8-10 minutes.  So the rice is cooked too early and sits waiting/drying.  Start the onions 4-5  before the rice, and you're good.
To be clear, once rice boils, simmer with lid off.
Finally:  it says to fluff rice at end...you can't!  It's underneath the beef and onions!!
Again, great flavours!!
Delicious comfort food
Frankly, I am deterred by the number of ingredients, dried or otherwise. Yeesh
@Gina Some dishes require a little more time in the kitchen, usually to better effect.
@Gina Well worth the time. I assembled all the dry ingredients the day before, so it was easy. Dried fennel was an odd one, but I had fennel seeds which I ground and included.

Ground meat is never used for biryani and rice is layered with meat or just a layer on top…
Biryani is such an iconic dish that it needs to be at least somewhat similar to the real thing
It’s like saying I’m serving up roast turkey and then serving turkey burgers for Thanksgiving…just to give folks an idea of where I’m coming from 
I’m open to variations usually but the ground meat part deters me in this…it’s more of a pulao

Oh, no, not at all!

Keema biryani is made with ground meat, by definition!  Keema biryani is very popular in India, so please don't say that using ground meat makes it not biryani!  It goes all the way back to the Mughal era!

@Squirrel Butler we can agree to disagree…hard to really verify these things one way or another. I’m sure one can get keema “biryani” or pulao (more accurate) all over India and it might be a nice variation on the real thing. But the genuine article it isn’t….

@Squirrel Butler I noticed that the intro of the recipe does mention that this is a “cheater“ version of biryani, which is quick and easy to make. So yes, I agree with that and should have read it before posting my comment. 
But no household in Lucknow or Hyderabad is going to invite you over for a biryani meal and serve Keema biryani. It might be served as a variation or be on restaurant menus, sure…

Do yourself a favor and mise en place all the spices ahead of time. One bowl with have first three items and second will have all the others. If you do this ahead of time, it comes together very quickly!
Puzzling that it’s a weeknight recipe with all those ingredients!

Most of the ingredients listed are spices, and this comes together quickly--it's really just "partially cook rice with broth and spices while you saute onions in gee; add tomato paste, more spices, then beef. Stir in yogurt off heat once browned. Use the beef mixture plus some milk to top par-cooked rice. Finish in oven."
@judith  Why not measure and combine the spices (excluding salt and pepper).  Store them all in a glass container and you’re good to go whenever.

@Andrea
Thank you! 🙏 I am a full time caregiver for my ill husband. Wondrous idea. Will share with others in a similar situation. 💕⭐️
I realize it’s probably not traditional, but I added peas and chopped red bell pepper and it was amazing!  Plus the extra pops of color added to the presentation nicely.

@Tim
I would consider adding baby spinach at the end to make it a complete one-pot meal. Well, two pots to sauté the onions and meat. But the baby spinach wouldn’t change the flavor much and would add the necessary veg. But I like your ideas of red bell pepper and peas as well.
Biryani’s has meat in the bottom and rice on the top.
@Akbar depends where you are… growing up, my mom’s was always a mix-and-bake.

I will cook this tonight. It sounds amazing. Thanks for the heads up about most of the ingredients being spices. Long lists do intimidate me, but I try to not let them stop me from discovering more deliciousness.

Ok, should add ghee to the pantry at some point, might as well be now. Maybe ground fennel, but luckily I can stop by the co-op and get a Tbs or so for a few pennies. Everything else is buried in the cabinet somewhere - I can dig it out. Cilantro is a dividing factor in the house, so I'll give it a shot without, maybe with parsley sub. Looking at the directions, the biggest thing is to get the misé en place ready, and premix the spices in a bowl or two. Prep doesn't look too bad.
@AN unsalted butter can sub for ghee.  I used fennel seed instead of ground fennel which I didn’t have. It was fine.

This was super delicious. But agree with others, the prep time is critical, and took WAY more than 10 minutes. Even having everything prepped before I started cooking, I think the cooking time was at least 30 minutes. Luckily I picked a Friday night, when I generally get home at a reasonable time and can spend more time cooking. On a Monday night, when I workout before I start cooking, I would not be eating this until almost 10 PM.

This was absolutely delicious, despite being out of star anise and fennel. Wonderful flavors and just enough spice - not too much for the kiddos. I doubled the onions because it's my favorite element of biryani. Will try again with minced chicken; definitely going on the rotation.

This is delicious! We love Indian food but it’s often a project. This recipe comes together really fast once you assemble the ingredients and do the prep, which you definitely should do first. I used ground lamb because I had it and it seemed more authentic (Hindus don’t do beef).  I had leftover cucumber cumin raita from a few nights ago so served with that, and sliced cucumbers and daikon radishes. Garnished with the cilantro and pomegranate seeds. Looked pretty and added a bit of flavor contrast. K

Always looking for something new to do with ground chuck (=hamburger), so gave this a try tonight. 

Mise en place is your game plan. Measure out your spices early in the day and prep your onion and garlic and you’ll be ready to roll at mealtime.

Used regular long-grain rice. After cooking the rice for 10 minutes per the recipe, I turned off the heat and by the time I was ready to combine the meat and rice, the rice was done.

3 T of ghee made this a very rich recipe. Next time I’ll use vegetable oil to sauté the onions ( which I’ll double ) and use 1 T ghee to bloom the spices. This is not a wildly spicy dish; next time I’ll kick it up a notch (more chile? More cumin?) to suit our tastes. I thought of adding chile crisp at the table.
Next time I’ll add peas and raisins, per other commenters.

Used ground chuck (85/15) and next time will drain some of the fat.

Some may think this needs more salt. I served it with lime wedges, which were much appreciated. Suggest you add a salad; some contrasting crunch would go well.
What’s the point of the milk drizzled on top of the beef rice mixture?

@Sumac
It adds moisture to the partially cooked rice.
Assuming Green Cardamom not black
@Bibendum I used black cardamom pods based on some research I did. Correct me if I am wrong. The recipe could and should be more specific here.

@Bibendum yes, these are green cardamom pods.  Black cardamom is typically not referred to as “pods”.  Also, 1-2 black cardamom pods would be sufficient for a recipe with these portions.  5 green cardamom pods are prefect here!

Used regular long grain rice and vegetable oil instead of ghee.  A tsp of ground ginger was substituted for fresh.  My cooking times were longer and a non-stick pan isn’t necessary for the onion/burger preparation.  Served with peas and naan.  Very good!

Pretty easy if you have all the spices on hand, but left me wanting a better biryani. Flavor combos are good (yes, green cardamom). If I made this again I'd use less beef, and double the chili powder (like Deggi Mirch). In the end the Dishoom Chicken Berry Biryani is about as much work and is much better.
1/4 tsp of anything is not enough. Add more.

There's not much to say in response except, No, 1/4 tsp is often the right amount of a spice.

That's why they make 1/4 tsp measuring spoons....

Made this recipee yesterday.

Using any more than the 1/4 tsp of fennel would have completely overpowered the other spices in the dish.

The amounts given in this recipe work very well.

From an Indian food snob, this is phenomenal. I was looking for something to do with a leftover pound of hamburger, I keep a lot of spices, and I had no idea such a treat was possible! It was delicious. I might consider toasting and grinding the spices next time, maybe crumbling some toasted saffron into the milk, but it certainly worked as written!
The chicken stock to rice ratio seems off. The grains won’t elongate. I’d think at least another half cup
Made with dark meat turkey, delicious. Next will try browned crumbled tofu.

This seemed promising for dinner tonight. However the rice is still not done after cooking for close to 20 extra minutes. I think the liquid ratio is wrong. Next time I will cook the rice separately. Hoping my comments saves someone else the frustration.
@Alyse I had the same problem. I’m going to have to recook the leftovers with more liquid and hope it doesn’t destroy the dish. Next time I’ll cook the rice first until done and just mix in the meat.
Anyone tried this with super firm tofu or chickpeas?
Seemed like too much rice to meat mixture, next time I will half the rice.
This is one the best recipes you guys have ever poster. My family and I devoured it and were so sad when it was over. Very easy to make but the result is impressive and absolutely delicious.
I really liked this dish. My husband thought it was good but not on the ‘great’ list. Will pack the leftovers up and have it ready to take to work this week.
Absolutely LOVED this! I know the ingredient list is lengthy, but we happened to have most of them in our pantry already (minus the star anise). It was still delicious!
Does it matter whether it's green or black cardamon pods?
@JIY Unfortunately these two spices are very different and I wouldn’t sub.
this sounds interesting, but I find myself 5curious why and not use ground lamb if you’re going to “cheat” on a lamb dish?  But maybe it’s just cause ground beef is so readily available.

I too have Indian husband so ingredients at hand. He usually works all day to make his own from scratch and it’s delicious. His Birthdays coming up so maybe I’ll give this a try! I’m not an all day kind of girl, so I’ll be curious and hopeful about the outcome.

We found this to be a very flavorful and delicious meal.  I followed the recipe as written but a few notes for next time.  I will par cook the rice a little less before adding the meat.  Also, I used ground turkey and I found that the meat seemed to dissolve into the rice.  Next time I will use either cubed chicken or beef.  Otherwise a great recipe.
Does ground fennel mean ground fennel seeds? Or is there also ground fennel out there?

@Dawn I grind my own fennel or just put it in whole.  Amazon sells inexpensive grinders similar to coffee grinders but with two interchangeable, washable grinding cups, one for wet ingredients, the other for dry ingredients.  They’re great for grinding spice mixtures, ginger garlic paste, and individual spices.  I’ve never seen ground fennel in stores.
added equal parts mint & cilantro
Can someone advise? It did not take 10 minutes for the rice to cook and I am totally stumped on what I did wrong!
The 1/4 tsp of cumin seed that go into the cumin raita should be toasted.  Preferably toasted and coarsely ground.  Raw cumin in yogurt is no bueno!
Really delicious - entire family gobbled it up and labeled it a "keeper". The only change I'll make next time is to double it.

@Bren I'll try grated super-firm tofu next time so our vegetarians can enjoy this too. See the NYT recipe for "I Can't Believe It's Not Chicken" https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1027269-i-cant-believe-its-not-chicken-super-savory-grated-tofu?smid=ck-recipe-android-share for technique. Terrific way to use tofu in a variety of dishes!

The payoff on your effort here is astronomically high. Though the ingredients list is long, the miseing is quick, straightforward, and mostly leaning on dry, shelf-stable ingredients, which means the shopping list will be short next time. The cooking is delightful, fun, and foolproof. And oh, Lord, is it tasty. My wife is away for work, and normally I wouldn't make something this elaborate for myself, but I will. My only fear is nothing will stop me from having these "4 servings" in a sitting.
Can I substitute ground cardamon for the pods?
Yes, 1/2 tsp ground cardamom is equivalent to 5 pods.  It worked for me!
This was tasty, but the portions were very large and I think 1 rather than 1-1/2 cups of rice would be enough.  I got 5 generous meals out of it.   It tasted great on reheating.
Great aroma, not enough flavor. If I cook this again, I'll use half as much rice and more chili.

The rice came out great. Used 1 cup rice with 1.5 cups water, and 1/2 vegetable broth cube. I think the beef part could use more spices and mine needed more salt. Forgot about the cilantro and adding peas and raisons. Was also going to add some fenugreek at the end which I forgot to try. Overall, has potential and was not bad. Perhaps my garam masala is not as fresh as it should be.

@Zozoxoe

It’s funny how the ingredients list calls for low sodium broth, and yet in the directions suggests adding salt four different times.

I'd like to try this, but cardamon pods aren't something I keep on hand.  I know people will say no way! or it won't be as good, but...would cardamom powder be a reasonable substitute?  If so, how much powder per pod?
@Loisp, I substitute with 1 cardamom pod= 1/4 tsp ground

Try buying decorticated cardamom (it’s the interior of the green cardamom pod) and grind that up when needed in a recipe.  I have a FinaMill grinder on my counter with separate grinder pods of salt, whole black pepper, and the decorticated cardamom (which I also grind into my cappuccino every day and into hot tea).

My family (both Indian and non-Indian) loved this! I used brown Basmati rice which I forgot to rinse. Next time I will add chopped raw cashews, and possibly sultanas, and serve on a platter edged with sliced hard boiled eggs. Delicious!!!
@Gina, sometimes Indian spice lists seem to go on forever! Assemble the spice containers first, then use small bowls to make the mixes that will be added together throughout the recipe. I didn't have Garam Masala, but Google suggested a recipe!
I often substitute game meat for beef. It’s leaner and healthier, I believe. Either elk or deer

This was heart-warming though a bit complicated.   It makes four generous servings, at least. Though I was skeptical the rice would get cooked as directed, the rice came out perfectly.  If I made it again would double (at least) the spices, except for the chile (though our spices may be a bit old).

Delicious! My first time cooking Indian food (I know, right?) - and this was the perfect introduction. Now that i have all the spices I'm ready to move on to the next challenge. Yummy, warm spices - a great one-dish winter meal. I added frozen peas so that we could get our veggies and still have it in one pot.

My teens did not really care for this, and if Indian food isn't your thing, this probably won't be a hit for you either. However, I thought it was amazing. It came together easily, and I was thrilled to be able to use many of the exotic (to me) spices that had been languishing in my pantry. I couldn't find ground fennel, though, so I crushed fennel seeds with a mortar and pestle instead.
Nice taste, but not so beautiful to look at. Our children used to call such plates "pirate's food", everything is mixed together...
@Madeleine that is a Biryani. I have never had one that made a beautiful plate but boy is it tasty.
i only have brown basmati rice. How would this change the cooking time?
I would double it and cook it to taste.
Err .. isn't the cow a sacred animal in India?! (ever wonder why beef isn't served in Indian restaurants?!) ..
@Russell Yes, the cow is sacred for Hindus, but other religious groups do eat beef. In India Hindus would make it with lamb.
My favorite Indian restaurant serves beef. I don't think generalities apply, in any case.
@Russell my in-laws are Muslim Indian and I’ve learned several beef recipes from my mother-in-law. The entire country isn’t Hindu.
Would this work with ground turkey?
@himself had it all except yne Chile lol. still a lot for a weeknight
can this be made with groundvchicken?
Plenty of Chicken Biryani recipes out there
Cool shortcut. There is even actually a Hyderabadi qeema pulao that is basically a ground beef biriyani.
Would be helpful if the recipe specified green or black cardamom pods.
Delicious! Lots of ingredients but quite straightforward to make. Served with sliced radicchio topped with the yogurt dressing. Will definitely make again.

Increased the rice (and liquid) for hungry teens and used less cayenne but otherwise made as it. Topped with Trader Joe's fried shallots and served with a veggie side dish. Everyone enjoyed it and agreed we could make it again. Next time would add a little more heat.
@LLinDallasTry finely chopped green chilies in the onion mix
Has anyone had good results subbing dried spices for the cardamom pods and cinnamon sticks?

If you have a well-stocked spice pantry, this comes together quickly and easily. We are huge fans of Indian cuisine, so this was a perfect weeknight meal that came together quickly with. Will be added to our regular rotation. Going to substitute ground lamb next time. Very delicious!

added carrots after the onion was cooked and then a cup of frozen peas to the meat before adding to the rice. 

added cucumber and lemon juice to the raita.

Very tasty.  Something I will do next time - put the spices in a cheesecloth bag (or spice bags which I get on amazon) so you're not picking cardamom pods or cloves from your forkfuls.

Next time I will also start the beef sautee at the same time I start the rice, as for me the rice finished first.

I had all the ingredients and spices on hand other than the ground beef and cinnamon sticks (discovered today TJoe's doesnt have them!) so I used roasted cinnamon powder and added that with the other dry spices.  Doubled the recipe for a large group.  It was delicious and took less than an hour from start to finish, including any prep.  Not sure it is at all traditional but I'd certainly make this again
Sorry, this was more Betty Crocker than Madhur Jaffrey. Lacked flavor and that Indian punch! Won’t repeat.

It was a hit with 3 out of 4 of us.  I thought it was delicious and my son and wife said to make it again.  My daughter, who loves rice and enjoys Thai and Indian food, didn't care for it.  Yes, there is a long list of spices so I measured them out in advance and put them in separate prep bowls with the others that were added at the same time.  Still took me longer than 20 minutes, mostly because the onion and beef took more time because our cooktop has just one large burner.

Made this with ground chicken and added some greens at the end (chard). The rice took me about 10 minutes longer to cook all the way through and I had to add more water to it. Otherwise a delicious, flavorful, and simple recipe!

Very happy with this dish!  For everyone saying the ingredient list is too long, I found it really helpful to prep the two sets of spices earlier in the day, along with slicing the onion.  Then the actual cooking came together really easily and quickly.

The recipe didn’t specify black or green cardamom pods.  I used black but I’m sure either would be fine.  The aromas in the house right now are amazing!

Great recipe - came together quickly and my husband got 3 servings! My 2 year old enjoyed it, too. As a plus - this is a great excuse to visit my local Indian market for some good spices. Will definitely come back to this one!

I cooked this. I had no issues with the ingredients as I have all the Indian spices required in daily cooking. However, in the end it was just kheema mixed with rice for me.  Nothing special. It tasted fine.
I learned of this recipe from @rachels_free_thyme and this dish was amazing! It’s worth the time to get the spices - it was extremely flavorful & very simple. Adding this to my recipe rotation!
Very easy (even with lots of spices) and definitely a comfort food.  Indian-style hamburger helper if you will.  Didn’t have plain yogurt so subbed out with sour cream and it worked beautifully!

This was extremely good, and extremely easy! A really big dividend on the time invested. This recipe will go into regular rotation. I had to add a bit more water to the rice, as my large dutch oven held too much heat after the parboil.
We enjoyed it with ground lamb swapped in for beef.
Am I missing something? I can't seem to find the instruction about what to do with the broth and when/where to add the rice?
Broth and rice are used in Step 1. I missed the rice at first glance, too.
Great recipe! I had the Curry Spice Blend from Auria's Malaysian Kitchen on hand and used it as a shortcut. Didn't disappoint. Local to Brooklyn, too!
@Categrace I see no reason why you shouldn't,  and as a lamb lover, that'd be my choice, too!
Having cooked beef Biryani many times (a couple hours at least), this recipe was excellent. Very flavorful, easy, can make it non-spicy or add more spice or a chutney on the side.

We enjoyed this but I def did adapt it baed on other folks’ comments. Was excited to use some ground lamb I had in the freezer, that is for sure the way to go. Added some leftover hash brown potatoes I needed t and some frozen peas I was glad to use up. I doubled the hot curry power (I get mine from Kalustyan’s) and slightly increased the garam masala, next time I will dial it up even more. Was grateful to read the other notes re how long the rice actually needed.
Really good, thought it would be a bit dry, but it was lovely. Added a small chilli for a little more kick, and also used minced lamb, as a couple of people had suggested, that worked really well.

Really good, thought it would be a bit dry, but it was lovely. Added a handful of peas, and a small chilli for a little more kick, and also used minced lamb, as a couple of people had suggested, really yummy, family enjoyed it, definitely worth doing again!
While I knew my husband probably wouldn’t like this, I made it anyway. He deemed it ‘okay,’ but I loved it! I’d like to try it with chicken! 5 stars all the way!

I had all the spices and aromatics so I made this last night.  It was alright, nothing spectacular. You would think the flavors and aroma could be overwhelming, but it was actually underwhelming.  This is not a recipe I'll keep iny rotation.

I used cayenne pepper in place of the Kashmiri chile powder and accidentally put in the 1/2tsp instead of the 1/4 tsp for the cayenne and man is this spicy hot.
However, I think the ratio of rice in the recipe is just a little to much but I like more substance to my meal than primarily rice. I may try it again but bump up the meat and onion mix or just cut back the rice by 1/4 cup.
The flavor is good minus the bit too much spice heat due to my mistake

Made as directed, and it was really delicious with wonderfully complex just right flavors. Next time I might add a bit more tomato paste to make it saucer and more colorful. Mine was brown and the photo looks more orange. Super satisfying!

Flavor was good, but I would recommend against the cardamom pods and whole cloves. They are very hard to spot/remove and biting into one as you eat is a fairly unpleasant experience. I understand using them makes it a more authentic experience, but seeing as how this is already a "cheater version", I would think using ground seasoning instead would be both more convenient and more pleasant.
You can double up the whole spices and put them in a cheese cloth bag - makes them easy to find and subsequently remove :)
@Seth. Tiesta Tea Filter bags are my go-to for whole spices, as well as loose leaf tea.  I’ve found them at the supermarket and also Amazon. Easy to remove once cooking is completed.
I thought cows were sacred? I’ve never in my life seen beef on an Indian restaurant menu, but ok, I’ll try it lol.

Made with ground lamb. Loved the spices and only had to sub one. However, next time I’ll completely cook the meat mixture separately from the rice. Don’t see any significant benefit since the meat juices can be mingled with the fully cooked rice before serving. Just my preference.
This recipe was incredibly delicious! I substituted the beef with 96 percent beef and used European low-fat yogurt. It turned out to be a super high-protein meal!

Made this for the family tonight, exactly as the recipe is written. Everyone loved it, even the 9 and 7yr old, and we all had 2nds and 3rds. Really delicious especially with the yogurt on top. All the spices matter!

Delicious! I prepped most of this the night before while cleaning up dinner dishes. It came together in a flash the next day. I use this prep one day cook the next technique a lot. The amount of time overall is about the same but dinner is on the table at least 45 minutes earlier when I'm not starting from scratch after I get home from work.
Has anyone tried this using brown rice? Trying to use some up!
Used shallots instead of onion, but otherwise followed the recipe. It was delicious! Will definitely go in the rotation!
Not sure what else I can say!
This was delicious. I added a wee bit of asafoetida to the onion sauté and some saffron to the milk. But it legit took 2 hours. I wish NYT Cooking would be more realistic.

A very successful easy recipe. I forfeited some authenticity by not layering the beef and the rice. I found it more convenient to fully cook the rice and its spices ahead, then add it to the cooked beef. I don't I think sacrificed taste, though. This was delicious.

Really tasty after some adjustments. Made as is, I think the rice to meat ratio is off and it's under seasoned. I reduced the rice to 1 c. and I almost doubled the spices.  I also added frozen peas for the taste and fiber, big fan of all in one meals! Lastly, I didn't make the raita the second time I made the dish. It was fine, but a big blob of yogurt was just as good.  With the tweaks, it's something we'll put on our monthly rotation.
Oh also, a few mint leaves added in with the cilantro is traditional, and a nice touch.  Especially if instead of ground beef you use ground lamb (which was my first thought when reading the recipe.)

Made this today but didn't have Basmati rice and reading other's comments that Basmati is needed, I cooked Jasmine rice in my rice cooker using the spices called for,  , stopping before fully cooked. The spices came to the top after cooking so were easy to remove. 
The rest of the recipe I followed as written. I had some goat burger left in my freezer, probably by one of my son's friends who has left other meats in the past! (Great kid). The goat burger had a lot of fat in it but I sopped it up with paper towels and that worked great.  I pushed the meat to one side in the pan, filled the rest of the pan with the rice and scooped the meat over it and spread both out evenly, then cooked a bit more to finish the rice and let the flavors marry.  I like crispy rice on the bottom of these type of dishes so let it cook a bit longer for some crunch.  Husband loved it. I liked it too. It made several servings. After we had our share there about 4 more servings . Lunch for the week!  

A now that I have made this once and understand the steps needed, I think I can pull it together more quickly but this took me 2 hours including prep-- and hunting down spices in my overflowing spice cabinet.
The timing on the recipe is a mess! Start the onions before the rice, and everything takes longer to cook than stated.

Delicious, easier, & faster. My family all asked me to save this recipe- which is a huge win!

I doubled the rice and spices to stretch the meal further. I used grass fed ground beef & homemade chicken stock for the rice. After reading the comments I put raisins on the side as a topping option for my family - they all added them so next time I will place them between the rice & beef. 
I am glad I started the onions first as I like them slightly caramelized. 

I was frustrated at myself that I didn’t plan for a vegg with dinner so we had fresh baby spinach on the side- thankfully a little bit of the cumin sauce made a nice dressing. For under $15 for an organic meal for 4 (including 2 teenagers) this is a win!
This was so good! I 1.5x the recipe but used 2 lbs. of beef and it still felt like too much rice to beef for our protein lovers in the family.  The flavor; however, was outstanding!
We loved it but next time I'll used 1.5 pounds of beef.

Great base recipe. When I make it again I’d use ginger garlic paste to save chopping time. 
I’d also double the spices, add cucumber to the Raita, and a generous squeeze of lime to the final dish. I also added hing because it was in my pantry, but not worth seeking out if you don’t already have it. Same deal with saffron. Great (and traditional) addition, but not necessary. 
Overall, a great place to start, but as a certified Indian food lover, I think amping up the spices a bit goes a long way.
Am I missing how to cook the mixture for the final 8 min? On a stovetop on low? In the oven (if so, at what temp)?
What kind of beef?
1pound ground beef (preferably 20 percent fat).  Duh!
Substitute for whole milk to avoid lactose issues?
I would sub with a little extra broth or water - or if you want the creaminess and nuts aren't an issue, coconut milk or cashew cream.

I want to make this for a group. Can I double or triple it? 
Would you consider adding that advice in more of your recipes? 
Many of them could be used at a dinner party not just a family of four.
You don't drain the fat from the beef?
Fat = flavor. You could use 85%, 90%, or 93% ground beef if fat content is a concern.

@Emmy
I used a very lean ground beef and it was a bit dry. Recipe recommends 80% which is likely better.
Any suggestions for substitutions for those of us who cannot eat mammal meat? Would ground chicken or turkey provide enough depth of flavor?
I substitute ground turkey (93%) often in similar Indian dishes that list beef, pork, or lamb and find the result very satisfying and more healthy.  Give it a try.
I can’t eat tomato or tomato paste. Would omitting it compromise the end flavours?
@Marney O Yes, but if you can eat red bell peppers, then a purée from roasted red bells would be a good substitute.
I never put it in biryani when I make it in the pressure cooker, I think you'll still have a delicious meal.
Does the dutch oven stay on low heat on the hob after adding the beef or does it get moved to the oven at any point? I’m a bit afraid of the meat not being done or the rice burning
Do you think this would work with leftover braised beef?
No, Beth, but thanks for asking
Why not give it a try?  I often recycle leftover roast into curries and chilis and, since this recipe has so many lovely spices, I feel sure that the result will still be worth the effort.
I gather that step 6 is the cumin raita, even though it only uses 1/4 teaspoon of cumin?
Would ground lamb work?
Yes, lamb will work in place of beef.  Lamb is more fatty than most ground beef so you will definitely want to drain the fat after browning the meat.
If you add ground lamb it's going to be very, very oily.

From my teen: “What the heck??? This was so good. You have to make it again.” 

Stayed loyal to the recipe except for the raita: added mint and shredded some cucumber into to yogurt instead of adding water.

Quick, easy and delicious weeknight meal. I only had to buy the ground beef, everything else had on hand. Served with a green salad and naan. Excellent. Will definitely put it in the rotation. Thank you!
But it's almost all spices! Not really so many ingredients, mostly beef, rice, and yogurt. @Gina
But, Judith, as others have pointed out, most of the ingredients are just spices.@judith

Good. I doubled the beef and trebled the spices. I also layered the rice so the beef was sandwiched in between and added a whole onion sliced and fried on top. Would be great with lamb and also some chiles in there.
It's just the two of us. Can we freeze half for another quick dinner?
Mmmmm… Sacred Cow Biryani!  Very traditional and authentic!  Or possibly an abomination?
Not all hold the cow sacred. Many see it as a prime source of meat.

So delicious!  Tons of flavor.  Relatively quick and easy once you have your spices organized.  
One question - ingredients list cumin seed, recipe refers to cumin.  I was tempted to use ground cumin for the raita as the seed didn't give a ton of flavor but wanted to stick to the recipe as written the first time around.  Has anyone used ground instead of seed?
@B. I used ground and thought it worked great
@B. I’m not an expert but the raw seed in the raita seems odd to me. I love it with ground cumin.

Delicious, but I had trouble managing it… even with doing almost all the prep up front, I was still back and forth to drawers and fridge and cabinet frantically.
I made the NYT lemon kale salad as a side, and my husband just dumped the 2 dishes into one bowl and said it was better that way.

Made this for dinner tonight—upped the spices in general but not a whole lot, and used chopped boneless chicken thighs in place of ground meat bc I just don’t love ground meats. Didn’t have enough cilantro, so augmented it with basil. Minor tweaks, I think. This one is definitely a keeper!
To me this is an Indian spiced version of hamburger helper. If I make it again I would increase the chili powder.

Why does Step 5 say to layer the beef over the rice....and Step 7 says to fluff the rice and remove the visible spices?  And why has no one else asked this question?  I mean, I have made Biryani before and can figure out what to do, but shouldn't recipes make sense?
Step 5 doesn't contradict Step 7. You cook the meat on top of the rice in the oven, and then before serving, fluff everything and remove the whole spices.
Grind your cardamom pods before putting in the dish.  I couldn’t find them all and it is unpleasant to bite into a pod
Good! use actual basmati next time
Question. I have a neurospicy son with texture issues. Anyone ever try this with stew beef chuck?
Your recipe doesn't say what to do with the ½ cup yogurt, cumin, water, salt & pepper mixture.  Is it a topping?

Step 6: 
Meanwhile in a small bowl, combine the remaining ½ cup yogurt, ¼ teaspoon cumin and 2 tablespoons of water and season with salt and pepper. Mix well.
Yes. It's a topping. You serve it on the side and drizzle on top before eating.
What’s a good cut of beef to swap in for ground beef?
I don't use ghee often enough to keep it in stock. Would it be very dreadful to substitute vegetable oil of some kind?
@JennyD use unsalted butter if not ghee
Anyone ever add squash of sweet potatoes to this?

This is very good and as advertised — a simple weeknight meal.  One minor comment that I haven’t seen previously — the amount of stock (2cups) is woefully inadequate for 1-1/2 cups of rice.  I doubled the amount of stock and it came out perfect.

Made largely as directed.  The only minor changes.  First, 2 cups of broth wasn't enough for the rice so I added another 1/2 - 3/4 cup and that allowed the rice to fully cook.  Of course, different rices absorb differently so you'll have to watch and make adjustments as needed.  Second, I didn't have any cilantro so couldn't include it.  Last, I added frozen peas to cook for the last minute and they made a nice addition. The spice blend and levels were spot on.
I made this dish exactly as written and my husband and two adult sons loved it to the point I had no leftovers.

In spite of the number of ingredients this is easy, and quick if you pull the spices together before hand. 
It was utterly delicious, but my rice did not cook fully. I’m hoping the leftovers will be moister, as the rice should absorb more moisture while stored in the fridge overnight. I followed the recipe very carefully, so the undercooked rice surprised me. Anyone else? Any suggestions?
@Katherine Davies mine too. I think I maybe turned it down too low? Need time I will cover it, as I suspected I should have :/

@Katherine Davies i just reread the recipe and realized they said to cover the rice earlier on so i didn’t cover it while it cooked. I generally wait for rice to boil then cover… the recipe states to cover early- but how do you know it’s boiling? lol

I've made 'proper' breyani many times, so was sceptic about the shortcuts in this recipe. But it really did taste like 'proper' breyani! (Minus the different textures of the traditional.) I doubled the recipe and left it to finish for much longer than the suggested 8 minutes - was delicious.
Could you not just do the first step in a rice cooker, then top it with the beef mixture?
I have made this as written, and was thinking the same thing!

Made this tonight.  I didn't have basmati rice, so used jasmine... probably not the best choice, it was mushy.  I think I'd like to try it using 100% wild rice?  I expected the dish to be more of a flavor bomb than it was, given all the spices used.  Except for the type of rice, I followed the recipe to the "T".  Husband loved it.
The timing seems off.  My rice never got fully cooked.  Even after 30min of cooking time.
This is a delicious dish, but the cumin was too pronounced. Easy fix: For the raita, brighten it by omitting cumin and subbing in peeled, deseeded, and diced cucumber; finish lemon zest.
How much of each spice would you use if using powdered spices instead of cinnamon sticks, whole cloves, etc?
Very easy to make and delicious!!
This was very good! As others have suggested I’ll steep a few Saffron threads in the milk and swap the ground beef for ground lamb to amp up the flavors even more. This was a total winner!

The most disappointing 5-star recipe I have ever tried on NYT cooking - and I have cooked a lot of them!  I even increased the garlic, ginger and Kashmiri chili and it was still just blah!  Not bad, just blah!
Twice the broth, more salt, in a 350 degree oven for 20 minutes. Good with pork too. Used fennel seeds instead of ground fennel. Added lemon juice to the raita for more tang

Loved this! Didn't have Greek yogurt, so used European style and it was fine - I just didn't need to add the water to the half used for serving. Would make double the amount of seasoned yogurt for serving next time.
Quick, easy and tasty. Made as written but would add a little more heat next time. Perhaps a serrano or two in the rice.

Dish was excellent and added yogurt at the end gave it  a really nice taste. Question: recipe says add 3 star anise and then to be removed later on.  Recipe doesn’t  explain how to remove them. They are all mixed in with the rice and meat.  This info seems to be missing.
@Cathy whole star anise are very large. Just pick them out with your hand or tongs.
I would imagine one could easily use grated forms of the whole spices called for in this recipe which might make the spice flavors stronger.

Love this!

I doubled recipe and used one pound of lamb and 1 pound of beef. I also added a green pepper. Easy. Scrumptious for the whole family!
Just great. Fast easy, delicious, it’s a keeper!

This is a comforting and yummy dish! I was missing the star anise and cardamom, so I upped the garam masala a titch. Two out of three kids gobbled this up and said they wanted leftovers for tomorrow’s dinner. The third child is fickle and will probably love it tomorrow. This would go well with roasted cauliflower :)
Really delicious. Added some cilantro and cucumber to the raita and drizzled a little lemon on the rice before eating.

I made only two small changes,  using regular yogurt as that is what I had (added a bit more to the meat and didn't add water to the Raita), and half the cayenne as I was worried it would be to hot (but it wasn't hot at all and I think the whole amount would have been fine).  It was delicious and I think it might hold the record for the number of times my husband said 'This is really good!' during one meal.
I made this ‘as is’ but with ground chicken. Next time, I’ll reduce the amount of rice or increase the amount of chicken.

This makes a lot of food, and it was tasty. I felt that it needed more of everything in terms of seasoning to taste like restaurant biryani. 1/4 tsp of anything is practically not there! The 1/4 tsp of cayenne possibly being too spicy was especially questionable.
Made exactly as directed but with 2X the quantity of spices.  Very tasty!  Took about 45 minutes in total, what with assembling and measuring out all the spices.
So delicious!!! And so easy if you mis en place the spices first! I used brown basmati and the cook time was obviously much longer (40 minutes before putting beef on top). It’s so good!!
If you wanted to add another layer of flavor and "authenticity" (loaded word), add a little rose water to the milk before drizzling it over the rice.  Barely a teaspoon, I'd say.

Nice as written. Our package of ground beef was a little over a pound and used it all. Perhaps that threw off the beef-to-rice ratio, but we didn't mind. Star anise was pretty powerful so might scale that back by half and increase the cumin seeds next time.

This was incredibly good!! I don’t eat beef very often - but this will definitely be a recipe I come back to. 
I didn’t add the chili pepper (didn’t want spicy) or the fennel (don’t like it). Used very lean ground beef, fat free yogurt and 2% milk. Came out great!
Delicious and comes together really easily as written. I decided to add steamed cauliflower for some veggies. Without that, there may have been too much rice. I would add a bit more beef next time.
Will make again.
Do you take the cinnamon stick, cardamom pods, whole cloves out after you cook the rice or do they dissolve when cooking?
Very good. I would suggest start the onion-spice-beef part first (at least get the onions started, and everything else prepped), then get the rice going. Use less ghee too.
As long as you have the spices, it really is quite simple. I recommend a wee bit less rice and adding currants.
Lol, “ready in 30 minutes.“ It would take me that long just to gather up all of the spices. Looking forward to giving it a try, but let’s be real.
Mine came out sort of greasy and gloppy. could have sworn I followed the recipe exactly so not sure what went wrong. Nice flavor, the texture was just unpleasant.
Easy, quick, and delicious!
this was so easy but so satisfying. we all loved it - even the kids!
