I had some lamb thawed, and was looking for a recipe, and found this. (I've been making a lot of Turkish keftes lately but I felt having something with more of an Indian flavour). I didn't have garam masala at hand so I used Floyd Cardoz Green Tikka Masala, and ground lamb instead of beef. I added a small amount of Chaat Masala as well (about 1/2 teaspoon). And I didn't have any ginger or anise so I used a store brand Pho broth to cook the rice that has both. It turned out very well!
I guess that’s Phoushion!
Very good and kicked up the spice w hot chili oil topping along w the cool yogurt.  Great on a cold winter night.

If this is a shortcut version, I cringe at the idea of the ‘regular’ version!  So many ingredients, so many steps!  It was however, FANTASTIC!  The flavors are amazing! I followed the recipe exactly except I used 1t. ground cardamom in lieu of pods, and I doubled all spices, as a previous commenter suggested.
What could I substitute for tomato paste in this recipe? We have a family member with tomato allergy.
Maybe not something you have on hand, but I didn't have any tomato paste, but I had some red curry paste that I used instead, and it turned out great!

This was excellent! Used brown rice and precooked for 30 minutes. Used venison instead of beef. Used soy milk and fat free Greek yogurt. Also used olive oil instead of butter. Even with these subs it was so delicious and easy.
Can you make in advance?
I don't see why not. There's jyst the two of us so we ate it over several days. Add a little moisture when re-heating it.
Absolutely delicious and so easy! Great spice mix.

This is a wonderful recipe but one thing confused me. It says in Step One to cook the rice for 10 minutes. The next instruction re the rice does not appear until Step 5 to cook the rice along with the beef mixture for 8 minutes. That is not nearly enough time to cook the rice. My rice is not close to ready after an additional 12 minutes. I'm concerned the additional rice cooking time will result in tough beef. Did I miss something here?

Susan, you did not. My rice was nowhere near ready after such a short cooking time. I removed the beef mixture from the heat until the rice was almost done. I cooked the beef with the rice for only about 5-6 minutes until everything was ready.

This is the same Susan who just wrote about rice cooking time. I just realized the problem may be that I used brown basmati rice that requires a longer cooking time that white rice. I assume I should have cooked the rice much longer in Step One.
I made this tonight, but used Impossible Ground "Beef" and plant based dairy to make this vegan. And it was delicious! Five stars
Just incredible - yielded the best dinner my partner and I have made together.  We added a generous spritz of lemon juice to the raita to add a little acid.

So good!! I did what others suggested and doubled the spices for the beef and it was delicious. The hubby and picky boys gobbled it all up in record time. I just mixed the rice with the beef at the end, rather than layering the beef over the rice and it still came out great. Definitely don’t skip the raita topping, so easy to whip up and adds another dimension of flavor.
Very tasty and easy recipe. I added a frozen bag of peas and carrots to get our veggies in. Big hit with the fam.

Rice dishes like this never seem to turn out for me in a normal pot. I think it’s a “me problem,” though. After I tried this as written and ended up with undercooked rice, I tried again in my rice cooker with great results. Rice, whole spices, broth and milk in the pot, the onion and beef mixture cooked as written and then scraped on top, then whole thing cooked with the “white rice” setting on my zojirushi. Same measurements for everything. Turned out perfect!

I used brown basmati rice, and as Susan noted below, my rice was not remotely ready after a mere 18 minutes. I cooked the rice longer while the beef mixture rested off-heat. When the rice was almost ready, I added the milk & then the beef mixture for another 5-6 minutes. 

Whole family loved it - the teenaged nephew ate two servings.

I probably said this already, but this is so amazingly delicious and easy.  I think I could eat this every week.  I made no changes, except I used ground cardamom because that’s all I had.  Just so satisfying.  A party in your mouth!

title: Laab Noodle Salad Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Larb is really Lao by origin. It’s their national dish. A lot of great Lao food gets credited as Thai
@kim n Thanks for the educational tip! Credit where credit is due!!

I’m a vegetarian and I used coconut aminos for fish sauce and gardein fake beef bits for the meat. I think this recipe needs that beefy flavor compared to tofu to be more authentic to the Laotian dish. I had no troubles with a 1:1 sub and will be making this again!
Used ground chicken instead of beef, and added chopped scallions and mint to the cilantro. Turned out great!
Made this tonight—increased the garlic, reduced the lettuce a bit, and added mint and sliced raw shallots to the salad. Delicious! Will make again

There are shared culinary traditions in many Asian countries, especiallythose that have common borders. Laab features more in northern Thailand, which shares a border with Laos. There are differences in their versions. Similarly, some southern Thai dishes have common roots with Malaysian cuisine. The history behind countries also has a strong influence on their culinary traditions.

Like the recipe but the proportions seemed off when I tried it. Biggest problem: way too much lettuce (8 cups? Seems...wrong? I mean look at the photo here, there's just not that much lettuce.). and I made a lot more sauce (mostly adding more lime juice and didn't regret it, and doubled the garlic. Adding a bit of mint was a good move also. But otherwise -- yeah great flavors will make again.

@Kurt Hoffman Did you find that 12 oz of romaine really came out to 8 cups? I feel like weight-to-volume greens conversions often come out wonky. That said, it does kind of sound like a lot of lettuce either way—suppose I can just eyeball it when I make this next week.

I love Larb, cook it often. There's a key ingredient missing. Gotta have that toasted rice powder! It really changes the flavor. For everyone saying, "I don't have that in the pantry!" You do! It's just toasted white rice (Jasmin is my preference). Get a cast iron hot, throw in some white rice. Stir it for 10 minutes and you've got it. I admit crushing it into a powder is a little more challenging. Use a mortar and pestle don't do much, you do not need a lot get it fine.
We enjoyed this versatile meal!

Nah this is not a good version of laab/larb. The green beans, unless exceptionally thin, will need more time. Ground beef is honestly the third choice for protein in this.  And the dressing is vastly underpowered and over-oiled. Also, more herbs like mint or thai basil/basil should be added. Gave 2 stars and that felt generous.
I almost liked this. Made a vegetarian version with Not Burger steak tips chopped fin. Problem waa the sauce. It should have been sweeter.
I ramped up the fresh herbs to finish this.  Nearly a cup of combined cilantro, mint, dill.  I was surprised but the dill worked well with the fish/lime flavour in the dressing.

Charred green beans were such a lovely addition to this dish! Followed recipe exactly and ended up with enough for lunches the next day. I agree with several comments about the excess romaine, however if you pack the greens separately it makes for a great work salad. Might try adding toasted peanuts next time :)

I felt this would be very bland as written. 

Added mushrooms, ginger, lemon grass, basil and hot dried Thai chili powder to the beef

Used sesame oil 

Dressing used two Thai chilis and one jalapeño, added lots of cilantro, used whole bag of limes 

Dressed salad generously then added very generously dressed noodles and topped with beef and cilantro 


It was amazing that way

Not bad but needs some tweaks. Omit the oil & salt from the dressing and add more fish sauce + a little sugar to taste. Toasted rice powder + green onions would have been a nice addition to the ground meat. Mint also needed with the cilantro.
Good but a little bland, maybe I needed more of the dressing?

Made with following changes-
Used 1 year frozen spiny lobster- boiled it and cut in small pieces (needed to clear freezer, this  looked like a good recipe) - instead of beef
Had egg noddles - used those instead of rice noodles 
Added cut up cucumber (clearing fridge)
No fresh chili available - used Gochujang paste
Languishing in fridge spring lettuce mix instead of romaine- I eyed what looked like the photo  for amount, definitely not 8 cups.

We loved it! Will do again with lobster - I am always looking for lobster recipes since my spouse dives and catches plenty in FL waters.
This was delicious as written, but too oily for me. I’ll make it on repeat, but will cut back the oil in the dressing and the beans.
Lapp originated in Laos and is the national dish. It's unfortunately very common for Lao food to be labeled and credited as Thai food.
We iked the salad  but it needed more flavor. Perhaps more lime juice and fresh mint.

Agree with others suggestions - drawing was too oily and needed some sweetness for balance. I made a few other tweaks suggested - added more herbs; mint and basil and green onions on top. I almost liked it. Will play with the dressing again next time.
I lived in Thailand for six years.  Believe me, this recipe has nothing in common with Thai (or Lao) laab.  I tried it but was very disappointed.  What a confusing mess.
does this keep well for leftovers? wondering how the noodles would do…
@carmen it does. I had two days worth of leftovers and it was delicious! Noodles were fine.

This was delicious! Next time, I will reduce the romaine and increase the noodles, but that is because I LOVE vermicelli noodles. I think the adding scallions and chopped peanuts would also add additional texture/flavor.
Lots of critique here, but I totally loved rhos salad.  I wish American "gastrobistros" would offer more salad variety and introduce customers to fabulous dishes like this.

The sauce risks ruining what starts out feeling like it’s going to be a great meal. It needs about 3x the fish sauce and, sugar! It’s way too sour and flat as is. I kept tasting it until it worked and the amped version made this dinner amazing. 
I also served it deconstructed and with rice instead of noodles with some sliced onions and adjusted the lettuce way down. Now it’s a keeper

I tried this as written because of all the raves but very much missed the usual slivered makrut lime leaves (rib removed), finely minced lemongrass, and further variety of herbs (mint, thai basil, etc) that I always use for laab. It was perfectly edible but missing oomph. Personally would also prefer chicken or pork over the beef, found the beef overwhelmed the other flavors, and cucumber/bean sprouts over bell pepper.
Could it be made with tofu for a vegetarian version?

I find that a half-firm tofu, half-mushroom mix (both chopped fine) works really well in laab, to provide umami and toothsome-bouncy texture (without turning to highly processed fake "meats"). I also usually increase the seasonings a bit to compensate for those ingredients' habits of soaking up flavor.
Tempeh broken up into tiny bits also works really well in any laab recipe.
Loved this. Light and flavorful and not too citrusy. I used oyster sauce and some red pepper flakes because I didn’t have a fresh chili or fish sauce. Will definitely make it again.
Anyone have an acceptable substitute for the fish sauce? It's not something I commonly use and am trying not to buy condiments that are just going to sit in my fridge.
@Amy O you don't need to refrigerate fish sauce. Just leave it on the shelf along with your soy sauce.

We liked this dish except for the hamburger flavor. Made it a second time with ground sausage and it was much better. A refreshing meal that isn’t too filling which may not be a good thing - it is easy to have a second and third round of this dish at the same meal. It is a keeper though!

I think touch more sauce next time.
Also used bean sprouts and Carrots for the vegetables. 
Good recipe.
Thank you

Added the whites of green onions and a diced Jalapeno with the green beans. Doubled the garlic and did an extra Thai chili. Reduced the oil in the sauce by a tablespoon and used EVOO. Another commenter was right, I probably used half the romaine… pretty good and would make again!
The dressing was disappointing: bland and too oily. Next time I'll try only half of the oil, and probably pump of the garlic.

title: Scallion-Miso Mac and Cheese Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody:
this african american household can offer some ways to improve the blandness to which some have referred. (let me also say that i believe that the ritz cracker-scallop crust to be a genius technique)

first: where it says season with salt and pepper, i recommend sprinkling onion and garlic powder LIBERALLY. compliments the creaminess with some needed savoriness to complement the umami of the miso 

second: learn from the vegans. a tablespoon of nutritional yeast goes a long way in complementing the nuttiness of the gruyere without adding gruyere cost. 

three, and i cannot recommend this enough: you do not need that much milk. makes sauce too watery. i did two cups milk and one cup of BROTH. flavor was hitting. 

fourth: maybe this will be tacky to the more refined palate, but combine sharp and smoked cheddar. 

follow my african american household technique and this recipe will slap, as we say in oakland, california.
@nick m. Agree! We didn’t have that much milk so only used 3 cups. Plenty!
@nick m. Thanking from a person of TASTE

@nick m. At what step did you add the nutritional yeast? My Southern African American household is shaking up the traditional holidays items. My mother makes the Mac and cheese normally, so I got a lot at stake here!

NYT must have some BIG scallions! 4 scallions gave me a scant 1/3 c. Ended up chopping about 8 scallions to get 3/4 c. Made for a very scallion-y topping which I think is the intent. Added some parm to the topping too.

Increased miso to a healthy 3 TB bec everything is better with miso!

Overall a 5 star dish! A big upgrade from traditional Mac n cheese.
@cheri I actually think you have very small scallions
@cheri right? Was just thinking the same thing! Hah.

@cheri
Are you sure they were scallions, not shallots? I've never heard of scallions that small.

Miso is great for adding flavor, but its real strength is all the healthy bacteria for the GI tract that it contains. So healthy. But boiling miso kills all that bacteria, so it should never be boiled. In this recipe, after taking it off the heat, before adding mustard & cheese, once the temp goes below boiling the miso can be safely whisked in.  Get all the incredible health benefits of miso that you are paying for.
@ds Doesn’t matter.  You’re baking it in the oven.
@ds as someone else pointed out, you’re baking it anyway. Sometimes using miso is just for the flavor.
Just made! Good but tastes slightly flat to me. I added chili flakes after baking and if I make again, would increase scallions by 1-2.
@Sarah agreed! Could be more flavorful for sure. I think less milk, more miso & definitely chili flakes.

V good with the following mods: 

1) double the dijon 
2) diced shallot, cooked in a little olive oil prior to making the roux 
3) onion, garlic, and porcini powder in the sauce (scant teaspoon of each) plus little pinch of cayenne
4) more cheese on top along with the crackers for a nice top crust

This dish was amazing!! I read the comments beforehand and tweaked it - 2 cups milk instead of 4, 3 heaping tablespoons of miso, about a teaspoon each of garlic and onion powder, an extra tablespoon of Dijon. Holy moly it was fantastic!
Flour and cracker substitutes for making this gluten free? I already use GF pasta.
@Marc Wortsman You can use gluten-free flour (I use King Arthur brand). Not sure of best substitute for the Ritz crackers.
@Marc Wortsman maybe GF panko for the Ritz sub
@Marc Wortsman I agree that GF Panko would be a good sub for Ritz crackers. Schar also makes a Ritz-like GF cracker - they call them “Entertainment Crackers.”
Because this is so rich, I think it’s closer to 6 servings than 4.
This is the best mac and cheese I’ve ever made. So perfectly creamy and delicious without being greasy. I used 2% milk instead of whole and it was so good. My husband and I cannot get enough of it.

Didn't have enough scallions for this so fried some leeks beforehand and added back to the pot before it went into the oven. Used more miso as others suggested, panko instead of crackers, topped with chopped walnuts and cilantro at the end. Served some cauliflower on the side...for our health!! Delicious and would make again!
Cooked garlic + onion (finely minced) in 2 tbsp of the butter before making the roux and it added a lot of flavor. Also added paprika! Pretty good recipe overall, would make again :)

I followed the recipe except: (1) I added 1/8 tsp red pepper into the milk mixture and (2) mixed some of the breadcrumb/scallion mixture into the pasta pre-baking. Really good! Make sure to use *at least* 1 lb. pasta, it’s pretty saucy.

I used panko because I didn't want the butteriness of the Ritz crackers. Put a generous helping of red pepper flakes in the sauce, and included some other fun spices in the panko+scallion topping. It was divine!
do not need that much milk... used about 3/4 of what they said
Rich and creamy mac and cheese for adults although next time we make this we will add a head of chopped up cauliflower to the end of the pasta cooking so it isn’t quit so saucy

This was very good; I’ll do it again with panko + salted butter. The ritz didn’t add much for us and isn’t something we usually have on hand. The servings are very, very generous if you’re cooking as written for 4. I’d say at least it serves at least 6, if you’re serving with a salad or green veg, and we have pretty healthy appetites.

this was delicious! I used heaping tablespoons of both miso and mustard, only 3 cups of milk, and mixed in some grated parmigiano into the breadcrumb mixture (I used panko since that’s what I had on hand).
We made the recipe exactly as written and it was just a bit underwhelming. Next time I would use more scallions and add them into the cheese mixture. A bit of hot sauce worked wonders.

Made this with gluten free pasta (the box was 12 oz) and gluten free panko and it worked fine.  Agree with previous comment that recipe is good but a little flat (though the scallion topping helps); I think it could use more cheese, maybe even double.

A delightful Mac and Cheese, with the miso and mustard adding really deep flavor. We found ourselves adding Sriracha to our bowls for the heat. Definitely can add heat as the bechamel gets going if you like, with chili flakes a good suggestion. We did make sure to get the full cup of scallions and that helps the texture and a nice punch throughout. It is a touch oversauced for just one pound of pasta but that's kind of the decadence of it.

This was soo good! I followed @nick m’s helpful advice and added more flavor early on - sautéed a modest mix of minced leek-shallot-garlic in the butter before adding the flour, reduced the milk (2%) and added light vegetable broth. Only having a stronger red miso on hand, I went easy with it, adding it right after turning the stovetop off to avoid overcooking the miso. We like a creamier mac & cheese, so I also stirred in pasta water to the sauce and it stayed a perfect velvety consistency. Overall, I reduced the butter and cheese by about 1/4-1/3 and this was still plenty flavorful and creamy. I was tempted to add a little nutmeg to the sauce but held off, not confident red miso and nutmeg would be compatible.

I made just half the recipe as I was cooking for just two people, and followed it to the letter. It is definitely delicious and I will be repeating it. But it is very rich, what with all the cheeses, whole milk and butter! I really cannot imagine the full recipe being just 4 servings. Even half the recipe was a generous 6 servings with a side salad, and so I froze four portions. For curiosity’s sake I calculated the total calories in the full recipe and it came out to 4910!! In other words, if you were to eat 1/4 the full recipe you will be consuming a whopping 1220 Calories. I think recipe developers should base portion size on calorie content and aim for no more than 500 Calories per serving. I don’t mean to be preachy, but I as a nutritionist I am eager to promote healthy eating. To do this we don’t need to entirely abstain from high calorie foods, but restrict the portion size.

Excellent, but I don't know why anyone would want to go to the trouble of making a bechamel sauce and cooking the pasta when Julia Moskin's version is so much easier, and just as tasty. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1015825-creamy-macaroni-and-cheese
@Daniel Starr because a bechamel makes it creamy and rich and not dry and gummy
Would a pinch of cayenne spice it up a bit?
Try drizzling some sesame oil on top (not too much) after it comes out of the oven or on an individual serving.

This was my girl dinner for tonight! I wanted to test the recipe before making it for my bf who is an adventurous and picky eater. Crazy combo but it’s true. 

The flavor fell flat and I didn’t love how grainy the sauce ended up being once it was served. I saw some comments say to add less milk so I’ll do that but I think extra seasoning like rubbed sage or even Korean chili pepper would be good. Probably won’t make it again unless my mother wants to try it

4C of milk was too much. I would do 2 or 3C next time. I used a mixture of regular bread crumbs and panko, and it turned out well but would have liked a bit more crust so will also make more bread crumb mix next time. I added more miso, and topped it with crispy chili flakes before eating to add some spice.

Stupid Question (beginner chef here)
If I wanted to make a smaller portion, could I just halve all the ingredients?

Definitely benefits from some seasoning other than just salt and pepper -- I used a pepper grinder spice blend that also includes some garlic powder, onion powder, red pepper and citrus zest, as well as some other spices. I was also used a lot of plain pepper in addition. My miso tablespoons were generous as well. Not bland at all, but benefited from some hot sauce on top to taste.

That said, absolutely delicious!
Can I dump into a casserole dish before sprinkling with crumbs and baking?
Is the pasta pre-cooked or raw (still dry) when it goes in the sauce?

George - You ask if pasta is pre-cooked or dry when it is added to the sauce.  See Step 1.  Cooked to al dente.  "Cook pasta in a large pot of salted water according to package directions until a few minutes shy of al dente. Drain."
Way too much cheese.

We made this as directed tonight but used the trick I learned from J. Kenji Lopez-Alt for oven baked pasta dishes where you soak the pasta in a bowl of warm water for 30 minutes instead of boiling it. It hydrates the pasta and then when you bake it, it finishes cooking. Worked great!
This is so good!! Served with a kale salad and some tomato soup. Perfect for a cold rainy night.
Miso paste or powder?
Cook the pasta last
Anybody add white wine? 😁

I listened to the comments here and halved the amount of milk and added extra miso and Dijon and it tastes great! Me and my husband both went back for seconds. I don’t even know wha the end product would be if you used 4 cups of milk, maybe like a milk soup with distant flavors? Either, halve the milk.
Delicious and easy and I would say double or triple the amount of scallions!

Absolutely delicious! We followed all of nick m's advice; next time will sub something for Ritz crackers which came out soggy. Used ~2 bundles of scallions to get the amount required and left out the mustard which we don't favor. Worked out to be ~7 servings for the two of us and paired extremely well with asparagus roasted alongside.
Nice recipe, and I adopted some of these suggestions but I felt like the mustard overpowered the other flavors. Would omit the mustard next time
I found this to be bland, despite following tips to spice it up a bit. I do not recommend any part of it other than the topping which was yummy.

I did most of the improvements people suggested in the comments – more miso, more mustard, less milk (I used just under 3 cups of oat milk and still found the recipe very intense/rich), garlic powder, onion powder, chili powder, nutrional yeast. Despite all this, I found it to fall pretty flat flavor wise. I don't quite get it because I love miso and it's usually a pretty strong flavor. It's a pretty easy recipe and the texture of the sauce was awesome but I wished the taste was underwhelming.

I thought this was tasty, but not so much better than other mac and cheese that it was worth some of the extra work and ingredients (and calories!). Did incorporate the top suggestions, and they were good.
Any thoughts/experience on adding tofu for protein?

I read many of the comments & tend to cook with what's in the house & needs to be used.  So I did not have milk, but I did have heavy cream.  I used one cup of it, 2 cups of broth. I didn't use it all.  I increased the miso to 3 tbsp, doubled the mustard to 2 tbsp, added some pepper (no salt needed), and pressed some fresh garlic into the cheese.  I used a mix of cheddar, Havarti, and Gouda with fresh Parmesan.  Stir-fried chopped broccoli & mixed in, then added 10 scallions to the panko for top
I do not think I have ever before seen a recipe that is more than 1200 calories per serving!

Like a good Trini, add some yellow mustard and a bit of ketchup, along with onion & garlic powder. And some scotch bonnet Trini hot sauce on the side is great, but this is not for the faint of heart, or stomach.

Made as written, only changes were less milk (1 ½ cup heavy cream and 1 cup 2% milk) and a little extra miso (red miso instead of white) because that’s what I had on hand. Also made a little extra topping, and used only cheddar. Delicious and subtle flavor. Will make again.

title: Mushroom Juk Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: This recipe would make a lot more sense if you rehydrated the dried shiitake mushrooms during Step 1. Once hydrated, separate them from the liquid and thinly slice. Set them aside to be added to the pot with everything else in Step 2. The reserved shiitake liquid could then be filtered and added during Step 2 as part of the 8 cups of liquid used to cook everything else. That gets us to Step 3 unencumbered. Otherwise, this recipe is delicious, satisfying, and a great option for plant based meals.

Step 2 made no sense, I agree. I soaked the mushrooms, sliced them and used the reserved liquid (did not filter it) as part of my liquid. You could also throw some dashi into the soaking water and then you can leave out the Kombu if you so desire. Other than step 1, I threw the whole shebang in to my rice cooker and walked away.
I always save my shitake liquid, you can freeze it and throw it into your bone broth, or any soup, casserole, I have added it when I am boiling little potatoes too.

Very nice! Made it for 2, so halved recipe except kept full amounts of garlic and ginger. Used broth for some of the water and didn't find the dried shitake, salt or soy necessary. Added vegs in the last minutes of cooking: diced chard stems and sliced bok choy. Easy and satisfying!
@Barbara Bok choy is an excellent idea will have to try that :)

Well, an easy recipe for midweek cooking. I don’t like tofu, so added shrimp. Flavors very mild, and added a bunch of Korean chili flakes and paste to give it some flavor. Did not notice miso at all. Might be a good idea to cook with mushroom broth to bring in the mushroom flavor more. This is the Korean equivalent of Congee, which I find more flavorful because the rice is cooked in chicken stock.  This is a good recipe for someone who’s trying to get over a cold.

Two interesting new-to-me things in this recipe to try out: 
1. "Finely chopped" mushrooms must be a way to incorporate them into the background of every bite with little notice. 
2. Caramelizing miso. I've generally been in the camp of adding miso after the heat is turned off, knowing that boiling miso can diminish its subtle flavor notes and kill its beneficial probiotics. However, I have made miso-glazed black cod, and experienced the tasty maillarding that occurs when the fish browns and creates a sticky, savory crust under a broiler. 

Looking back at it, it looks like #2 is not new to me, but this application is.
I wonder how it would come out different if you add the miso after the heat is reduced.  Have you tried?
I halved the recipe and used brown rice. Added 2 cups of broth and 2 cups of water. Delicious, saucy rice. Could use more liquid if you want a soupy porridge. I also pan fried some enoki mushrooms.

I have never cooked with kombu and I see the recipe calls for a 3" piece.  Am I wrong in assuming that it would be removed after cooking?  Or does it disintegrate?  Or should it be chopped (although the recipe doesn't indicate).
@Anne A yes remove it after cooking.
@Anne A, I have also shredded it very finely and added it back in, or eaten it separately with a vinaigrette and chopped or grated fresh veggies.

This was great. I char miso in the oven to use in various dishes, so loved the inclusion for caramelization with the mushrooms. Didn't mind fishing out the shiitakes and slicing them in step 2 like some others - totally fine...

We had the works for toppings: fresh tofu, herbs from the garden, kimchi, spring onions, chili crisp, dark soy sauce... Would be easy to incorporate fresh veggies at the end.

Easy dinner with leftovers for breakfast. Will go on the rotation...

Delicious and warming for the first snowy day of the year! We used 4 C of mushroom broth, 2 of veggie broth, and 2 of water. Didn’t use the dried shiitake but went heavy on the fresh mushrooms and it was very flavorful. Topped with some cubed silken tofu, lots of cilantro and scallion, and a tiny bit of plain sesame oil.
Looking forward to making. Should I be using previously cooked rice? That’s what I’ve done in the past when making rice porridge.
Do the instructions mean fishing the previously-dried but now rehydrated shiitake mushrooms out of the pot in order to slice them thinly and return to the pot?

@Gala
Yes. See Step 2.
Could this be made with brown rice? Thank you in advance for advice.

Has anyone made this w short grain brown rice? I’m thinking I’d parboil it for awhile , giving it a head start before I chop and slice and sauté the mushrooms, then add it in where white rice is added in the recipe.

@Barbara we used sushi rice, which worked well. My understanding is that brown rice will also work, possibly even better than white rice, but the time you spend parboiling and amount of water needed might need a bit of experimentation. The dish seems pretty forgiving, so you should be ok...
What liquid modifications, if any, would I need to make if I were to substitute brown rice? Or would this not work with brown rice.
Has anyone made this ahead of time and then reheated just before serving? I’m concerned that in the interim, the rice would continue to absorb the liquid and the whole thing would become a solid.
@Juliet Jacobson in answer to my own question: no, do not make this dish ahead of time. The rice absorbs all the liquid and becomes a solid. It still tastes good, but the texture is all wrong.

I <3 jook/congee. Soaked the dried shiitakes first-- often grit is left in the liquid, you need to strain it. Added the mushroom soaking liquid to the 8 cups of water-- as written the congee only has whatever salt you sprinkle on in the initial sauté. It made for a too bland finished porridge, even with the miso-- I stirred in 3 tbs. of soy sauce and that fixed it, but sheesh. Once properly salted? Delicious, + great for vegans.
PS the next morning heated up leftovers and topped with a fried egg drizzled with sesame oil. Amazing!

This recipe has an INSANE effort to taste ratio. Didn't have dried shitakes or kelp, so added some chicken bouillon to the water for extra umami. Go crazy with the toppings - we're partial to the sesame oil, cilantro, and chili oil, and a lil furikake

title: Simple Seaweed Salad Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: The description inspires me to make the dressing early and use it to marinate small cubes of tofu, then add the dressing and marinated tofu to the seaweed, and serve over rice.  Now all I need to do is find a reliable source of wakame.

Made exactly as the recipe described - perfect way to use up pantry stuff and eat something super quickly! (This is quite possibly the first NYT recipe i made in far less time than the recipe mentioned) If you’re crazy like me and are eating this on its own for dinner - I would say it’s maybe 1 or 1.5 servings. One thing I will mention is I was left with a ton of dressing. I think the sauce can accommodate .75-1 full cup of dried wakame and still be really delicious.

I love this and make it all the time. It’s become a lunch staple. So easy because all the measures are using the same tablespoon. I use coco aminos in place of the soy sauce and tahini in place of the miso (allergic to soy).

I’ve been looking all over for an easy seaweed salad that tastes like my favorite restaurant one. This is it! Literally whipped together in 15 minutes. I also added tofu to make this a main course and love the contrast of textures. Definitely a keeper.🔥
Measuring dried wakame by cups is messy and imprecise. Using weight to measure would be so much better.
Can this be made with nori sheets sliced into strips and soaked? I have plenty of nori.
No, nori sheets are different.
No. They have very different textures. Nori will turn to mush. Try wakame as the recipe calls for. Then try nori with this recipe. Then you can see why it’s s wakame dish.
Has anyone made this with fresh seaweed? I live by the Atlantic, but have no local source for wakame.
@JB The meteic version says 20 grams of wakame.
I have very little seaweed experience beyond adding some kombu to dried beans on the stove. Has anyone tried this recipe with kombu?

title: Chicken Piccata Meatballs Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: It is much simpler to make the sauce and return the meatballs into the sauce to continue to cook. The sauce will have more flavor from the meatballs. If you coat the meatballs in flour and then brown, they will help thicken the sauce and make a nice coating on the meatballs.
@Lorraine Fina Stevenski This sounds like a great tip. Did you not use the oven at all in this method?

Great recipe! Meatball mixture was indeed very soft so I called into duty a small muffin tin into which (after spraying with nonstick spray) I put a scoop each of meatball mix. I chilled, then cooked for 10 minutes, then turned for another 10 minutes, then turned into the sauce to finish.

Delicious.  I broiled the meatballs until browned, flipped, browned.  Perfect. 

For the sauce, I sautéed 2 T whole capers and 1T chopped garlic in olive oil, deglazed with white wine.  Added 2 cups chicken broth (Better than Bouillon), 2 t lemon juice, 4T butter and cooked 8 oz thin spaghetti in the sauce.  If the sauce starts to bind, add 1/2 C hot water.  Easily feeds a family of four.  YUM
@DB added chopped shallots, substituted 1/2 cup stock for wine to deglaze. Absolutely delicious. The starch from the pasta naturally thickens the sauce. Brilliant.

These are great! I used 1.5 lbs of ground turkey, since that's how it comes from Costco, and I basically doubled the seasoning, but not the milk so they would be a bit more flavorful but not so soft. I freeze them as individual meatballs so I can make what I need. 

Tonight I did a bit of a wag on the sauce and it was better - 1/4 white wine, juice of 1 lemon, 3-4T butter, zest of 1/2 lemon, about a 1/4c of pasta water and some capers. It was better than the sauce with the chicken broth.

These are great. The meatballs started to fall apart a bit while browning, but firmed up nicely in the oven and had a wonderful texture when done. Flavor was fantastic and made for a unique meatball! I agree with other reviews about doubling the pan sauce.

I love the idea of the recipe but it's missing... something. Maybe some acid? Or fresh herbs in addition to dried basil? I felt like it needed something to brighten up the flavor of the meat -- would love to hear others' ideas. I added red pepper flakes and appreciated the kick it gave. Next time I might also take the amount of breadcrumbs down to 1/2 cup so there's less filler. I refrigerated the meat mixture for 2-3 hours before and the meatballs stayed together fine. Will definitely try again!
Dried basil? The recipe calls for Parmesan, capers, garlic, oregano, salt and pepper in the ground meat.
@Richard X my mistake, I meant in addition to the dried oregano.
@KA I agree about the meatball flavor needing something perhaps more herbs plus soy sauce or Dijon mustard?

I thought the flavor of the meatballs was great, even though I only had ground chicken breast on hand. I wonder if the flavor and moisture would be enhanced with ground dark meat as the recipe suggests. I also agree with the comments about doubling the ingredients for the pan sauce, and I think next time I will add a bit of cornstarch to give the sauce some body.
I think that these could be adapted to an air fryer to get a more even browning.

I made this three times. The first two times were too wet. Most commercial ground chicken is too wet. Do not add a 1/2 cup of milk. Just add the panko directly to the meat and let it absorb the moisture. One trick I learn in another recipe on NYT cooking is to add mayo to ground chicken to give it some fat. The piccata sauce is missing shallots. Even in another NYT recipe they call for shallots. 1 tsp of lemon juice isn't enough. I squeezed a whole lemon in there and then also the lemon zest.
I could have used zest.  I did extra garlic but shallots a great idea. Absolutely a whole lemon

This was good. I zested the lemon and added the zest to the meatball mixture (used ground turkey instead of chicken) and used the juice of the entire lemon in the sauce (I think the flavor profile of piccata calls for more than a mere teaspoon) and it was zingy and good. Served over noodles with some roasted broccoli scattered on top. Will keep this in the rotation. And yes, chilling the meatball mixture for a few hours makes the meatballs hold together more easily.

These were delicious!  I'd suggest a bit more salt & capers.  I used fat free 1/2 & 1/2 rather than milk.  Also I refrigerated the mixture for a good 45 minutes (maybe more) and had no problem forming or frying them.
Liked the meatballs fine but found the pan sauce very bland. I realized as I started it that of course it should have been made with garlic and white wine, not broth.
I was quite disappointed in the bland taste of the meatballs.

I wondered if I could make these in the slow cooker, and they turned out great! Doubled the sauce and used equal parts wine and broth, as other commenters suggested. Tossed the meatballs in flour before they went in and cooked on high for four hours. Delicious!

I used ground turkey breast meat (what I had on hand), and these meatballs were a hit with my family! We all liked them and thought they were flavorful and moist. The sauce was disappointing, however. Despite boiling it down for three times the stated amount of time, it was still more of a broth than a sauce. In future, I will keep the meatball recipe and skip the broth in favor of a wine deglaze with some butter, olive oil, with the parsley and lemon to coast the pasta (and extra parm on top!).
Flavor was good. I ground my own chicken thighs at a coarse setting which worked well. Next time I'll chill the mixture well before forming the meatballs.
These were delicious. I subbed almond flour for panko 1:1 otherwise made it exactly as outlined...big hit and will make again. Served over Jovial Tagliatelle. (Just mentioning for any GF folks.)

The meatballs were wonderful. I followed the recipe exactly when them, except that I used plant-based milk instead of dairy, omitted the cheese, and made them half the size the recipe calls for (so twice as many) in order to get more browned bits. I found the sauce a bit bland; I added some anchovy paste and also thickened it with some flour to make it more of a consistency to cling to the pasta. Next time I might try rice, which would absorb better.

Just bringing together various suggestions from other commenters. I added sour cream to the panko instead of milk, since I usually do that with my chicken burgers to keep them moist. I put the meatballs in the freezer while I got other ingredients ready and pan seared in oil without them falling apart. Browned and finished in the air fryer…they came out perfect. I took advice of another commenter and used white wine in place of broth to deglaze the pan, which gave me the idea to make a sauce to mix with pasta. Added shallots and a whole lemon thanks to another commenter and flour with the butter to thicken. Made angel hair pasta reserving pasta water, and placed pasta in pan with sauce. Adding pasta water produced a glossy sauce that was perfect with the meatballs.
@Michele C. Did you add the same amount of sour cream as you would milk?

We didn't have ground chicken, so we used ground beef and the meatballs  were *delicious.* If you use beef, make sure you drain the meatballs after you pull them out of the oven.

Like other commenters, I would double or even triple the pan sauce -- we used up all our sauce on just 4 meatballs. I felt bad for the other 8.
The ingredients really should say broccoli if you are going to show it served like this.

DELISH every time, but espesh w/this fix: Add lemon rind to meatball mix. Flour-dust meatballs before browning (crunchy! thicker sauce!). Double the sauce, using a mix of 3 parts white wine to 1 part broth, & extra butter and lemon juice. Finish meatballs in sauce, letting that ever-so-light flour crust and the yummy browned parts break off into sauce to add texture. Add a pinch of lemon rind at the end. Serve over egg noodles and pour all that yummy sauce over everything.

Sorry, but my advice is to skip this one. It sounds intriguing, but in the end, meh! I like the technique of soaking breadcrumbs and milk for a tender meatball and will keep that one. I doubled the sauce and it's nice over noodles, but the meatballs were really bland and non descript.
Parmesan, capers, garlic, oregano, salt and pepper in the meatballs and you found them "really bland and non-descript?" Which of those did you skip?

@Richard X
I tend to agree with you.  But, I was very generous with my measurements of cheese, oregano and capers when I made this recipe. Maybe that made the difference?

I've made these a few times as mini meatballs to serve as finger food (with little wooden forks) and they come out great. I don't do the sauce or pasta and they've been raved about by themselves.  I do use just ground chicken, NOT ground breast. This time I'm going to try making them the night before and refrigerate until cooking time. Fingers crossed!

I loved some of the suggestions and took them.  I added 2 tbsp of mayonnaise to the chicken, and just added panko crumbs, without soaking them.  Also added about 1 tbsp dried dill.  I refrigerated the mix an hour, and used a scoop to put them in 400 oven on an oiled parchment lined cookie sheet. Cooked ten minutes, turned and cooked for another ten minutes, then transferred them to the saucepan with the sauce and cooked them, with some broccoli,in the oven for 5 more minutes.  Delicious!

The meatballs are very soft. I used oatmeal instead of panko breadcrumbs for a gluten free version. Make sure your pan is super hot before searing the meatballs so they keep the meatball shape. I also doubled the broth, butter and lemon to make sure I had plenty to add to my egg noodles. Topped with more parm cheese and capers. Delicious!

Great recipe! Tried it out for the first time last night. I did double up on the sauce measurements and added capers and lemon zest. I also used a bit of heavy whipping cream to thicken up the sauce. 

I would not skip the step of cooling the meatballs in the freezer or refrigerator before cooking. I put them in the freezer for 15 minutes and it helped hold them together during the cooking process.
Most ground chicken recipes suggest using that from dark meat for more flavor. Is ground dark meat chicken sold in stores or do you have to grind it yourself?

Added lemon zest to the meatball mixture. Only used about 2 tbs of milk. Chilled in the refrigerator for about 20 minutes before rolling into balls. I guess I rolled them smaller because I ended up with about twenty. Cooked them entirely in the oven at 400. Made the sauce like I always do when I make regular chicken piccata - broth, white wine, butter, and lemon juice. Added some more capers, as well as parsley, to the the sauce. Served with cavatappi and roasted broccoli.
Didn’t love these. It felt like more work than normal chicken piccata.
These were quite tasty but I found them to be too wet.  Next time I will cut back on the milk a bit.  I’d also consider tossing the pasta in the sauce.

A "swing-and-a-miss" for us. Missing ample sauce studded with capers and loaded with lemon. Distributing the panade evenly through the meat mixture required hands as folding resulted in glops of bread. Keep the bread crumbs and milk separate or use less milk to soften the crumbs. The NYT portion size is a little out of control - making 12 meatballs means they are HUGE; I made mine about 1 - 1.5" in size and got 18.

This is what I took away from the comments:
- add more garlic, lemon and capers to kick up flavor
- wine instead of broth or combo wine/broth for flavor dimension
- definitely refrigerate before cooking

I would never use white breast meat. bland. bland bland.

@Jude
Breast meat ground chicken does not have to be bland. It all depends on the seasonings, be assertive. I make them all the time, our family loves them. Chicken is so versatile, you can season them to complement any world cuisine. With the appropriate  sauce, they can be the star of many a meal.

These were great and really easy!  I ended up with ~16 smaller meatballs, which were the best size for my family.  Will make again! (N.B. - don't forget that the skillet is, indeed, oven hot when it comes out of the over; my hand is still lamenting that lapse in memory...)
Has anyone tried to freeze them? Without the sauce of course? I did doublebatch.

Needed these to be dairy free- used almond milk and used vegan parm cheese. 
The meat stuck to the pan so badly and I’m impatient. Next time I think I’ll skip the skillet cooking and just line a cookie sheet with parchment paper and bake till mostly done and add to the sauce.
I used a non stick pan and then also an old cast iron pan. Neither stuck.

Two points: one, without chocolate, it’s not champurrado; second, Rancho Gordo’s Mexican chocolate is really superior and I highly recommend it. It’s sourced from a young woman from the mountains of Guerrero. And one more! Add some mezcal to Mexican hot chocolate and you won’t regret it.

I agree with the other comment - it is easier to return the browned meatballs to the sauce and finish everything together in the skillet. That includes adding steamed broccoli to the sauce at the end, so that all flavors combine and there is only 1 pan to clean up. Very tasty!

Delicious. Made as written except for using 2% milk as that is what I had on hand. I didn’t have time to chill the meat mixture and didn’t have any issues with shaping the meatballs or flipping them as they browned. Will make again.

Meatballs were lit, but my pan sauce was actually not super flavorful (yes, I seasoned it liberally with salt and pepper). There was also not enough of it. I will double it and add some extra capers to it next time to really tie in that piccata flavor. Don't skip the advice to serve roasted broccoli on the side!

Boiled orichiette, added broccoli in last few minutes.

Doubled the sauce (2x broth, lemon juice, butter, 1x parsley).

Added pasta and broccoli to sauce at end, added a little olive oil and salted butter to finish.

I bought the ingredients but the real credit goes to my fiancee who is so good at cooking things like this. Will try again!

This was delicious! I made 24 smaller meatballs instead of 12 big ones. They still took 10 minutes in the oven. I cooked a pan of broccoli florets in the oven with s&p, and olive oil on them and added it on top of the pasta at the end (just like the picture), once they were carmelized (about 15 mins). I put a chunk of Pecorino Romano on the table and we grated it over everything. Awesome!
What's the calorie count? The recipe doesn't specify the number of servings.

This is similar to the Giada recipe that I tried and actually liked.  Hers calls for shallots, real bread to mix with the milk rather than panko, and broiling, but really similar flavor profile wise. I have used ground turkey with this when that was what I had, but added a few more spices. The capers really make the dish, along with the obvious lemon. I did use some zest as well.
Made this recipe as written, but put the meatballs in the air fryer -400 degrees 5-7 minutes. Crisp on the outside and very soft and tender inside.

We absolutely loved this. I made the broccoli pictured  as well. I tossed the florets with olive oil, salt, pepper and red pepper flakes. Put them on a sheet pan and then used the same bowl to mix the meatballs. I added some finely chopped shallots to the meatballs and the sauce. I only had fine bread crumbs so used a little less milk, and I also added lemon zest and parsley to the mix. I think it’s important to leave the ground chicken in the refrigerator until right before it’s added so it’s nice and cold. I had no trouble forming and they stayed rounded. I coated the bottom of an oven safe skillet with oil and formed the meatballs right in the pan before I turned on the heat. Once they browned on one side, I moved to the oven. I deglazed with white wine and built the sauce around the meatballs rather than take them out of the pan.

The sauce works if you considerably increase the amount of lemon and use wine rather than broth. I tripled the amount of sauce, let the meatballs finish it, and then took out the meatballs and let it reduce and thicken. It was quite good!

I make this and it was ok. I made it with Banza rotelle pasta and added roasted broccoli and more chopped capers because I love capers. It tastes good but it's pretty dry, I feel like it needs a lot more sauce

Instead of making the sauce after the meatballs came out of the oven, I added the sauce ingredients (broth, lemon and butter-cut into small chunks) to the meatballs, in the pan they had already browned in. Popped it into the 425• oven for 10 minutes. 

After it all baked, I gently stirred it and added more lemon and parsley. The meatballs were a perfect texture, browned and a little crispy on the outside and very moist inside, thanks to baking  with the liquid ingredients. Plus faster and easier too!
Delicious!  I made them as written, but omitted the capers (not a fan).  I served them with gnocchi and broccoli.  You can too!
can you freeze uncooked meatballs? What about cooked? Success?

Because I have an issue with my tiny little pied-à-terre cooker, the oven and burners will not work simultaneously, I did all the cooking of the meatballs on the stove top making it about 20 minutes total cooking time in the skillet. Other than that, I followed the recipe as written, it turned out great, and I have to say out of the many hundreds of New York Times recipes that I have made. This is without a doubt one of the very best.
Good not great. It’s a little bland, and the sauce isn’t much of a sauce. Just make chicken piccata honestly, it’s easier and tastier.
Absolutely delicious. Definitely double the sauce!

The flavor profile was good but the meatballs were way too soft and I ended up breaking them apart and frying in a pan after the oven step because the texture wasn’t good in the interior, like Rice Krispies left in milk too long. And the exterior integrity only held a bit where there was sear, and fell apart at the seams even with delicate two spoon scooping for turning. This was not actually a meatball with the necessary integrity to feel good about its mission in life. 

In the end, I love the capers idea, otherwise the recipe is a not again for me.

A very nice recipe.  I read other's comments before I made it and as one suggested skipped finishing the meatballs in the oven and did so in the sauce.  This did produce a flavorful sauce and lovely textured meatball. I will add more garlic next time to make my garlic loving husband happy and may make the meatballs smaller but this is just personal preference and not really needed.

My 8 yo son chose this recipe from the 18 Kid Friendly dinner line-up. Followed the recipe and HOLY COW. I honestly wouldn’t change a thing. Perfect as is. The meatballs are tender  and loaded with flavor. The sauce, while on the thin side, came across as luxurious. These meatballs do not need “gravy”. No leftovers, unfortunately. Served with wide egg noodles and thin asparagus.

Phenomenal! This literally went exactly as recipe directs... so very very easy and looks beautiful in a large casserole with angel hair noodles, meatballs on tops and sauce poured over... I generously sprinkled fresh parsley on top. Good with some flaked parm as well! 

One note, I would make a double recipe of the sauce... everyone loved and wanted more on their plate! The meatballs are a great make ahead and freeze for a quick dinner as well. Only need to make noodles and sauce fresh!

I browned (but did not cook through) the meatballs and then transferred them to a plate.  Sweated another clove of garlic in the olive oil and then deglazed with a little white wine.  Made the sauce and then put the meatballs back in to finish.  I also finished with a touch (maybe 2T?) of heavy cream as the lemon juice and white wine needed a little calming down.  Definitely would make again.  The meatballs are very tasty.

I love chicken piccata and I loved this.  I doubled the sauce, added shallots and (more) capers to the sauce, and served over pasta and broccoli that I cooked in one pan together. This technique on the meatballs made them gorgeously browned without burning
You must use a non stick skillet! Excellent flavor and texture, but even on low they stuck to cast iron and Dutch oven.
Oh, I didn’t have any capers, so I chopped up some dill pickles.  Worked well.

Used this recipe as the base for spaghetti and meatballs last night. Cooked in the pan (brown and remove, make sauce, return meatballs, remove to finish spaghetti), but would try cooking completely in the oven and with less milk next time for better texture and browning. Simple sauce was fine for pasta, deglazed with water and sherry, slightly increased the butter and used the entire lemon (zest and parm for finishing).

I forgot to add an egg, but had taken the advice of adding mayo and mixing the panko directly with the meat, and the meatballs did not fall apart and had a nice texture.  Also, I broiled them first and then added them to the sauce to cook
Delicious!  Will double the ingredients for the pan sauce next time. Definitely a keeper.
Comfort food!
Lovely and tender.
Made with 85/15 ground turkey instead of chicken and it turned out really well. I recommend roasting broccolini in the oven as the veggie.
I paired the meatballs with wide noodles.  Good, but kinda bland.  My tastebuds didn't pick up much piccata flavor.  The sauce could use some capers as there was nothing pushing piccata there.
Could these be frozen and cooked before or after?

I made this with gluten free panko that I got at Whole Foods and it worked very well! The product I used is Jeff Nathan Creations Gluten Free Panko Flakes. BOTH my 3 and 7 year old ate it (and that never happens!).

These were tasty and well received by my crew! I did add a bit of extra lemon juice and I think I’d put the capers in the sauce instead of the patties next time. I think I also made mine a little smaller; I used a 2 tbsp scoop. Definitely a keeper, though. I served it with rice and broccoli.
Love the nod to Marcela Hazan’s classic meatball recipe with bread soaked in milk, the secret to that perfect texture.
These were absolutely delicious!
I cooked them briefly in the air fryer in my Breville which took care of the problem of them breaking up.

I cooked the meatballs on air fry in my Breville for a little while to firm up the outside. This stopped any falling apart and then I finished them in a pan. Following other comments I used some rose and doubled the sauce. I think these would be delicious in the Marcella tomato sauce with butter and onion.
I followed the recipe exactly excepts I used gluten free panko. I did not find the meatballs to have enough flavor and I agree about doubling the sauce.
I added 4 cloves of garlic and scallion.  Didn’t have both so I added wine.

I love chicken piccata and thought it would help me love the ground turkey I bought (couldn’t find ground dark meat chicken). Wrong! The taste is still distinctly turkey which I didn’t enjoy with piccata flavors BUT the texture is really good - the panade of milk and panko really helps keep the meatballs moist, and frying and then roasting helped. I didn’t find them too soft to shape but they def didn’t stay round when flipping in the pan.

Loved it. Made16. Did have difficulty sautéing in cast iron pan but it really didn’t matter in the end.  White wine reduced, doubled broth, reduced, 1 lemon squeezed, butter. Reduced a tad more. Just a keeper.
No shade on this recipe, which I haven’t made, but I have made several times Giada de Laurentiis’s chicken piccata meatballs and for flavor I think they would be hard to surpass. Try both!

We followed the directions to the tee. Even after refrigerating the meat mixture and freezing the meatballs for a bit, they collapsed a little in the pan. But they were delicious  We’ll make them again but double the sauce recipe and add more capers to the sauce.
Delicious meatballs. But as several other comments reveal, the sauce, prepared as written, is a fairly flavorless broth.

There was nothing wrong with this recipe. The flavor and texture of the meatballs were quite nice. But the mixture was very soft, making more of a thick patty than a ball. I also didn’t taste much piccata flavor. One of those decent recipes that I probably won’t make again.

I made this recipe and I halved the amount milk. I did this based on my experience with other meatball recipes that are too wet and sloppy to work with. I did put the formed meatballs in the freezer for about 15 minutes before putting them into a cast iron skillet. Everything worked out well! I don’t think I’ll try the recipe with the recommended amount of milk.

I want to serve these meatballs as part of a house hopping progressive dinner that is a Christmas Eve tradition with several friends. Due to the quantity needed I really need to be able to make these in advance and freeze them so that all I have to do is warm them that evening. Will it work to freeze them in the sauce? If they need to freeze without the sauce, any tips on making the sauce the day of, when I won’t have the pan drippings?
These are great meatballs! I'm using ground chicken intead of beef moving forward for my meatballs due to this recipe. Read the comments about the sauce, as it does need a little help.
So so good - followed the recipe exactly and LOVED it!
These were good. I personally didn’t get a big piccata flavor, maybe I needed more lemon. My kids ate them so big win!
Meatballs fall apart when cooking, even after freezing. Recipe tasted good but effectively was ground chicken piccata
This was bland, despite following much of the advice given in the comments.  It was fine.  I won't make it again, though. I love chicken piccata and had high hopes.

Really good recipe—delicious. Be sure to make extra sauce to pour over the rice, noodles, or veggies on the side. Next time I will serve with roasted broccoli. 

 I added a healthy splash of wine to the broth and extra lemon juice for a stronger, brighter sauce. No time to chill the meat before forming balls, so I had meatblobs.
Delicious and easy. Yes definitely double the sauce, especially if you want to have enough to cover some pasta also.

I was concerned with all of the flavorful ingredients going into the meatballs that they might become overseasoned, but don't hold back—they can take a lot more seasoning than you think. As others have noted, the texture of the mix is very loose; in the future, I might cut back slightly on the milk. Nonetheless, a tender and flavorful meatball that I'll definitely be making again.
Cooked as indicated. Tasty but the meatballs were quite dry.

I made this twice. Once for dinner with risotto and also as a holiday appetizer. Both times - delicious with many compliments from guests. 

With leftovers, I wrapped them in a tortilla (crunch wrap style) with the sauce for lunch. So good!

the flavor of these are great but the method is definitely flawed. they’re much too soft to pan fry and forget about doing a double batch. i would maybe try again w ground pork for a firmer texture or just straight onto a hot sheet pan …?

The meatballs were ,as others have said, wet. I think if I did this again, I'd take one commenter's advice and bake the meatballs in a muffin tin. It would save so much mess! mine came out looking more like pucks, which was fine. The flavors were good. It was the clean up I could have done without.

@Tina

At first I found the meatballs wet and a little tricky to form, too.  But I reviewed the recipe and followed the recommendation to moisten my hands after using a cookie scooper to portion out each meatball. I found that very helpful and making the rest of the recipe went smoothly.
These are good but lack the deep lemon flavor of a true piccata.

Having followed the recipe exactly, I found the meatballs to be a bit bland and the sauce insufficient. Next time I’d add more flavor to the meatballs (perhaps some Dijon mustard or soy sauce plus more herbs such as thyme) and double or triple the sauce. With the recipe as is there is scant sauce to flavor your noodles, pasta or rice. I’d also thicken the sauce with corn starch or flour.
Made with cream instead of milk.  Bread crumb/cream mixture was very stiff & hard to break apart when mixing with meat, but meatballs turned out well & very tender.
Sauce os a bot thin for my liking, would probably thicken next time even slightly
I did put them in the freezer to chill. This did help hold the shape. The fresh lemon squeezed on it in addition to the sauce is very important for flavor.

I make these with dairy alternatives. Oat milk and faux Parmesan cheese. They work very well  And I have to say- these are so good. I cook up the meatballs in a wide skillet and then add all the sauce ingredients and let it all simmer together.
Great comfort meal.  I substituted the capers with feta and it's delicious.  I used eggs noodles which I added directly to the pan after they cooked.  Easy and satisfying.
A great take on traditional chicken piccata.

Made this basically as written.  I used better than bouillon instead of stock and added spinach and let wilt in sauce. Was great first night and better as leftovers over pasta (added parmesan and pasta water to sauce).
Meh, too much work for an average tasting meal.
This was delicious!  But I recommend doubling the sauce.

Not a fan. I used fresh ground chicken from the butchers and it was impossible to achieve a ball shape. The result was more like a mini burger shape. The flavors just weren't there for all the work involved in these very average tasting odd shaped meatballs.

"Thank you" to those who take time to share their experiences and alterations. So helpfful!  I'm in a dinner group & always looking for dishes I can  prep ahead so I'm not exhaused with a destroyed kitchen when the guests arrive.  If anyone has suggestions please share!  I did add extra lemon zest, more salt and red pepper flakes to the meatballs as suggested by some. I will leave out the Oregano next time. Doesn't seem to fit a piccata flavor profile. Made a sauce from another recipe I like.
Made these as described, I thought they were nice. I would add lemon zest to the meatballs next time, maybe less breadcrumbs? Overall great though and easy to do the meatballs ahead.
So very delicious! Added wine to the broth. Spooned over egg noodles. Fed only 3 of us with no leftovers.

I had *almost all of the ingredients for this- no parsley and light on the lemon juice for the sauce...but no matter. This was so so good, this will be repeated in my house! Will definitely try making the sauce with wine in the future, I just happened to have an open carton of chicken broth so just used that up. You can't beat the capers-paremesan-garlic-oregano in the meatballs, not sure how anyone could think they were bland unless they left out additional salt?

I followed the recipe exactly and I thought these were delicious! The meatballs were moist and had a nice rich flavor, I wouldn't make any additions. I served them over buttery mashed potatoes instead of pasta. They did fall apart a little when I was sautéing them, I may try broiling them instead, next time.
Made this for my dinner at the beach. Served it over rice and tripled the sauce. Rave reviews. Everyone asked for the recipe.

I've made this recipe several times and it's always a hit with my toddler and husband. 
Delicious as written (although only one clove of garlic doesn't compute for me), I've also tried it with some modifications. 
Adding a bag of frozen, chopped spinach to the meatball mixture is a tasty and easy way to get more veg into my toddler...and husband. 
Personally, I find it easier to cook the meatballs in the oven rather than on the stove. I didn't find the flavor suffered. 15ish minutes at 400 convection setting worked nicely. 
Turning the sauce into a gravy by starting with a roux is also a yummy way to switch things up. 
Tasty, easy, and flexible. Recipe hat trick!

So delicious these taste like fancy chicken nuggets! We halved the milk and used a whole head of garlic (duh). Served it with broccolli and rice and subbed the sauce for a homemade lemon garlic aoli. Def going to make this again!

I have been cooking for 34 years and have excellent cooking equipment and this recipe was an absolute train wreck. Even upping the panko it was unflippable it looked like burnt meatloaf all clumps.  ZERO stars

This recipe was FANTASTIC! 

I made it dairy free by subbing: 
- coconut milk (full fat) for milk
- homemade vegan parmesan for Parmesan 
- high quality olive oil and cornstarch for butter in the sauce to make it all come together. Bone broth makes for a richer sauce too.

The meatballs definitely benefited from staying in the fridge over night since the mixture is quite soft.

this was SO DELICIOUS! I opted for orzo, which was delightful. I didn't make all 12 meatballs so I had leftover meatballs to prepare for lunch a couple days later... For a keto friendly option, I put the meatballs on top of quinoa this time around, added a heaping scoop of cottage cheese and some sliced cherry tomatoes, with a drizzle of olive oil. It was DELICIOUS. (I'm not sure if tomatoes are keto friendly? Anyway, tried to eliminate extra carbs). So, so, so yummy.

This was a bit bland of a dish.  Did some things to jazz it up.  The lemon juice wasn’t enough, so I grated a whole frozen lemon and keep the shavings in the freezer.  Added some of that, much more salt, pepper and a dash of cayenne.  Also cooked noodles to al dente and doubled the sauce.    Added the noodles directly to the sauce and cooked on low for a short time so noodles soaked up sauce a bit.  Added grated Parmesan and stirred quickly to incorporate it into the sauce. Lastly added about a tablespoon of sour cream.  Family loved it after all that.  Good base recipe for me to play with.
Made as written, delicious. I would double the sauce.

Delicious! I doubled everything except the meatballs and finished off the meatballs back in the pan with the sauce instead of in the oven. Per other comments, I did not use milk and included the zest of the lemon in the meatball mixture, as well as some Dijon mustard. I didn’t measure out the lemon juice for the sauce - just included juice from the half of the lemon I didn’t slice for serving. Chopped extra parsley for serving as well and served alongside roasted broccoli.

Was phenomenal. Read the comments and used half the milk, also mixed everything together for the meatballs at the same time rather than making a panade with the milk and panko. Meatballs ended up holding together beautifully.

title: Chicken Mazemen  Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: The tahini made the noodles pretty rich, so I put probably a teaspoon of chili oil in my bowl and a ton of cucumbers to cut the creaminess/heaviness and it really helped. The contrast of the cold, crunchy radish and cucumber was necessary for the meal to feel complete for me.

Great recipe! Definitely add (I used TJ’s) fried shallots if possible for extra crunch and flavor. I used a large daikon radish and it was delicious but using other radishes makes sense depending on what you like. Very quick and easy to make and fed four well, and could sit on the stove for people eating at different times.
Made this tonight. Hubs and I were surprised at how tasty it was because the ingredients seemed a bit random upon reading the recipe. We added edamame and bok choy micro greens on top. Delicious.
Don't discard the ramen seasoning packets! Save them to season some roasted veggies - we especially like with broccoli or cauliflower.

This was a winner. I really liked the creaminess conferred by the tahini, which in some recipes I find to be too bitter. The bonus with this recipe is that here’s plenty of sauce to throw in extra veggies that you have on hand. Then it becomes a complete one dish meal in no time.  I added julienned carrots, red bell peppers, and cabbage, plus basil and lime juice. I subbed coconut aminos for the sugar, which I do in all Asian recipes that call for sugar.
@sjaustin did you add those extra veggies before cooking soup?

Tasty and easy! Suggest NOT adding the noodles to the pan and assembling each individual bowl with noodles. This will allow for a better leftover experience with noodles that aren’t soggy. Store the leftover meat mixture, noodles and veggies separately in the fridge.
Good call on the separate storage!

We had Mazemen Monday yesterday, because Kim Severson said it was. It was a hit. My husband doesn’t prefer noodles usually but he loved the flavors of this dish. My teens said it was a win, and the 9 year old ate his ramen plain (a win).  I loved having a dish with garlic and ginger, and I love tahini, which was surprising but excellent. The radish and sprouts added a crunch and per another reader I also added cukes. Next time I will double the meat/spices and serve with lettuce for my non noodle fans.

This was sooo good. The noodles came out amazing and so flavorful. The tahini was a delicious addition. The toppings are customizable but I wouldn’t leave to out the scallions or grated radish! Served with lime and Laoganma chili crisp — sooo good!

Used udon noodles instead of ramen. Used yuzu/mirin instead of ponzu.  Used a little less soy and did not add salt as suggested in final step. I did use normal chicken stock (not low sodium). Added chopped bok choy and Thai basil at the end. VERY good and easy to add on veggies, toppings, etc. Versatile base or good as is if you want to keep it simple!

I made this and the aftertaste on the noodles was really strange. Could have been the tahini I used which had lemon juice and the ponzu sauce was lemony so maybe too much lemon? The kids did not like it at all and I tried to like it but ended up throwing the rest away because it was so unpleasant. I followed the directions exactly. Specific brands of ingredients could be helpful.
I found a squeeze of fresh lime really helped to bring together

Really easy and great.  We were didn't have miso so I substituted an equal amount of fish sauce.  I cut down the tahini to just about 1 to 1.5 tablespoons, and omitted the sugar.  I also threw in some edamame with the noodles.  To serve, I squeezed some lime juice and drizzled over Trader Joe's chilli oil crunch.
I don’t love Ramen noodles. What’s a noodle with flavor and a bit of bite to it? Like the kind in a crispy noodle dish? Egg noodles?
@Easter Definitely not egg noodles. They are VERY soft. Maybe you could try it with udon noodles? They are kind of toothsome!
@Easter are you thinking of yakisoba noodles? Like for stir fry? I’d give that a try. I’d also consider this with room temp soba noodles!

Made this tonight with Brown Rice Udon noodles and ground chicken breast … all the ingredients came together to make a delicious sauce… I didn’t feel the need to add the sugar, but I didn’t add some green beans and  appreciate d the crunch from those and the the radishes and bean sprouts. A complete healthy one- bowl meal!

My whole family loved this! Made it last night. The store didn't have any sprouts, so I added oyster mushrooms instead. So good. I agree that the radish (with scallion greens) were the perfect garnish to add freshness to the umami. Thank you!
Loved this and so did the rest of the family, want to make it again ASAP!
This was bland, and a lot of prep work.  The tahini should be in the sauce where it can dissolve.  I would not make it again.
@Faith yeah too much lemon zest does have a floral/medicinal aftertaste. Too much lemon juice is just tangy only

Delicious! The sauce is rich and complex. Prepared as directed except I used Liviva Dried Shirataki noodles - they were sturdy and tasty. Garnished with bean sprouts, radish, cucumber, pickled red onions, avocado, Okazu chili crisp, and toasted sesame seeds. Yum yum!
The tahini makes it heartier and adds protein. Not sure if I made it wrong but my dish was much more soupy. Still tasty though, will probably make again (it makes a lot)
Yummy as written
Used a tamari ponzu combo sauce that I had.  That was the only alteration.  Family loved it.  A hit.  Will repeat.  Thank you!
Wasn't sure about this... but LOVED it!
everyday pantry ingredients
Simple, easy and delicious! Used daikon and broccoli sprouts. Will definitely make again!

Definitely worth a try- I didn’t have everything on the sauce list but I did have the miso and tahini- definitely riffable.  I also did the cucumbers and scallions, radishes and I added sliced celery because I wanted more vegetables.  It was sooooo good! I had to use dried rams but found decent quality.  Can imagine what fresh ramen would have been like!  Try this recipe!

The recipe says to "season with salt and pepper" three different times. It is salty enough between the miso, soy sauce and ponzu sauce. No need to add any extra salt and pepper, let alone three times.

Made it for dinner tonight as a way to use up our last packets of dried instant ramen, a scant handful of dried mushrooms, some fresh broccoli florets, fresh carrot (which I substituted for the radish). A hit! I’ll be making this again!

title: Grilled Skirt Steak With Smoky Cucumber Chimichurri Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Delicious! I was worried the meat would need to be marinated, but the olive oil, salt, pepper and oregano were perfect. And the sauce was divine. Nice crunch and grilled flavor from the cucumbers, tang from the lemon, and salty umami from the capers. The one change I made was to add cilantro. Because I love cilantro. Definitely going in the rotation!

This was delicious! I replaced the parsley with cilantro, because I don’t like parsley. After tasting the sauce, I ended up adding more garlic, lemon juice, and crushed red peppers. The sauce mixed with yogurt was even better!
Excellent meal. Paired with coleslaw (such as Pickleback) and thin frites.  I did add more lemon juice to the Chimichurri. Now on heavy rotation!
Excellent as described!
Recipe was fantastic as written!  Next time I may try chili crisp instead of just red pepper flakes, and sub out some of the olive oil with rice vinegar.
Question: do I peel the cucumbers?
@AMM no- Persian cukes don’t need to be peeled
Can this be done in an air fryer?
I grill everything, or so I thought! Grilled Cucumber? Who knew!? Perfect as is–loved everything about it! Thank you Kay!
I don't see how you can get the smoky flavor necessary to the cucumbers in an air fryer @DebDoo
Does the steak need to sit in the rub for a certain about of time? Or put it straight on the grill?
@Meg no- I put it on right after I seasoned- was delicious

I made the recipe exactly as written and found it delicious! It came together quickly. NYT never accounts for all the chopping but in this case it wasn’t terrible—quartering the cucumbers, then a quick mince of parsley, garlic, and capers while the grill got hot, and the final chopping of the cucumbers once they came off the grill. This made a nice summer evening meal with a simple green salad.

@AMM 
Shouldn't be necessary. Just wash well.

title: Spicy Gazpacho Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Don't discard the pulp after you strain. Add a little hot sauce and it makes a great salsa!
Removing the seeds in chilis does nothing to reduce the heat. The capsaicin is in the pith
This is basically my recipe, but I prefer roasting the red peppers, for a silkier texture, something I got from an old Bon Appetit recipe.

This is very close to Diana Kennedy's gazpacho recipe in her "Nothing Fancy" cookbook. We have used that recipe for years and give it a Texas touch by adding a small Serrano pepper. Kennedy's recipe does not add cucumber to the initial blending and opts for a green bell pepper, plus red wine vinegar. She also uses a food processor and adds the olive oil via the drip pusher very slowly as the last step which helps keep the oil blended.
So you don't have to cook this over heat??
No, you eat it cold.
This was a bit too oily for me.  Also, really small servings after going through the sieve.

title: Roasted Eggplant Noodles With Cashew Sauce  Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I made it exactly as written, except served with rice. I made it for 2, but kept the eggplant chickpeas mixture for 4. This makes me think it could do with more of that when actually cooking for 4, because we ate it all 😉
This was good! Added lemon to the sauce and finished with hot pepper sesame oil. Might try with different type of noodle.
Has anyone made this using tahini instead of Cashew butter?

@skyandstars
Yes, I used tahini instead of the cashew butter.  It worked well as a substitution.  I used a little more salt than I normally use to bring out the flavor.

This is a great recipe base! After reading the comments, I did the following to spruce it up a bit: 

-added green beans (roasted)
