-roasted the chick peas, eggplant, and green beans on separate pans to let them crisp. I also salted the eggplant and let it sit in a colander for a while to help with the bitterness. 
-added fresh ginger, raw garlic, lime, and Serrano pepper to the sauce  
-subbed almond butter (Justin’s) since I didn’t have cashew butter or the energy to make fresh cashew butter 
-topped with scallions, Serrano pepper, and chili crisp. I wish I had cilantro because I think that would have added even more! 

This will be going into rotation. If I were to make this for guests I’d probably do two cans of chickpeas. Tofu would also be delicious.
@Philadelphia union worker - this was extremely helpful  especially the tweaks to the salad dressing

I made it in an oven skillet and added limejuice etc for the pan sauce to the pan - delicious. I didn’t have half pickles in my supermarket; went for ‘normal’ pickles, which kind of tastes like the pickled veg the Indonesian kitchen often uses in their dishes, so fine, but next time I’ll make the cucumbers in rice vinegar like suggested here.

@Vera what are half pickles please? And how do they fit in this recipe? 
Thank you.
Why half pickles?
@Vera I don’t see anything in this recipe that says anything about pickles, half or otherwise. Or is this another case of the NYT Cooking Notes getting wires crossed with a different recipe?

Very yum my family says. We never tried ponzu before and everyone likes it. Had to go to 3 stores before I found it. We followed most of the recipe except basmati rice sustituted for the noodles, and tofu, roasted with tamari, evoo and sesame oil on another sheet pan instead of garbanzo beans. 2 thumbs up, not difficult to make. Thanks!

I made this and it was great! I added a lot more oil to the eggplant when roasting, and to my surprise, the pieces turned soft and sweet.   I doubled the sauce portion because a couple of the reviews said that it was dry, the sauce is delicious. I also added lime, which elevated the flavors.   I was out of green onions, so I saw online a suitable substitute was shallots, so I thinly sliced shallots and added them instead of the green onion and it turned out well. Thank you for a wonderful dish. I’m going to add it to my rotation.
Looks colorful, but lacks distinctive flavor. Highly recommend  lots of hot sauce. This and the cheesy bean bake recipe are two of the most disappointing recipes I have made from this website

It was excellent but as with almost all nyt dishes I modified quite a bit - recipe was jumping off point. Peanut butter was fine instead of cashew because less than 1/2 the price. Tossed veggies in a bowl with dressing first for even coat. Didn’t use ponzu because couldn’t find any without sugar so just added tamari and rice vinegar for sauce. Added a tray of diced chicken thigh. Coated veggies and chicken in olive oil, tamari, balsamic and garlic powder. Served with left over brown rice and kale.
This was a little bland.  The eggplant did not caramelize well even after extra roasting time.

There is a good idea in here somewhere but this recipe ain't it. 1.5 oz noodles per serving is not enough for a meal but if you up the noodles, the sauce is not enough. Even with the given amount, the sauce isn't punchy enough to carry the dish. Next eggplant will go back into the eggplant adobo.
I can’t find roasted cashew butter in my local stores either. I used dark roasted peanut butter as a substitute. Didn’t peel eggplant. Otherwise made as directed. Nice flavors but rather dry.

I made this last night following the recipe exactly. It was good and my family said they liked it, but I agree with another commenter who thought it was dry. I can’t decide if the veggies needed more oil or a good squeeze of citrus or what, but they needed something. If I could get it to a juicier place, I’d probably also serve it over rice rather than noodles - I felt like I was fighting to get all of the flavors/textures in one bite with the noodles. Rice would also absorb the (delicious) sauce more easily.

@Tara:  Made this with double sauce over long grain brown rice and it was great. One issue:  needs to bake long enough to really soften the eggplant but not so long that the chickpeas harden. The doubled sauce helps remoisten those chickpeas if they do dry out a little. (And it’s a very tasty sauce.)

Skipped the noodles and the chickpeas, but this was a great way to make eggplant. I did not have ponzu so I used vegan soy sauce plus lemon juice. Also used raw cashew butter and it was still delicious. Added a spec of chili crisp. Great with cilantro if you like cilantro.

Prepped as written but discovered no rice noodles in pantry. Used  fideo noodles instead. Still good but will try rice or even make sure rice noodles on hand. Also, I think a pinch or more of sumac in sauce and/or on top will give this a tangy kick.   Will definitely make this easy recipe again.

I enjoyed this dish! I used Japanese eggplants cut into rounds and subbed tahini for the cashew butter. I also added a fair bit of red pepper flakes to the sauce bc i can't help myself when it comes to revving up the spice level on anything and everything.
@Name nolabelle, sorry for the late reply; that was a comment for another recipe that accidentally wound up here XD Sorry for the confusion!
added ginger and garlic to the sauce and mixed the noodles and sauce before serving. delicious!!!
Flavor combination was good. But it was very dry. I most likely won’t try it again.

Dang. Yet another NYT recipe that sounds good but with unavailable ingredients. "Roasted Cashew Butter", be sure to not use raw as it won't work.  Not available in any grocery near me, not even on Amazon. Does anyone know where to get it?

@Sniggins in my area cashew butter rarely says roasted on the front but you can suss it out by reading the ingredient list. Most of what supermarkets carry will have “roasted cashew” or “dry roasted cashews” as the first ingredient. Usually it is only the high end “natural” brands that have just cashews that aren’t roasted. Hope that helps!
if you have a blender or food processor, you can dump a cup of roasted cashews in there and whizz till creamy. throw in a little water or oil to loosen up.
@Sniggins:  I did find Wilderness Poets brand Organic Roasted Cashew Butter on Amazon.

don't see half pickles, thinking new pickles?  do see salt and pepper twice.
very allergic to fish (iodine), so no ponzu. suggestions? might try w zucchini instead of eggplant.

@Sharon

Check online for something to your taste: they basically say to mix soy sauce with some citrus and mirin and maybe a little sugar
What is Pozo?

@Dan: This recipe calls for ponzu sauce (not "pozo"). To make this recipe, I quickly found and ordered a bottle of ponzu sauce online. Later I also found various recipes online for homemade ponzu sauce, most calling for a blend of citrus juices (lemon, orange & grapefruit), soy sauce, mirin, lemon zest, dried bonito flakes (or, for vegan, dried shiitake mushrooms), and kombu. I found it to be delicious!
Are we peeling the eggplant?
The recipe says 1 1/2 lbs eggplant - about 8 cups. Is that correct? Maybe I’m wildly wrong about the weight and mass of eggplants, but I also feel like 8 cups would be overwhelming in this dish.

We enjoyed this.  Roasted green beans also, a nice addition.  Didn't have ponzu so mixed
soy, lemon and mirin.
Awful!  Not much taste and eggplant skin was tough.  Should be peeled but not making this again.
I think it means half-sour pickles. I wouldn't trust any brand other than these folks. https://batamptepickle.com/products/pickles/

I made this with toasted Chinese grocery store sesame paste, pickled nasturtium berries, hot pink Middle Eastern pickled turnips, and a quick side of cucumber black vinegar salad. The pickles cut the blandness and are a salty pantry accompaniment.
I made it with sunflower butter and added basil but otherwise as written, it was so good and everyone loved it. If you use sunflower butter use less water.
I am wondering if toasted almond butter could be a sub??
For people coming here from The Veggie newsletter, note that this recipe is not strictly vegetarian because of ponzu sauce, but can easily be modified to be.

Underwhelmed by this. Cold noodles, hot veggies and room temperature dressing made an odd combination.  And the dressing was bland.  Added fish sauce and hot sauce to give it some flavor.  If I could handle raw garlic, I think it would have helped.

Surprised at how meh everyone felt about this. I thought it was quite good and a quick meal! I crisped the chickpeas in my cast iron because I had so much eggplant. It was a welcome change to use rice noodles, something I don’t normally eat at home!

This was really good, but like others I found the ratios a bit off. I made it as written and there was not enough sauce for the noodles. I had ran out of cashew butter so used tahini to make more sauce, which I think I prefer. I would also increase the amount of eggplant and chickpeas next time. I am not usually a huge fan of spring onions (scallions) but these are essential here, as is the hot sauce.

This is a favorite dish in our house. I make it as written, otherwise upping the noodles from 6 oz to 8 oz, and the sauce to noodle ratio manages the increase just fine. It’s a solid, easy weeknight recipe that has a unique and delicious flavor profile.

I used this as more of guidelines:
- subbed tofu for chickpeas
- used soba noodles
- subbed tahini for cashew butter, since that's what I had on hand. But Tahini is so much thicker than cashew butter so I wouldn't recommend equal ratios next time (maybe 1/4  C).
- I was also out of sesame seeds and subbed some toasted cashews.
It didn’t work for me. Kind of bland.

This was a "meh" for me and my family. I think what was really missing was something acidic like vinegar or lemon.  I may play around with it as I think the sauce has potential, but as is, I would not make it again.

No cashew butter so used crunchy deep-roasted PB instead. Very good! Will be making again. Not quite filling enough for us though, so gonna add crispy tofu or Samin Nosrat’s coconut tofu (recipe is on here) next time.

title: Skillet Pork Cutlets With Fresh Peach Butter  Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: This was good but could be better. This seems like the first draft of a solid recipe. Next time I will add the peaches to the butter and let the juices cook down to get the peach butter a little more jammy. I threw some lime juice and that helps bring everything together a bit.

Oh man, you guys??  I tried the just the peaches with brown butter and it wasn’t so great. Decided to follow the suggestion of cooking it down a bit in the butter, then adding lime. It was excellent with the pork, had it with roast potatoes and green beans. 

But I had some extra sauce, tasted it, and thought “vanilla icecream!!”. Drove to the grocery store, bought the most expensive vanilla icecream they had, got it home and and slathered it with the sauce. It was FANTASTIC. Skip the pork, have it for dessert. I bet a sprinkle of crushed ginger snaps toasted in butter would take it out of orbit.

What a sumptuous, luscious way to bring pork to life. Yum. "Medium-ripe" threw me off for a second. A medium-sized ripe peach? A peach that's medium-ripe? It doesn't matter in the end. Also, I didn't bother using room-temp butter. I just cut up cold butter so it melted quickly and uniformly.

"Medium-ripe peach": the hyphen is between medium and ripe, so the peach should be medium-ripe. If medium sized peach were called for, it would be "medium ripe peach" without the hyphen. The recipe calls for 1/2 cup of pulp and juices; that quantity should help selecting the size of he peach.

The peach butter was pretty good as written, but a bit tart since my peach wasn't super sweet. I added a bit of honey and that made a huge difference. Excellent. Will by trying the butter on grilled chicken and corn later this week.
Too much butter.

This is not "peach butter." It may be buttery peaches, but it is not peach butter. That would require long, slow cooking of a puree to reduce it to a very thick consistency. And then usually canned for preservation.

This recipe describes more of a quick jam. But it's really just pan-cooked grated peaches. They end up jammy just as would tomatoes onions or anything else cooked with this method. But peach butter, it's not.
Has potential but needs somethings… shallot? Wine? More like a pan sauce?
Followed the instructions to a T and it was perfect for a quick summer meal.

title: Summer Corn and Seafood Chowder Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: The steps or list of ingredients should be revised to include taking the corn off the cob and scraping the cobs, slicing the leeks, mincing the garlic, cutting the potatoes into whatever size pieces are optimal, and cutting the cod into whatever size pieces are best to cook at same time as the shrimp.  I know to do these things, but one would expect these details to be included in the recipe.
FYI for others - prep instructions for ingredients are on the website and not the iOS app (idk why)
@Hugo The ingredients list has ingredients listed in their prepped form.

Excellent soup. Boiled an extra potato (separately) mashed with extra coconut milk, thickened the soup nicely. Did add chili flakes. 
Insecure cooks could be stumped with no chopping instructions.
Made pretty much according to the recipe. Delicious. I would add a pinch of red pepper flakes next time.
For us less experienced cooks:  how is leek prepped?  If using frozen corn, how many cups? Are potatoes whole? Thanks. Looking forward to trying

I put the corn kernels in with the spinach, they don’t need to be cooked that long.  I, too, added red pepper flakes. Next time I’ll add chopped cilantro and change from lemon juice to lime juice. I think that would be very interesting with this. It was delicious.

Delicious and a keeper! Yes to adding red pepper flakes. I added the ginger earlier and don’t know if this would make a difference. Also opened a few cans of chopped clams which gave plenty of clam juice plus some chopped clams couldn’t hurt!

My Celiac partner is gluten-free and lactose-free, and I've struggled to find a comforting chowder recipe that meets his dietary needs. This soup is so flavorful and filling--it transports you to summer on the New England coast. I'm always hesitant to use coconut milk as a dairy replacement as it sometimes overpowers with too much coconut flavor, but it works perfectly in this recipe with the sweetness of the corn and fresh seafood.
Chopping instructions are there!  In the ingredients list.....

I used cilantro instead of ginger.  I also substituted 2 cans of minced clams w/ juice instead of the bottled clam juice.  I didn't have the shrimp and it wasn't missed.  Instead of cutting up the cod, break it up as it cooks in the broth (much easier to know when it's done too).  It's a very adaptable and delicious summer recipe.

This was a big hit with my family!  I will make again. But I don't understand people's comments about the lack of instructions regarding sizes to chop the cod and the potatoes. Those instructions are clearly included.

This was amazing! For such a relatively hands-off recipe, it had surprisingly deep flavors and a really nice consistency to the broth. I took the suggestion of one reviewer and used lime and cilantro instead of lemon and chives… also added bay scallops. Satisfied even my teen sons, who kept saying, “it’s so fresh!” Truly delicious!

Made this as listed and it was awesome. I used a mix of white and black pepper to pipe in a little heat. The next day I reheated the leftovers over low in a saucepan and it was just as nice the second day.

I added carrots for color …diced….used barramundi not cod (none available) added chicken stock for water,doubled the clam juice and used the whole can of coconut. It was great. Delicate lightly sweet flavor

This is delicious! The only thing I will do differently next time is chop the cod and potatoes into larger pieces.  I thought they cooked down too small, but this is just my personal taste.  I also added a bit of chopped parsley.
I always slice and rinse leeks - they tend to carry grit.  Leeks also dehydrate nicely so I always put up a few jars when they’re in season.
Half & Half makes a nice substitute for coconut milk. I'll echo the suggestion for adding red chili flakes or Aleppo chili powder for a nice kick.
How much is a “bottle of clam juice”? It comes in different sizes
@Christine bottled clam juice usually comes in 8 oz. bottles and costs a few dollars. It can usually be found near the canned fish in the supermarket.
Adapted slightly for dietary needs, but super easy and really delicious.
Added a tablespoon of green curry paste, a few dashes of fish sauce plus soy to amp the umami, mami. Made it for the fam and they loved it.

This is delicious, even made it
successfully in our rental house, without ideal tools. That said, it would be great if NYTCooking would fix the app/website recipe disconnect. In terms of the recipe, my only changes were to add some fresh chopped clams, since they were available and decreased the cod. Also used small potatoes, so did not peel. Added red pepper flakes and a dash of smoked paprika (because it makes most things better). Really delicious and easy.

Also meant to say, used stock rather than water, since I don't use water when I have stock. Will try this with all fish stock next time. And would be interested in hearing from those who try this recipe with frozen corn, since it would be lovely to eat when corn season is over.

Mediocre!  The cod fell apart; the potatoes were tasteless. I used fresh corn and left the cobs  in but the fish stock and water made everything tasteless. Swordfish might have been better (and sturdier). It tasted like WATERY POTATO SOUP WITH A HINT OF CORN. BAD!

I added the corn at the end with the spinach, and used the full 5oz of baby spinach that comes when you buy it.  Also added a couple of teaspoons of fish sauce when stirring in the coconut milk to bump up the flavor a bit.  It was a huge hit with my guests.  I served it with ATK's peach/cucumber salad (which also has a vaguely Asian flavor profile and just right for late summer).

What a wonderful soup. I am allergic to milk. Coconut milk was my answer.
I tripled the recipe. I am not fond of fish in soup.  I put in the shrimp, added 2 lbs of small scallops and 2 cans of small clams with the juice in the cans. I added 2 cans of coconut mild and 2 small cans of coconut cream.  I added 5 ears of corn and a can of creamed corn, the rest was as recipe said. It was one of the best soups I ever made. Thank you for this wonderful recipe
Add a generous amount of lime to lift the flavor, and add red pepper flakes to taste.

It was good but a bit bland, I feel like it needs a bit more of everything, more corn, more time simmering with the corn cobs in, more potatoes, maybe some fish sauce, a bit of heat…… I like the suggestion of lime juice as well…..
I made this yesterday and it was delicious. I used only the cod, but the whole can of coconut milk. Also, a stick of celery, because what's chowder without celery?
Added green curry. Ab Fab!

WOW!!! SO GOOD! I used chicken broth instead of water, half and half instead of coconut milk, only shrimp (dislike cod), and lots of lemon juice to finish. 

It’s 90 degrees in the house today, so we are eating this cold, and I think I like it even better like this! Adding to rotation!

Substitute 2 inches of fresh or frozen galangal (kaa) root for the ginger, fish sauce for the salt, lime juice to taste and some Thai chillies, lemongrass, and magrood leaves for the spinach and you have Tom Ka Pla

The best summer soup so far this year! I made with Lobster broth, shells left over from another meal. I too thickened by adding extra potato. Perfect for leftover corn and during corn season it is the best! I did cut my shrimp as prefer smaller pieces and used salmon once.
Made this tonight as instructed with the exception of adding a stock cube into the water and it was brilliant. Came together quick, very little fuss, and went down a treat with everyone.
what is a good substitute for the coconet milk?
Ok, this is seriously good.
This is absolutely delicious. Used it as a base for small adjustments (e.g., no ginger, regular whipping cream rather than coconut milk, seafood broth rather than bottled clam juice).
So very good! Made exactly as written. I’ll add that for us, it’s more like 5-6 servings.

Thanks for that cheery restatement of the obvious. Must be a blast at parties.

Very good recipe, agree with adding a few pepper flakes. If only our CSA did cod and shrimp, too. ;-)
Can this be frozen?

I just made this for four friends and everyone loved it. I found that the ratio of broth to fish was too small, so I added more liquid. Instead of one C of clam juice I used two 8-ounce packets of Brodo ginger and turmeric bone broth. Excellent!
Tried it as written and it was tasty! I augmented after tasting and added a splash of fish sauce because it had Tom Yum vibes. Yum!

Has anyone  substituted salmon for
The cod? It seems like it would work but don’t want to serve it to book club without some input
Superb!  As suggested by other commenters, I added the corn with the fish and used a sprinkle of red pepper flakes.  Rave reviews from my guests.

Followed directions but this was the worst! Smelled delicious but tasted like poor potato soup.
I make seafood chowder and corn chowder frequently but this did not work. Disappointment.

Great recipe. I subbed about a dozen fresh basil leaves in place of the spinach, which I avoid due to the high oxalate level.  I used lime in place of lemon juice, and micro-planed some lime peel into the soup. Also chopped a fresh hot cherry pepper into the soup.  Served on rice noodles in bowls.  Probably more of an Asian taste, but a really good meal.
The recipe is missing something—umami.  I added a few dashes of Tabasco in the bowel and it helped it a lot.  Next time I may add some anchovies or fish sauce.
Some great ideas here.  Thanks!  I riced a 2nd boiled  potato into the pot and added 1/2 cup chopped celery sautéed in butter to just beginning to soften.
I don’t know what I did wrong, followed the recipe,  but this was boring. So sad I wasted all that shrimp on it.
Substituted finely chopped cauliflower for the potatoes as I do not eat nightshades. Delicious.
I made this with Chilean red shrimp and added some lemon zest and extra salt and it was great.

This was fantastic. Grocery store didn't have leeks so I subbed shallots and green onion. Used the whole can of coconut milk. Added some red pepper flakes. I'll be making this again using lime and cilantro next time. This is a keeper.
So I took the liberty of replacing the spinach with cilantro, the fish with Italian sausage, and of course added garlic. Yum.

My husband and I love this chowder! I added a can of clams in juice the last time I made it (I didn't have clam juice alone) -- it made it even better.  I seasoned my fish and shrimp with the salt & pepper recommended, but also added Old Bay seasoning -- good addition.  And lastly, when I use lemon juice in a dish, I always zest the lemon, too -- also a good addition.

I doubled the recipe (big family). Added red pepper flakes, lime & cilantro instead of lemon & chives as suggested by others. Delicious! My teenage grandsons loved it. Will definitely add to our rotation.

Excellent recipe. Chowder attested rich without being coconut-y. I used clams instead of shrimp, cooking them separately and using the broth instead of the clam juice. I also threw in a few halved cherry tomatoes that needed to be eaten. I had planned on adding basil cilantro or parsley, but there was absolutely no need for extra flavor.

2 cups of water is too much for me. One cup of water along with a can of coconut milk work for me I was looking for something a little less soupy
I would simmer and reduce the liquid, but the fish will flake Oh well next time flavor wise very good. I added just a touch of red pepper. Flake. I did not put potatoes in.

Made this for bookgroup after reading Dark Stormy Shore in order to emphasize bounties from the sea.  It was a hit.  I added oyster mushrooms, used southern style hashbrowns to save time and imitation crab because I can't resist.  Adding a tin of smoked scallops and smoked oysters made it a lot like Cullen Skink, which I love.  Easy and wow producing.
I add 1 or 2 cans of minced clam meat with juice instead of the shrimp.  Delicious!

title: Whipped Ricotta Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: DO NOT be tempted to use a stand mixer fitted with a whisk attachment instead of a food processor. A mixer does not achieve the silky texture that a food processor does - it was still grainy even after 5-6 minutes of whisking. I've made it both ways now, and using a food processor produces a far superior product and is definitely the way to go. Serving suggestion: For breakfast, spread on toast, drizzle with honey, then top with avocado slices. Divine.

@Deb W I enjoy something similar- just got back from a local farmers market and topped ricotta on toast  with a drizzle of local honey, slices of fresh strawberries and peaches (both deliciously in season!) and sprinkled toasted sliced almonds on top. Sometimes for dessert I’ll skip the toast and have a bowl of fresh berries, peaches, or nectarines, topped with ricotta, a drizzle of honey and toasted sliced almonds.

I just made this yesterday, added lemon zest & juice and also a few cloves of garlic.  Then I added the blended ricotta to a made from scratch tomato sauce, served over pasta.  The lemon and garlic appear in the final dish as very subtle.  Everyone loved it.

This was so easy & the texture is heavenly! Me being me, I added salt, pepper, lemon zest, & a sprinkling of garlic powder & Aleppo pepper - I know, I know, not the same but it’s really the texture that this recipe reveals & creates a lovely base for imagination.
Whisk it by hand with a little heavy cream. Easy and comes out light and very creamy.
Transformative! I used a good local ricotta, and spread it on fresh sourdough bread, topped with thinly slide peach and drizzled with good honey. So simple, but so delicious!
Add honey and lemon juice to whipped ricotta. Finish with fresh thyme leaves.
Try it with some pine nuts sprinkled on top. Delicious.
Looks like it could substitute for cream cheese, which I don't tolerate because it's cultured like most cheeses. That would be fantastic!

Made today following directions using mini-food processor.  Excellent results.  Tonight will use with Serious Eats' "beet and citrus salad with ricotta."  Thanks for all the suggestions for other seasonings and uses -- will try them.
Much better flavor made with dry cottage cheese curds. And yes to the food processor!
Never, never use ricotta other than Polly-O. There is no brand that compares. I have tasted them all.

@Eric C. In the rural area where I live there are three different local cheese makers (including a sheep farm) that make and sell fresh ricotta. I know some of them are carried further afield, even down to NYC. I bet other folks in different areas might have access to the same. I’d guess they might be worth trying, and possibly better than an industrial sized brand. Of course, homemade ricotta is a cinch as well. I know cost can be prohibitive if you’re trying to get things far from their source, but I encourage everyone to search out milk and cheese from happy cows, and support small scale farmers where you’re able!
Has anyone tried this with a blender?
Yes, I use a high-power blender (Vita-Mix) and it comes out perfectly smooth.
I have whipped ricotta cheese for another purpose:  making an Alfredo sauce without the heavy cream.  Just add milk til you get the same consistency as cream.  Delicious and more protein than fat.
With which crackers?  My favorite brand is no longer available.
Delicious but I found 3/4 tsp of salt to be way too much and I love salty!

This was not a great dish. The ricotta blended smoothly but was too salty and goopy. I served it with a spicy tomato chutney and a flatbread cracker and people liked it. But the texture just seemed off putting.
Try with cottage cheese and some of the additions in other’s comments.
Delicious!  I used Hot Honey for the drizzle.
@Mary me too! Very good.

I found this way to salty as written (and that’s something I almost never say!). I suspect it has a lot to do with the ricotta you are using. Would recommend starting with no salt and adding to taste as you are in process of whipping the ricotta. The saltiness became more pronounced once whipped. I added some lemon zest and lemon juice to help balance it out. The smooth texture was very nice. Used as a base for roasted cherry tomatoes (Ottolenghi recipe)
Used honey to make a sweeter version, for use on a Dutch Baby with sliced, sautéed apples. Hit with the hubby!

This was a tremendous hit for our Thanksgiving dinner. After reading the comments, I abandoned my plan to use a hand mixer and used a food processor. I added lemon zest and a touch of lemon juice, finishing it up with olive oil and cracked black pepper. I doubled the recipe, thankfully, since it was gone in a flash.
WAY too salty (and I like salt)! I made per the directions (but tripled the recipe) and found it inedible. Had to throw out the whole batch.

@Melissa Teller
Please note that kosher salt is not nearly as salty as table salt. If you used regular table salt in this recipe then that is the reason it was way too salty. 3/4 teaspoon of kosher salt is equivalent to 1/2 teaspoon of table salt.
would an immersion blender work instead of a traditional food processor? also, how long would this store in an airtight container in the fridge? thanks!

We had this at a restaurant last week and I was thrilled to make it as an Easter appetizer today. I added honey and then drizzled with pumpkin seeds & more honey. Love how flexible this recipe can be.
Seasoned ala my N. Italian grandmother's manicotti filling: parmesan, nutmeg, lemon zest, salt/pepper. Dolloped onto pasta in red sauce. Delicious.

title: One-Pot Chicken Arroz Rojo Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody:
@glork grating a tomato is easier than you think. Place your grater over a bowl. Cut the tomato in half and cup the skin side of the round part in the palm of your hand. Press the cut side into the grater and grate away. The skin will save your hand from injury until you use up all the flesh.

It's a fun technique that you can also use to make a fresh summer tomato sauce by grating directly into some gently cooking garlic and olive oil. Hope this helps!
@Sunny Good one! We used that technique in a few restaurants I worked in when I was young.

@Sunny
That’s the move for fresh tomatoes! The box grater is my best friend for fresh tomatoes.

I enjoyed the simplicity of this meal and found it to be a nourishing and satisfying one pot meal. I would definitely not skip the jalapeños and cilantro, though. They add a needed heat and complexity to the dish that rounds it out well. I tried hot sauce on it but enjoyed it better just with the crunch of the jalapeños. To avoid undone rice, I simmered the pot for 22 minutes (a little more than suggested) and then pulled the pot off the heat and let it steam for 7 minutes further. The result was perfectly cooked basmati and thoroughly cooked chicken. Nice dinner.
This recipe has potential but was a bit bland. Next time I will swap the grated tomato for a small can of Rotel, add a small can of mild diced green chiles, and double the amount of cumin.

@Lindsay @Lindsay I make a similar dish and use a T of Knorr chicken and tomato buillon and a can of El Pato tomato sauce (both found in Hispanic aisle).  The El Pato is spicy and would certainly liven it up!
@Lindsay agreed - it was very meh without some added heat. I was hoping for more flavor complexity from the tomato.

This recipe is super close to what my Mom in México use to make for us, it is easy and delicious.  I don't want to be a pain changing this recipe,  but if you feel like having fun try frying the rice along the poblano, garlic,  etc...add your tomatoes step later, just get that rice golden color. And for the people with not cooked all way rice just add a little chicken broth or water...I added a cup of beer, like a corona, nothing heavy,it comes out delicious.
Great idea! I had to keep adding broth. Wish I’d had some beer!!
Followed the recipe exactly. This is basically a burrito without the highly caloric wrapper. Serve with classic toppings of cheddar, salsa and sour cream. Delicious!

Delicious, I doubled the amount of broth per some other comments and it seemed to be the right amount for the rice. I also added kale for some greens, just tossed it on top of the chicken before letting the whole thing simmer.
Hi, I do liquid 2x the amount of the dry rice. (except 3x for wild rice, which is not the same as "rice")@matt
Substitute brown rice? What difference in cooking technique and time?

Definitely not enough liquid for the rice. Just made it, and the rice is not cooked. Added more liquid and cooked for an additional 15 minutes, still not enough. Used a basmati rice. I recommend following the instructions for the rice you are using.
Is 1 3/4 cups liqid adequate for 1 1/2 cups of rice?

Yes, b/c there's also liquid from the tomatoes. My personal suggestion is: after step 3 move the cooking into an instant pot and set it to 5 minutes, with 2 minutes natural release. release the pressure, open, stir and let sit for a few minutes. I make arroz con pollo that way and it's perfect every time.

@matt. I looked at several arroz rojo recipes online and yes, the amount of water (or broth) is standard.  The pulverized tomato adds the extra liquid.  Several of the recipes did, however, call for putting the tomatoes and cooked onion with the water in a blender to make a slurry.  I don’t know whether or not that makes a huge difference, as I have not yet tried this recipe,
Just did it and yes, with all the other water (tomatos, beans, corn)
Sadly, no poblanos in New Zealand in winter (or most other times), so I used a red bell pepper. Also I substituted pinto beans for black beans. Delicious.
Used Mexican Oregano since that felt appropriate. I will say that the rice came out ever so slightly crunchy. Might add a little extra liquid next time but overall a nice recipe!

True Mexican rice (arroz con pollo or arroz rojo) should use long grain rice fried gently to a golden brown.  When I make either dish, I cook whole skin-on chicken thighs (and breasts if you have white meat lovers) in water with onion, salt, and plenty of cumin in a separate pot.  I brown the rice in olive oil in a second large pot, add the chiles, finely chopped onion, and spices and cook while stirring to make the flavors pop.  Add cut up cooked chicken and use the broth you just made.
@John Viescas do you fry the dry grains until golden brown?

I thought this was great. I made it as written with the following minor modifications:
- used Mexican oregano and generally doubled the spices
- added ancho chili powder
- used boneless chicken breasts
- used 'spicy' black beans
- melted a little colby jack on top prior to serving
corn was fresh from the farm stand.
Once again, thighs. IMHO, chicken breasts are better, and is my protein choice. I really don’t like thighs or any dark meat, even though I’m “supposed” to
For me, thighs tend to be much more flavorful and less prone to overcooking.  I'm sure you could use breasts, just be careful with the timing to not overcook.

I've always wanted to try adding chicken to my mexican rice and this gives me courage (and steps).  One thing I'll do differently is at some point, saute the rice till it's lightly brown like one does with mexican rice, to get that fluff. Not sure when I'd do this though.

I was drawn to this recipe for it's simplicity and freshness. Mindful of notes that it's bland, I bumped up all the flavors (whole onion, more garlic, big chili, doubled cumin and oregano) and deglazed with wine before adding the tomato. There is NO good reason to grate tomato, unless you want to. Sub canned tomatoes. even tomato sauce, and cut back on paste.  Squeeze over some lemon and serve with tortilla chips! Needs acid and texture for sure!

The grated tomato is also the technique used to make pan con tomate, my all-time favorite breakfast in Spain. Utter simplicity and pure flavor: fresh grated tomatoes on toasted local baguette-style bread with a pinch of salt and olive oil. Heaven on the Costa de Sol!
Discovering the technique of grating tomatoes in recipes was an AHA! moment for me when we moved to Spain- it's a game changer for rice dishes!
I can’t have corn. Is it possible to leave it out? Also, I prefer chicken breast to chicken thighs. Has anyone tried substituting breast instead of thighs?

Added some smoked paprika and ground chipotle powder to the chicken seasoning for some extra smoke, and this dish was amazing. Also if you're using fresh corn (like I did), you probably need some extra water to take into account the ice in the frozen corn bag, because my rice turned out a little firm

Nice recipe. The flavors aren't going to blow you away, but it's wholesome and comforting home cooking. The key to making the flavor pop a little more is going to be salt, and the ambiguous instruction "season with salt and pepper" isn't much help. My rule of thumb, which I learned from Melissa Clark, is to use approximately 3/4 teaspoon of Diamond Crystal kosher salt for each pound of chicken. I used 2 teaspoons here, and that was about right.
This recipe grossly underestimates the quantity of liquid required to cook the rice.
@Sturgis Riobinson. Absolutely!

After step 3, move the cooking into an instant pot and set it to 5 minutes, with 2 minutes natural release. release the pressure, open, stir and let sit for a few minutes. I make arroz con pollo that way and it's perfect every time.

This was tasteless. 
But I liked the combination of ingredients. 
Next time, I will double the oregano and cumin, the corn, and definitely the garlic. I will also add a hot pepper to the poblano. 
I will also reduce the amount of rice, and therefore the chicken stock. 
1 cup of raw rice is more than sufficient, especially because this mixture contains beans.

Recipe is annoying.  Followed it to a T, and rice was not cooked.  I looked back to ensure it said to drain the black beans.  Yep.  I looked back to see if I was supposed to use instant rice (yuck).  Nope.  I used extra long grain rice.  1-3/4 cups of broth is not enough for this amount of rice especially when a step says to cook down the liquid for 5 mins after adding the grated tomato.  I’m on 40 mins now and had to add about two cups more liquid so far.  And this is with a heavy Dutch oven lid and no peeking other than when timers went off.  Please test and correct.
Dish is fairly easy to make but I added some sazon for extra seasoning and also substituted the beans for pigeon peas which I think work better in this dish. Everyone loved it.
the rice takes forEVER to cook :(
I found this to be very disappointing. There was way too much rice, which overwhelmed everything else.

Did it all in the InstantPot. Used the sauté function, then switched to high pressure when everything was combined. Five minutes on high, two minutes natural release, then quick release. No problem with the rice not being cooked sufficiently . Subbed fresh corn for the frozen and some leftover San Marzano canned whole tomatoes for the fresh, grated tomato. Cilantro for the win!  I would definitely do this again, maybe with a squeeze of lime at the end. I thought it was tasty, and easy enough to tweak to personal preferences if so desired.
Echo that the liquid isn’t a sufficient amount.  Spicing is boring.  This was a bland gummy mess.

Made this exactly per the recipe and was impressed - easy to make on a week night, and tasty. It's super 'tweakable' to your own taste. I might make it a little more spicy next time around, but otherwise, great.
Recipe reads as if it will be mouth-watering- I'm just curious about grating a tomato, Anyone have any suggestions ?

Just made it tonight. It’s delicious. You’re overthinking the grating of the tomato. The thick skin of the tomato will save your palms I promise. I fire roasted the jalepeno because I didn’t want the intense heat. Nice Smokey flavor
I can't wait to try this recipe. It seems ideally suited for an InstantPot / pressure cooker. Curious if anyone has tried it using an IP and what cooking times were used.
@John B See Edith’s comment about using the IP. Melissa Clark even endorsed her comment in the NYT Cooking email today.
No beans for this cook! But it should be wonderful anyway!

If I use some additional vegetables and more black beans to replace the chicken to make it vegetarian does anyone know if that would work?
I imagine vegetables such as green, red, orange peppers and maybe chopped onion along with a two cans black beans would work. 
Just curious if anyone has tried that.
What kind of tomato is most flavorful?

@kristina could you use maybe a jalapeño or some spicy pepper but remove the seeds and blanch them for 2 minutes, this would remove the excess heat (in case little children or grown-up not liking the spiciness)? can you get some kirean green Chiles that are not spicy ? poblano is mostly not spicy. sometimes my Mom would mix a bell pepper and a jalapeño  and broiled them , that gets you closer to a poblano.
Can one use  boneless chicken breasts?  How will this affect the cooking time?
Kind of meh for the amount of work involved. Needed almost 2x as much broth & simmered for close to 40 mins before rice was softened.

One pot recipes always feel like bait for failure. Disappointed in this recipe because as suspected, the liquid ratio wasn’t enough and it’s too late to take my trust back from the recipe. Gloppy failure. Thanks for nothing NYT! Cook rice separately if you want a chance at an edible dinner.

After reading this recipe online I searched my pantry for the required ingredients and had enough of them to proceed. It was easy enough and turned out great. This recipe is close enough to Arroz con Pollo so I added Sliced olives and served it with mayo on the side. It made a lot for the two of us and there was enough left over for another meal

I had slightly less than 1 1/2 pounds of chicken thighs, but next time I will make the full 1 1\2  pounds. I didn’t have a fresh chili, so I used a scant teaspoon of Kashmiri chili powder. 1/2 cup of corn would get lost in this dish, so I used a generous one cup of roasted frozen corn. I increased the spices considerably, double and almost triple, because black beans would definitely soak up much of the spices. In light of the other comments I used 2 1/2 cups of chicken stock and checked to make sure the rice was fully cooked. I ended up adding a splash more stock about 15 minutes into the covered simmering time. All in all, this was a very tasty one pot dinner. It makes a ton, so I froze some for a night I didn’t feel like cooking.
This was really bland…followed the recipe exactly but added 1/2 c more broth at the end because the rice was not cooked through. very ordinary and not worth the effort.
I had to add another splash of liquid and cook for 10 more minutes.

Delicious! I used overripe heirloom tomatoes because I didn't want to waste them. They were too soft to grate, so I chopped them. They produced a lot of liquid, so I used double-concentrate tomato paste. The tomatoes dissolved and I didn't mind little pieces of skin. I always taste chiles so I can adjust the amount. The one I bought was really hot, so I used half, no seeds. Just enough heat for me. I used long-grain brown rice and parboiled it so it would be done in 20 minutes.

This was so flavorful and easy to tweak! I used skin-on bone-in chicken thighs, shisito pepper, and no beans. I cheated and just used grape tomatoes cut in half, sautéed a bit with onion and pepper. I didn't really stick to the measurement and I tend to use spices and herbs quite liberally.. and it came out so so good. Didn't even need hot sauce, and I am a big fan of spicy food. Go for it, don't be afraid of spices and herbs and have fun!

Going in I thought there wouldn’t be enough liquid. Three cups of liquid needed for a cup and a half of rice. It was crunchy at 20 minutes. I added the extra water and it was enough after extra time. The cumin and oregano make the dish. Cilantro and some hot sauce heat help. I freeze dollops of tomato paste. That was easy. There is no way I’m going to grate a tomato. I do cheat with my summer garden and cook whole stemmed tomatoes in a large pot, all summer. Cook until they can be reduced and mashed some. Then blender portions twenty seconds and into the freezer bag. Even with the skin on it makes a fine sauce when thawed and reduced on the stove for spaghetti day. Tangy fresh. Anyway that’s what I used, 3/4 cups. Did this recipe work for anyone as written?

@TDaddyTodd I had a similar experience with some of the rice still crunchy at 20. Seems that the liquid in the grated tomato is very important to overall rice cooking, but the amount is difficult to pinpoint with grating, so it didn’t work for me.
I loved how this came together, but needed more liquid. A cursory glance at the liquid-to-rice ratio would suggest it needs more liquid. It is delicious, and will make again

It’s summer, why not use fresh corn for more crunch. Cut back on the rice by a third so it’s not such a carbo load, add medium dice red bell pepper for even more color. Someone wrote more cumin, I agree.  Other than that, a tasty meal!
Great dish but bland. Don’t be shy on seasoning. Next time we make it we’ll add some chipotle peppers from the can for more heat and seasoning.
Made as written but added 2 tbsp of tajin, and a bouillon cube. Delish!
I added 2c of broth, not 1.5, which  worked beautifully. Addresses the issues others had w crunchy rice!

Not impressed with this recipe, though I usually like what I find on this app.   Very bland, rice was under-cooked using the time suggested.  In addition to being bland, the time spent preparing was long compared to the result.   Definitely do not recommend.
Needed more liquid as rice cooked unevenly and not fully. I guess it depends how juicy the tomato is…Added habanero while cooking and some lime to top.

I liked this and would likely make it again. I agree with it needing more spice, even when I used 1/2 cup of mole sauce instead of the tomato paste. Used fresh corn which has so much more flavor, sauteed the rice with the onion and chilies before adding everything else.  Also I would increase the beans and decrease the rice.

Really good. Simple to make, but complex flavors nonetheless. This time of year, with fresh tomatoes and fresh corn--excellent. I followed the suggestion to use Instant Pot after step 3 and  was very happy with the result.

I followed the recipe exactly and it was great. Not always the case though. The jalapenos and cilantro were necessary. The second day when we ate the left overs, we added sour cream and sriracha and it was also great.

Followed advice of others- doubled spices, added paprika. For extra liquid grated 2 tomatoes. Cut grape tomatoes, avocado, cilantro, limes, grated cheese, sour cream for toppings. Took more like 25 minutes. Delicious! Would have used Instantpot but didn’t have it.

Love this recipe. And here's the obligatory, "but I changed this" ... I hate grating tomatoes so I used a small can of Rotel (or any diced tomato will do), doubled the poblanos and seasonings and I unfortunately have the soap thing for cilantro so left that out. Everything else to a T except I needed an extra 5 minutes cook time. 20 min was still too soupy. Definitely a new addition to my meal prep list.

Very bland, needs lots more seasonings and a big squeeze of lime. 
Too hot to cook here so I adapted it to the instant pot.

Made this tonight for supper. The only changes I made was using a small can of mild Rotel for the grated tomato. I seasoned a little more aggressively with the cumin. 
Very tasty. I can see making it without the chicken and using as a side with grilled meats or a taco bar.

Tried this - made according to directions except I added a little extra chicken broth (noticed in the comments that people said the rice was dry.) It was okay, but not a lot of flavor. I’m glad I added cilantro and jalapeño to spice it up a little. I probably won’t make it again.
I agreed that this recipe is surprisingly bland.  It really does need the jalapeños or hot sauce.

Quite bland. The amount of spices could easily be doubled. Also, for 1.5 cups of rice, you need 3 cups of broth, maybe a little less if your grated tomato is very juicy.

If I try this again I think I'll add about 1/4 cup Lizano Sauce, which is used in Costa Rican Chicken and Rice. That'll help.

This recipe is flawed as it's presented here, but it has potential to make a tasty and healthy one-bowl meal with a few changes. (e.g., use brown rice and adjust liquid and simmer time accordingly.)

Loved this recipe, made a few adjustments to simplify:
1. Substitute a 14.5 oz can of diced tomatoes instead of grating a tomato
2. Use 1 cup of uncooked pearl barley instead of rice - boost the fiber content and introduce a chewier texture
3. I didn't have poblano peppers or cilantro in the house, so I skipped those and used jarred jalapenos and it worked beautifully
4.  Throw it all in an instant pot, all at once. Pressure cook for 15 minutes. Done & easy!

A little bland.
Next time I'd use a full onion, more tomato, double the corn (I used fresh)and double the spices.

I made this and it was a success with caveats. This is one of those recipes you really need to use only as a guide and adjust for your own tastes. What I did:
* used the prescribed amount of rice and broth but,
* grated 2 fresh tomatoes. When cooked with the tomato paste they didn’t end up too liquidy cos you cook it down a bit. 
* used 1kg of chicken instead of 680gm
* something like tripled the spice - used garam masala cos I was missing cumin and loads of oregano plus 4 garlic cloves and about 1.5 cups of onions. Sorry I don’t measure this I just eyeball it. 
* added about half a jar of chipotle in adobo during the tomato paste stage. This gave tons of flavor 
* add a teaspoon of msg cos why not
* used madame Jeanette and jalapeño instead of poblano cos I live in the Netherlands and that’s all I could find in terms of chili
* used a whole can of corn - about 285gm instead of frozen 
*my rice was jasmine rice and unwashed. 

Cook time once I added it all together was about 20mins. Rice didn’t need extra liquid and wasn’t gummy.
Read all the comments about needing more liquid for the rice, added more liquid and ended up with mushy rice. C’mon y’all!
Ummmm that prep is not 20 minutes… more like 40 for me and I cook every night!
It was a bit on the bland side.

Meh. Agree with the comment that it tastes exactly like the inside of a burrito - and I'll add a mediocre burrito. Added cheddar and lots of Cholula and wrapped it in a tortilla because might as well. Tasted fine.

Definitely a problem with rice to liquid ratio! I added water several times and cooked it at least twice as long and it still had crunchy rice (and a scorched pan). Very disappointing; wish I had read the reviews first.
I’m looking for some warmer weather dishes  to make ahead and freeze. Any reason this wouldn’t be a good candidate.
@Jocelyn I freeze arroz con pollo all the time in individual portions for lunch. Go for it!
I followed the recipe exactly and could not get the rice cooked. After 40 minutes it’s still crunchy. I don’t know how people are getting tender rice in this recipe. Waste of time and ingredients.

Try par-boiling the rice in salted water until it's "al dente", rinse and drain, then add to the pot per the recipe. After 20 minutes of cooking, remove from heat and the rice will continue to absorb liquid when left covered for an additional 20-ish minutes.
When would I add fresh corn to this recipe? TIA
IDK where I went wrong, but I needed lots more liquid and lots more time.  Likely due to "operator error," but finished product was wonderful.
@Patty same. Poor recipe.
I do not know what a Dutch oven is 😭
@Victor, it’s a big heavy pot with a kit. Usually cast iron. Sometimes coated with enamel. Mine is my favorite kitchen item. They are fantastic to cook with

After the chicken is browned & removed from the pot, there is no need to add more oil. There was plenty of rendered fat from the chicken thighs to sauté the onions, peppers, garlic, etc.  I used brown rice & used 2x the broth & twice the time and it was a great meal!

This was really delicious! makes for fabulous leftovers as the tastes permeate more (if you like leftovers)
I usually just look at the ingredients and make it to suit our tastes. Loved it!
if you cook it low enough the amount of broth listed works well.

Very simple, easy pantry meal. Aside from the poblano everything was already in the kitchen. That said I found the result to be a bit flat and on dimensional. I might try adding some additional spice to the kitchen next time and perhaps fire roasting some of the produce. Definitely more complicated but will likely address some of the lacking flavor in the original recipe.
I had high hopes but should have read comments before preparing. I think it can be tweaked, but as is, it just lacks flavor.

I made this entirely in my InstantPot. Even steps 1-3, using the Saute function. But after step 3, when I switched it to Pressure Cook, after a few minutes it would display a "food burn" message. I added more chicken broth, stirred it and scraped up the burned bits, and tried again. Same food burn message. So I just shut it off and let it sit in the pot covered for a couple hours. The rice eventually softened up, but it's still very dry. I also used smoked paprika when sauteing the chicken.
This was really tasty. I added some smoked paprika and aleppo chili powder. My main criticism is that I needed to add way more liquid than what was called for in order for the rice to cook properly.

I make a version of this in my instant pot using canned tomatoes with chilis and leftover rotisserie chicken. It seriously comes together in moments, cooks in minutes and makes a big batch that freezes well. Great served with shredded cheese and sour cream.

I saw this recipe and thought it sounded delicious.  As was mentioned by someone, I too found it bland.  1/2 teaspoon of ground cumin?  I was trying to follow the recipe but had to use more cumin!  The rice to liquid ratio worked fine.  I love the idea of Rotel tomatoes, thanks!  I thought it could have used a squeeze of lime juice, but didn't have one on hand.  Certainly more onion.  Overall, okay.  I would have baked it in the oven but it's nearly 90 here and I don't have central air.
This was okay. Made recipe exactly (first time). It was bland. I would add more cumin, more onion, more garlic. I did not have any issues with the rice, my rice came out perfect.
Definitely make sure to add more chicken broth so the rice cooks properly. I also added some smoked paprika and more cumin than the recipe called for.
Definitely agree! My rice was a little crunchy after 35 minutes cooking. Otherwise pretty tasty.

I would advise adding at least double the cumin and using native field tomato in season,  otherwise canned puree. The jalapeño and cilantro topping are a must. Chopped green olives with pimento also enhance flavor.
I just made this. In was better in charred tortillas with a little cheese otherwise it was meh.
Can this be done in a rice cooker, after initial prep?
I felt like this needed more flavor so I added 3 squeezed lemons and 1tsp smoked paprika as well as adding half as much more of all the spices.

Hi Friends, I made this twice and it’s a hit! The only issue is that my rice becomes too wet and mushy. I’ve tried reducing the water by 1/4c but it’s still too wet. Can I get your expertise what I should do? I’m not sure if it is because my grated tomato adds too much liquid.
A tasty dish! Don't omit rinsing the rice, or simply use more broth. We failed to rinse, and it was too dry. But when we reheated the leftovers, adding a bit more liquid, it was delicious.
Made with long grain basmati brown rice.  Pre-cooked in the broth (amount according to brown rice recipe).  Did this concurrent with Step 3.  Came out VERY nice!

I can't understand how these ingredients came out so, so bland. Made it exactly as written, but now realize I could have likely doubled the cumin and perhaps the oregano. As it was cooking and I was sampling it and realized how weak it was, I found the comment above about adding ancho chile, and tried that. It didn't save it.

Also, if you are using brown rice, double the cooking time (and maybe drop the chicken in later as a result).
I would at least double the cumin and the oregano on the chicken.  Probably sub in a large beefsteak tomato (or two) or perhaps a couple cans of Rotel
I did everything Greg suggested in other reply and it still did not save it.

The family loved this dish and asked that it be included in the regular rotation.  I skipped the cilantro (because I can't stand it) and the jalapenos (because I have a daughter with Crohn's) and served with sriracha on the side for those of us that wanted a kick.  A winner.
This is a really nice base recipe. Neither me or my heartburn were interested in adding jalapenos or hot sauce, but a spritz of lime right before eating brightened it up nicely.

This recipe is super close to what my Mom in México use to make for us, it is easy and delicious. I don't want to be a pain changing this recipe, but if you feel like having fun try frying the rice along the poblano, garlic, etc...add your tomatoes step later, just get that rice golden color. And for the people with not cooked all way rice just add a little chicken broth or water...I added a cup of beer, like a corona, nothing heavy,it comes out delicious.
Substituted green hatch chili (one can) and canned diced tomatoes (15 oz can) for fresh. Excellent.
This was OK but agree with comments on lack of noteworthy flavor. Personally prefer the NYT Arroz con Pollo over this.

Great recipe. I made it with brown rice that I parboiled in plenty water for about 20 minutes, otherwise followed the recipe closely. Only change for next time is to be less timid with seasoning the chicken - and yes, I’ll try the smoked paprika as one commenter suggested.
Re. the liquid to rice ratio, some lids might not be tight fitting/heavy enough and/or the heat is too high, so too much steam escapes after adding the chicken.

This recipe is too good! Typically when my friends gets together I cook for everyone and I always decide the menu. When I first cooked it my bandmates straight up told me that this has to stay in the rotation permanently and I have no say. Haha

So I bumped the spice level WAY up and it still was pretty bland. Also, not enough liquid (or something). Seems like all of the "one pot" rice dishes end up with rice that is mushy with a crunchy center.
Not my favorite recipe. The recipe came underwhelmingly lacking flavors.

too much rice
bland
poblano pepper didn't add much flavor

Slight mods to recipe
1 3/4 lbs chicken
1 C onion
No poblano
1 tsp cumin 
1 1/2 tsp oregano
2 C beef stock 

Easy and very tasty. Served with cilantro & hot sauce and yogurt. Comfort food!
Delicious! I doubled the garlic (I pretty much double the garlic in every recipe.) I also like more tomato so I will use more next time. But it is very good as written.

Excellent hearty dinner.  The family loved it.  I followed the recipe as described and served with chopped cilantro and pickled jalapeno slices as suggested, but also served with Mexican crema.  We found the crema added a nice, creamy touch.
As is this is too bland to eat, even after adding a ton of other spices and some salsa it was still just meh, would never make again.

I love this recipe! I increased the amount of cumin and oregano. Since I can’t get poblanos where I live, I know I’m missing an additional layer of flavor. Even without them, this recipe is a keeper my family loves.
This was just OK. Edible, but not great. Followed recipe exactly and everyone agreed it was too bland.

My rice was way undercooked following the recipe as is, though I didn’t rinse the rice first and that may have contributed to that issue a little bit. I was actually quite shocked that this recipe was not as fool proof as everything else I’ve made using this app. I won’t be using this recipe again.
If you don’t have a tomato but you do have salsa (writing from the American Southwest here)—use a bit of salsa instead!
I needed more liquid to get the rice to cook (and it took 30 min) and I added a lot of red pepper flakes. Other than that I loved it.

A rare miss here. First off, the broth to rice ratio is just wrong. My rice didn’t cook. Based on packaging instructions for the rice I used and factoring the extra stuff in the pot, it should have 2.25-2.5 cups of broth. Or, maybe just bump it down to 1 cup of rice. (I think that would come closer to the pictured dish.)

Also, some have said, this is pretty bland as written. Definitely could use some additional spicing and/or a helping of salsa/guac/cheese.
Need to double the broth

I cheated by shredding a rotisserie chicken and cooking the rice in a rice cooker with chicken stock. Cooked the onion, poblano, garlic, added the oregano and cumin to the tomato/tomato paste, cooked that down, added the cooked rice, beans, corn, and chicken with as much stock as necessary to maintain desired moistness. Was a little bland so added more cumin and some cinnamon to make it more interesting. Delicious and saved time.
Very bland—made to recipe. Next time I would add more spice.
The recommended condiments (jalapeño, cilantro, hot sauce) take this to the next level. We also added a squeeze of fresh lime juice on top and Greek yogurt on the side.
I added some smoked paprika for depth. And way more human than called for. Next time I will add lots more salt and/or serve with a wedge of lime
Followed recipe exactly. Rice was completely undercooked after the 20 min cook time. It required an additional 30 minutes of cooking and additional liquid for the rice to be cooked properly.
Very dry.weak flavor. It needed too much tweaking.
I made half the recipe. Skipped the beans and doubled the corn. Small can of diced peppers instead of fresh. Excellent.

I didn’t have a tomato, so used a small can of diced tomatoes. After 20 minutes, the rice was nowhere near done. I added broth and simmered on for 10 minutes. Still not done. Took more broth and 20 more minutes.

A tasty dish that my family loves, but I can’t seem to cook the rice fully without burning the bottom layer. I keep trying to tweak it with little success. Also, I found the whole recipe takes much longer than indicated. The prep alone is fairly extensive.

Would triple the cilantro and poblano peppers. Ratio seemed off. Added 2 Jalopeno peppers to give more kick. Too much rice- 1 cup would be better.  (Used basmati rice and heard this from others but didn’t modify first time cooking) Otherwise great recipe!

I made this exactly to the recipe. The good is that it can be on the table in one hour. That said, I found it pretty one note in flavor. The addition of the fresh jalapenos helped as well as hot sauce and a dollop of sour cream. But overall, just a bit flat lined. I think if I were to make it again, I would omit the chicken and make it as a side.  Also, my wife forbids a box grater in my home. :) So I seeded and chopped up the tomato. I think next time I would sub in a can of diced tomato.
I used salsa instead of a tomato. All I had was Tatemada tomatillo salsa and the sour taste really enhanced the carnelized tomato paste!
I feel like the rice to stock ratio is a fair bit off in this recipe, I ended up having to add almost another cup of stock and cook it a fair bit longer than recommended.

title: Grilled Tofu Salad With Honey Chile Dressing Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody:
Tasty and easy to make! I ended up subbing the birds eye chili with regular red pepper flakes, because I couldn't find the former at the first two stores I hit up and didn't feel like running to too many stores. I also ended up baking the veggies and tofu instead of grilling. Just extends the cooking time a bit if you like things on the crispy side.

The vinaigrette is really good, though I do think you could sub in soy sauce for a fully plant-based, if slightly different vibe.
Chile is a country. The peppers are chilis. C’mon NYT.
You're right that Chile is a country. But...in this context, chile refers to Capsicum peppers, while chili refers to chiles mixed with other spices or a spicy stew made with chiles.
But now we get to call it Honey CHILE (rhymes with 'style') Dressing :)

@Madeleine Sann
Here in the southwest we call the spicy peppers “chile.” Heaven forbid anyone call our green chile “chili.” How gauche!
My wife is a vegetarian and also allergic to fish. Can I sub soy sauce 1for 1or in some other proportion?
Your kitchen, your rules, go for it -- but I'd use Tamari instead if you can find it.

I found vegetarian fish sauce at my local asian grocery store. I think you could reasonably sub in soy sauce for the dressing. I made this salad and the dressing was my family's favorite part. The sweet combined with the chilis pleasing.
the sauce makes it.  added corn from cob to the uncooked components.
Broiled the  green beans with oil, salt, and pepper for 5 min since I didn’t want to them to fall through my grill grates.  Love the dressing.
For vegetarians, what can we replace fish sauce with?
Added dry rub marinade for tofu 1/2 tsp each salt, pepper, smoked paprika, onion powder, coriander, 1/4 tsp each garlic powder, cayenne
Used chicken, because that’s what I had on hand- delicious!
While this appears to be a vegetarian recipe, I would either eliminate the fish oil or substitute it with another oil such as avocado, sesame or olive oil.

title: Calamari Salad With Potatoes and Olives Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I used TJ tinned calamari and it turned out well
I made it pretty much exactly as written. It was easy and delicious. Next time I'm going to make it with shrimp.
Quick sautée or roast of the protein provides more flavor than boiling. I used a mixture of calamari, shrimp and bay scallops. Delicious!
Excellent. An easy, tasty, bright and light meal for a hot summer night. Next time I will ramp up the garlic, pepperoncini, and capers, and garnish with parsley
Made the recipe, no alterations.  Terrific flavor, great summer dish.  Wife requests more potatoes next time.  That’s just per your preference.
Excellent. Quick and delicious.

Didn’t have potatoes or pepperoncini. It was still great as a calamari salad. Used Kalamata olives instead of green olives and a tbsp of capers. Used less of the vinaigrette since there were no potatoes - reserved the rest to use for salad dressing. Light and tasty, great for summer. Packed individual small bowls for a picnic - Kept them in the cooler with the drinks.

title: One-Pot Bean and Tomato Stew With Cod Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Anchovies are the unsung,quiet, heroes of the kitchen. Yum!
Would anchovy paste work in place of the anchovies? Opening a tin of anchovies that might not be used for a while would be a waste.
@Michael Napa Of course the paste works. But you could always use more of those little fishes. We would use  the whole can in this household.
The cod will not swim away without whole anchovies in the mix. Anchovy paste is fine.

@Michael Napa. Another option:  open that jar or tin of anchovy paste.  If a tin, put remainder in a glass container.  make  sure the little fish are covered with olive oil.  Refrigerate.  Last a very, very long time!

This sounds delicious! And also like something I would make without a recipe.

Except for the anchovies. 😊

Truly more than a sum of its parts! Yum! First, I’m a home cook and I’m the only one who eats fish so I halved everything. A few mods due to what was available. Fish market was out of cod so I used haddock. Subbed anchovy paste, lacking tinned anchovies, and subbed dried oregano since the market didn’t have fresh, Haddock took about fifteen minutes to cook to what I thought was perfect. Easy, quick and so very delicious! Will definitely make again.

Cooked small white beans (Alubia Blanca from Rancho Gordo).Used about 3 cups plus some of the bean broth, no additional water needed. 2 hake fillets (1 lb. total). Didn't need to add the butter. Complex flavors, really excellent.
Wonderful flavors abound. Loved this recipe. A new way to serve cod, which always needs a little boost. Will make again for sure!
Made this for dinner tonight. Only change was using pinto beans instead of cannelli, as that's what I had on hand.  Served with salad and crusty bread as suggested.  Yum and healthy, too.

My husband and I both work from home. In these dog days of summer (fall cannot come soon enough!), I’ve been finding it harder and harder to figure out what to make us for weekday lunch. I decided to open the NYT Cooking app, filter on lunch, and make the first recipe for which I had the ingredients on hand. This was the winner…and I have to say, it’s a winner! I know modifications are annoying (and I really do try not to make any the first time I try a dish), but I do recommend a squeeze of lemon over each serving. 

Also, my cod was frozen (Costco!) and I just threw it on the stew and let it poach for about 20 minutes until it was thawed and perfectly cooked. 

And the best part—my husband says he likes it too!
Made this for dinner tonight.  Only change was using pinto beans instead of cannelli, as that's what I had on hand.  Served with crusty bread, as suggested.  Very tasty and healthy, too.
Made per the isructions, a very nice and easy meal.  The flavors are subtle and lovely.  Could add small amount of paprika, smoked or not, to boost the flavor profile if you'd like
Why is the fish in the photo lightly browned when it’s supposed to steam with the beans?
It's sauce

Very forgiving and flexible recipe! Had cod and some but not all of the ingredients, so mixed and matched - chickpeas instead of white beans, a few shallots for the onion, no anchovies but added capers and olives. Subbed half white wine for water, and used dried oregano and a liberal sprinkle of red pepper. Even with all the modifications, delicious!

Outstanding on a cold damp night!  Mades subs based on what was in hand: olives=anchovies, sun dried tomatoes = paste, and blended canned tomatoes = fresh.  Added baby spinach to fulfill green veggie requirement :-) Had a ball of pizza dough and made foccacia on the side.
Great recipe, made greater, if I may, by subbing the water with white wine or fish bouillon. A serious pinch of crushed peppers also heightens the dish adding at step 4. Just me.
Very bland--more anchovies, more oregano!
Made this last night. No notes! Fantastic!

This was good, and easy. The anchovies added umami rather than flavor, making it richer. I mostly followed the recipe, using the tilapia I had on hand instead of cod. I will add more herbs and hope to use  good summer tomatoes next time for more flavor.
Nice. I'm using gigante beans instead of cannellini for more of a Mediterranean vibe.
We enjoyed this fine, but agreed that the "bean-forward" stew overpowered the beautifully poached fish, which should have been the star. Would not repeat this one.
Has anyone frozen this to serve later (adding fish at time of defrost)?
Delicious!  I didn’t have a carrot and used anchovy paste.  Will be making often!
The flavor was pretty bland to me—I even added extra tomato paste. My tomatoes were on the unripe side, but still. I wouldn’t make this again as-is.
@Emily did u add enough salt?
This was tasty and easy, but I think it needed more herbs for a little bit of punch.
Delicious! I added two extra anchovies, but I think adding the whole tin would boost flavor even more.
This did not taste of much, unfortunately.
This was delicious and simple. One question: is there a technique I could be deploying to soften carrots in 5 minutes as the recipe suggests? I find it takes considerably longer to soften those guys.
Make sure to pick ripe tomatoes! Folded in spinach with the butter and parsley at the end for some extra greens; gently to avoid mushing the beans.

Just heavenly. The NYT is so reliable when it comes to beans and fish. I used a bit of bouillon (1 tsp) as is my habit when adding water to any stew and likely at least doubled the garlic (which is also my habit) with beautiful and delicate results. I used cod, true to the instructions, dry oregano as I have never come across a fresh oregano sprig, and added some dried basil. Other details: an extra anchovy made it into the pot and I think I had the freshest and sweetest cherry tomatoes of the season (in Arizona). Sorry to brag. This was perfect.

title: Honey Walnut Shrimp Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Simplified this by 1) candying and frying nuts in the same pan: added walnuts to a dry pan, toasted for a bit, then added 2Tbs brown sugar mixed with 2Tbs water until the water boiled off, then added a swirl of avocado oil to the pan to fry the sugar-coated nuts. This worked great. 2) shallow-fried the shrimp in about 1/2” of oil in a cast iron pan. Dipped a shrimp in cornstarch, added it to the pan, flipped the previous shrimp, and removed the second previous shrimp (that had been flipped when the previous shrimp had been added.) 3) I made the sauce after the first fry, before the second fry, so nothing was hot on the pan while mixing it up. It all came out great!!

I love honey walnut shrimp.  But 8 - 10 cups of oil to fry 20 shrimp seems excessive, particularly if done in batches (I will note that I've been cooking for 50 years, and am not a novice).  I will watch comments to see what others have to say.

@LGL I had the same reaction to all that oil. The other observation I had was this much frying and so many steps to put this together creates a lot of clean up in the kitchen for a weeknight meal. When I think of weeknight meals, clean up after is a factor for me.
@LGL totally agree! 8 cups of oil is spendy and a big hassle to clean up! This can be pan fried in far less oil.

If you want to use less oil, use a small saucepan. Try to find one with higher sides. The saucepan part of my 2-quart Farberware double-boiler is what I use. Yes, you will have to fry in multiple batches but deep frying only takes a few minutes, so it’s not a big deal. I only needed about 3 cups of oil for this recipe.
The final dish here sounds tasty, but I’ll look for comments here about simplifying the process so it’s not so head-scratchingly inefficient. As written it seems unnecessarily time-consuming.

For Sharon Bakes

Look for condensed milk in a resealable tube or squeeze bottle.  Very handy when you want a Vietnamese coffee!

You can also find one ounce packets

Aside from rice, broccoli side is a must for honey walnut chicken. Cut to florets, a quick stir-fry with neutral oil and gentle portions of ginger, garlic, cooking wine and salt and an optional light sauce of corn starch slurry, salt, a whisper of sugar and and oyster sauce.

I once had this dish at a long-since-gone restaurant in NYC and the thing that put it over the top was that it had chunks of honeydew melon mixed in. It may sound odd but it was absolutely delicious! If I find the time to make this rather involved recipe, I’m definitely going to add the melon.
My local restaurant includes cantaloupe.  It's delicious!

I am more than happy to use all the oil, dirty all the pans, and follow all the steps if this gets me close to the magical walnut shrimp I ate many times a large round tables in Hong Kong restaurants with other ex-pat friends pre-1997. Those were fun and delicious times!

This is a dish we order every time we go to Chang Chang or Ni hao, two of our favorite restaurants in DC. While tempting to try it at home, it sure looks like a lot of work. And oil. Looking forward to reading comments from braver folks who try it at home.

i substituted greek yogurt for mayo and didn’t deep fry the shrimp but instead used a few tbsp of oil to cook both onion (had no scallions on hand) and the shrimp. was delicious. Restaurant quality with those substitutions. 5 stars.

I made this recipe the other night in our outdoor kitchen due to the residual odor frying leaves in the house. I found the recipe disappointing. In step 2, the walnuts turned out drenched in a sugary syrup that quickly thickened. For frying, I did use a small Dutch oven. Eyeballed the oil, maybe used 1 to 1.5 cup of oil. But when I added the walnuts, the walnut skins shedded leaving behind burnt walnut skins in the oil. When the shrimp was fried the burnt skins attached to the shrimp adding a very bitter flavor to the end result. The sauce flavor was just okay, not very flavorful. The sauce could have been doubled. But overall, I felt the recipe lacked that wonderful flavor enjoyed from a restaurant preparation. I would not make this recipe again.
I let out a slight gasp when I saw the 2T of sweetened condensed milk.  What are everyone’s thoughts on using the rest of the can?
@Sharon Bakes check out the recipe for Brazilian lemonade on New York Times Cooking. I am happy your post reminded me of it!
@Sharon Bakes You can use it up making Magic Bars or 7 Layer Bars. They both call for pouring a can of sweetened condensed milk over before putting in the oven.

I made this tonight as an early Father’s Day dinner - my dad loves honey walnut shrimp. It is not an everyday dinner, and I made quite a mess while putting this together with sides and dessert. That said, it was worth it. Delicious, perfectly crisp shrimp, and the walnuts were so much better than previous versions I have attempted. Our family loved it, and I would definitely make it again. I don’t mind using a good amount of oil for frying in that I cool, strain, and reuse frying oil a few times. I could see that being a deterrent to some though.
This is a very popular dish in 'fusion' style restaurants. There's no way a kitchen would have that many steps for a dish like this. Got to be a simpler method.
My initial impression of this recipe is that it must not be authentic - I have not heard of Chinese people cooking with Dutch ovens.

@My name

Probably bc many people who read this app don’t have woks

An Asian/Spanish restaurant near me had this on their menu. They changed hands a few years back, the dish is gone. I had it a few times falling madly in love with it, so I tried making it, it came out just ok. I figured it was not the healthiest, but 8cups of oil??? Yikes.

So, at the last minute I found out one of my guests doesn't eat seafood! So, I cut up some chicken thighs I had in the fridge, doubled the sauce, and we had honey walnut shrimp AND chicken. It was GREAT and everyone loved it. I did NOT use that much oil, and shallow fried everything in a cast iron skillet. Otherwise, everything was the same. This was a really special treat of a dish, and not too difficult.

Suggestions regarding simplification are worth heeding. Another simplification step: get smaller, peeled shrimp (~30ct), and skip butterflying.
