Smaller shrimp also have more surface area and will keep their crispness well (similar to butterflied large shrimp). They’re also cheaper and skip a fiddly, annoying step.

There are basically two recipes going on here: frying candied walnuts and frying shrimp with a sauce. Scroll around to see Katy M’s shortcut suggestions for the walnuts. 

I’m intrigued to try the double frying technique for the shrimp, and I’m so glad people have reminded me about using the burner outside on my grill to avoid the smell. I’m also always looking for another way to make nuts interesting as a bar-type snack. 

All that said, I actually truly dislike this particular dish, whether it’s from Hong Kong, it’s Chinese-American, or whatever, it’s just really not my thing. 

I’m happy to check out the recipe though, because I could see using the methods with different seasonings and sauces. There is just no way that I’ll ever like sweetened condensed milk mixed with mayo.

@LGL
Cooking mostly for one, I have always struggled with the amount of oil you use each time deep frying. What works for me is a Japanese style enameled pitcher with built in charcoal filter for storing fry oil. That paired with a stovetop Japanese style deep fry pot (oil volume matches pitcher) minimizes waste and keeps the oil well for longer than I anticipated. Sourcing the charcoal filter replacements reasonably is a bit of a challenge.

This has been one of my dad’s favorite dishes for decades. It’s wonderful to be able to make it for him now that it’s harder for him to get out. He reports that this recipe rivals any he’s had in a restaurant. It’s simple enough to make with our little Fry Daddy, and I served it with bro
Additionally, I loathe mayo. Loathe. It. So, I set aside some sauce before adding the mayo and used that for my portion of shrimp. Perfect!!!

Made this for the first time as my mother in law was craving Chinese food for Christmas Eve and our favorite Chinese restaurant had closed! Thoughts:
- used Kewpie mayo 
- subbed potato starch for corn starch. ChatGPT tells me this is more ideal and authentic to Asian cuisine. I was impressed with how the crisp held up to the glaze. Perfect crunch.
- my walnut glaze was pretty liquidy. When drying the walnuts I had a lot of sugary debris in the oil which was pretty annoying. I strained it as best I could into a mesh strainer into a narrow and tall pot, did a quick wipe down of the wok with tongs and wet paper towel, then carefully poured the hot oil to the wok leaving the debris that made it through the strainer into the bottom of the pot. Probably a sub 5 min detour quickly handled in the kitchen. The walnuts were fantastic… there are easier ways to candy but the outcome was ideal even though the process was a pain
- butterflying was a pro tip. The shrimp were gorgeous!
- family that didn’t “like” shrimp were joking with my husband about who would finish the last two shrimp
- my husband was skeptical about the idea of making it at home and ate his words ;)
Any suggestion about a substitute nut that would work well instead of the walnuts (allergy)?
You do not need all that oil. Just two tablespoons.  Cook it outside and you will be happy.
Has anyone made this with tofu? Curious if you'd need to adjust seasoning to account for its inherent blandness.
What a disappointment. So much effort for a bland meal. I followed the recipe as written. I thought the sauce would save the twice-fried shrimp but even the sauce wasn’t great.
followed this recipe exactly and it was absolutely delish, worth the clean up which is really not too bad if you have a dishwasher

Don't be put off by the amount of oil or the steps. I worked with 1/3 of the volume of oil in a medium sauce pan and it came out well.
Deep frying the walnuts as written did not work well for me. I ended up with burnt sugar bubbles in my oil and a very smokey kitchen. I will follow Katy M's recommendation for the walnuts next time.
We added the cornstarch directly to the shrimp and egg mixture to create the batter which worked very well.
The sauce was the best part. I added salt and lime juice to taste rather than to the recipe, and it was delicious. It helped tremendously that I was using a beautiful homemade version of Kewpie that was already very flavorful.
I'm looking forward to making it again.

title: Chicken Jorim (Soy-Braised Chicken) Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Seriously so easy and tasty. It feels hearty but not too heavy. I left the potato skins on because I’m lazy and fiber never hurts. Will be adding this to my rotation.
So delicious! I used sweet potato and added carrots. I only had 300 grams chicken thighs so I upped the mushrooms too. Lastly, used maple syrup instead of sugar. Versatile dish, will make again!

Another deeply flavored, delicious dish that the kids inhaled, and that was *VERY* easy to make. The dutch oven braise takes a little extra time, but it's a passive process, making it weeknight-friendly. Definitely plan to make again soon.

Another fantastic recipe from Kay—absolutely delicious! I added about 1 tablespoon of hot red pepper since we like it spicy, and also added carrots and daikon over the potatoes, which brought even more flavor to the dish. It was so easy to make and turned out amazing!
Maybe just a tiny bit sweet to my tastes and will try with half the sugar next time.

Made a few modifications:
- added potatoes earlier
- closer to a tablespoon of ginger
- more water to begin, longer coverless simmer on the back end

Overall very tasty, definitely needed some more heat. Next time I would cut the sugar by maybe half of a third and add some kind of heat—we ended up finishing with chili oil and chili crisp, which both helped greatly with balance. Would suggest adding maybe a 2 teaspoons or more of red pepper flakes while it’s cooking.
@Gabe - your suggestions are spot on!  I was going to write the same 😀
The mushrooms!!! It soaked up all of the amazing sauce. Next time, I will add more.

When I sauté mushrooms, I almost always start them all alone for a minute or two before adding anything else. If you keep stirring them, they don't stick, but they do start to brown nicely. I suspect that this approach would prevent them from soaking up all the sauce? (That might be a good thing, depending on how much sugar, salt, and flavor you want specifically in the mushrooms.)

Delicious! Did not have mirin on hand so subbed rice wine vinegar and also just used white mushrooms: worked fine. 

Potatoes needed longer to simmer but I probably did not cut them small enough, but due to the longer simmer it was even more tasty.
The coq au vin of Korea! Rich, with lots of flavor and texture. Super easy to make. I half-peeled the potatoes and used honey instead of brown sugar. 5 stars!

Next time I make this. I’m going to double the garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and mirin. The flavor was subtle and tasty, however I feel increased volumes of the braise ingredients will amplify the flavor perfectly.
I added a tbsp of fish sauce for extra flavor and feel like it really pulled it all together
delish and easy! I used sweet potato instead of the regular, half the sugar, and added cilantro + a bit of a cornstarch slurry at the end.
What can I use in place of mushrooms?
Would this work with tofu instead of chicken?
There's a recipe for Tofu Jorim as well on here. It's on constant rotation in my kitchen!

Why is the sugar added? As someone who doesn’t have any sugar or other sweeteners in my house I am curious why so many recipes call for sugar, honey or syrup to be used in meat dishes.  Besides turning the food into candy what does it add?
@Aaron Of Gladstone I agree. I avoid sugar as much as possible, and find that often, the dish is just as good.

@Aaron Of Gladstone sometimes sugar is used to balance the flavor especially when there is soy sauce in a dish. Leave the sugar out if it bothers you but you might be surprised at how much it adds to the dish.

Easy and delicious. Added more soy sauce and Serrano pepper. Jalapeño would also work nicely. Won’t recommend pepper flakes or gochugaru or gochugang or hot sauce. If I had added some carrots, then it would be just like from my Korean childhood.

Easy, great dish that I've made 2x this month. Very good to prep the veggies and sauce components in the AM and finish when you are ready.

I modified by adding more potatoes and mushrooms (like 8oz of mushrooms and 1.5 lb of potatoes) so I could have more leftovers.
doubled the ingredients for the sauce (and doubled the water as well) because sauce is delicious. I did not peel the potatoes.

I browned the chicken and onion a bit before the ginger and garlic so I could render some of the chicken fat.
Followed other’s advice and added carrots, which balanced beautifully. Second time we made it we added a heaping tablespoon of Dijon mustard at the end, off the heat, which was fantastic.

Delicious but no visual appeal.  Either make with more potatoes or skip them & have it over rice.  I thickened the gravy with cornstarch & added spinach at the end.  Baby bok choy would work, too.  Next time, I’ll add some red peppers with the onions.
Very simple and tasty- I used carrots instead of mushrooms and served over rice.

Would recommend bone in/skin on chicken thighs and removing the skin to render the fat to cook with (bonus crispy chicken skin to sprinkle on at the end.) Can also cook the bones and then add them into the water and sauces and let it stew to make the water more brothy
We enjoyed this. Had no kim chi so should have added a little red pepper for a kick. Next time.

Excellent recipe, easy and tasty! I made it just as written and think next time I will add a bit of kimchi to the braised chicken just before serving and have additional kimchi on the table. The addition of kimchi takes the dish to a whole new level.
The potatoes in the photograph are much larger than what’s called for in the recipe so they would take longer to cook.
Less sugar, possibly add spice during cooking or to garnish when plated. Some suggest adding carrots or even daikon.
not sure if it was just me but i needed like 3x the water this recipe calls for. also added extra soy sauce to match. otherwise turned out well!
Used daikon instead of potatoes and cut sugar in half.  Worked fine.
Double, triple, or quadruple the mushrooms. Otherwise this dish is seriously great, even my 2 year old inhales it.
Delicious and super easy. I also love having a quick Asian dish on rotation that isn't a stir-fry. With a bowl of hot rice, this is a saucy, comforting alternative and I'm here for it.
Made it without mushrooms or potatoes and it was kind of plain. Not enough sauce to full braise the chicken
The comments suggesting the addition of carrots are right on. I needed less water than provided by the recipe and added some hot peppers to spice it up. will definitely make it again.

I forgot to cut up the chicken beforehand but I don't think it actually mattered.  It was pretty tender by the end of cooking so we didn't even need knives to eat it!  I doubled the shiitakes and next time I might add some kind of vegetable at the end to make it more of a one-pot-meal.  I made spicy cucumbers as a side dish and that was a nice complement.
Coconut milk in place of water….

title: Squid Ink Pasta Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Why bother using bottle clam juice when you just use fresh clams. No fresh clam? How about shrimp, scallops or mussels?

Yes, just toss in debearded and scrubbed mussels in lieu of the bottled clam juice, cover and steam just until open and they'll release their juice. Fish them out so they don't overcook and reserve to add when you combine the pasta at the end.

You don't need the reserved cup of pasta water. The clam juice and tomato mixture was enough. 
I also doubled the lemon for some extra zing. Delicious dinner!

Made this as written and it was lovely! Did it once with the squid ink pasta and once with regular pasta and both were delicious. The second time around we used scallops instead of calamari but we treated the scallops like the shrimp (seared at the start) - they were perfect. Highly recommend.
Squid innards can add 30-50% to the weight of the squid.  I’m assuming the 1/2 pond specified here is for squid AFTER cleaning, yes?

I made this tonight and it is without a doubt the best dish I’ve ever made. I’m actually depressed no one else was home to witness (rather, taste). I have never cooked mussels before, and felt slightly sorry for them as I was scrubbing the shells, but I added them in at the step for clam juice. WOW they added that magic flavor! I will never need to go to a restaurant again!!! This dish overall is absolutely divine!!
I have my doubts about the shrimp browning at all in just a minute, in which case why not just start with bite-sized shrimp, only cooked at the end? Less work and less expensive.
Maybe I am missing something but, if you don’t use squid ink pasta and use plain pasta (given as an option), how would this then be “squid ink pasta”?

@Lucie it would
Be just regular pasta cooked - prepared like one would ink pasta.  ;)

Yes, just toss in debearded and scrubbed mussels in lieu of the bottled clam juice, cover and steam just until open and they'll release their juice. Fish them out so they don't overcook and reserve to add when you combine the pasta at the end.
Loved this! Subbed fresh clams for shrimp, and added fresh mint and julienned sundried tomatoes to enhance depth. Even better the next day.

Where I live, there isn’t easy access to clam juice, so I made an anchovy broth (dried anchovies, kombu, green onions, water) as a substitute and it added an amazing umami! Also zested the skin of the lemon I used for juice to add some freshness. Definitely recommend it!

Absolutely delicious!  Made this tonight for guests who loved it.  I substituted red onion for the shallot since that’s what I had on hand.  Definitely more than 4 servings! 8 easily if paired with a salad and a piece of crusty bread.

I didn’t have a shallot so I used a small onion. I don’t have clam juice but I did have a small can of whole clams in juice so I used that. I didnt have any squid, either, and it turned out great! My family loved it. It’s an easy recipe and relatively quick to make. It took me about 35-40 mins total.

This came out wonderfully made mostly as written. Couple changes: 
- I had fresh littleneck clams so I added those in lieu of the clam juice (2lbs). Took them out as they opened, removed the clams from their shells, and added the clam meat back in at the end with the pasta. They released enough liquid that I did not need to add any pasta water either. 
- I cooked the sauce in a skillet large enough to add everything else to at the end, I don't see the point in transferring the "tomato mixture" from one pan to another and bringing back to a boil when it was already happily simmering where it was. 
Other notes: 
- Throughout the entire recipe, there seemed to be more liquid in my pan than described. No browned bits to scrape up etc. Still delicious. 
- Also don't understand par-cooking the shrimp at the beginning; shrimp cook so quickly that I think I'll just add chopped raw shrimp at the end with the calamari next time.

title: Tanghulu (Candied Fresh Fruit Skewers) Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: My teen loved this sugar on sugar treat but it wasn’t my thing. Tasted like sugar, flat, prefer fresh fruit alone. Took longer to reach ideal cracking level temperature, despite 300 degrees. Had to wait longer. Fun one-time project.
This recipe is really fun and easy. It took a couple of tries (the first one burned) they are all so delicious! 😋😋
Are you sure you want to pour the sugar over the water?  Shouldn't it be the other way around?
@Roxanne W are you asking about “”spread sugar in an even layer and pour over ¾ cup of water” ? I interpreted that as “pour water over the sugar”.

This was ridiculously easy to do, very clear instructions, first time was a great success and it was all so quick! We did green table grapes (always try one at the store to figure out which grape is best), strawberries, and mandarin oranges. We did it with the neighborhood kids and had a blast right after the bus stop. I was over here thinking this would take a couple tries, and I would have mess ups. Nope! Perfection. Tastes exactly like what we got in NYC last week.

title: Braised Pork With Leeks and Mushrooms Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Added a thinly sliced fennel bulb along with the leek, also pumped up the garlic a bit. It was delicious! We had it with noodles and loved it.
@Mark Amen. I used potatoes and half and half, then threw in a half cup or so of white wine. It was delicious.
An amazing alternative to beef stew. So much lighter and more refined. Wonderful, simple, and delicious. Crusty bread to sop it up is highly recommended!
Delicious! My only regret was adding the cream at the end. It turned the beautiful brown stew an unappetizing white/beige color. Some kitchen bouquet helped correct it.
@Tom I think the cream important from a flavor/texture perspective. Close your eyes if you don’t like the color, lol.
@Tom add a teaspoon of Kitchen Bouquet after adding the cream. You’ll have a nice golden brown gravy. And the flavor will be delicious.

THis is a riff on a blanquette de veau and the light colour - and the refined taste - is what distinguishes it from a more fundamental stew.  You certainly don't have to add the cream but your antipathy to the traditional white colour of this meal and preference for colouring dark is puzzling
If you don’t want to make 2 lbs of meat I believe boneless “country ribs” are actually pork shoulder. Sometimes hard to find but Safeway seems to always have them.

Boneless 'country ribs' are thick cuts from a boneless loin of pork; ie. the the loin was separated from the ribs before cutting into thick boneless chops. Then the meat is broken down further by cutting the 'chop' into 2 pieces, each somewhat like a rib shape. There is more fat on country ribs than on tenderloin, but pork shoulder fat is better distributed and cooks down to a more 'fall-apart' texture. Tenderloin would be a distant third best option for braising. I speak from experience.

“Country Ribs” are from the end of the loin near the shoulder and cut in strips to look “rib-like”. They make an excellent substitute for shoulder when you don’t feel like buying a big shoulder roast.
What would be a good dairy free sub for the heavy cream for those with an allergy? I know coconut milk is pretty common but I don't think that flavor would do well here.
@Janis I think you could easily use a dairy free half-and-half here, dairy- free butter alternative, or even just an extra glug of olive oil.
@Janis  Perhaps some cashew cream? It’s easy to whip up and pretty tasty and thickening in most soups. It’s the kind of thing you might want to try to see how it works.
@Janis could sub cashew cream.

Did this in an instant pot to save time. Browned the pork in batches on Sauté setting. Removed the pork cubes, sautéed and seasoned the leeks, garlic, mushrooms and carrots to heat them up. Stirred in the broth, rosemary and a couple of thyme sprigs. Didn’t have a Parmesan rind on hand. Covered and set to manually Pressure Cook for 18 minutes. Let sit for 10 minutes and then used the handle of a wooden spoon to depressurize fast. I fished out the rosemary and thyme stems—the thyme leaves fell off in the cooking process. I turned on Sauté and stirred in the buerre manie, let it cook for a minute, then turned off the power and finished with half and half — to cut the calories. Delicious!

Ah there’s the comment I was looking for!

I’ll give this a try, thanks!
Could you use pork chops for this recipe?

Lots of people asking if you can use pork tenderloin or other leaner cuts of meat. In my experience the pork shoulder when slow cooked is a lovely tender pull apart meat. I would think a pork tenderloin would not "fall a part" in quite the same way but I'm willing to try it and find out if it's still delicious. Will report back.

@Carol
I made it with a pork tenderloin the first time cause that’s what I had in the fridge. Since 1.5 hours in the pot would kill the tenderloin I gave the pieces a hard sear on one side and put it aside. I cooked everything else as the recipe calls for then added the pork at the end and cooked it for another 15 minutes. I made it again  with shoulder and liked the tenderloin version better. Not as porky but lighter. The tenderloin was closer in texture to veal.
Can this be prepared the day before serving?
It would probably taste even better as most stews taste better the next day.
@Ronni  Others may weigh in…I would prepare a day ahead but add the cream once slowly reheated and top with fresh herbs at time of serving.
Could pork loin be substituted for pork shoulder? Or would it be too lean?
Would anyone know how to adapt this recipe to a crock pot?  It's summer and too hot to turn my stove on for two hours!

Five Stars!! Used cubed pork shoulder because its the tastiest cut these days and I added 1/4 t. Red pepper flakes. Otherwise followed the recipe but put the covered pot in a 300° oven for an hour, adding carrots for an additional 30 minutes. Good enough for guests!

I was worried about the high fat in pork shoulder, so trimmed a lot off. I made it exactly as written except white culinary chicken broth instead of plain chicken. It was tasty but if I make again, will reduce salt, add more fresh herbs to serve.

Very good. Used country ribs. Used 2 leeks and 8 oz of mushrms. Deglaze with white wine after garlic. 3 cups broth is plenty. Add Tbsp vinegar before beurre manie. Served over noodles, topped with parsley and chives. With salad and bread, worthy of special meal.
Is there a slow cooker version of this recipe? Would love to try it!

This is a good basic recipe.  I included a thinly sliced fennel bulb with the leek (as sugggested by Mark) and the sauce was delicious.  However the meat still tasted bland.  Blanquette de veau calls for more herbs, including thyme, onions, and white wine, or dry vermouth.  Would the dish be more flavorful  with those additions?  Looking for opinions.

All tips aside, it’s perfect as is. I’m embarrassed to say I was maybe 60 (a decade ago) when I learned the value of a Parmesan rind, now one is never discarded here! Really a super recipe. Sure, it takes time, but much of it is time you can do other things. I’ve done laundry, fed horses, wasted time on Netflix, etc etc.

When cooked as written, this is a super delicious way to introduce some variation in your stew menus.  When the weather is cold, nothing beats a stew - beef stew, veal stew, pork stew...having variation (with tomatoes or not, with red wine or not, chicken stock or not, with celery or not) makes each one seem like a delicious new discovery.  I keep them all in my rotation.  Not include the cream?  Have you lost your minds??  And be sure to chop some herbs for the finish.  It elevates.

Hmmm. Recipe geeking here.

Why buerre manié versus butter paste? Does it make it taste better? 

I understand a term like roux--it's much shorter than its description, which is a whole recipe.
@Blue cowboy - a roux by any other name is still a roux. I don’t get it either!

@Blue cowboy Roux and Beurre Manie are very different things. Both are fat and flour, but BM requires butter, where a roux can be made with another form of fat such as oil. The main difference is that BM is added raw to your dish, where a roux is cooked for several minutes, then the sauce or soup base slowly whisked into it. You would never dump a cooked roux into a finished stew as you do with the Beurre Manie in this recipe.
Has anyone adapted this recipe for an instapot? Thank you.

I haven’t found it in a comment but I’m going to try it. I’m thinking start it in a pan on the stove, then when you get to the part where you cover it, dump it in the instapot, cook for 20 minutes with pressure? 

I’ll ask my roommate, he’s the master of the InstaPot. But since all of these ingredients are readily accessible I’m definitely going to give it a try.

Made this in an instant pot for 20 minutes with quick release. Thanks to the first post for the suggestion. I used 3 cups of broth and it was too much. Next time, I’ll use two. Delicious recipe! Despite the 2 pounds of meat, after trimming, it really is just four portions, and we are not big eaters.

This was simply wonderful! I didn’t need to cook it for 90 minutes. By 60 minutes, my pork shoulder was sooo tender. The added cream makes the whole dish so rich and comforting to eat. I had a rich homemade chicken broth which I used.

I can not get real leeks where I live, but I could get a large Naga Negi/long Japanese onions similar to leeks but with a different taste. I added more button mushrooms as I like lots of them in a dish like this. It was perfect. And the Rosemary and Parmesan rind are a must! The resulting sauce is ambrosia! We ate it with a baguette but I will try fettuccine or a short frilly pasta that will pick up lots of sauce next time.

We loved this recipe: I added small potatoes to the stew and did not serve it on noodles. I only had 1.3 lbs of pork stew meat. Due to a dairy intolerance, I skipped the parmesan rind and used NutPods "creamer" instead of cream, though I did use real butter for the beurre manié (for flavor, since the dairy intolerance can handle a tiny bit of dairy). After bringing it to a boil, I let it braise in the oven at 325F for 1 1/2 hours. Wonderful!
Delicious recipe as written. The only change I made was to deglaze the pan with a little white wine right before adding the garlic. I just couldn’t let all that fond go to waste!
The pork fell apart! Simply phenomenal. A show stopper that will impress a crowd!
Followed Jonquil’s Instapot suggestions. Absolutely delicious, but next time I would reduce liquid to 3 cups. Used a large rosemary sprig and it was perfect.
@llw I did the Instant pot too, and thought 2 cups would be more than enough!

5/5! I took another commenter's idea of adding a fennel bulb but otherwise followed the recipe to a T. Trimmed the fat off the pork shoulder a bit but it still had plenty of flavor. Final result looked a bit like beef stroganoff. It was delicious! Will definitely make again.

I learned a valuable cooking lesson… never use purple carrots in a stew or sauce.  Despite the delicate braised pork and blend of spices and herbs and hearty broth, it was purple, like squid ink purple. I did use a homemade pork broth made from pork chop dinner bones and it was excellent!  Other than purple carrots no deviation from the recipe although adding other ingredients is pretty okay.
This was so much better the second day. Next time I’ll try the addition of fennel.
how would you do this in the pressure cooker? Instant pot
I have a frozen pork tenderloin.  Any chance it would work for this recipe, or must I go to the store and find a boneless pork shoulder?
can tenderloin be substituted for shoulder?
For those who don’t eat pork, beef chuck is a great substitute in this recipe.
Could pork tenderloin be substituted for the boneless pork shoulder?
I've made a pork stew that calls for pork shoulder with pork tenderloin. It's good enough and won't take as long to cook.
You can buy dairy free heavy cream at most grocery stores.
I hate mushrooms.  What can I sub? Or can this be made well just leaving them out?
Could you substitute a beef roast of some kind? I just have a lot of them in freezer and these flavors sound nice.
Has anyone tried this with Turkey thighs?

I would make a roux out of the butter and flour to ensure no raw flour taste.  

4 cups of stock was too much.  It ended up watery.  Start with 2 cups and see how it goes.

Delicious! Amped up veg by using three carrots and tossing in some sliced raw asparagus for the last 5 minutes of simmering. We also used a mix of sour cream and heavy cream to more closely ressemble creme fraiche.  We served it over gemelli pasta as that was what we had on hand.  A great, colorful braise for spring!
Sorry, I guess I’m a novice. How much of a “Parmesan rind”?

Excellent.  I followed the recipe except I used baby carrots that I added during the last 45 minutes of a 1 hour, 45 minute cook on low.  Carrots were perfect.  The only tweak I'd make is cooking the mushrooms longer so they carmelize more. Crusty bread is great for sopping the sauce.  Used fresh snipped chives from my windowsill.

Doubled the recipe but skipped the cream entirely and added uncooked pasta (I used gluten free shell) into the pot to soak up the voluminous chicken broth. Came out wonderfully and we're still eating on the dish days later. It just keeps getting better!

Country style ribs as sold in most supermarkets are typically cut from the shoulder not the loin. To aid in identifying the right cut to use here look at the price per lb. Shoulder cuts are less expensive,  delicious and loaded with fat that breaks down during the cooking process resulting in a tender, flavorful cut of meat.

I made this for the first time, and I really want to like this recipe, but it was lacking flavor and the sauce didn't thicken enough (or hardly at all, really) after I added the flour and butter mixture.  I might try thickening the leftovers and see what happens.

Yes, “country-style” ribs are sometimes cut from the loin but for this recipe you’ll want to purchase “ribs” that are cut from the shoulder or the butt. More fat, better taste! Great recipe as written a keeper for sure.
Made this tonight. Followed recipe but baked it in pie plate. Phillo comes in 2 packages so that was helpful.  Delicious and would make again.  Thank you.
It’s the best recipe on the website I’ve cooked thus far. Pork is ridiculously tender. Careful not to over salt it.
We used bone in lamb shoulder. After trimming the meat off the bones made a broth instead of chicken stock. Delicious!
The next time I make this, I’m going to cook it the night before I plan to eat it. On the night I made it, it was very good, but the leftovers on the next night were out-of-this-world delicious.

Made this last night. Wegman's sells 2.xlb pork shoulders that aren't labeled as roasts, but it worked perfect for this with a bit of additional trimming. I served myself a massive bowl on egg noodles and probably ate it faster than anything else I've cooked in the last few months. I'd say I have an easy 2 large meals still left.

The butter/flour didn't seem to really do much, I think I'm going to double it next time around and hope for more body.

Made this with fresh leeks from the garden - delicious, but much better the next day. Next time I serve this, I’ll make it the day before. Also, chunky carrots, not slices are an important ingredient.

I doubled the recipe (other than the stock, but added a glug of white wine) and did it in the slow cooker without browning the meat. After 6 hours on low, I strained the liquid, added some cornflour instead of the butter and flour, reduced it by a quarter, then added the cream. It was excellent. 5/5 from the family.

Horrible stew, very disappointing... I wanted a wonderful autumn dish and it sounded so delicious! I made it exactly as the recipe called for and it was still thin and sad, despite using prime ingredients. I do not recommend this in any way. I have never ever given a NYT recipe such a bad review.
Very good as (almost) written :) Based on comments, I doubled the leeks and added another large clove of garlic. We served over mashed potatoes. We will definitely make this again!
Why not use a butt instead of a shoulder?
I made it last night and it was delicious. I agree that the cream isn't needed. Next time, I'll add some port wine. My pork shoulder was tender after 1.5 hours.

This is delicious! The only thing I changed was gluten-free flour (no effect) and used beef broth and water b/c I grabbed the beef one by mistake. 3/4 people in my family loved it—4th one has Celiac and lots of sensory issues with foods like mushrooms. 
Do take the timing seriously. The full simmer time really makes a difference in the pork and the overall married pot of flavor. The leeks melt into the sauce and the pork is delectable so tender. As with all NYT Cooking time advisories, do your prep and then start the clock.

Oh my, this was one of the best recipes. My pork was so tender and succulent after 2 hours. The depth of flavor of the broth was amazing. I used less broth than the recipe calls for, since many commenters found the dish too thin. I think 3 cups is about right. I deglazed with some white wine. Other than that I followed the recipe exactly and am glad I did. I thought the beurre manié and touch of heavy cream provided the perfect finishing touches. Am sad for those who left off the cream!

Has anyone else found that the sauce is a bit thin? I thought I followed the recipe closely, although I was a little short on mushrooms. I also had to supplement the open chicken stock I had in the fridge with water and Better Than Boullion. I wanted to serve this over egg noodles but I’m afraid it doesn’t have the viscosity to cling. Should I add more flour and butter?

Made this exactly according to the recipe, and it was good, not great.  Although tasty, it was a bit on the bland side, and the sauce was not as thick as I would have liked.  Definitely needed some bread to sop up the sauce, which largely slid off the noodles. I may make this again, but will use more carrots (and might try turnips or rutabaga) add some white pepper, and use more flour to thicken the sauce.
I wonder if this could be made, at least in part, in an insta-pot ? Has anyone tried it? It sounds wonderful.
Can this recipe be made using chicken breasts?
@dianne I think chicken breasts do not take to long cooking times. It would end up very very dry, I fear.
I would love to try this and my electric pressure cooker. I could brown meat first and then does anyone have advice on what setting (high or low pressure) and for how many minutes?
Can pork tenderloin be used instead of boneless pork shoulder?

I need to make this dairy free for a dairy intolerance. Instead of cream, I'll use unsweetened/unflavored NutPods as I usually do, with some flour if it needs more thickening. But does anyone have any idea what the Parmesan rind adds to the recipe and how I might replicate it without dairy?
@BayCook, for this recipe, I think the Parmesan rind adds a good umami punch. So, you could maybe sub in some mushroom powder or browned tomato paste (~1 Tbls).
Is there a non-dairy substitute for the heavy cream?
I use cashew cream for a dairy sub. It works really nicely in most recipes. Soak raw cashews in boiling water for 30 minutes then blend them until smooth.

I’d like to make this, but unfortunately I’m a mushroom hater. When I make Coq au vin and other stews/braises that call for mushrooms, I generally just leave them out. But is there something else you’d suggest adding instead, just to keep some complexity?
Could this be place in an oven? At what temp? And for how long?

@Patrick definitely works out well in a 300° oven for one hour. Then add the carrots for an additional 30 min. I poured the liquid into a fat separator and made a gravy with 2 T. Butter, 2 heaping Tablespoons of flour plus 2 cups of the hot defatted liquid. Added about 1/4 C. Light cream and more salt and pepper to taste. Fabulous.
I expect lamb would make a good substitute?

made this.  It was great but very rich for a spring evening. i might try it again without the beure manie, and just put some cream on the table so people can add as they like. the flavors were terrific
Only had a 1lb pork tenderloin, brined it for 3 hours prior to starting this recipe.. had extra mushrooms.. and extra carrot.  Passed on the heavy cream, as I didn't have any.   Came out good.
We found this pretty bland and the sauce a bit thin. 3 stars at the most.
Excellent.  Did it in the multi/slow cooker.

I used four leeks, 24 oz shiitake and Bella mushrooms, 6 cups duck broth, and diced the carrots. Followed recipe with the above changes and served it at a party last night. It was fabulous and very easy!
This is super satisfying. I keep planning to add some chopped kale in my leftovers, but haven't yet. I suspect that will be delicious.
Wow, made as is in a cast iron Dutch oven except I used just a splash of 1/2 & 1/2 instead of cream. Used boneless country ribs. Served over noodles.  Very delicate flavor- recipe is def a keeper.

Delicious!  A few people asked about slow cooker - I used it because it's hot here this week. Seared pork as directed in large cast iron pan; transferred to slow cooker.  Sauteed leeks, mushrooms, and garlic in the same cast iron pan, except I deglazed the pan with white wine to make sure I got all the little bits.  Transferred to slow cooker, added broth, carrots, herbs, parmesan rind, and cooked on high 4 hours.  Completed Step 5, and served over noodles. Came out very well.
Preparing 2lbs of meat for 4 servings makes the splash of cream a nutritionaly irrelevant addition. It's a good recipe, and can be done in advance - giving you time to invite a few friends over.

Absolutely delicious, I added a few more carrots then asked for but other than that... recipe is pretty much the same.  Egg Noodle tip, salt the water so it's like sea water then cook... what a game changer that is.  Everyone had seconds... so little effort really, but planning is needed I prepped everything at noon, and then took 30 minutes to assemble it 2 hours before we planned to eat.  So read the timings on the recipe so you dent' rush a step.  Braising requires time.

This was time consuming and resulted in a dish that was bland in flavor (despite adding extra rosemary and some white wine), with pork that was rubbery and unappealing despite giving it extra time to cook.  I will never make this again.  Yuck.
I had exactly the same result and reaction. The meat drowned in all the broth, and the flavor was icky.
can you freeze once you've stirred the cream in?

Wow this was fantastic. I started with the best of intentions to follow the recipe, but alas, some detours had to be made due to disappearing (and found) ingredients. 

I used very old dehydrated mushrooms as the fresh ones got used in another dish by mistake and I was too lazy to go get more. I used a combo of rehydrated mushroom water and water from boiling potatoes for another recipe with some Better Than Bouillon chicken base for the broth. I had some leftover chickpeas that needed a home, so they went in. A forgotten bunch of fresh collard greens were found, so they went in too! I didn’t have any Parmesan rinds, but found some other rinds, so used that instead. I recently banned heavy cream because the unused portion would get whipped up and enjoyed by me, and I need to lose weight, so I skipped it entirely. I ended up buying 1.7kg of meat, so I increased the leeks and rosemary. I also trimmed quite a bit of the fat from the well marbled pork shoulder. 

Despite all that, it still tasted very rich and flavourful, and was ridiculously delicious. We had it with buttered noodles the first time. The second time, I made freekeh with some sautéed onions, garlic and Better than Bouillon chicken base. I liked it better with the freekeh. 

Really a fabulous meal, and will be on the rotation going forward.
Making this for company in a few days. Can any of this be prepared a day ahead.
Sure. Start it the day before you plan to serve. I would say you can complete everything through Step 3. Put it in the refrigerator overnight, then reheat and continue with Step 4 to completion.

We had farmers market lamb stew meat from Truest Farms, so we used what we had on hand- and it was fabulous! We also had to sub yoghurt for cream at the end. Overall, an amazing meal we will do again! We did the veg chopping in the morning, did a long bike ride, had cocktails during the long simmer. Thanks, Times!

Delicious, tender but thin despite the beurre manie.  As often, as I was adding the broth, I was thinking "this seems like too much liquid", so next time, I will decrease by about 1/2 cup. I substituted the heavy cream (my husband can't tolerate it) with non-dairy heavy cream substitute but that seemed neutral, so I think next time, I will just omit it. I will definitely make it again.  It seems like a great meal for a crowd!

Eating with children who needed to deal with fatty bits and “boing” due to a well-marbled piece of pork shoulder.  Next time I’ll just cut into 2-3 large pieces that are cooked longer (3 hours?) and can be de-fatted and de-boinged and shredded before serving.  Would add carrots for last 1.5 hours of cooking and maybe throw in some frozen peas.  We ate it with boiled potatoes due to another egg noodle dish later in the week.  Skipped the cream, but would add it if serving over egg noodles, which I think we would do next time.
Silk brand makes a non-dairy heavy whipping cream, I used it and it was a great dupe!
Ugh this was so fabulous. Kay Chun has NEVER let me down but this was especially scrumptious. I may sub it in for my traditional Thanksgiving Eve beef stew (sorry Kenji). It’s just that good!!

Very good, though not quite worthy of five stars. Made exactly per the recipe but used a pork tenderloin. Served with noodles, sauce was delicious but next time I might double the butter/flour to make it a bit thicker sauce.
I made this tonight with 2 variations- half and half instead of cream, and I added a can of rinsed cannellini beans. It was delish! I think it would also be great with veal stew meat

Definitely a keeper! As @Mark suggested I added a fennel bulb with the leek. I also doubled the garlic, swapped 1/2 c brandy for part of the broth, and added peas at the end. Served with rosemary focaccia. Great.
Forgettable, unfortunately. Followed the recipe exactly.

I cooked this recipe as instructed and I loved the soupy sauce but the pork shoulder pieces came out pretty dry. Is there some modification to the recipe that I could try that would result in more moist meat?
had the same result after the 1.5 hour simmer - so I kept simmering. Took about 4 hours to get the meat moist and tender, but the result was great!

Cooked following the recipe and served over egg noodles.  It came out great!  Just for a twist, next time I make it I may top it with drop dumplings -like the ones I use for Chicken & Dumplings.  It would make it heartier for the cold winter months!
We really, really enjoyed this meal but I found it to be a bit thin.  I used some corn starch to thicken it up.

Delicious, but the cook time is way off. After simmering for 1.5 hours, the meat was dry and tough... To get the pork tender and juicy, a very long simmer is needed - mine took about 4 hours, but the results were worth the wait
Can you make this in the crock pot?
This recipe was incredible but didn't necessarily need the cream. I tasted it prior to adding the cream and it was already rich and wonderful. I can't wait to make this again, thank you!
This was an amazing dish as written!  I cooked it very slowly for at least four hours—the pork was incredibly tender.  I did add parsley for serving.

Followed the recipe except I subbed lean beef chuck roast for the pork since I had several roasts in my freezer from my CSA. It was absolutely delicious and pretty easy - just leave yourself plenty of time to braise the meat until tender and let the flavors meld.

I didn’t have a leek so I used two small yellow onions. It turned out great. I also did not have fresh rosemary so I used dried. I have celiac and used gluten free flour and gluten free noodles. Honestly do not use gluten free noodles to serve with just get some bread, it’s too easy to taste the noodles
This was maybe 3-4 stars the first time around. But leftovers were a definite 5 stars! Something about braised meat dishes… second day is always better!
Really yummy. I used non-dairy cream

Made as written, it was delicious. I might edit to say “chicken broth to cover,” as sauce was quite soupy and I ended up doubling the beurre manie, but that was not a biggie. Definitely recommend adding the cream for both flavor and texture.
Delicious flavor and super tender pork. Was a bit brothy so next time I will decrease by a cup and see what happens. The fennel suggestions sound delicious as well.

We made sure to give the pork a thorough browning for crisp edges, which took more time, but the results were phenomenal. 

We also added fresh parsley to brighten up the flavors, and plenty of Parmesan. It was absolutely delicious. And even better the second day!

The meat alone took me at least 20 minutes to prep (honestly, why bother putting a prep time in when it never reflects reality?). Cooking time was accurate though, with it taking almost the full 1.5 hours for the meat to be fall-apart tender.
Change nothing except double the recipe.

Like others, I found the sauce to be a bit thin, even though I made a half recipe but used the full portion of beurre manié - easily remedied by allowing the stew to cook for a while at the end with the cover off. I was impressed with how tender the pork became, even though i had used loin steaks cubed, rather than pork shoulder. I'm still unclear about what constitutes "a parmesan rind" - how big???

This was excellent with chopped spare ribs and served on wide egg noodles. I didn't have a Parmesan rind on hand, so I added a 1/2 tsp of Better Than Bouillon roast beef base, for a similar umami punch.

title: One-Pot Sesame Salmon and Quinoa  Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I will definitely try this dish, as it sounds very appetizing.  I know I am probably in the minority by requesting this, but I wish that some of the ingredients that may be unfamiliar to readers be marked with an asterisk and/or an explanation provided as to what they are.  For example, ponzu.    I know I can Goggle these, but perhaps these recipes take a lot for granted with readers' familiarity with some ingredients.

@Beverly I always take their use of unfamiliar ingredients as an invitation. Something new! They’re trusting their audience. I don’t choose to feel alienated by something I’m not immediately unfamiliar with.
@Stacey I wish they would offer a tip for what can substitute for ponzu. I doubt it’s available locally and I have having an item I use only for one recipe.

Confession: I did not follow the recipe because I do not like overcooked broccoli and my salmon quality was okay but not amazing. 
Approach: I cooked the elements separately, then combined. The dressing is very good and key to the dish. It was interesting to combine these flavors with quinoa...it felt vaguely Peruvian. There is an idea here that I will return to.
@Lynne this is the way! I followed the recipe hoping to learn something but wish I trusted my gut which is what you described here

Directions are unclear as to what to do with the broccoli after it’s cooked in Step 1. I assume it’s removed from the pot and added back to the bowl to serve, but it’s not mentioned at all in the recipe after step 1.
@KC It is cooked in with the quinoa.
won't the broccoli get overcooked, if it's cooked with the quinoa?  It seems to me that one ought to remove the cooked onions/broccoli and add it back at the end.
@Monica D just made it, the broccoli was definitely over cooked and there was plenty of water left over in the pot as well.

I didn't trust that both the broccoli and salmon wouldn't get overcooked, so I roasted the salmon in the oven and steamed the broccoli separately and slapped everything together at the end. it was fantastic. The tahini ponzu sauce is *chef's finger kiss*. I almost didn't buy the almonds, but I'm glad I did - they added a satisfying crunch. This one is going into the regular rotation.

I made this tonight. We enjoyed it. Used chives as well. The almonds give it a nice flavor and crunch to what otherwise would have been rather single textured. Good balance of flavors with the tahini sauce and lemon.
I've never used or bought Ponzu sauce.  Is there a substitute? I hate to buy something I'll rarely use.
@Julie Ponzu substitute half and half soy sauce and lime juice, change proportions to taste.
Going to make it tonight, but definitely will not be cooking the broccoli 13-15 minutes!

Made this tonight for a group and it was a huge hit! Used chives and dill for the herbs, may try cilantro next time. Didn't have any fresh lemons, but that might have added some more punch. Will definitely use this again.
This was a hit even with just fresh chives for herbs.  I used squeezable garlic instead of fresh, didn't notice any difference.

I'm cooking this now. The brocolli is super overdone and I've not added the salmon yet!  I am waiting for the quinoa to cook but don't think there was enough water in the recipe.  And steaming it isn't making it cook as far as I can tell.  I'll stick in the salmon and cook but I don't think this will be very nice.
Cooked per directions. Broccoli was way overcooked. Overall it was fine but won’t be making this recipe again.

Made this last night and really liked it. The sauce is especially good. (I found a recipe for Ponzu online with ingredients I had on hand.). However next time I make it I will deconstruct it. Skinning raw salmon is no fun, so I will cook it separately. And, I will just roast the broccoli.
@Deborah you can slowly pour near-boiling water over salmon skin and it will shrink and pull off easily.  There are YouTube videos out there for reference.
Broccoli is wildly over cooked. Separate from quinoa. Further quinoa doesn't like fats when cooking, water or stock only. Again sauce is tasty, but gummy. I'll do it again, but my way.
I think the broccoli has to be cook separately or the water is too much. I still have soup in my pot and the broccoli is mushy. Not sure what I did wrong but it turned out very mushy for me.
Is it possible to sub the quinoa for rice?

Yes, but the quinoa cooks in half the time as white rice. I'm leaning toward cooking either the rice or the quinoa separately, then add oil to the pan, add the starch and some water to help steam the broccoli and onions, and let the fish steam under the lid.

The author notes that quinoa is gluten-free, but she calls for Ponzu sauce, which contains soy sauce, which contains gluten. It would be wonderful if more recipe writers offered truly gluten-free substitutions/options in their recipes.
Big Tree Farms sells a soy free/gluten free version made with coconut aminos and it's delicious!  I have found it at some Whole Foods when travelling. (We don't have a WF in my area).
Tasty and easy. BUT ( I can’t believe I’m saying this ) I’d cut both the almonds and Tahini down by 1/3. It made a healthy dish somewhat heavy

Everyone who said the broccoli is overcooked is right. Cook it separately. 

For those who adjusted the dressing to be less tahini-heavy, I would like to know what you did!
I may have missed it, but I don’t see any indication of sesame used in this recipe
Is the ponzu the pure citrus variety or the citrus/soy combo? Can anyone clarify?

This recipe is one of those perfect examples of "use your best judgement when following a recipe". Cooking broccoli for 5 minutes on their own and THEN boiling them for another 15 minutes is obviously going to nuke them. Take em out! 

I also don't really like the way boiled/steamed salmon turns out, so I tossed it in the oven instead. Mix it all together at the end and it's an awesome recipe (provided you're able to tweak it a bit)

I made it straight from the recipe, which was VG! As others have said, the sauce is spectacular! I made my own ponzu the day before from lemon juice and soy sauce and fish flakes and kelp and rice vinegar and mirin- easy. I could see taking the broccoli and onions off and putting them back in at the end, but I did not find it to be over cooked. I could also see adding quarter rounds of water chestnuts or some chopped bamboo shoots to give more crunch. I could also see using sesame oil instead of olive oil for a more Asian flavor.

Yes, the broccoli becomes hopelessly overcooked. Better to saute it until crisp-tender while the salmon cooks and mix in with the herbs and almonds. Snow peas might be a better bet than broccoli. And better yet, just skip either and along with the sliced avocado, add in chopped Persian cucumbers.

Based on all these comments, I cooked the quinoa separately and wished a bit that I’d cooked the broccoli separately—maybe even roasted it instead—it was pretty good but not amazing. Steaming the salmon with the broccoli already finished was already too much. Should’ve done onions first, then broccoli and salmon together, quinoa separately and then add, plus the herbs and nuts. 

The almonds are necessary. I cooked it without and it does lack a certain crunch that would be helpful. Might try with some crusty bread or something else to give some crunch.

I used more broccoli and I cooked it separately after reading all the comments. I used pistachio nuts instead of almonds.
I used half salmon and half tofu. 
It’s totally delicious!
Well/ this was underwhelming. i kept on thinking i was missing a major spice component. And trust me, I am NOT one that loves super spicy! But this so bland. Maybe fresh ginger?
A Lebanese coworker told me that tahini and lemon is commonly used as a fish sauce. It's also used on felafel balls.

I just cooked this last night.  Very good, but I would like the tahini/ponzu sauce to be a tiny bit sweeter, so I think I would add a little honey to the dressing next time.  I didn't have enough broccoli so I used a bunch of curly kale and that was great.
My family hates broccoli. Any ideas for subs?
@ELA the ratio for quinoa is 1.75 liquid to 1 quinoa. So this would likely leave the quinoa a little underdone.
This was not a hit at our house. I won’t make it again. The salmon was overcooked. The veggies mushy. The sauce lacked flavor.
Followed the recipe exactly except I steamed the broccoli separately and added it to the dish together with the herbs.  Delicious!

May be the only NYT recipe I've made that I found inedible; turned into a gelatinous mess. Trying to retrieve expensive morsels of salmon out of dish.  A true disappointment. It must have just been me, but I don't know what I should have done differently
Great ingredients, but benefit of using only one pot is

Dig all the ingredients (thanks for the soy lime fix for having no Ponzu) but I question the ultimate value of having used only one pot. Next time I’ll cook broccoli separately so it doesn’t overcook.

I did not have a problem with overcooking the broccoli, as others noted. Possibly, it's because I kept the heat to medium and low as per recipe instructions. That said, I might consider putting the pieces of broccoli stem in earlier than the bits of floret. 
This is tasty, but I am not sure I like the ratio of tahini & ponzu yet. May adjust that in future. 
Will see how this does reheated. I did not add herbs into the pot, but will add those, avocado and dressing after reheating.
Where is the sesame?
@Kristina tahini is sesame paste for the most part
Not very exciting flavor wise.
Made it per the recipe the first time and then for the second time I used canned salmon and added a can of chopped green chilis with the broccoli and onions.  Both times were excellent!

Kind of a dud. Hopes were high for a flavorful one pot dish with the high nutrition of quinoa, salmon, and broccoli. Did not cook or taste as expected. Quinoa took much longer than it should have. It didn't end up tender, and the salmon was overcooked as a result. The broccoli was lost in the dish. The ponzu dressing was not a real flavor punch. The whole dish was kind of bland and overly filling. Crunch of almonds might have helped a little, but I had left them out because I don't like them.

I’ve made this twice. A great recipe! The second time I added fresh corn (one ear) and one red slivered bell pepper with the other veg, and a mix of cilantro and Italian parsley for the herbs. Used Eric Kim’s recipe for ponzu sauce.
When do you add the garlic? It’s in the ingredients, but not seeing it in the instructions.

This look promising, I'm glad I went through the comments first and based on the feedback decided to cook everything separately. That was a good call! Nothing espectacular, I won't be making this again

This has become a staple in my family for an easy weeknight dinner. I do a super simple version by buying a dill sauce and skipping the tahini, almonds etc. I love that it takes less than 15 mins and is healthy and tasty!
"Sesame"?
Tahini is like peanut butter for sesame seeds.
This recipe should be renamed. “Sesame salmon” is misleading. You don’t think of tahini as sesame. You think of it as tahini. Sesame salmon evokes seeds.

Absolutely loved this. Very few modifications. Didn’t use the almonds though I could see the allure. Could have used more broccoli as it kind of disappears. I did add some zucchini which was a nice touch. 

Adding the broccoli at the very end of step one helped it not be overcooked. Didn’t have ponzu so substituted soy sauce, rice vinegar and more lemon. Don’t be shy with the tahini, lemon or sauce in general. It really makes it pop. May try some samba olek next time. And green onions. 

Can’t wait to make this again!
Tasty, but there ended up being more water than expected in the quinoa. Also added a couple Serranos, some cumin, and some paprika.

This reminds me of a warm bowl you might get at a fast casual salad place. Next time I will throw the broccoli in to steam with the salmon in the hopes for more texture. I love the sauce, and also could see adding some crushed red pepper or additionally garnishing with a spicy sauce.

I took advice from others and steamed the broccoli separately. I also did a mix of quinoa and jasmine rice. I really liked the tahini sauce but my partner wasn't a fan. He tasted it before adding it to his bowl and opted for drizzling the ponzu on his serving. We had never had ponzu, but now we are fans! This is definitely a make again for us.
@john j  tahini is sesame paste

I skipped the broccoli, for reason many people said. Instead, I had two fat leeks, about the same weight as the broccoli called for, sliced them thin, omitted the onion, and it all worked out. Very delicious. I think next time I won’t cut up the salmon, just a slab on top of the quinoa. Since I’m only one person, I think this will  better control how I store and use the leftovers.
This was most disappointing.  I read comments and made adjustments; cooked broccoli separately and added lemon, lots of lemon! The sauce did not impress either.  Too bad.

Just like most comments - I suggest just cooking each component separately so you are certain to get the textures you want. So many things went wrong for me and its ALOT of food to have ruined if you don’t get it perfect on the first try.

This is a great recipe. I am lucky to have more than one Asian supermarket nearby so made a quick trip for ponzu, an ingredient I had not previously purchased. 

I had some cooked broccoli on hand so just added after the salmon was cooked. 

Made this with Trader Joe's Harvest Grains, which I use all the time. I like the texture and it was perfect in this recipe. 

Sauce is delicious! Will definitely put this recipe in rotation.

This recipe feels disjointed. The flavors are regionally all over the map and none of them really sing together. I feel like the same concept (one pot salmon over quinoa with veg) could have been executed way more coherently. (I smelled the sauce and the freshly chopped dill/chives I had ready and almost was nauseous. Using cilantro instead, but even so, this feels like a sketch of an idea, not a finalized beloved recipe.

title: Beef Fried Rice Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I'd recommend cooking the egg after browning the beef and setting aside or adding it after the onion and carrot. Cooking the egg after adding rice will turn it gooey, by adding moisture and cooling your pan too much. At least that's my experience working a busy wok station.

@Bored headache
Both are accepted techniques of Asian cooking.

@Bored headache
It depends- if you like discrete pieces of egg, then yes, cook it separately, then chop up before adding it to the rest of the dish. If you don’t care for discrete pieces ( like my husband) then cook it as described in the recipe.

Great dish. Much enjoyed. A few modifications;
1) yes do the eggs after meat (not once rice is in wok)
2) rice; instead of adding to wok to breakup, instead place rice in bowl and spray with oil as you break the rice up by hand. This makes the rice less clumpy 
3) make often and enjoy
@BGE the recipe says to leave at least 2 tablespoons of oil in the pan after cooking the beef – if I’m cooking the eggs after the beef should I cook them and all that oil?

Y'all. I made this with hamburger meat. That's right; I velveted the hamburger meat and while it is not a textural profile that some may enjoy (I'm honestly on the fence still) - this was an explosive favorite with the kiddos and hubby. And I'm about to make it again. Just putting it out there as another option for that package of meat you might have.
FYI - I come to the comment section exclusively for substitution recommendations.

Another option to help tenderize the beef is to add 1/4 tsp of food grade sodium carbonate to the velveting marinade. Sodium bicarbonate can also be used but it tends to leave a metallic taste vs sodium carbonate which is tasteless.

@Scott that’s why use use common bicarbonate of soda (or whatever name your country dictates) and wash it off well afterwards. I don’t see why you should spend more or waste unnecessary carbon (which we should all worry about!) buying online when both products literally do the same thing (It’s not a matter of quality)

For the beef, many cuts will work-- my inclination would be to go with boneless beef ribs.  However, about the marination: you'd do better to not add any salt, to add a slosh of shaoxing wine (not "cooking wine-- which has salt added) or sherry, and increase the time to a minimum of 30 minutes, an hour if it's convenient.

@Mike in Maine yes!! Shaoxing wine!! I looked to see if this in an ingredient and sadly it is not. One of the most important ingredients in Chinese cooking - just a tablespoon or so brings out the most authentic, restaurant quality flavor. Buy a bottle and level up your Asian cooking.

Prep took longer than expected. I usually velvetize beef with aluminum-free baking powder so this beef was a bit tough for me. Too be honest I am no spring chicken and neither are my teeth. I used onion, red pepper, peas and lots of celery since I love it but otherwise followed the recipe. My husband loved it. He has better teeth!
@Joan H. I’m with you on the celery, I need some in every stir fry as well as cabbage!
Helpful tip - par freeze the beef for 30-60 minutes so it’s just firm for easier thin slicing
Delicious!
I’m baffled at the idea that carrots, even finely chopped, could soften in three minutes. And also what is “finely chopped”? Is that different than diced?
@rlalumiere.     Diced means cutting in cubes chopping is using a knife to cut finely
@rlalumiere Save time and buy yourself a bag of frozen peas and carrots.
12 oz of steak is 3/4 lbs. If I ask my butcher for 12 oz of steak they'll probably look at me funny.

Delicious! I made two changes. I cooked the eggs as a flat omelet by themselves, then rolled the omelet up and sliced it into strips. I added the strips to the rice with the meat. Also, I used only about two and 1/2 cups of rice - that's all I can make at one time in my rice cooker!

@Janice that’s how my Chinese cooking instructor taught me to make the egg. Fry the scrambled eggs in the wok first, roll up, julienne, then toss into the rice at the end. Also add a tiny splash of soy sauce to the scrambled eggs.

You can cook rice then cool it fast in freezer for fried rice. 4 day is excessive.
Marinate meat tossed in baking soda then rinsed off will give you tender Chinese beef
The recipe calls for 4 cups of day-old rice, not 4 day-old rice. ; )
@Barbara in Alaska she said 4 cups of day-old rice, not 4 day old rice lol
Simple. Nice. Needs some spice.
Ginger, garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil-- Not sure what spices it needs.

@quaas if by spice you mean heat, I added Old Grandma chili oil with black beans. Any excuse to eat that stuff…… also…. Had no ginger so used some sugared ginger bits and no scallions so grabbed a handful of chives from where the dogs can’t pee on it (still washed it…) and last years frozen peas and final product was great!

TJs has a frozen vegetable fried rice that can shave a lot of time in preparation. I usually "kick it up a notch" with some extra ingredients, peas, corn, garlic, eggs, sesame oil, etc and use it as a side or add a protein of choice to the mix.
I've tried the other TJ fried rices but the vegetable one is the only one I'd recommend.
Wouldn’t this be better cooked at high heat in a wok, to get that wok hei char? It was fine in the nonstick skillet but…
@Kitfo woks work great if you have something akin to a commercial-style high BTU burner, but most people don’t have that capability on their homes ranges, especially if you have electric.

This was yummy! Steak was so tender and delicious! Only changes were to double garlic and ginger and I added red pepper flakes to the rice mixture as it cooked. I’m betting this is even better tomorrow!

As someone trying to manage high blood pressure, I appreciate the use of low-sodium soy sauce here, and for anyone else in the same situation, I highly recommend using coconut aminos instead. Better flavor and lower sodium.
Will this dish work if the rice isn't a day old? Want to make it tonight!
@Nancy - I made the rice then cooled it in the fridge then popped it in the freezer to dry it up a bit like day old rice! Turned out great!

@Nancy technically yes but you’ll get a different texture. Some folks prefer the texture with ready cooked rice, most don’t. It’s true ready cooked rice doesn’t soak up flavourings or fry well for the same reason - ready cooked steaming rice is full of moisture, but as I say some folks/family members enjoy it this way.
@Chelsea Much easier than having to think about making this a day ahead. After steaming I spread it on a small pan and left in uncovered in the fridge for a few hours.

I followed previous advice about the eggs- no problem there. The meat was tender but lacked flavor. I feel like I should reverse the process and cook the veg first so the meat can absorb those flavors more from the pan. Thoughts?
@Laurie I agree.  My meat was also flavorless, even though I marinated it for the full 30 minutes.  I also cooked the eggs after the beef.

Best part was the beef. I like crispy, tasty beef with rice and vegetables. This was a nice change for dinner. Husband did the chopping which made my part go quickly. We included some other vegetables. Thank you for something other than chicken.
Pretty good. Could be more flavorful. Added extra veggies from the fridge
Cooked almost as written; subbed snow peas for peas (added in last minute of carrots and onions). Absolutely delicious and perfect. Thank you!
This recipe is not good and surely not deserving of 5 stars. Very very very bland and basic. Would not recommend nor make again.
I’d recommend using leftover steak or ro
This was a big hit with my family of four, despite not having ginger (I subbed kimchi paste and felt the absence). There was plenty leftover for lunches tomorrow. 100% will make this again!
It’s always been my understanding that velveting meant blanching in oil or water, not just marinating it in cornstarch and liquid. Why do you call that velveting? Why does it tenderize?

This recipe required way, way, way more oil than is stated. Seemed like every step required more oil. I cooked the eggs separately as others suggested. I also allowed the chopped carrots to steam with both water and oil with a lid on the non-stick pan, stirring once in a while and testing the carrots for crunch before I moved onto the next step.

We added Snap Peas, Green Onions, Broccoli and Asparagus.   Yum!
I also did the garlic, ginger and carrots in the small food processor.   So much earlier than chopping
It was really good as written. Thanks!
I followed the instructions exactly and it was easy and delicious.  None of the flavors were overpowering. Thanks again NYT Cooking!
Prep took longer than expected. Although good, the dish needs MUCH more flavor. I had to quadruple the amount of soy sauce, and still was missing something to make it very tasty.

I wish there were more guidance in this recipe on the amount of salt and pepper to add.  There are four places where it says to "season with salt and pepper."  As a novice cook (and this is meant to be an"Easy" recipe), I am unsure how much to use at each stage in the process.

Yum, this recipe is so tasty.  I typically make fried rice with chicken, but the beef is so good.  The velveting of the meat is so helpful to guarantee tenderness.  I wish I had been a little more patient and cooked in smaller batches so I could get a crispier exterior, but it was still delicious!

I followed the general structure of this recipe with a few mods based on what I had on hand. I used a frozen vegetable mix which I zapped briefly in the microwave to defrost. Such a flexible recipe that came together easily. I’ve never tenderized meat like this before—totally worth it. I’ve made fried rice hundreds of times and this just pushed me to try a different path to making it. I enjoyed it with a side of kimchi. Delicious!

Great tasting dish and easy to make. A couple of things I would do differently: Slice the meat much thinner and try other meats. Both chicken and pork would work just fine. I would also use more eggs to make that part more visible. I did my dish in a non-stick wok and that worked well for me.

Delicious.  Some tips:
Prepped ahead so everything was at my fingertips before I started cooking 
Marinated the meat for 45 minutes 
Sautéed all the meat at once in 12” non stick pan Made an omelette in a separate pan and sliced the eggs into strips, added them with peas
Sauteed chopped green pepper with onions and carrots for 3-4 minutes
Added extra soy sauce and crushed red pepper 
I love Asian food, my husband not so much. He gave this a thumb’s up!!
This is only okay. Needs some help to punch up the flavors. We wound up adding soy and a light Japanese BBQ sauce to help. It worked but not sure we’ll make again.

Quite tasty, but a lot of work and mess.  And the picture shows way more beef vs. rice than my results.  Could use more soy sauce - and why specify low sodium soy when you're going to add salt to the dish?
The low sodium is used  exactly because  there  is salt added at every stage.  It's a common sense thing: either add low sodium soy sauce & salt or add regular soy sauce and omit the added salt .
Great with kangaroo too!
Delicious!  The meat was so tender and juicy!
Fish sauce… but I put Red Boat in most everything
4 cups of uncooked rice is an insane amount, not sure if i misinterpreted, but definitely dont use that much unless you want to eat this for a week. It was very good though and very easy
@JW recipe calls for COOKED rice, and 4 c was hardly enough as recipe turned out great!

This is a great recipe that the whole family loved. I had cooked a flank steak the night before and used leftovers for this. I cut it up into small pieces and tossed it into the cornstarch/soy mix. It came out delish!

@rlalumiere:  whenever a recipe calls for carrot to be chopped or diced very small, I put it through a coarse grater instead.  You still get the orange flecks, but are guaranteed it will always be soft.
I don't eat meat so I am going to substitute meat with Beyond Meat Steak.  And maybe try aquafaba for eggs or use tofu.

The prep took longer for me than 20 minutes, but this turned out so delicious! I added fried wonton strips to the top, and that gave a nice crunch. I will try the two suggestions below next time I make this, which will be soon!

YUM!!!! Only had leftover brown rice and it worked well. Wanted a veggie side so steamed broccoli florets then gave them a quick sauté with sesame oil and topped them with some rice vinegar and put the florets on top of the finished plate -fabulous. The thick broccoli stalks were chopped up and added with the carrot and onion. So very tasty. In this recipe I found that had I not had my mise en place ready to go, it could have made for  quite the clean up. Oh, and topped the finished bowls with chili crisp. This recipe is just too good!
Can this recipe be frozen and rewarmed?
The eggs are super delicious if you scramble them in a little sesame oil and some scallion whites!

Great, easy to throw together recipe using anything from the fridge/freezer. 
Yes, definitely scramble the eggs BEFORE the rice is added.

I was told  by a reliable source cornstarch will make the beef crunchy not act as a tenderizer .. with the cost of beef I’ll try a tried and true soya marinade not experiment with baking soda  vs cornstarch .
Proportions are way off. 2 cups of rice was more than enough.

I used skirt steak which I usually like but this recipe only has it marinate in the soy sauce/cornstarch mixture for 30 minutes. I found it really needs much more time to really tenderize it. Next time I'll marinate it longer or try the sirloin. We really did like the recipe even if the skirt steak was a bit chewy for our taste.
Why are the commenters talking about a wok when the recipe calls,for a frying pan?

Aaahhhh, so that's what we use to do with beef before the Great Meat Famine of 2026!  Its like deja vu to read a recipe with beef in it, kinda like going to Costco to admire the beef case and remember the good ole days.

Jus sayin
I was with you until it came time to add the egg. The egg is unnecessary, imo.

title: Stir-Fried Cabbage and Pork in Fish Sauce Butter Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Perfect combination of tasty, quick, cheap, and relatively healthy. When I make it again (which I’m sure I will) I’ll use a wok or a Dutch oven instead of a large frying pan, as it was a little too full to comfortably stir. Great recipe.

Very good weeknight dinner! It definitely needs a good bit of salt- 1 lb of cabbage   1 lb of pork requires a lot. I wish NYT recipes would recommend at least a base amount of salt to start with. “Season with salt” implies a sprinkling (to non-chefs), which is definitely nowhere near enough for most dishes.
@Carly Fish sauce is quite salty, and varies according to brand. This will determine thee need for additional salt.
@Carly totally agree. I wonder if it’s so they don’t get flagged or filtered out of searches for sodium content. Unhelpful!
@Carly agreed, needed maybe a tablespoon.

Just made this for dinner with ground chicken instead of pork and really enjoyed it. I also used gochugaru instead of red pepper. Hubby and I think we may use it as a bibimbap-like base for leftovers.
@Shana Ditto on the gochugaru!
@Shana Terrific substitute!!!

Yes! Fish Sauce is brand dependent. One can be so much saltier than the other. My preference is to go light on the salt and check.
Personally, peanuts will be an addition to the recipe the next time I make it.

Such a tasty and easy dish using a Dutch Oven.    I added 1 diced Jalapeno with the garlic, ginger, and green onions for the second step (no crush red pepper).    I used Napa Cabbage (better flavor IMO), followed by additional salt and pepper before adding the pork mixture back.  Juiced one whole lime and more salt + pepper.  I used Squid Brand Fish Sauce (available at H-Mart).  Great one-pot recipe!

This is a killer recipe. It's inexpensive, it's fast, it uses easy-to-grab-on-the-way-home ingredients, and it's bursting with Southeast Asian flavors that elevate it well beyond the usual weeknight meal. Sensing it would make good leftovers, I doubled it, cooking the pork and aromatics in two batches but managing the do a double qty of cabbage in a single wok. Tip: don't be shy about the lime juice and fish sauce - they really sing here, and blend nicely with the buttery sauteed cabbage. 5/5

As others have commented, fish sauce can vary greatly in its saltiness.  Most grocery store fish sauces are heavily diluted with water and then loaded with salt to make up the difference making their flavor almost all salt.  “Good” i.e. not diluted- fish sauce adds a lot of funk and some saltiness.  I love Thai food so require good Thai fish sauce - best brand I’ve found is Red Boat-ingredients  “anchovy, sea salt.”   
Great recipe for a variety of proteins.

@MaryBretired. I concur… we have tried several different brands of fish sauce over the years and have concluded Red Boat has the most authentic and consistent flavor.  We use it in everything.  It is easily acquired thru Amazon if one cannot find it at their local grocery store.
Splendid, exactly as written.
I used a wok and that let me build a good char on the cabbage which added another layer of flavor and prevents over-crowding.

This was excellent. I used a Taiwanese cabbage, and a leftover jalapeno instead of the chili flakes. Served over brown jasmine rice. A single tablespoon of fish sauce is very salty, but it is  not enough for the whole dish, so it is important to add a bit of salt at the other stages as indicated. Next time I might try using oil only instead of butter, whose flavour might have been overwhelmed by the jalapeno.
This was good - I added chopped red bell pepper and used already prepared coleslaw (already had a bag in the refrigerator - no dressing😊).  Made for a very easy, healthy and delicious dinner.
Try this precisely as written before making changes. It’s easy, tasty, and satisfying exactly as is—a perfect example of the right kind of fusion cooking.

I didn't have any ground pork, so I made this with ground turkey with some bacon fat in addition to the butter to give it some extra fat.  It turned out great!  Super quick and easy.  I also added some onions in addition to the scallions, used a dried bird's eye chili, and threw some fresh basil in there as well, since I had it on hand.  I'd love to try this another time with vermicelli noodles instead of rice and make it as a cold salad for lunch.
Thought this was delicious and easy to make! I subbed crumbled tofu (coated with flour and corn starch) for the pork and it was great! Also added some asparagus in with the cabbage
@Irene thinking of doing tofu too!

I made this tonight exactly as written.  It needs way more ginger, garlic, and fish sauce than the recipe calls for.  I think it's on the right track, but using the proportions as written in the recipe it was pretty bland.  I'll make it again, but bump up the spices and fish sauce.

This was yummy! I used 1/2 lb of pork because a full pound for four servings seemed like too much for me, and nearly 2 lbs of cabbage. It was still full of delicious porky flavor. We used a wok, which I highly recommend. Otherwise made as written.
To paraphrase chief brody, you’re going to need a bigger pan.

@Dana

Love that line!  Thanks for the chuckle.

The point of seasoning with salt is becoming experienced with the level of salt that works for you. The wonderfully named Hugh Fearnley-Whittingstall (of River Cottage in England) encourages a good seasoning with Kosher salt when carmelizing meat, veg, etc. Kosher salt lets you be bolder.
I feel like adding cashews to the stir fry would be good in this too!
