Despite all the ginger, garlic, scallions, jalapeno, fish sauce and lime, this was rather bland.  OK as comfort food.  Maybe more fish sauce and lime and hot pepper?

Great minimal effort stir fry. I personally adjusted it to double the fish sauce (im addicted to the stuff), used white pepper, and finished it with a little chili garlic sauce, but that’s me. Easy to customize this one anyway you see fit.
@Susan same in my house. I used 2 T soy sauce and it was good. also included lime zest.
Any good substitute for fish sauce? My husband cannot abide it and he can taste it.
@Susan I will try miso mixed into a little water, I also dislike fish sauc
I used a bit of soy sauce and Worcestershire sauce, since I didn't have any fish sauce on hand.

Try Braggs Liquid Aminos (or coconut liquid aminos) -- I've been using this since the 1980s and love the stuff. Also made from soy beans, I prefer it to soy sauce -- a less strident, softer, richer, warmer, more umami-esque flavor. If soy intolerant, don't use. And use carefully, though -- it's salty -- don't need additional salt if using.
Simple and delicious! Will definitely make it again, I may bump up the fish sauce a little and reduce the butter next time.  I will for sure be adding this into the rotation.
New favorite weeknight meal! Used napa cabbage that we had on hand. Perfect recipe as written.
10/10... I did it with marinated pork chops (soy sauce/ fish sauce/ garlic) and added furkaki to the rice. TOO good. yuuuum!

Excellent dish! We prefer cabbage well cooked so after stir-frying it I added two or three tablespoons of water to the wok and put a lid on it to let it steam until cabbage was well cooked and tender before I added the meat mixture. I added a little extra lime juice as well and used a tablespoon less of the butter called for. I will keep this recipe on my favourites list.
this is REALLY good. I think 3 tbs of butter would have been too rich, i just did the 2 with the cabbage. I drained the pork so it wasnt so greasy too.

-- Dutch oven
-- peanuts 
-- cabbage really needs to be done at a blazing heat so the internal sugars can char and caramelize without it getting soggy
-- add light brown sugar when adding the cabbage or at the end to balance the flavor

4 out of 5 as written. Followed it closely with the exception of gochujang subbed for red pepper flakes, which I would def recommend. My two additions/alterations for next time will be

- use a wok or heavy bottom skillet as cabbage really needs to be done at a blazing heat so the internal sugars can char and caramelize without it getting soggy
- add light brown sugar (or palm sugar if you have it) when adding the cabbage or at the end to balance the flavor. It was desperately needing a sweet note. 

Overall, a very nice, straightforward, low effort, minimal cleanup dish. Perfect for a weeknight. Will report back when I try the updates. Happy cooking!

Nice - but of course you need to double the fish sauce, lime, garlic, ginger (or triple the garlic and ginger) - use the full recipe proportions for half the pork and cabbage.  Also, gochugaru is a welcome addition for heat.  Serve on rice noodles, too.

OK, this was bonkers good. I didn't have scallions or cilantro so I added extra garlic/ginger. It was amazing, flavorful, and easy to cook on a weeknight. I used a large enameled Dutch oven to get maximum sear/flavor.
We used thinly sliced chicken thighs instead of ground pork. Big hit!
Does anyone think that tofu instead of pork would work in this recipe?

I'm not a big cabbage fan, BUT this recipe was absolutely delicious, easy, and quick to make! It's going on our regular rotation. I added a bit more fish sauce (Red Boat brand) than called for because it needed the extra seasoning. So yummy!

I made this with tofu and it was very good. I eyeballed the fish sauce and lime juice, erring on the generous side with both. Didn’t add spice since my children were having some, but this could be good with some gochujang.
I love this recipe, it’s on a regular rotation. One note is that 1lb pork and 1lb cabbage don’t fit in a 12 inch skillet. Use a Dutch oven to avoid spillage!
Very tasty weeknight meal. I subbed a block of crumbled tofu for pork and it worked out well w additional oil and seasoning (soy sauce and salt) to taste.

Wanted to love this but with twice the seasoning and a fair amount of salt we found this pretty bland.  It is fast and economical.  We served with Jasmine rice and garnish of cilantro, lime wedges and chopped peanuts.  Glad we tried but moving on.
Delicious! Used a Wok, as nonstick pans are toxic.  Added a teaspoon of sugar, as it looked like they were going for a Thai-style sauce, and swapped high temp oil for butter. It slaps!
Swapping sesame oil for butter and using Nappa Cabbage, which cooks quickly so watch it if you want it crisp.

My husband and I loved this dish. Thought it might work well as a lettuce wrap. Next time. This is definitely in the rotation now. I agree with previous poster that it is perfect combo of tasty, quick, and cheap. Also agree that a deeper pan works well. I used a Dutch oven. Thank you Kay Chun for yet another delicious weeknight recipe. You are making me look good!
There has to be a better name for this.

Discovered from a prior NYTimes recipe: roasting Napa cabbage in the air fryer produces a sublime product. The pork can be added into the basket at the end (yes, it is no longer one-pot, but there is not a bowl to wash).
Good stuff. I added chopped dry roasted peanuts the end.

Great recipe, very tasty! Ended up being a bit oily for my taste between the oil/pork fat/butter that even the cabbage didn’t end up soaking up, but maybe next time I’ll just skip the last tbs of butter at the end.
This recipe is my favorite recipe on NYT cooking! It is so easy and quick, and the flavor is out of this world! it's like a flavor explosion in my mouth! Make this! you will not be sorry!

I made this exactly as written with 1 addition: as I waited for the cabbage to cook, I zested the skin of my juicing lime into the pan. The freshness of the lime juice and the zest gave the overall dish a very nice aromatic. Great with Thai jasmine rice.

Sheer perfection. So easy for such a big payoff. I salted moderately as I went and used Red Boat fish sauce. Ended up adding more fish sauce and lime. Basil would be fantastic if you don’t like cilantro. Will be making this again and again!

This was delicious! Looking forward to making it again. I can imagine subbing half the pork with ground turkey or chicken, as the pork flavor was less prominent than I expected anyway. The garlic/ginger/lime/fish sauce stole the show.

Had leftover Vietnamese Pork Ribs (see excellent NYT Cooking recipe here) so subbed that for the ground pork and made only the cabbage part. 

Awesome! Really good use of ingredients, so much more than just the sum of its parts. Will definitely make again. 

Don't be shy about using other proteins⁰0 here, tho I think it'd be best if it were flavored well.
Didn't like the combination of butter, fish sauce, lime and aromatic spices.  Also, this needs a lot of salt or fish sauce.

This was so incredibly delicious and easy. Perfect for a weeknight meal. As someone else mentioned, I may try napa cabbage next time. I cooked mine in a dutch oven and it came out perfectly. I may use a little soy in place of some of the Kosher salt next time and maybe a smidge of sesame oil at the end for an extra bit of dimension.

This was a real simple dish to prepare.  It literally takes no time. I did find the recipe to be a little bland so I added a little soy as well as some 5 spice powder. I served with a little chili crisp on top.

Very tasty and pretty easy/quick for family meal. A few notes: Agree with those cautious about over-salting. I put extra fish sauce and a little soy sauce for more flavor, before it had time to meld, making it way too salty.  I had to add a pound of ground beef (that’s what I had ) to balance it out. I added some honey– salty, spicy, umami AND sweet. Pulled it all together.  I used the basil suggestion too.

Great dish. Made it as written sans cilantro because I didn't have it on hand, but I will use it next time for added depth. I used Red Boat fish sauce and still needed a good amount of salt, so taste as you go and add accordingly to your tastes. The recipe reminds me of a Moroccan Beef & Cabbage recipe I've been making for years that includes golden raisins. Going to try adding some next time for a nice burst of sweetness. This is definitely going in the "weeknight" rotation.

Serviceable for a weekday meal, but a little flat. It felt like a comfort food. 

The butter in step 3 is important, but it didn’t need the bit at the end. We doubled the fish sauce and sprinkled on a little sugar. Next time we might try adding sliced water chestnuts, then sprinkling 
more scallions on the finished dish.

A great weeknight meal that is so much more than just cabbage and pork stirfry. The combo of fish sauce and butter - wow! Will it be the next miso-butter umami pairing?? Who knows!
So add that little extra bit of butter at the end - it's worth it.
I added some cooked rice noodles at the end,  doubled the sauce ingredients, and added oyster mushrooms. Delicious and easy!
I made with ground turkey and added kale. It was delicious, we will definitely be adding to the menu :)

The browned butter is the magic ingredient in this dish.  I used ground beef (my wife hates pork).  I failed in adding enough salt, so it was too bland at the end.  But the table was impatient, so in desperation I threw in another Tbsp each of fish sauce and lime.  Bam!  That brought it all together and the freshness of the cilantro and lime popped right out.  The reviews from the table were glowing.
I caramelized the pork and did it all in a wok so got some nice charred bits of cabbage.  Outstanding!

Made this in a wok, and it was quite good in the end. I had a lot of cabbage on hand, both Napa and green cabbage, which probably needed more than the small dash of salt I put in at first. While I used two Tsp. of fish sauce at first, adding more fish sauce and a strong squeeze of lime juice gave it deep flavor. (Even better with Sri racha.) I used ground turkey, which was okay, but not as sweet as ground pork. Next time!
Loved it and my leftover averse wife was happy to dig in a second time

Totally delicious. the recipe appeared when I had excess cabbage, both red and green. There was a small amount of leftover pork sausage in the fridge, so I tossed that in for a flavor boost, tThe result was wonderful.

I added some firm tofu after the pork was mostly cooked, cooked the cabbage with some shallots, then added some chicken stock once the pork and tofu were added back to the pot. I added a little chili crisp for some heat. An easy and tasty weeknight dinner.

Great easy recipe. I added 2tbsp of fish sauce instead of 1, and I also didn’t add any spice, instead had chili oil at the table. Fly by Jing pairs really nicely with it, while spice sensitive folks can just skip and it’s still very tasty!
Made this for family dinner and added Thai basil and mint along with cilantro as passed herbs. And pickled carrot/daikon as well for a bit of sweet to balance the fish sauce. Very easy, rave reviews.
So good as-is, but because of this, I'll need to double the proportions for the future - this worked for 2 adults with no leftovers!
Made a second time, this time with ground lamb, and loved it even more. A sprinkle of lemon zest adds some extra brightness.

Good stuff!  I used a small chunk of leftover pork tenderloin and it got nice and crispy-edged when cooked with the garlic, etc.  Added a bit of diced serrano pepper before it met its maker, and the rice was from the day before so I scooted the goods to the other side of the pan, added a dash of oil and cooked the rice a bit. Stirred it all together and it was great!  How much do we love cabbage?  Tons!

I made this the first time because I had some ground turkey in the freezer and half a cabbage that needed a home. It's surprisingly good, My teenager requested it for dinner tonight--sounds good!
definitely use a dutch oven.
I used ground beef (no ground pork in store).  Good recipe, easy to make.  I don't know if it's cheap - (depends on what is already available in kitchen).

Great, under 30 minute recipe to use up that last piece of cabbage and in our case spinach and onion.  very salty because of fish sauce so we had a light hand on extra salt. Definitely going to be in our rotation.
I used chicken instead of pork and a mix of Asian veg instead of cabbage and it was still perfect. This is a new favorite family recipe. Absolutely easy and soooo delicious!

Wow this is so good… and easy. I added a finely chopped jalapeño to the ginger and garlic, which I used liberally. And added part of a red pepper with the cabbage, cooked cabbage longer than suggested, and used Thai basil and mint to finish because that’s what was in my garden.

Ok, ok, I did NOT follow the recipe as written. Sorry, not sorry. I'm under the weather, didn't have ground pork. So not my intention to*disobey*, but had to use what was available.

Sauteed cabbage in bacon fat. For protein, defrosted, then sauteed shrimp.

YUM! Amazingly, it worked! Added zest from lime B4 juicing. Everything else, same as recipe. 

Just shows how versatile and wonderful this recipe is!

Delicious! I love cabbage and am always looking for different ways to use it - this was excellent! I did add a little more fish sauce, as my grocery store brand is kind of puny, and a bit more lime because I love it. Great reward for minimal effort - it's going into the regular rotation!
Enjoyed this stir fry very much. Had 90% of the ingredients on hand which made it even better!
Love this recipe! Just as written… looking forward to my leftovers!
Delicious as written. Charred cabbage with butter is a revelation!
This needs chili crisp condiment!! I’m going to do it with Taiwanese cabbage.
I loved this, but I added little soy sauce, seseme oil and hoisin, to make it a little more interesting. They would good, easy additions.

Outstanding. I couldn’t get ground pork, so used boneless pork rib and did a coarse chop of it: would do this again. I bumped up the aromatics and spices as suggested, used a teaspoon of chili crisp for the heat. Golly it was good. Dumped the leftovers on the remaining rice and tossed that around. Reheated a portion for lunch, then had a couple poached eggs over the rest for breakfast this morning. Fast, easy, really delicious.
basic. added maggi sauce, lite soy sauce, extra garlic and ginger all after tasting original recipe and if making again, will add sliced onion.
Absolutely loved this dish (didn’t want it to be over lol). I didn’t have access to good cilantro so we subbed Thai basil and it was absolutely delicious. Serious 10/10!

This recipe affirms why cabbage is my favorite vegetable! (And I love them all.) Half red and half green made for a very pretty dish. I subbed ground turkey for ground pork and it was still delicious. I, too, used a Dutch oven. The cabbage was beautifully caramelized.

Yum! Used a wok on high, 1/2 lb pork which browned when up the sides w white pepper & salt. Added ginger, garlic, leeks (scallion would be better) and reserved. 1 Tbs butter until foamed, red cabbage, celery, white pepper, little bit of salt; when softened fish sauce (add more), lemon (lime would be better), some chili flake, small amt of sriracha to taste. Tossed in vermicelli noodles soaked while cooking. Cilantro/basil and more acid/zest would be good on top. Fast, easy, fun!

I’ve cooked this several times. Always easy, satisfying, and very good. I love cabbage so I usually add extra.  Adjust lime juice and fish sauce to taste. I sometimes add extra veggies like roasted broccoli.  Maybe next time I’ll add a package of cubed tofu for extra protein and saute it with the pork or ground turkey. Very riffable.

I cook a lot of Asian cuisines and had just finished cooking up a Thai cashew chicken dish before I made this for the first time.

Instead of using salt I added soy sauce to the pork.  Many Thai dishes use both soy and fish sauce.

Coarse gochugaru is a must.  It adds a smokiness that red pepper flakes don't.

Invest in a butane torch.  It helps to char the cabbage.

Super tasty and fresh! I used closer to 1.5 lbs of pork and just slightly upped the seasonings, since the first time I made it I found 1 lbs to be a bit skimpy. I also enjoy this topped with peanuts and a drizzle of Sriracha and Kewpie mayo.

Wasn't expecting this to be a hit with the picky spouse but he really liked it. He has an aversion to fish sauce, so what I did was add soy sauce in addition to the called for ingredients. It was so delicious. Willvmake again for sure.

Third time I’ve made this. I use boneless pork loin rib, chopped bite-size (I keep a pack or two thereof in freezer). Double the garlic, ginger, fish sauce. Teaspoon of chili crisp. Easy to make and ingredients not too exotic, but truly delicious. Mix it with white rice after both are cooked and refrigerate. Good for any meal, even breakfast with a poached egg.

Wow!  We loved this, and it was an easy recipe. I swapped green beans for most of the cabbage and also added some zucchini halfway through the veggie cooking step, and it was delicious.  Excellent taste to effort ratio for a weeknight dinner.
Really good. I used crumbled tofu and vegan fish sauce to suit my diet. Otherwise made as written. Next time I may add some honey or something for a bit of sweetness. Yum!
This is so delicious. I've made it twice as written, with two minor changes: I grated the ginger and used a wok. It's easy, inexpensive, and craveable!!!
I couldn't find fish sauce in the supermarket and decided to use oyster sauce instead. It was a delicious dinner and I will definitely make it again!
Very easy to make, and quite delicious. I substituted pre-cooked rotisserie chicken for the pork, and it was fabulous. Would definitely make again.
Wonderful flavor! Light and easy. A terrific idea for a meal and one to be repeated often.
I don’t like the taste of butter. Is there an alternative that wouldn’t make the dish too oily?
@Kim a neutral vegetable oil
What’s a good substitute for fish sauce?

If you need a vegetarian alternative, I'd recommend vegan fish sauce (it's made with mushrooms and seaweed). I don't think regular soy sauce or coconut aminos would have the earthy funkiness to bring this next level the way the fish sauce does.
I enjoyed this but next time will add in a bit of sliced celery.  Just not enough veg for me but tasty.  Will do this again
Double the cabbage, use 12 oz pork, and triple the sauce.

This is excellent!! I did not have lime so used lemon and still good but I am sure the lime gives it that special acidic punch! Also used oyster sauce instead of fish sauce. I do not think you really need the last pat of butter or even 2 tbsp to brown the cabbage honestly. Will definitely make again!!!
Tasty and easy recipe.  Used napa cabbage and mint instead of cilantro for a family member who can't stand cilantro and thought that worked well.
It was a big hit for my family; the kids (ages 2 and 8) loved it! I added gochujang sauce, carrots, and bok choy since I had them. Costco microwave sticky rice was a super quick and easy add!

Followed the recipe for the most part but add some crunch: celery, red pepper and shallots.  I made many stir fries and the secret is sesame oil--it adds that Chinese restaurant taste which, to me , is lacking with other cooking oils.  A couple of squirts of hot sauce and dinner was ready.

Yum! This is delicious. Used just 1/2 pound of ground pork, and 1 jalapeno rather than crushed red peppers. Otherwise, followed the recipe exactly. I make a lot of Asian food and I was skeptical about the butter, but I think it made the dish. Great mouth feel. Will definitely make again.

I doubled the cabbage, garlic, scallions, and ginger, while keeping the amount of pork the same. Because I ran low on fish sauce, before serving rather than adding salt per the recipe, I sprinkled each dish with a few drops of tamari.
We made this as written, and while my choice of Napa cabbage was challenging in a stir-fry sense, we both loved the dish. Fusion cooking at its comfy best.

This dish is great. I used some pork belly I had braised and cooled, and rice vinegar as I was missing limes. Probably should have gotten limes but still 10/10. Topped with some fresh celery for crunch.
Given the amount of fish sauce I expect this to be quite salty.  Currently looking for reduced salt fish sauce.

Just happened to have a head of white cabbage in the frig. Used it to make this last night for dinner.  Used ground turkey instead of pork, serving it over rice linguine instead of the recommended white rice.  Absolutely delicious.

Can see putting a “Pad Thai” spin on this by adding sugar/honey/tamarind juice w/lime juice (& maybe a splash of vinegar to taste if needed) & sprinkling w/chopped roasted peanuts to serve. LOTS of possibilities for personalizing this great "template" recipe!

I added a few stalks of celery, but next time I make it I think I'll add carrots and/or peanuts (as others mentioned). I also omitted the final tbsp of butter, as it didn't look dry and I don't really need that extra fat! Definitely use a large skilled or wok, as it does get full. Excellent, fast recipe.
This is a good, week night dish, but I feel it needs a bit more liquid such as more fish sauce or a hit of soy soy sauce or even some water.
I made this with oyster sauce instead of fish sauce since that’s what I had on hand and I think it still turned out great. Very flavorful
We seasoned the meat and the rice with Penzey's Revolution (a combination of flake salt, tellicherry black pepper, cane sugar, tumeric, orange peel andcoriander.) Fantastic! This recipe is a keeper!
simple and delicious!
Ground turkey instead of ground pork, bok choy instead of cabbage.  Just as yummy - less fatty.

Tasted way too greasy even though I didn’t use any oil with when browning the 90% lean meat, used half the butter for sautéing the cabbage and didn’t add butter afterwards. I would use a small amount of peanut oil and toasted sesame oil, maybe one tbsp total I’m to sauté the cabbage. Added the gochugara powder as suggested by another commenter. Liked the addition. Might add a tbsp instead of tsp next time. Might also sauté some Shitake mushrooms with it next time I take a crack at this.

Very good. Cooked mostly as written.  Used 12” skillet, wasn’t non-stick - no issues.  Was heavy on ginger, and included a chopped red bell pepper for color & taste.  Yum.  Added 1/2 tsp sriracha when serving over rice. Quick, easy, gratifying.

Great recipe! Already a favorite 🤗 
I used red cabbage and added  two finely chopped shallots and two dried Facing Heaven chilies. I also added a little soy sauce and some caster sugar when frying the cabbage.
Couldn’t get pork so used ground chicken. It was great! Added a bit of extra fish sauce.

Really a delicious recipe. Used a chopped up Thai chile instead of red pepper flakes. Would double it next time for leftovers. Definitely worth getting a nice char on the cabbage. Good back pocket quick meal, and wouldn't hesitate to sub ground chicken (or turkey, not my thing but would still work).

I didn't have scallions, so I chopped a small onion and added that after I had started the pork.  I also sliced a red bell pepper to add after I started the cabbage.  I used a little less butter, and less salt, but it still came out just a bit salty for my family, but still very popular and asked to be put into the regular rotation.  Next time I'll season to taste at the end.  And at least once at some point, I'll swap the butter for coconut oil.
what a delicious weeknight dish with just a few simple ingredients!
Easy and delicious as written.  I'd give it 6 stars if I could.

I've made this now twice. Cabbage is an all time fave and it works really well here (the butter is what transforms this dish). Used what I had in the way of veggies, including serrano, zucchini, and baby broccoli along with cabbage and onion. Very forgiving and works well with ground chicken or turkey. Served with cauliflower rice.

This was good and I’d do it again — but the cabbage generated a lot of water in the pan and I really wish I’d poured it off before adding all the other components. This should have been a flavor bomb but it was super diluted by all the water.

This was delicious, better than anything we’ve had lately in a restaurant! We followed the recipe and only change was to substitute parsley for the cilantro as didn’t have cilantro. I used 1teaspoon salt on the meat and about 1/2 teaspoon salt on the cabbage. I also used red boat fish sauce. Yummy!
Bland…added soy sauce and that helped somewhat?
Fine. Good jumping off point. Butter and cabbage are nice together. Agree that it’s a nice way to get cruciferous vegetables in at a reasonable price. Might or might not make again.

Very quick and easy but maybe a little simple and forgettable. Needs generous amounts of salt and lime to wake things up. Wok is the right pan for this. Could probably use a Serrano or birds eye chili to make this more interesting in the future

This dish was good, but not nearly as good as the description made it out to be.  I think the oil, butter, and fat from the pork were a little heavy--cabbage felt greasy.
I served with brown rice (as suggested) and pickled red onion.  Others at the table appreciated the lime juice, but said the pickled onion really added a needed tang.

Excellent, inexpensive, flavorful meal. Made as written, except that we hate cilantro. Subbed in some fresh mint. Served over long grain basmati rice and some steamed haricot verts alongside. We like spicy food, so in addition to the red pepper flakes, I added a finely chopped red thai chile pepper. TIP: buy a package of them and keep in freezer for use as needed.
Made as directed. The fish sauce and lime work their magic. I wanted to dress up this dish a bit, so made rice with half can of coconut milk.  Yum!
This is one of those recipes that is so simple that you are suspicious of how good it could possibly be, but it is worth the hype.

This is easy and delicious, especially if you buy your cabbage pre-shredded. I added more lime juice and crushed red pepper flakes, as well as roasted salted peanuts for a little bit of crunch. Easy and delicious week night dinner.

We loved this. Did double the fish sauce for our family, but I think the recipe's 1 TB is a good place to start. You can always add after cooking if need more. We had it with jasmine rice, but you definitely don't need it if you are watching those carbs!
Two thumbs up from 5 year old twins! And quick and easy. A new staple in our house. Thank you!
@Christine H. don't freeze chillies, they lose their spiciness. Birds eye/Thai keep super well in the fridge (mine are 1 week beyond the best before end date and haven't deteriorated)

We have been making a super simple variation of this recipe for years which has two ingredients and a box of jiffy corn bread mix.  Cook one pound of sausage in a large pot and then add one small to medium chopped cabbage with a little water.  Cover and cook on medium low until cabbage is tender while stirring occasionally.  The sausage is all the seasoning needed.  Serve with the cornbread.  We have made this when camping by taking a frozen pound of sausage which keeps in a cooler just fine!
Tasty, quick, and satisfying. Made exactly as specified.

This worked well for us, which kind of surprised me given my ambivalence about cabbage.  Definitely needs more of the flavouring - more ginger, more fish sauce, more lime. But easy and quick to make.  Will make again.

Absolutely delicious and quick to make! I halved the recipe, using only one chicken breast--cut in 1 inch cubes and pulsed in the food processor.  Added the coarsely chopped ginger and some fresh turmeric plus the garlic to the last pulses.  This saved a step in an already quick prep.  Added a sprinkle of black sesame dish to each serving.  Our fish sauce was a salty one, pointing out the value in tasting at the end!
I followed the ingredients and directions exactly. It turned out delicious and was easy to make.
Tasty and quick recipe and adaptable. We added some sambal oelek to our servings.

Delicious, easy weeknight dinner.  To save calories, made as directed using riced cauliflower and pork tenderloin ground in my food processor (vs higher fat ground pork from the meat market).  Will definitely make again!

Love the dish.  Don't think I would change a thing although I used kosher salt.  I would suggest that some seasoning (salt and pepper) is good as you cook the dish, but then just adjust at the end. And you definitely want to use the fish sauce as it adds a lovely tang to the dish.
Not bad…the cabbage came out a smidge firmer than I’d like, but I’d make it again.

Oh lord this was good. Easy, fast, tasty. Butter and fish sauce is even better than butter and miso. I probably used more ginger than was called for but whatever. Will try it with gojuchang or gojucharu next time as someone suggested. This goes in the “Go-To” recipe box!
Quick and easy, and pretty delicious as written.  Next time, I might add a bit more heat (maybe some thinly sliced fresh chili pepper).
I've been making something similar to this for years, only I use a mandolin for finely shredded cabbage and noodles instead of rice.
Also good with rice noodles

I made a double batch because it is ideal for a quick reheating and lunch. The flavor profile was surprisingly umami-goodness. The proportions are quite good. We used 1/2 tsp Aleppo pepper instead of red pepper. You might want to add a sprinkle of chow mein noodles for crunchiness but it is a solid quick recipe. Use a big wok.

In my opinion, this dish needs a great deal more seasoning. I'd suggest doubling the fish sauce, and maybe adding a healthy dose of hoisin as well. And as others have said, you might need a good deal more salt, which I found to be true even with a sprinkle of MSG, but that can be remedied at dinner time with a splash of soy sauce.

I loved this recipe. The pork was perfect and the cabbage was delicious. There’s a lot of prep here to start cooking, but once everything is in place, it comes together quickly. The fish sauce and the lime juice add great flavor to the final dish!

One note: I used parsley in place of cilantro. I didn’t have any in the house, and the parsley worked perfectly.
@bocaboy thanks for that comment because I don’t have any cilantro

The night I cooked this I thought keeping the oil from the ground pork made everything too glossy and oily, but I did eat the dish without rice that night. 

The next few nights I ate the leftovers with rice and everything was perfect. 

My only small modifications were double the pepper flakes and a little extra cabbage. When reheating, it goes well with soy sauce if you’re looking for a little more salt and liquid. Speaking of salt, I used about 3/4ths tsp for the pork and 3/4ths tsp for the cabbage and was happy with overall salt flavor.
Made with ground chicken. It was fine, but not any better than a "regular" stir fry.

This was surprisingly good! Reminds me of Nam Sod. I had some cooked Italian sausage that I chopped up and used. I don't understand why they would add salt into the recipe, just add some extra fish sauce - otherwise it will be way too salty.
Great recipe! Certainly need a wok or bigger skillet. I cut the last tbsp of butter and bumped up the spices
Could you use spinach in place of cabbage?
Incredible! My family of cabbage haters couldn't get enough.
Used a wok, didn't add salt, and it was stunningly good!!!
Pretty good just as written. I think it could use a bit more fish sauce. Maybe some chopped peanuts on time if you are feeling decadent.

We just loved this recipe! Made it exactly as written except I took Michael L’s advice and doubled everything so we could have leftovers.  Just loved the Southeast Asian flavors.  And yes, don’t be shy with the fish sauce and lime.  My husband loved that we’ll have it again tomorrow!  5 stars.
Loved this dish!  I used ground chicken instead of pork and it was still delicious.  Will definitely make it again. The flavors are great and easy to make.
This was easy to make and absolutely delicious!  Next time I might use more red pepper flakes.  I think it could use a bit more kick.

Made this a few times now. Latest one I used gochujang instead of red pepper flakes, added earlier to jazz up the cabbage. Yeah, it steps it away a bit from the Thai influence, but still was delicious!
I think white cabbage is a much better fit in both texture, firmness, and taste.

We doubled the  garlic and ginger + 1 tbs of each: oyster sauce, tamari, 1 1/2 tbs of hoisin sauce and 1/2 tbs chili crisp + a bunch of asparagus. We cooked that recipe for 4 friends tonight,  they all loved it. We almost doubled everything and there were no leftovers !

I thought the pork needed a lot more cooking and a lot more seasoning.  The cabbage was perfect.  I ended up cooking onions in a separate pan, adding the pork to continue cooking, then putting it all into my food processor to break up the pork chunks even more.  Then I added water and cooked it a while until the pork was tender.  The first night I served the pork and cabbage separately.  The second night I mixed them and the entire dish tasted much better.
Very yummy. I put more garlic of course.

Made it tonight and we agreed it was terrific.  My only error was salting the cabbage .  Did not need it since I used salted butter and as others mentioned the fish sauce already adds a salt element.  But wow, easy and low effort/mess.  Served it on brown rice.
Very easy and excellent!  Definitely add the squeeze of  lime juice and the cilantro at the end. This will be added to our rotation!!!

This was so delicious and yet so simple. I don't really like pork, so I use ground turkey and added a bit more garlic and ginger to make sure it was flavorful.  I also added some rough chopped peanuts at the end with the cilantro, and it was outstanding! I did not add salt a second time, owing to the fish sauce, and it was the perfect level of salty for us.

This is just so good. I've made it many times: sometimes with chicken, often with less meat and more cabbage, and it's always great.
Six out of five stars.

Wow. I could eat this once a week for the rest of my life.
This time I used chicken (that's what I had.) It is a great meal, fast and simple, for autumn.
Excellent quick supper!
Made as directed except I used ground turkey and Napa, which I had on hand. Hubster thought it could use more heat; I thought it was fine.
Great weeknight dinner. I doubled the garlic and ginger and added extra red pepper. Very tasty.
This was absolutely fantastic and SO simple! Only change I made was some extra garlic and to serve it on rice vermicelli tossed with sesame oil & sesame seeds (I didn’t have rice).
I used left over pork roast and sliced it very thin and short pieces. The rest was totally following the recipe. This was the best cabbage I ever have eaten. I will make this again

It's easier to add salt than it is to remove it. I always take recommendations for salt in recipes.with a grain of salt. I'm 85, and I got this old by using salt very sparingly - if at all. Recipes that call for soy sauce, fish sauce, and other salty ingredients usually require no additional salt, IMHO. Taste first, and then add if necessary.

I love braised cabbage. I prefer it cooked to a soft stage with loads of onion and butter. I would think with the brief cook cooking time that this cabbage would still at that ‘squeaky’ stage when you bit into it, if one is using standard green cabbage. For a tenderer result with a quicker cook time, use savoy cabbage or Chinese cabbage. One could complicate things by sautéing the thicker stem pieces with the pork and adding the leafier green sections near the end to add greater textural form.
I only used 1/2 as much pork and it was plenty.

Used ground turkey instead of pork. Step 2: Add snow peas and sliced carrot and celery. Step 4: Add a tablespoon each of soy sauce and chili crisp or sambal oelek. Step 5: Garnish with sliced green onion.
This dish is outstanding. So good and so fast.  I wouldn’t change a thing.

I always read the comments before cooking. I usually disregard the too salty comments. In this case, trust the comments. It is way too salty. Didn’t like it because of the salt and too much fish sauce, in my opinion.
Scaled this up because I wanted to use the whole bag of lentils.  I used water, but will use 1/2 stock next time.  Added a little curry powder as well.  The herbs are a must!
I made this pretty much as is. It was surprisingly delicious and very easy. Something about the butter with the fish sauce... I used a very large pan, it was not non-stick and it was fine.

I added a can of chickpeas to boost fiber.  Subbed small onion for scallions.  Otherwise made as specified. Served with bone broth rice.  Fish sauce and lime juice in combination with butter and pork really give this simple dish some wonderful complexity. Absolutely delish!

This was the weeknight trifecta of easy/fast, "in this economy?" approved, and absolutely phenomenal. For two people I used 1/2 lb pork and half a large cabbage, kept all the aromatics etc the same. Served over white rice and we both had second helpings.

I used white pepper which really complements the pork. Thank you Kay Chun!
Quick, easy, and tasty! Love that it gave me a chance to use up the half of a cabbage that's been in my crisper, seemingly forever.
used pork tenderloin tails cut into thin strips, otherwise followed as written, with amplified amounts of Red Boat & lime juice to great effect. yes to peanutz

Thai pork and cabbage is delicious Vietnamese cold salads with fish sauce and the seasonings. Here is also delicious. This is a mixture of the two that doesn’t work. This is the worst recipe I’ve ever tried. I have never given anything but four and five star reviews and this one gets it too. I just want my ingredients back.

I was lazy and pushed the ground pork, garlic, ginger and scallions to the sides of the pane, and put the butter and the cabbage in the middle.  I added cremini mushrooms I wanted to use up (who doesn’t love pork and mushrooms?) and covered the pan for about five minutes.  Open it, stirred it up, and added the rest of the ingredients.  Love it.
I cook a lot of NYT recipes and this might be my favorite. So easy. And so delicious.
Easy. Tasty. A great fast meal when you’ve had a long day and just want to sit down and eat.

Made this for dinner and it was delicious! I used dew drop cabbage that I got from Trader Joe's, added shitake mushrooms, subbed red chiles flakes for a bit of gochugaru and reduced the butter from 3 to 2 tablespoons. Will absolutely make this again for a quick weeknight meal.
PHENOMENAL! No notes. Made zero modifications and it was perfect. Extremely easy and quick. Will be adding to the rotation!

Made for the second time. Half recipe, but full amount of garlic, ginger, and fish sauce. Subbed basil for cilantro (I have that gene). 

Really, really tasty, big flavors, very quick for a weeknight, becoming a family favorite. Great with a Viognier or southern Rhône blend.
Such a great recipe! Made it exactly as written—and it almost took me only 30 minutes.
Good recipe, though I found it a little bland in the end despite the fish sauce funk and salt. A little drizzled of oyster sauce did the trick to round out the flavors for me.
Delicious and simple!
This was so so good for how cheap and fast it was to make. Immediately added to our dinner rotation.

Did not love this. Followed recipe exactly as I always do on the first try. I wanted to like this after reading all the reviews, but this was rather bland. Orzo also came out kind of gummy but firm on the inside. Would not bother making again, but my husband liked it enough out of 3 of us.
@Sarah. Orzo?  There is no orzo in this recipe,,,

Great weeknight dinner. Super fast to put together and a nice change of pace. I added in a minced jalapeno when cooking the pork to add a little extra heat. Had soy sauce on the table, as it needed a little more salt.
Loved this. It probably did not need so much butter as the ground pork is pretty fatty. Might try with ground turkey next time

I read the top comments first and I'm kind of puzzled why so many said it didn't taste like much. I loved it as instructed... I was generous with the pepper (I used white). It was a lifesaver actually because I was pressed for time today and this recipe was quick and easy to cook.

Great weekday meal. I used green cabbage and almost doubled the ginger. It needed a little something at the end so I added sesame oil and based on another suggestion, peanuts. Excellent over brown rice
The butter just makes it !  Very easy and fast for a quick meal.

My husband and I enjoyed this a lot. Loved the cabbage. My only modifications were increasing the garlic and ginger, and that the cabbage took a couple minutes more than 5 mins to cook. May have been the size of my chop though. Also followed the suggestion and made in the wok.  So Easy and delicious and will be in the rotation for sure.
BLAND!  I even doubled the sauce and added chili garlic sauce.  It makes a lot so I am hoping to salvage the leftovers by making Nuoc Cham sauce and adding it.  What's with the 5 stars???

This was an eye-opener. I was searching for a recipe that was not cole slaw to use up extra cabbage and this looked interesting enough to at least use it up with. Wrong. What a fantastic mix of flavors, different than any other recipe I make. And the fact that it was quick and easy was just a bonus. This will go into some version of my regular rotation.
Butter was overwhelming but otherwise a great recipe!

Fairly easy recipe yielding great comfort food results.  Made this with one mod and a couple suggestions.  1) added one thinly sliced jalapeno (or maybe a serano?) pepper in either step 1 or 2; 2) double the amounts of both fish sauce and lime juice for a little more punch; 3) I'd use a wok if you have one, because it's a lot of cabbage (i used a whole head of cabbage).  But without mods?  Yes, a comfort meal.
I substituted bok choy for the cabbage and added chopped radishes as a garnish. It is delicious!
Made with sliced boneless country rib meat since that's what I had.  Came out fine.  Certainly easy to make and healthy

Very good, and I will make it again. I agree that the brand of fish sauce makes a difference. I added salt to steps, as directed in recipe, but used only 2 tsp salt. For me, it was a bit too salty (and I like salt). The fish sauce almost over powered the other flavors. Next time I'll use a different brand and reduce the amount of additional salt. I'd also use a bit more lime juice. The cabbage (5 c chopped) cooks down considered, so I wouldn't hesitate to use 6 or 7 cups to balance the proportion of pork. overall, though, very tasty!
Just made this and due to the comments rather than pepper flakes I put in some gochugaru sauce..made it nice but not overwhelming..will make again

title: Fried Cabbage  Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Too fussy. Fry the cabbage in a mix of olive oil and butter w/ s & p. When it's getting gold in spots and crisp on some edges, squeeze half a lemon over it and serve. It's delicious and far easier.

@Susan

Sure! but that isn’t southern style.

I've cooked cabbage like this for several years. I haven't used mustard, but will try it. I often finish with a splash of balsamic vinegar or a small scoop of brown sugar, or both. Everybody raves about it - even kids.
I make this with red cabbage and add a chopped white onion and finish it with champagne vinegar.
Thoughts on adding a can of, say, cannellini beans to the dish? For those of us seeking a main dish and wanting to minimize animal protein. I so want to try this.
I had the same idea, and am going to try it tonight.  More news later!

One can of cannellini beans, drained, made a main dish out of this.  Very nice meal!!  The small dash of mustard at the end helped balance the sweetness of the cooked cabbage and bacon.  A squeeze of lemon juice or a dash of your vinegar of choice would probably do so as well.

My mother (and now I) has been making something like since for 40 years. We use ground beef and often I add sliced bell peppers if I have them. Sometimes I throw some balsamic vinegar in at the end. 
Easy weeknight dinner when it is made with ground beef. I call it Polish Fries and my kids love it. One of my favorite dishes from childhood as well
Love how low cal this is. We cut the onions into bigger chunks so they didn't all fall to the bottom of the bowl and doubled the mustard and bacon to make sure there was enough for every bite.
You can add hot sauce to this when finishing the cooking for "hot cabbage" a truly Southern treat.

We make a similar weeknight meal by adding the uncooked bacon for the cabbage after it’s half cooked (crispy bacon doesn’t work as well here), replace mustard with red pepper flakes and serve over spaghetti.

This was a nice change from the typical Slavic way that I sauté cabbage. I did add at least double the mustard. To make it a more complete meal, I served it with a poached egg on top. With the leftovers, I cooked up a small amount of orichietti and added it to the heated leftover cabbage. I used the pasta water to loosen everything and make it saucy. I grated a half a cup of Parmesan cheese in it and gave it a few generous grinds of black pepper.
Adding sliced apple, reducing mustard and using a splash of apple cider vinegar gives this a sweet and sour punch. Delish!
I've done the same thing for years with brussel sprouts, sliced into ribbons.

I make this regularly; the kids and I love it! We will often substitute rooster (sriracha) sauce for the mustard or red pepper flakes. While some consider the bacon optional, bacon makes everything better!

Simple, easy, delicious. (Halved the recipe and ate the whole “2 servings” portion for dinner.) I hadn’t thought of using mustard before, but it added a nice acidic note and earthy flavor to the sweet, salty, caramelized, umami notes of the rest.
Put a fried egg on top with a splash of Tobacco for a delicious paleo breakfast!

This is just as delicious (if not better) the next day! Reheated by spreading everything on a baking sheet and popping it into the toaster oven for a few minutes until the cabbage was slightly charred around the edges.
Instead of bacon, I sautéed with 2 tbsp balsamic vinegar, 1/4 cup red wine and 1 tbsp brown sugar. Takes awhile for the cabbage to get tender, but worth the wait.
I subbed tempeh for bacon, used extra garlic, and used some tamari at the end. A success.

I used my left over potsticker filling of ground pork, minced ginger, garlic& scallion whites instead of bacon because I had to use it up. Followed your recipes going forward and it was incredibly delicious.  Trying it with bacon this weekend.

My mother was a Southern citizen ok from Arkansas. This is just like her recipe, except she would have had only yellow mustard on hand. Thanks for the memories. I will cook now that I am reminded of its delicious and comp flavor.
Usually there’s nutrition information for NYT recipes. Could it be added?
So good!  Made as written sans onion (didn't have) and was absolutely delicious.  Next time more mustard.  Didn't miss the onion.  Will try this as a filling for pierogi.
I so wanted to love this. I followed the recipe to the letter and found it meh. Might try the variations listed in comments.

This made for a simple, quick, and a very satisfying dinner with whole grain toast and goat cheese. Used a Savoy cabbage, whole onion and 2 garlic cloves. For bacon, I used tesa from NYT home cured pork belly recipe.
Question: can you freeze this?

Delicious. Sweet from the cabbage and slightly vinegary. Made it for breakfast, added 3 carrots in 1" pieces, added with onions, rosemary halfway through steaming and served topped with lightly fried eggs. Yolky herby cabbagey goodness!
Use a cast iron skillet, properly seasoned.  Non-stick cookware is coated with Teflon, a carcinogenic, toxic chemical.
Hey folks--use the bacon!

title: Galbitang (Short Rib and Korean Radish Soup) Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Great base recipe that you can totally make your own! I added some dried shiitake and kombu for a boost on umami. This went into the Instant Pot for 50 minutes and I seasoned to taste with cane sugar, fish sauce, and salt. Served with rice, sweet potato noodles, and kimchi. This was a very simple yet nourishing meal. I’d definitely refrigerate overnight to remove the excess fat next time I make this.

Simple, comforting and refreshing. I made the stew as written, with an extra inch of ginger. I did not defat the broth as aggressively as I could have and wish that I had - otherwise this was perfect. Served with kimchi and short grain rice on the side, it is a perfect light lunch or dinner. If short ribs were cheaper ($15/pound near me) I’d make this more often - I wonder how chuck roast or beef stew meat would fare?
Made this oxtail, which wss delicious. Added some shiitake too

title: Gombaleves (Creamy Mushroom Soup) Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I'd make this again 'as is' except I would dirty the extra pot to caramelize and sweat the mushrooms separately (while caramelizing the onions in the main pot).  Then add them together and proceed. Even in my large Dutch oven, the large onion + 1lb of mushrooms put out a TON of water, added a lot of cooking time, and made it hard to get that sweet sweet caramelization. Otherwise, so easy and delicious.

I've found the best way to cook mushrooms is start them first, without any butter, oil, or salt. (Pepper is ok at this point.) Stir frequently, and after a few minutes, add the butter, salt, and the onions

@Dan W
Great idea! Will try it out

@Victoria

I toss the mushrooms with oil and herbs, then roast them. They turn golden with no effort. It’s so much easier! I think 425°. Sure, you have to wash the sheet pan but you won’t burn the bottom of your pan or smoke yourself out. Then just add them into the pot and go from there.

This is almost identical to the Hungarian mushroom soup I make. To amp up the mushroom flavor, I also add about an ounce of dried porcini mushrooms that I have rehydrated in 1/4 cup hot water. After the mushrooms have softened, I chop them up and add them and the water used to hydrate them to the soup. Also, a little dill goes a long way in our house, so I often just use thyme.

@dkrieger  Except for the last sentence, I could have written the exact comment!  However, the last sentence does strike home because I love dill and my wife hates it!  100% on the dried mushrooms (any kind) and adding the mushroom water to the stock.
@DKrieger- fantastic idea. Porcini is the butter of vegetables - makes everything better!

@DKrieger
Thanks for the dill comment. I’m extremely allergic to dill and was wondering if more thyme or other herbs would work.
This is almost identical to the The Moosewood Cookbook’s,  “Hungarian Mushroom Soup.”

@Shari Forcina can you let me know how it's different? I made the Moosewood recipe often and with great success in the '90s,  but every time I've revisited it in recent years, it hasn't been as good. I think I did something with the roux back then that I can't remember how to replicate. I'm wondering if the roux directions here would help.

@GG
I am an iconoclast when it comes to roux.
1)  I use less butter/oil and I cook the roux until it starts to look dry or crumbly. That cooks the flour past the "raw" state. 
2) When I add the liquid, I make sure it's cold, then pour it in all at once and immediately whisk like crazy. And I *never* get lumps. My theory is that the cold liquid solidifies the fat and whisking like mad breaks up the solidified fat and flour mixture and eliminates the lumps before they can happen.

I am 77 years old and I have been making bechamel, etc., using this technique for well over 50 years, and never a lump. 

This is a challenge to the NYT test kitchen: Try it. Several of you, please. (Melissa Clark? Eric Kim? Vaughn Vreeland?) And please report back. I'm longing to hear about your results. And remember: immediately whisk like fury!

@GG i made moosewood’s this weekend, I was surprised to see the identical soup here! yes, a roux with 1 cup of milk cooked 10 min, and then add 1.5 cups broth. all the same spices and seasonings EXCEPT no thyme, and tamari instead of worcestershire. i usually add a touch more broth :)
Hi. Could Greek yogurt replace the sour cream?
I did, full fat whole delicious Greek yogurt. Yum!
@Margo if you want it to.
@Margo I used nonfat greek yogurt and it was delicious

This is the first NYT recipe I didn't have to tweak the flavors. It is delicious as written. I caramelized my mushrooms first in butter and red wine. Removed them from the pan, added a little more butter, and sauteed the onions and garlic. I added the mushrooms back in at the end. Next time, I would add another cup of mushrooms and finely dice them to ensure you get mushrooms in every bite.
So good! Def double this!
Moosewood Cookbook was my first cookbook. This recipe, though familiar is lighter with the addition of lemon juice. I do use at least three kinds of mushrooms and mix my onions. I love this soup!
This is almost identical to the Moosewood Cookbook's Hungarian Mushroom Soup. You are remiss if you do not cite that as a reference.
Why in the world would you serve this  in  tiny cups as an appetizer? This soup begs to be lunch, in a big bowl.

It would be helpful to have some kind of quantity indication any time you write "season with salt and pepper." A light sprinkling? 1/2 t. each? Something else? Since this comes so early in the recipe it's a while before we can taste and adjust.
@Bike Walk Bake Barb this seems to me like it would fall under the “personal preference” category….
@Bike Walk Bake Barb I SO agree with you! Having a starting point would be so much more helpful than “salt to taste”!

@Bike Walk Bake Barb Generally, when you see salt added early on to a sauté its primary purpose is to help draw the water from the veggies. You do not need a lot, and you do not want to overdo salt this early in a recipe because you can always adjust salt at the end. Personally, I save pepper for the final seasoning of a recipe. In other words, it’s just a light sprinkling this early in the recipe. 😊

It took me longer than the recipe called for to sweat the mushrooms. I added more lemon and a bit more garlic than the recipe called for. Very good and next time I would add some kombu flakes at the beginning.

A/A+. Try again.  As per tips;  Cook mushrooms separately without butter until all liquid evaporates then stir in butter.  After onions , garlic, spices cooked add mushrooms.  Also dehydrate 1 oz.porcini in 1/4 c hot water after dicing, including soaking water.

Hungarian here. Never heard of dill or MILK ?? in mushroom soup. We make the roux with just butter (or actually goose fat/lard) and flour. And much more common today would be to mix a tbsp of flour in the sour cream and thicken the soup that way, omitting the roux entirely.
This was wonderful! Lots of flavor and although lots of steps, easy to prepare. I cheated and used a food processor for chopping…..
I made this soup and followed the recipe to the T. Delicious but while I was eating it I wondered about using a T spoon of Sherry instead of the lemon. May try it the next time I make it.

Question: You suggest freezing. Is it possible to freeze a soup/sauce with flour in it? My enchilada sauce breaks down if frozen, have tried both flour or corn starch, but that recipe uses a roux to begin and this soup calls for four incorporated toward the end.  So maybe that is the difference?

@Patricia freezing cheese sauce aka macaroni cheese sauce works and that uses a roux and no acid. Enchilada has tomatoes aka acid. Acid’s job is to break down stuff, that’s why vinegar aka acid is a good cleaner. I think it’s the acid and thawing processes happening simultaneously that’s breaking down the flour after the sauce defrosts. But if you post your question on quora or reddit, you should get a more qualified answer (a food scientist hopefully). I’m just a nerd

I added 1/2 cup of uncooked wild rice at the end of step 2 and cooked it much longer (about 50 min to soften the rice) before moving on to step 3. Added a bit extra liquid but it would have been fine without, too. 

Highly recommend adding wild rice!

I also subbed Greek yogurt for the sour cream (it’s what I had) and it worked great.

This was incredible. I was surprised how well the flavors of dill and paprika blended—neither comes across too strong. I topped bowls with dried mushrooms rehydrated in cognac and then sautéed in butter. Highly recommend.

To add a bit more umami (if you don’t have dried porcini) you can add a teaspoon of miso paste to the mushrooms toward the end as you caramelize them - just don’t add more than a pinch of salt! Also, be sure to use a silicone spatula to keep it from sticking to the bottom of the pan and burning.

As others have said, this is almost identical to The Moosewood Cookbook Hungarian mushroom soup which I've been making since the 1980's, always to rapturous reviews.  You can make it more "refined" by increasing the amount of mushrooms or more "earthy" by increasing the onions.  Use good Hungarian paprika.  I also will make a big batch of the mushroom/onion/paprika/dill and freeze to pull out later and add the stock and white sauce to finish the soup.

I made this for my local elks lodge. I had never cooked anything and thought it would be a simple starter recipe. Boy was I wrong. First of all I couldn’t find any elk meat so I had to hunt my own. That took a week and almost lost my eyeball. Then I had to find a lodge but there was none at the market so I had to build one. By the time I got to the salmon course I was tuckered out. Fortunately my local elks only had one member, me, so I didn’t get a ton of negative feedback for my first attempt. But I will say, 116 degree weather is not ideal for a hot steaming bowl of soup made of elk and wood.
Yummy yummy in my tummy
Do they mean Hot Hungarian Paprika?

The recipe calls for regular paprika, which is often sold as "Hungarian" paprika. That said, you can use hot paprika if you want a spicier soup, or smoked paprika, or a combination. I use a blend of smoked and regular to delicious results.

To clarify: you can use any paprika, but Hungarian is generally of higher quality than your generic grocery store paprika. The heat level of the paprika is up to you. Sweet, hot, or smoked all work, or a combination of two or more of them.

In Gordon Ramsey’s Master Class, he says to cook mushrooms without releasing so much water, get your pan Very Hot. Then add a little olive oil then the mushrooms and cook for a few minutes. Then add butter to brown the mushrooms a golden hue. I’ve done this several times recently and No Water! I’m not sure it would work in a Dutch oven so I’d probably do as others in a separate pan first.

This is a great soup for mushroom lovers -- it made me think of a goulash but lighter and meatless. I tried to wing it on a second try but it didn't quite succeed the way the first batch did. It does take time for the onion and mushroom to get to their favored consistency.
How critical is the 1 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce?  I’m generally not a fan of it.
I LOVE this recipe.  Even though there is no meat, the flavor is rich, satisfying, deeply flavorful.  Will cook this again (already have since the first batch disappeared so quickly!)
Can you substitute non-dairy milk for whole milk?
This is basically the Moosewood Lodge recipe. I’ve been making it for years.
Made with 3 types of mushrooms for a dinner party- just stopped at Step 3 until I wanted to serve the soup. SO WONDERFUL! Everyone loved it.

Only complaint is that it’s all gone same night I made it - this should be scaled up to leave some left overs
It was great
Absolutely fantastic. Myteenage  son who despises soups and is all about meat in his diet loved it. So tasty, so nutritious

This is one of the best soups I have ever made. I used cremini mushrooms as the base. I followed other's suggestions: used separate pans for sweating the mushrooms and onions; coarsely shopped about a third of the mushrooms; added 0.5 ounce rehydrated dried porcini mushrooms -adding the excess water to broth. Will definitely make again.
Made as written for dinner over rice.  Delicious!
Anyone tried this with low fat milk?
Any suggestions on how to make this dairy free that does not include coconut?
I also boosted the broth with 1 cup water that had steeped dried mushrooms, and added a little bit of caraway seeds. The flavor is great, will definitely make again!

made a couple of substitutions based on what I had on hand -- Greek yogurt for sour cream and red wine vinegar for lemon juice.  absolutely delicious.  rich and comforting on a fall evening.  Pinot Noir the perfect compliment.
This was Really good! I used Paprika I got recently on Budapest. It was hot!  For us, it was perfectly balanced with the creamy soup. Loved it and will make again as the weather gets colder.
Did not get the some colors (red) as shown in the recipe. Mine turned out mire greyish.

Years ago, I worked in a restaurant that served homemade Hungarian mushroom soup every night. This recipe is a perfect replica. I used a combination of oyster and cremini mushrooms. The only paprika I had on hand was Spanish smoked paprika, which worked well in lieu of the Hungarian paprika. Served the soup with a big loaf of crusty bread and butter. It's quite an easy recipe to make.
Not so fabulous. Wouldn’t make again . No distinctive flavor
If my grandmother were alive, she'd tell you that sour cream is never optional in Hungarian food!
Great flavor!

Really delicious! Even though I made the mistake of using Better than Bouillon veggie broth and mushroom broth concentrate for the 3 cups broth, and it's way too salty. Will make again, just with different broth. Oh, and I followed Victoria's advice and cooked the mushrooms and onions separately. It would have taken forever if that were all in my soup pot.
It took me about 20 minutes to brown the onions and mushrooms. I added some white wine. Used mixed mushrooms. Added the flour and milk as directed by the recipe. Delicious

I love, love, love this recipe. After the first time I made it, I doubled it. It does take forever to cook the mushrooms and onion slowly, but the caramelization is worth it.. I saw a comment where they did it in two separate pans, that seems like a great idea. The only thing I do different is add a dash of Cayenne pepper.

It felt like a lot of steps, but was ultimately worth every minute. This yielded a rich, creamy soup that was perfect for a cold winter night. Made it exactly as written; next time I would swap in a teaspoon or less of smoked paprika for some of the regular.
"This is savory."
excellent flavor but missing some texture -- crunchy pepitas or breadcrumbs maybe

Excellent soup! I tripled the recipe and split the mushroom and onions across the pot and a pan for caramelizing. Even so, it took probably 20-30 minutes to caramelize them. The rest of the soup came together quickly though so I think it was worth it. Great flavor—very earthy and thick with a little kick of heat which I didn’t expect (must be the paprika).
Delicious soup, I used a combination of foraged oyster mushrooms and crimini. Many compliments!
Made as directed. Ever so delicious!
I have questions. What kind of Hungarian paprika, smoked or sweet? Can you use plant milk in place of the dairy milk?
This was soooooo good! The ultimate comfort food on a cold, dreary day. I followed the recipe and wouldn't dare try to improve a thing!

This is very lovely but it is without question an appetizer. I made it (following the suggestion to cook the mushrooms first!) and enjoyed but made the mistake of serving it as more of a main course. Oops.

Wonderful! Made almost exactly as written, but I may have had a few more cremini mushrooms than called for, and also added some chanterelles I had on hand. Perfect as a satisfying main course served with a piece of toasted, good quality bread on the side. And the leftovers for lunch were just as delicious!

Serious Eats just had an essay about the Moosewood recipe and I was inspired. I made it using a half cup of porcini soaking liquid that I had in the freezer and 3 cups of chicken stock. Used 2x the dill, because we like it.
I skipped the roux- just cooked it down for a half hour and then puréed half of the soup. Excellent.
Wow delicious. Amazing balance of flavors. Comforting and warming. Served with bread and noodles.

This is like a delicious beef stroganoff soup! I haven't gone through all the comments so someone may have already said this. I did the recipe exactly as is and loved it. It's definitely an app and not a main, so I cooked a steak and made a steakhouse salad to go with it. Soooo good.
My partner who actively dislikes mushrooms had two bowls. The whole family was raving. Outstanding!

made exactly as is - but to the mushrooms, as many commenters have added more time
is necessary. also - i turned up the heat to high - since the mushrooms expelled a ton of water and it would’ve taken forever to get a full evaporation on medium heat. flavors here are amazing - love it!!!
@TCookie thanks for sharing. I made the moosewood version for years. and now am doing low carb so I was wondering about not using flour
Worked out very well.  Next time I would make a white or blond roux instead of adding flour to milk,  I found the final product still had a bit of raw flour taste which the roux would eliminate.

Really good and super easy.  I used a packaged mix of mushrooms from the store, 1 T of Worcestershire sauce instead of 1 tsp, and 1.5 T of cornstarch instead of flour (to keep it gluten free).  Goes well with fresh baked bread.

I want to try this again with some tweaks like deglazing with a splash of dry sherry, or perhaps roasting the mushrooms in the oven to coax out a little more flavor from them. All in all it was a very silky soup and the seasonings were good. I've made a similar soup in the past with brie melted into it and perhaps that's why I feel like it's just missing something.

I made as directed and was surprised at how thick it was and felt there was a bit too much dill- after reading the comments I would replace the sour cream with full fat Greek yogurt and dice an additional cup of mushrooms to disperse more mushrooms throughout the- I agree, replacing the Worcestershire sauce with tamari would be good

This is so good! I followed the recommendation to roast the mushrooms instead of browning with the onions. It made them nice and chewy. I also had no idea what Hungarian paprika was, so I just used smoked paprika. Worked out great!

Delicious! I made ours vegan (plant based butter, sour cream, and oat milk) and although I misread the recipe and started with 1 tsp of paprika (doh), luckily I realized before I served. Also used fresh herbs instead of dried  I’ll try again following some of the suggestions, I think this will become a household staple!

Absolutely delish.  I wasn’t sure if my “Hot” Hungarian Paprika would be too much for my wife so I went half and half with “generic” paprika.  Perhaps next time I will use my smoked paprika.  Anyway, the wife was pleased!
It was very flavorful without being spicy.

Very good! I used half cremini mushrooms and half baby bellas. Two teaspoons of regular paprika and one teaspoon smoked. I made a couple of minor changes as follows: I sautéed the onions first then added the mushrooms and cooked them down, but didn’t brown them. And for the acid I added a tablespoon of sherry and a teaspoon of lemon juice. I’ll make this again.

I doubled this because 4 appetizer servings were not going to be enough!  Even though I usually make half recipes at this stage of my life.  I wish the Times had a scale function.  I always have to write out the revised quantities when I double or halve a recipe, because if i don’t it’s a sure thing that I’ll add the wrong amount of something.  

This came out great.  Browning 2 lb of mushrooms took forever so I was glad I heeded the commenters who said to do them separately.   I then did the onions in the same Dutch oven and added the mushrooms back to continue the prep.

One of the tastiest things I've made from NYT Cooking.  Used crimini & shitake mushrooms.  Followed other comments and caramelized the mushrroms first, removed them and did the same with the onions which added a few minutes to the cooking time but worth it.  A bit of smoked paprika too.  Killer soup.
Great taste, will double next time for sure"big" bowls of soup.
Any thoughts on using vegan sour cream? I usually replace whole milk with Oatly oat creamer with good results. But not sure about sour cream in this soup... anyone try it?
Really delicious soup! I didn't have sour cream but used full fat Greek yogurt and that worked well.
I cook this soup each fall. Using chanterelles and adding some stripes of green peppers. Fresh dill is a must. I call it Chanterelles chowder here at the Oregon coast.

title: Mornay Sauce Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Recipe could use a tip to warm the milk in a microwave. Warm milk mixes into the roux more easily than cold.
I always simmer the milk with 1/4 onion stuck with a couple of whole cloves per Julia Child . It wakes up what can be a bland sauce

My favorite use of Mornay sauce is the traditional French one: as an accompaniment to seafood: salmon, char, halibut, even scallops. But let each person at the table decide whether to put a spoonful on the fish, or on the potatoes, rice, or vegetables instead. Almost everyone (children included) will want this rich but mild sauce on something, right up to apple tart.
Oeufs Chimay: eggs, duxelles and mornay.

That's right, agreeing with other comments; Béchamel has a hint of onion. One good way is to stud a half onion with a couple of whole cloves and let that simmer in the milk while heating in a saucepan.
This is exactly the base for my Mac & cheese, & how I “build” it. I incorporate 4 different cheeses in average, including some additional shredded as assembled.
Milk needs to be heated and slowly whisked in warm. Won't incorporate well if cold.
I always warm the milk, brings the sauce together more smoothl, and faster.  And I've been using oat flour, works fine, reduces indigestion for me.
Add dry mustard and sub a little beer for the milk for Welsh rarebit
Am I the only one not feeling keen to press plastic wrap on my hot sauce?  Do we have a 21st century alternative to plastic wrap to prevent a skin from developing?
@MARGARET G. Use parchment paper!
Was very good as a sauce with Salmon. Very beginner friendly.

Reduced fat milk works just fine in this recipe; the butter has enough fat to deliver the texture you expect from such a sauce.
Wonderful for ham & asparagus crepes!

title: Gai Lan (Chinese Broccoli)  Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: Gai lan, or “green vegetable” (青菜), is a common and very simple side dish in the “water country”around Suzhou, Shanghai, Hangzhou and Shaoxing. In fact, adding a splash or two of Shaoxing wine into the pan might not hurt things a bit…
Gai lan is 芥蓝 in Chinese, not "green vegetable/青菜." The latter usually refers to bok choy.

Slicing leaves in half and stems into 2” to 3” large angled pieces makes the Gai Lan cook quickly.  I cook with minced garlic and ginger in a few tablespoons of olive oil.  After 2 to 3 minutes stirring on high heat the Gai Lan is a beautiful deep green and doesn’t need any further ingredients.
The sesame oil can be applied when plated. You will get the full fragrance of it that way.

Agreed - I would never use toasted sesame oil for the heating stage of cooking, only added at the end for flavor, or else in a marinade. I made this with a generous amount of peanut oil and even used defrosted frozen garlic and ginger cubes (from Trader Joe's) because I was hungry and tired -- and it was simple, bright green, and delicious.
This dish cries out for a couple of dollops of oyster sauce!
Okay to use common broccoli?
Why don't you give it a try? What is the worst that can happen?
We like to top it off with oyster sauce.
We've always stir fried, usually with beef, garlic, etc.  Never boiled or steamed.

I found the ginger was too overpowering. I would reduce the ginger to 1/2 teaspoon. Instead of adding additional oil. I prefer to blanch the green and then toss it in a teaspoon of garlic that has been heated in a tablespoon of olive oil.
Would it be wise to use baby bok choy if gai lan isn't available near me?
I always enjoyed this dish in China. I’ve found that here in the USA, with my other menu items like roasted salmon and rice, I like to add hot sesame oil as a garnish before serving.

title: Chicken Lo Mein Recipe
author: Kay Chun

comments.commentBody: I recommend making a 1:1 slurry of light soy sauce and corn starch (1 Tbsp of each), whisking it together, mixing with the sliced chicken, and letting this sit covered on the counter for 30 minutes before cooking. It much improves the texture of chicken in stir frys, even when using chicken breast, and the residual corn starch on the chicken with help thicken sauces. Also, instead of rinsing the noodles, drain them and put them on a tray in the fridge to dry. This helps keep them springy.

@Frank I agree with your very good suggestions. I sometimes make a slurry using Chinese cooking wine and arrowroot powder (1 Tbs each) which likewise marinates the chicken and helps it retain good moisture while stir frying.

Shaoxing can be replaced by sherry fairly easily. Black vinegar is harder because its flavor it very unique—kind of roasted and deep. The only thing that comes to mind is balsamic but I wouldn't go that route. I think there's enough other flavors and such a small amount of it that something like cider or malt vinegar will give you the acidity and a little flavor without getting in the way or punching up flavors that might not work with the dish.

Love a recipe that doesn't over complicate by using half tablespoons this or three quarters teaspoons of that--this is a recipe that will easily go together with one trusty implement--a tablespoon and easy to measure components!
Thanks from someone who hates having twenty measuring spoons out, and wonders how much did that recipe say?
My wonderful local Asian grocer directed me to “Chinkiang Vinegar” which is “Chinese Black Vinegar”. However, no where on the label does it say “Black Vinegar”.
@Bob does it need to say that it is black vinegar since you already know that it is black vinegar?
this comment helped me find the vinegar. I'm thinking it's a helpful comment for others.@Mike in Oz
What are the best options for replacing this vinegar and wine for something I may already have in my kitchen?
Try balsamic vinegar for the Chinese black vinegar and dry sherry for the Shaoxing wine.  These are both common subs.
white pepper always

I have read that Worcestershire sauce is a good replacement for black vinegar.  Balsamic vinegar can be too sweet.  That being said, I have black vinegar, but I live near,any Asian food stores and also have ordered it online (kept in either the fridge or a cool place, it lasts a very long time.
As for the cooking wine, someone commented on another NYT Cooking recipe that manzanilla sherry is a good substitute and I concur!  Also, sake is another alternative.  Bon appétit!

You could substitute pretty much any protein for the chicken.  I used beef because I had a small piece of flank steak in the freezer, but I think pork, shrimp, extra-firm tofu, turkey, maybe lamb, etc. would work just fine.
@Don—or any leftover meat and/or veggie can be used, cutting length of “cooking” time.
I learned how to make almost the exact same dish watching my best friend's mom cook.  I've had many people ask how can somebody as white as you make something like this?

I’m confused by the direction regarding thinly sliced snow peas?  I think I’ll either leave them whole or possibly cut in half after I de-string them and trim the hard little end. Much smaller and I’d think they’d be difficult to pick up with chopsticks.
How can I get that smoky wok hei flavor without a blow torch?

Why is EVERY oriental dish these days destroyed by pounds of Cabbage ? Almost everywhere you go nowadays - you order Chow Mein & it is almost totally Pan Fried Noodles (Good) & a large Bowl of Cabbage (Huh?)  with a Thimble full of tiny Frozen veggie mix from a bag. Sorry that ain't Chow Mein. 
It got so I missed decent Chow Mein SO much I searched it out - found some recipes & Advice & learned how to make it myself. A few times it turned out very good - better than take out for sure
Because cabbage is dirt cheap!
Yes ,that makes sense ( "Because it is cheap") - but doesn't make it good ,especially if a person dislike Cabbage
@Horase it was $5.69 a head at Stop n Shop here in Westchester, NY this week! I’ll sub broccoli slaw.

I’m someone who loves having leftover meals in the fridge but hates having leftover fresh ingredients in the fridge. So I made this with several tweaks with that in mind and it came out great:

Used a whole small Napa cabbage and just halved it and sliced it with a knife (I’m sorry but I cannot imagine grating a Napa cabbage). Cut out the bean sprouts, used a large carrot but grated it with a box grater, and used a whole package of snow peas that I just ran a knife through. Did the velveting technique on the chicken where you marinate it in a bit of cornstarch and soy sauce while you prep everything else. Doubled the sauce quantities. Used a whole 1lb package of shelf stable chow mien egg noodles prepared per package directions. 

It all barely fit in my 15” skillet and it took a while for the veggies to cook down but the result was quite tasty and made a lot of leftovers. If you prefer having two extra ready-to-eat meals in the fridge instead of half a Napa cabbage you have to use before it goes bad, give these tweaks a try.
For those saying the ratio of noods to veg is too noodle heavy, I always at least double the amount of veg (and adjust the sauce amount) in any recipe that combines a carb with a veg

I have heard that a mix of balsamic and rice vinegars is a good sub for black vinegar, I think I read that in a Milk Street recipe.  

But black vinegar is a great thing to have in your pantry if you can find it and like cooking asian food.  It has a very interesting flavor which will immediately remind you of Chinese take out.

I have saved this to my recipe box.  Will try to develop a version containing a reasonable amount of sodium.  I started tracking my sodium intake and it is way above healthy levels.  This recipe has so much sodium in it, maybe I would just go ultra-low sodium on the next day or two to even things out.

I made this with Mark Bittman’s Chinese BBQ pork instead of chicken. Otherwise followed the recipe, except for subbing out snap peas for snow and left out the bean sprouts as I couldn’t find them in dead of winter. Was rather delicious IMHO
